title.rendered: Episode 867: Making Positivity a Habit

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Norm Dominguez joins the podcast this week to share his Positivity Pillars of Strength and Positivity Umbrella to help you excel in networking, business, and life.

The Positivity Pillars of Strength

The Pillars of Strength are the core values of positivity.

  • Never give up.
  • Show that you care.
  • Accept responsibility.
  • Treat with respect.
  • Keep your word.
  • Always think “Remarkable.”
  • Enjoy a little craziness.

Norm’s Positivity Umbrella

A Positivity Umbrella is a collection of two-word messages that you can own–and repeat when you need them. Norm’s messages are

  • Be Yourself
  • Abundantly Grateful
  • Think Remarkable
  • Stay Calm
  • Reality Check
  • Intense FAITH (Freedom, Attitude, Imagination, Trust, Hope).

Links

  • Visit Norm’s website, www.possiglobal.com.
  • Read Norm’s book, The Magic of Positivity: Be Positive and YOU Will Be Happy.

Complete Transcript of Episode 867

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in the Philippines this week. It is my first visit to BNI Philippines, and I’m really excited to be here and meet all of the BNI members in this country.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. And who do you have with us today?

Ivan:
I have a long-term friend of mine, Norm Dominguez, Norm. I first met Norm as a [BNI] member back in the 1980s, and [he] became the local regional director and Executive Director, and then became the National Director for BNI. He came to work at BNI headquarters, and the Chief Operating Officer and the Chief Executive Officer of BNI. He’s been in the organization for more than 36 years. It’s more than 36 years – we’re coming up on 40, Norm.

Norm:
It’s 36. We’re 36 and a half now.

Ivan:
All right. Well, you’ve been in a long time.

Norm:
A long time.

Ivan:
A lot of different positions. You’re now a member of the BNI Titans Chapter in Scottsdale [Arizona U.S.A.), I believe.

Norm:
Yes, sir.

Ivan:
That’s fantastic.

Norm:
It’s fun.

Ivan:
I’ve got Norm on this podcast. He is the co-founder of Possi Global, a global positivity movement that strives to make the world a better place. He is the author of “The Magic of Positivity 3rd Edition, Be Positive and YOU will Be Happy!” And he hosts a weekly program on Zoom, The Positively Remarkable Forum, and he’s been married for more than 46 years with three children — three adult children and four grandchildren. And one of your children, Nate Dominguez, is [BNI] Executive Director in the Phoenix / Scottsdale area [of Arizona].

Norm:
Yes.

Ivan:
Great to have him as an Executive Director, and I’m going to be introducing him in the not-too-distant future as one of the Guest Experts on BNI Podcast. So welcome to BNI Podcast, Norm. It’s great to have you on.

Norm:
Hey, Ivan; our friendship does go back a long ways, and it’s a pleasure to be with you today.

Ivan:
It’s good to have you here. Your book is a really good book. I like it. You want to talk a little bit about the Pillars of Strength that you talk about in your positivity book?

Norm:
It will be my pleasure. You know, we think of Pillars of Strength, but it’s a term we use that you could substitute “life values.” And when we were looking up life values, we couldn’t find anything. So all of a sudden, let’s create our own, and we want to call them something else — so it’s equivalent of values of life, and we call them Pillars of Strength, and there happened to be seven of them. They range from never give up, show that you care, accept responsibility, keep your word, enjoy a little craziness, treat with respect, and always think remarkable.

Ivan:
Your favorite word, remarkable. Ever since I’ve known you, whenever I’ve asked you how you doing, you’ve always said remarkable, which I think is interesting, because it could be remarkably good, remarkably bad. But generally speaking, with you Norm, I would say it’s remarkably good.

Norm:
Well, I’ve enhanced it a little bit, and because I’ve improved remarkable by 100%. Because now if you ask me how I am, I’ll just say “positively remarkable.”

Ivan:
There we go. I like it. That’s good. So talk about the Law of Positivity, which you talk about in the book.

Norm:
The way we define the Law of Positivity is, when you have and practice ‘positude’ or positive attitude, you will become more excellent whatever you do. And then our tagline follows that, and you’ve already mentioned it, Ivan, Be Positive and YOU will Be Happy.

Ivan:
You know, I think you know this, Norm. I’ve done surveys a few times with BNI about what it takes to be successful in networking and in every survey I’ve ever done, having a positive attitude always comes up in the top two or three categories — the top two or three items in terms of what it takes to be successful at networking, you know, being positive. And I think that’s interesting, because I didn’t really expect it to be in the top two or three when I first wrote the survey. But now, whenever I do the survey, it always pops up there. People want to do business with people who have a positive attitude, which, in a way, doesn’t shock me, but it did at the time when I first did it.

Norm:
Well to me, positivity, or being positive, is a foundation. I mean, the way I look at it is, and if I can refer over to what was developed by BNI, and that’s Core Values. And I look at your Givers Gain core value as the centerpiece and there are seven of these core values, but the bookends that hold that all together is positive attitude and building relationships. And so there you go — Givers Gain, Positive Attitude, and Building Relationships. You blend that and with us, we picked positivity as a mode of operation to establish our global positivity movement.

Ivan:
Mm hmm. Tell me about your Positivity Umbrella. What is that?

Norm:
Well, an umbrella is a way to protect yourself, so to speak. And a Positivity Umbrella consists of a half a dozen two-word messages, and they have to be owned by the individual. So, for example, my Positivity Umbrella consists of: be yourself; abundantly grateful, which I am abundantly grateful to be with you today, sharing this information; always think remarkable — we’ve mentioned that; stay calm. You’ve got to take a deep breath. Ivan, many, many years ago, you recommended a book called Stay Calm, and I also met the author of that, and I was going into a difficult situation, that’s why you recommended it, and it really helped me deal with that situation. [It] required a trip to Europe, but by golly sakes, it all worked out as we go; reality check — how you keep yourself in balance; and then finally, intense faith.

And when I say intense faith, having traveled around the world, I took the word faith and made it an acronym. The F represents freedom, the A represents attitude, the I represents imagination, and the T stands for trust, and finally, the H represents hope. And right now, in the state of affairs around the world, we have to really, really, really have a strong feeling of hope so we can get through all this.

Ivan:
Yeah. During covid, I came up with the phrase “BNI is a beacon of hope in a sea of fear.” World is kind of a crazy place, but BNI, really, I think, gives a lot of businesspeople hope, like you’re saying.

Norm:
I agree, I agree.

Ivan:
And can I just say about remaining calm. Of all the people I have known, of all the businesspeople I have known in the last 40 years, you are, without a doubt, the calmest person that I have known in terms of dealing with chaos and stuff around there. And it’s been truly amazing watching you be calm when I was ready to pull my hair out.

Norm:
Well, I think, you know, it’s operative in terms of what we’re surrounded by. We know we’re going to have situations crop up. One of the things I learned and stay calm – take a deep breath. You don’t have to respond instantly. And in that deep breath, I really start thinking of positive things. How are we going to make this situation come out the way we need it to come out? And now it’s just as I’ve become a little more seasoned in life, whether it’s personal, whether it’s professional, or whether it’s staying something in between, being calm is the operative way to handle it.

Ivan:
Yep, as much as possible, I couldn’t agree more. One more question for you. Why is positive attitude so important in all facets of life?

Norm:
Oh, gosh. It impacts the way we engage in all of our activities, be they professional, personal or… plus, it influences how we can make the world a better place. And to me, I give attention to joy, happiness, love, passion, and peace. I don’t want to make that sound like I’m exaggerating about it, but it really gives us the perspective to level things out and move forward in really a positive way. That’s why we have our pillars of strength, and that’s why we call it the magic of positivity.

Ivan:
Yeah. Really strong book. I really like it. Norm. And for those of you who are interested in checking it out, go to www.possiglobal.com, check out the book. It’s available on Amazon, isn’t it?

Norm:
Yes, sir, all around the world.

Ivan:
Yeah. So, you can also get it all around the world.

Norm:
[On] almost every Amazon platform, you either type in the title or just type in my name, Norm Dominguez, and it pops up.

Ivan:
Any last thought before we wrap up, Norm?

Norm:
You know, I’m excited about the direction of not just positivity, but taking control of the things that we are surrounded by. And when you do that, the outcome is going to be generally in your favor, and you can be helping others at the same time.

Ivan:
Well, thanks for being on, Norm. We’ve been friends for many, many, many years, and I look forward to continued friendship with you. And you need to come on out to Austin again, sometime soon.

Norm:
I will. And as long as the good Lord is willing and I can make it to 80 years old, then we’ll be able to celebrate over 50 years of friendship. So I look forward to it.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely, I look forward to that, my friend.

Norm:
Indeed.

Ivan:
Thank you for being on BNI Podcast.

Norm:
Thanks. Ivan.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla: Okay. Well, that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information from both of you.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for such great information from both of you.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Norm Dominguez shares his Positivity Pillars of Strength and Positivity Umbrella to help BNI members excel in networking, business, and life.

title.rendered: Episode 866: The Influence of the Minority

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week, Dr. Misner welcomes Tim Roberts as the first of his new Guest Experts. Tim and the other Guest Experts will be co-hosting the podcast from time to time.

There are three things that determine your success in BNI:

  • Whether you’re active,
  • Whether your actions are effective,
  • How many people you’re engaging with.

The only way for BNI members to be unbelievably successful is to have a chapter large enough to generate multiple referrals per week for every member, one where all contact spheres are full.

Some long-time members resist growth because the smaller chapter works for them–but it’s not a successful chapter until it works for everyone. Leadership is not about command and control; it’s about mobilizing and inspiring, and that’s what leaders in a chapter really need to focus on.

Listen to Tim’s The Power of One podcast.

Complete Transcript of Episode

866-BNI-Podcast

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am home in Austin, Texas, this week, and it’s hot as heck here in Austin.

Priscilla:
Oh, is it?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
And who do you have with us today?

Ivan:
Well, I have someone who’s been on two other times; this is his third appearance here on BNI Podcast, Tim Roberts. And Tim, I’m introducing him this time as a Guest Expert for my podcasts. I’m going to, over the next year or so, bring in a couple of other Guest Experts who are going to run the podcast with you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
I’m kind of doing… I’m going to really date myself and do Johnny Carson here. Johnny Carson would bring in some guest hosts who would run his show, and Tim’s going to be one of those people over the next year or so, and I’ll have some Guest Experts working with you over some of my podcasts over the next year.

Priscilla: 
Well I welcome that. I think it’ll be great.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’ll be good. So Tim, I’ve introduced him a few times in the past. He is the past [BNI] U.S. National Director. He’s an Executive Director for BNI New Hampshire, Florida Central, and Florida Northeast. He has well over 100 active chapters with more than 2600 members, and his regions have passed over $1.5 billion [USD] in Thank You for Closed Business over the last 10 years. And he also has his own podcast, the BNI Power of One Podcast, which is one of the reasons that I thought Tim would make a great guest expert over time with BNI Podcast. Tim, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Tim:
Thank you for having me, honored to be here.

Ivan:
It’s great to have you here, and it’s great to have you kind of take the baton on some of these podcasts with me. I appreciate it. Your topic today is The Influence of the Minority, which I think is an interesting topic, and I certainly have a few things that I want to add color commentary to. But how do you think the minority in a group can negatively influence a chapter and the members, which is, I think, the main theme of your point?

Tim:
Yeah, 100%. So we actually were talking about the story, and I’ll share the story of where this was kind of an aha moment for me, one of those BFOs, I think you like to call it.

Ivan:
We should tell everybody, BFO is a blinding flash of the obvious.

Tim:
Exactly. So I’ve been working with chapters for many, many years; I’ve been in BNI now for over 20 years. And you know, we have chapters from time to time that will, from our point of view, be struggling or be stagnant and always seem to fight change and fight growth or fight those different things. And I was actually meeting with one of these chapters one time and went to visit them and talk about the importance of growth and expanding their opportunities.

The chapter was about 16-17 members at the time, and while I’m doing the presentation, I started getting pushback from a couple people saying, “You know, Tim, we know you want us to be bigger, you want us to grow, but we’re doing really well. Yeah, we’re smaller, but we do really, really well. We average a referral a week to each other.” And so I stopped. I said, “You average a referral a week?” And they said, “Yeah.”  I said, “Just by a show of hands, raise your hand if you get a referral every week,” and only three hands went up. And it was obviously the three people that were really, really pushing back.

And it kind of hit me that in a group setting, especially in longer [time established] chapters, maybe the chapters that have been around for a long time or have members who’ve been involved for a long time, that those members might be highly effective. They might be getting a lot of results from BNI, and newer members will look to them for leadership and kind of guidance on what they should be doing, what they shouldn’t be doing, what the group should be doing.

And there are many times where that minority can really have a negative impact, because they may be comfortable, they may be getting the results they want, and they will be the ones who fight things like expansion or growth or more activity, and thus negatively influencing others who may not be, and definitely are not getting, the same type of results. They will listen to them as – these people have been around for long. They’re the longest-standing BNI members. They must know what’s what, and what works and what doesn’t. And this can have… could be very much in opposition of their own best interests.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. You know, I think that’s a great question to ask. And I think if you’re listening to this podcast, that’d be a great question to ask with your chapter. You know — how many of you are getting a referral a week? If you know they’re averaging a referral a week, you know how many of you are getting that? And to see the number is very telling. Look, the truth is, the only reason I want chapters to grow is they generate more referrals. That’s the bottom line. You know, I could retire from BNI. I don’t need this anymore. Why do I continue to do this? I continue to do this because I want BNI members to be unbelievably successful. But the only way to do that is to have a chapter large enough to be generating way more than a referral a week per member and you can do that with larger groups. Yes?

Tim:
Absolutely. I mean, again, as long as Contact Spheres are being well developed, right? We talk, I talk a lot about the three things that impact your results in BNI are: Your activity — are you active? Your effectiveness in that activity, that would be your skillset things. We’re the only organization that trains on how to do those things.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Tim:
And then how many people you’re doing it with. That’s it. And so, you know, you need to grow. You need to strategically grow, to increase the number of opportunities you have each week. And growth can look like a lot of work to people, and sometimes, again, those who might be comfortable with their results will be the loudest ones against that. And I don’t think it’s intentional. I don’t think these people were intentionally trying to negatively impact the others. They just didn’t see it until…

Ivan:
Because they were doing fine. Nobody else was, or very few others were, but they were doing fine.

Tim:
Right. And so that was a, it was a BFO moment for them as well, right? Because I can then say, “Look, I get that you three are, but look around. Every person here is a member of this team.”

Ivan:
Did they respond to that well?

Tim:
Well, they did. They did at first. I think once you leave sometimes, you know they come up with their own excuses at times too, but they did it. They kind of highlighted… you could see them sit back a little bit and go, “Oh no, it’s only us who were in a contact sphere together.” So they were naturally referring to one another. But to be able to sit back and say, “Everybody here is a team member. So if you really care that they’re here a year from now, because they’re having success, and we need to look at what strategies will make that happen, and the only one is growth.”

Ivan:
So I have a close friend who does a lot of training programs with people that get together on a regular basis, and they had somebody in their training group that just was constantly negative and bringing down the whole group. And you know, if they were saying left, this person was saying right; if they’re saying up, they’re saying down, and the person ended up quitting the training group, and it completely turned around. The training group completely turned around, became very positive, very supportive. It was a whole different environment. And I’ve certainly seen that in BNI groups, where some people are bringing down… they’re anchors rather than engines. And what you want, and I talked about this in [my book] “Who’s in Your Room?”, what you want is a chapter full of engines, people that are driving the chapter forward in a positive way.

Tim:
100%, and we need a little bit more empathy, I think in some of our chapters, to actually care. You know, I think we talk about Givers Gain, and it’s the right philosophy. It’s driven my life for the last 23 years. It’s one thing to say it. It’s another thing to live it and to believe it, and to actually take, you know, to take a step outside of your own results, especially if you are an influential leader in a Chapter and start looking at what are the other people experiencing, and not be afraid of helping them and highlighting that.

Ivan:
Yep.

Tim:
It’s an invisible problem for a lot of chapters, I think, because they get stuck on a number, right? We got to be 20. We got to be 30 members. Oh, I’ll tell you just another real quick story on that. We were doing some training on our Contact Spheres and the idea of balancing them out. We had a chapter back when I owned Maine [BNI Region}, who had a roundtable, the gentleman told me that they hit 32 members, so they canceled their visitor day.

Ivan:
Oh, good Lord.

Tim:
Yeah. And I was like, why’d you do that? And he said, “Well, we really think 30 is the right number. We really think that’s the chapter we want to be at. And so we canceled the visitor day.” So my first question was, obviously, “So you inducted number 31 and 32 by accident?” I don’t understand how that happened.

But we did this — we broke down all their Contact Spheres. I said, “Write everybody in your chapter and what Contact Sphere you think they should be in.” And I showed them that to get all the smallest ones to the current size of the largest Contact Sphere — to balance the chapter out, they needed 29 more members.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Tim:
And I said, “So look, you’re far away from growing, unless you want to go to all these other members and say that being 30 [members] is more important than their success.”

Ivan:
Yeah, what a great way to put that.

Tim:
So that one made them sit back.

Ivan:
We’ve all seen chapters of 50, 60, 70, members that are generating dozens and dozens of referrals per member per month. At least, certainly, many referrals per member per month. And it just, it is quality, but it is also quantity. Both are an important factor in a successful chapter.

Tim:
Yes, there’s just more. Again, it’s the opportunity that exists, right? You can only do so much activity. You can only get so skilled at that activity. I tell people, “[If] you want to make your membership more efficient and more cost effective… Well, if you’re going to do a weekly presentation, it’s better to do in front of 50, 60, 100 people than it is 10.”

Ivan:
Isn’t that the truth, yep.

Tim:
Same activity.

Ivan:
We’re almost out of time. Talk to me a little bit about the importance of leadership and focusing on the whole group.

Tim:
I think that’s everything. I think it’s from our organization top down, is having the right leaders are looking at what’s best for the team, not what’s best for them in that moment. So even if you are getting tremendous results and you know, maybe not as motivated to put in more because you’re getting what you need. It’s to have that empathy and have that vision, and to be able to see what’s the experience everybody’s having. Because the truth is, in every chapter, every member’s not playing exactly the same game. They don’t have exactly the same opportunity. And so to be able to see where those troubles lie, and be able to have empathy for that, and to be able to want to give back an effort to help make that even for everybody, is what a true leader is.

Ivan:
I think oftentimes we get caught up in BNI with the management of a chapter, but leadership and management are different. Leadership is not about command and control; it’s about mobilizing and inspiring, and that’s what leaders in a chapter really need to focus on, in my opinion.

Tim:
That is a far more eloquent way of saying that, yes.

Ivan:
Thank you. Tim, thanks for doing this podcast, and welcome to being my first Guest Expert who will be running some of these podcasts in the future. Everyone look for Tim on some future podcasts this year and in the future, as we go along, we’re gonna have a couple of guest experts, and Tim will be our very first one. Thanks, Tim for being on and for those of you who are listening, where can they listen to your regular podcast?

Tim:
They can find it same place as yours: Apple podcasts, Spotify, Google podcasts, anywhere you get your podcasts.

Ivan: BNI Power of One.

Tim:
Correct, yes. [https://bnipowerofone.com/]

Ivan:
All right, great. Tim, thanks again for being on. Back over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The only way for BNI members to succeed is to have chapters large enough to generate multiple referrals per week for every member.

title.rendered: Episode 865: A Beacon of Hope in a Sea of Fear (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 680, first released on November 4, 2020.

Synopsis

In 1985, Dr. Misner started 20 BNI chapters with only one piece of paper and his passion to help people grow their businesses. Today, BNI has all the marketing collateral any director could hope for, but the key ingredient is still passion.

BNI is in a position to help more people than ever before. BNI wants to help businesses restart the world. It’s time to double down on your efforts and inspire people to help other people.

  • Share your reasons for being passionate about BNI.
  • Make your visitors feel incredibly welcome.
  • Stay actively connected to other members.
  • Focus on helping more people in your community.
  • Reach out to entrepreneurs and show them how a group can help them.
  • Invite people to be part of changing their circumstances, changing their communities, and changing the world.

Let us know what inspires you about BNI. Share your story in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 680

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you? And where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, I have been traveling. It’s been online, but I have been traveling. Over the last week or so I’ve been to Singapore, the United Kingdom’s conference UK/Ireland, Australia, the United Arab Emirates, Italy. I have really been talking to BNI members all over the world in the last week or so.

Priscilla:
That sounds like fun, actually.

Ivan:
It has been great. And I think today’s message is one of the more important ones that I’ve ever done on BNI podcast.

These are really difficult times and some people want to give up. Don’t. It’s actually time to double down on your efforts, not abandon your network. BNI is here to help you get through these challenging times.

In fact, I believe we are in a position to actually help even MORE people this year than we’ve ever helped before. Others may struggle during this time, but you’ll be in a position to continue to do referral business every week because of BNI. If there’s something you need, you’ll have access to your referral partners to help you with that need.

Today we have the most comprehensive set of collateral material and technology that I ever thought possible at our fingertips. We have every resource that we need to serve more members. In 1985, I opened 20 BNI groups in just one year. Now, that’s a good number, but it’s been matched by some Executive Directors.

Here’s the really interesting point about that statistic. I did it with only one sheet of paper. That’s it. All I had was one sheet. We had no collateral marketing materials, no visitor information sheets, no brochures, no invitation cards, and forget about the internet, a website, email, or social media. None of that even existed yet. I was able to open 20 chapters and all I had was that one sheet of paper. It was a typewritten sheet with the meeting agenda typed up by me on an IBM Selectric typewriter. Remember those, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I think I had one.

Ivan:
Now back then I typed “Columbus” style, you know, seek out and discover. So I’d invite people and they’d say, “Do you have any material?” And I’d say “No, you need to come experience a meeting.” And they’d say, “Well, surely you have some brochures”, and I’d say “No, you have to experience a meeting.” They’d say, “You have nothing?” I’d say, “Well, I’ve got the agenda.” And they’d look at the agenda and they’d say, “Well, this doesn’t tell me much.” And I’d say, “I know. That’s why you have to experience a meeting. Come to the meeting.”

Here’s why I’m telling you this story. I had one other thing. In addition to the one sheet of paper, I was passionate about spreading the word, and helping more and more people succeed in their business.

Nothing great in life has ever been accomplished without a little bit of passion. I love the technology, the processes and the systems we have today. And the one thing we need in order for members to succeed and chapters to grow is passion.

I want you, if you’re listening to this podcast, I want you to inspire people. I want you to inspire people to inspire other people. That’s really what BNI’s mission is all about. Take away all the stuff and you can still grow. Trust me, I know. I had nothing but one sheet of paper and a ton of passion about what BNI could do for people.

Today, more than ever–today, more than in 1985, people need BNI. Communities need BNI. Countries need BNI.

Take a moment and think about one of the reasons you were first well and truly inspired by BNI. Grab a hold of that. Use that. And any other memories you have about what first made you passionate about this organization and share it with others. Share it widely. Inspire people. They need it right now, and you can give it to them.

Make your visitors feel incredibly welcome. Give them a great experience when they attend your meeting. Stay actively connected to your members, make sure they continue to have a great experience.

I am passionate about helping people change their circumstances. Passion. It’s the one thing no one can give you, but you. With that passion you can make a difference. And more importantly, you can change lives. I believe we can change lives. Let’s make a difference in more people’s lives. Let’s help people who are scared and desperate to get through these turbulent times.

Forget about all the noise all around you. There have always been distractions. There will always be distractions, some bigger than others. Focus on your passion. Focus on helping more people in your community. They need you, today more than ever. They need BNI, today more than ever.

BNI members have survived the most challenging economic times since the Great Depression. I’m really proud of how members and directors have helped one another with unexpected business disruptions and personal life issues. I’m not surprised. But I’m incredibly proud. I’ve seen members come together and support one another after Hurricane Katrina, the tsunami in Japan, the wildfires in Australia and California, and many, many other occasions.

One in particular is very poignant for me. It was California’s Northridge earthquake in the 1990s. I attended a big group in the San Fernando Valley just a couple of days after the earthquake.

And when I got to the meeting, we all discovered that the restaurant was condemned the night before because of so much damage from the quake. I watched as the President circled everyone up in the parking lot and held a meeting, standing up right there. He started the meeting by asking everyone how they were. How are they doing? Are they okay? Later he ended the meeting by having everyone go around one person at a time and share with the membership, anything that they needed.

I watched member after member support one another by sharing food, water, power generators, you name it. It was incredible. That was the embodiment of our Givers Gain philosophy. The restart process in California began then and there.

BNI wants to help businesses restart the world. That’s the messaging. There have been many amazing stories of how members have survived and thrived during this period. We’ve helped our members generate billions of US dollars’ worth of business. In the midst of these crazy times, we have generated billions of dollars in business for our members.

BNI is about helping and supporting one another in good times and in bad. Reach out to entrepreneurs and show them how a group can help them. Reach out to business professionals and show them how BNI can support them. Be a beacon of hope in a sea of fear. We can help people change their circumstances. Let’s restart the world. And let’s do it through the support that BNI communities can give people.

Invite people to be part of changing their circumstances, changing their communities, and changing the world. Help them to help you, and to help each other. Givers Gain is more than a phrase. It’s a way of living one’s life. It’s a perspective to view and interact with the world. It’s an attitude, not an expectation. And when it’s applied properly, it’ll change your life. And when it changes enough lives, it’ll change the world.

Don’t give up on BNI. BNI won’t give up on you. I won’t give up on you. Today more than ever people need their network. Today more than ever they need BNI. That’s my message for this week, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s a great message Ivan, I love it. It made me think about when I first heard about BNI was from one of my very close friends. And I was so excited about going to my first meeting. I’m not kidding about this, I couldn’t sleep all night. And it was because I was so isolated in my business, running it by myself. I didn’t have any community or anybody helping me. And I just couldn’t wait to go to the meeting. So it made me think of that.

Ivan:
Well, I’m glad, and I hope it makes a lot of members think about the excitement that they had in the beginning. Because it’s so important to hold on to that, to hold on to that passion. You know, I’ve seen a couple of people who, particularly in the early phases of this pandemic and the resulting recession, who said, “Well, you know, I can’t stay.” They’ve got to leave. And unfortunately, then they went out of business. This is the worst time to leave your network. This is the time you want to embrace your network, hold your network close, and use your network to help you get through these challenging times. Today, more than ever.

Priscilla:
It’s wonderful to have a BNI community like that, just for the support in every way, not just financial but emotional. It really is.

Ivan:
Yeah, I couldn’t agree more. And I hope many of you listening to this podcast feel the same way. Chime in. I’d love for you to share how BNI has helped you during 2020. Put it here in the comments on the BNI podcast. I promise you I read every single, every single message that gets put up, I read. So, I’d love to hear your story.

Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You’re welcome. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Don’t give up on BNI. BNI won’t give up on you. Today more than ever people need their network. Today more than ever they need BNI.

title.rendered: Episode 864: Creating a Layered Referral Machine

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dawn Lyons demonstrates how you can create a layered referral machine by helping other BNI members in their businesses, starting from the list in Episode 125/852: Take Off Your Bib.

  1. Create a business intake form that asks about other services your prospect may need.
  2. Display other BNI members’ cards and brochures in your office.
  3. Endorse a referral partner in every email you send out by adding a P.S. to your signature.
  4. Include referral partners’ brochures and cards in your “leave-behind” folder for clients.
  5. Add a “Resources” section to your website with profiles of referral partners.
  6. Highlight referral partners in your newsletters.

Choose your best referral sources and put this process in motion to expose your clients and prospects to them wherever they interact with you.

Links from Episode 864

  • Listen to Episode 125/852, “Take Off Your Bib”
  • Read Room Full of Referrals®
  • Listen to Episode 135, “The Card File Thingy”
  • Read “Add a P.S. to Your Email Signature”

Complete Transcript of Episode 864

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at home this week in Austin, Texas. It’s my favorite place. I’m always happy to be home. I’ve traveled so much in my life, it’s great to be here. But I have a good friend on this podcast that I am really happy to share the podcast with. I’ve known her for a number of years. This is her fifth appearance on the BNI podcast. She was on Episode[s] 135, 297, 580, 788. (I have a really good assistant; Dana is fantastic. And she gave me all that information.) And I’ve got Dawn Lyons here today.

Dawn is the [BNI] Executive Director, she’s been Executive Director for 24 years for the San Francisco Bay Area. She has over 60 chapters, well over 1000 members, and is a Hall of Fame director. She’s the only woman in the history of the organization to open 20 chapters in one calendar year. That is impressive. There’s only a handful of people that have ever done it. I mean, like four, and she’s one of them.

Dawn and I co-authored an Amazon best-selling book entitled, Room Full of Referrals, which is about behavioral styles, and how to network with people with behavioral styles. And we’re going to be adding this episode today to the multiple episodes that we’ve done over the years. Dawn, thanks for being on. We’re going to talk about layered referrals — a layered referral machine. What are you thinking about this topic? What do you have in mind?

Dawn:
Well, what I love about this topic, Ivan, is that I actually kind of took it from your recent podcast number 852. So I highly encourage the audience to listen to that. And the title of that is “Take Off Your Bib.” I truly just… I love it so much. But I wanted to create some action items around it to show people how to use a layered referral strategy. I think to get it started though, we kind of have to have you tell the story of where this came from the whole “Bib, take off your bib.”

Ivan:
Yeah, so I mean, I heard that…  it was a BNI Director in South Dakota [USA] and a BNI member, I think his name was Bill. And the episode 852 is a classic. It’s a remake of a podcast; you know, we repost podcasts that we think are great, and it’s time to do again. And rather than start them from scratch, we repost them. And so that was a classic. Bill was a prospective member, as I recall. And he said to the [BNI] Director, and it was I think it was the Director that shared this with me, he said to the Director,

“I love this concept. I’ve always really been about sales, and I’m in the sales business. And it’s all about selling, selling, selling. But I realized as you were talking about this philosophy of Givers Gain that BNI has, that it’s really about us as an individual taking off our bib and putting on an apron. Meaning it’s not what’s in it for me–what can I get, but what can I give?”

I just thought that was so powerful and so amazing. I did a podcast a long time ago, and we did the classic. I think the original podcast was 125, and we reposted a recently. It’s just a powerful podcast and I recommend people listen to it.

Dawn:
Absolutely. And I love it. And so what I wanted to do today is — on that podcast, you gave twelve different ways that an individual can serve their fellow referral sources. So we don’t have time to go over the twelve today. What I really wanted to focus on is sharing some strategies to have people layering their referrals, so they truly create a referral machine. So Ivan, real quick question, going back to your consulting days, when you got a new client, did you do some type of an intake form with them?

Ivan:
I did. I sat down with them and I asked them a number of questions, and most of those were related to what their pain points were. And what their pain points were, what are the problems that they have and particularly, what are their “afters?” What are they looking for AFTER I do what it is that I do? And so yeah, my original interview with them was to find out what issues they had so that I can help them.

Dawn:
Yeah, absolutely. And I believe that most businesses, especially our BNI members, have some sort of intake form. And my question is, though, really, are they utilizing that form for them, but to also possibly generate referrals, right at the same time, for their other referral sources? And so Ivan, what professions were really simple for you to refer to when you were a business consultant?

Ivan:
So anybody in marketing, attorneys, HR people, CPAs, believe it or not — computer experts, because you go back to the early 80s, when I was a consultant, computers are like “new.” And so I worked with a lot of computer people. So those are the kinds of [people]. And sometimes what I would do, particularly after I started BNI, is that as I was writing something up to maybe do a contract with them, I would hand them my business card file with BNI members in it. And I would ask them, “Are there any professions in here that you’re looking for that you might want to meet?” And that was a great way for me to give referrals?

Dawn:
Yeah, absolutely. I call that the business card thingy.

Ivan:
Yeah, there’s a whole podcast on that.  

Dawn:
Yes, exactly. All right. So going back to the intake form, here’s my thing. I believe that what if everyone’s intake form, had specific questions to ask, from the possible new client, about those people, those referral sources that are the easiest for you to refer. I think that we all know that they’re going to need those services at some point, and you could be the one to refer them to your trusted network. So the intake form is the first step in a layered referral strategy. So audience, if you don’t have a business intake form right now for you, please build one, create one, not just for your business, but for the sake of your referral partners as well.

Ivan: 
Yeah, that’s a good point. It’s a great start. But can you explain what you mean by layering, because that’s the title of this podcast?

Dawn:
Absolutely. So you had twelve different things that you could give to someone else, right? What I want to do is I want to take a few of those, show people how to automate it, and kind of have this set-it-and-forget-it, because then people will come to you. So, let me take number six, for example, on your list of twelve. It’s display their literature in your office. This is so simple, all we do is we get a multiple card holder or brochure holder, we put it in our office and we fill it with our members’ info. Then the automation part is fill when needed. I just recently took a brochure out of my chiropractor’s office, and it just made my life so much easier.

Then we could roll on to number five, which is endorse people’s products and services. And to make this automatic, what if you endorse a referral partner in every email that you send out by adding it into your signature line? So a great way to do that is to go into your signature, go into Edit, and start with maybe a P S. And it might sound something like this. “P.S. I love doing business with local professionals. And this month, I want my network to know about Mr. Eric Sherwood at Foundation Body Therapy. Eric has been my personal massage therapist for over six years. He is so good, I see him twice a month. If you need a highly skilled massage therapist to help you with your aches and pains, I would love to introduce you to Eric.” Now this is just at the bottom of your signature line. And then I wouldn’t even put in his website address, I would put in his booking URL. That way people go, “Oh, Dawn’s recommending him, I can book right here.” So it makes sense.

Ivan:
I’d like to jump in real quick. I just recently did a blog on www.ivanmisner.com, where I talked about using a P.S. in your signature line. And I gotta tell you, it was one of the most popular blogs I’ve done in the last year.

Dawn:
Awesome.

Ivan:
I didn’t recommend this as a suggestion. I think it’s a great suggestion. If I were rewriting that blog, I would add this as a P.S. to the signature line. I think it’s a great idea.

Dawn:
Excellent. All right, so we’ve got two or three layers so far. Now we’re gonna move on to number seven [from your list], which is distribute their information. So many businesses have a folder or some kind of ‘leave behind’ that they do with new prospects. So you can easily add brochures in there on your referral sources’ behalf. Again, this is once it’s in your folders, it’s kind of set-it-and-forget-it. It’s done.

Then my next favorite one is number twelve — publish information. Now, I want to think a little more high-tech on this one. So what if you are publishing their information by adding them to your website? Yes, I said your website, meaning what if you added a “Resources” tab, then you simply have a profile for your trusted referral sources. Meaning, if I’m willing to put them on my website, people are going to know that I trust them. You know, you can also highlight them in your newsletters; there’s lots of different things to do.

But let me just show you how the layers work, Ivan. So if we did this, and let’s say that I’m a chiropractor, and I get a new client. In my intake form, first layer, right? — that’s the very first layer, I ask, “Do you get massages currently? And if so, who do you go to? Do you like their work?” Right? I just asked them some questions. Then I give them a small packet of information about me. And I could put, let’s just say Eric’s brochure in there, too. That’s my second layer, I send them some kind of “Thank you for coming in” email, and Eric is being promoted in my signature line. Now that’s my third layer. Then the person comes in the office again, they see the business card holder with Eric’s card in there–fourth layer. They go on my website for something and sees that Eric is on my resources page, fifth layer, right? So that new client is definitely going to know that I would trust Eric to do an amazing job, if I’m highlighting him in these kind of layered effects. So that’s what I mean by layering.

Ivan:
It seems like a lot to do. I mean, BNI members are gonna think about this and go, “Wow, that’s a lot.” So talk to me about that briefly.

Dawn:
It is a lot. And what’s our philosophy?

Ivan:
Givers Gain®.

Dawn:
Givers Gain, right? So the more that we’re promoting people in a layered effect, the easier we will be able to give more referrals. And I want people to create these little referral machines so that giving referrals becomes easier for them.

Ivan:
Yeah. And then they end up getting referrals as a result of the fact that people are supporting them so substantially. And although this takes time, you know, time spent doing things like this, really represent your marketing time, in my mind. Would you agree?

Dawn:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Any closing thoughts before we hand it back to Priscilla?

Dawn:
No. My ask is, please start this, please try layering this. Choose your best referral source; make it as reciprocal as you possibly can. But I believe that every person can create a few true referral machines for several of their best sources.

Ivan:
I think that’s fantastic information. I appreciate you being on, Dawn. Priscilla is in your region. And Priscilla, I haven’t done this probably enough; tell us how people can get to your website, which you’re in Dawn’s region.

Priscilla:
Oh, thanks, Ivan. That would be great. They can go to www.LiveOakstudio.com and see all the services we provide and all the amazing projects that we’ve worked on.

Ivan:
You bet. Dawn, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast again. You’re one of the most active participants in the BNI podcast and I appreciate everything that you’ve contributed.

Dawn:
Absolutely. Thank you, Ivan. Thank you, Priscilla.

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla. And everyone, you know, leave a comment here on BNI podcasts; I read all your comments. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, that was just great. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dawn Lyons demonstrates how you can create a layered referral machine by helping other BNI members in their businesses.

title.rendered: Episode 863: Students and Networking in the Job Market

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Unemployment for recent graduates is higher than it is on the whole. This week, Dr. Misner provides six networking tips for college students, recent graduates and anyone else looking for a job. Many of these tips apply to BNI members, even if they’re business owners.

  1. Cultivate a confident mindset. Desperation is not referable.
  2. Clean up your online presence. Be professional on social media.
  3. Categorize your contacts into active, passive, and dormant connections.
  4. Leverage existing relationships. Let your active contacts know you’re looking for a job.
  5. Tap into diverse resources. Expand your network to increase your options.
  6. Network in person. Engage in face-to-face networking by visiting associations.
  7. Bonus! Suggest a working interview. Offer to do a small project for free.

Links

  • Read The Strength of Weak Ties by Mark S. Granovetter.
  • Read the April 2024 BLS report on student and graduate employment.

Complete Transcript of Episode 863

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hey, I’m in California this week. I’ve got one of my kids that’s got a little health issue. And so I’m traveling this week, but I’m happy to be doing the podcast.

Priscilla:
Great, Ivan. And so what are you going to be talking to us about?

Ivan:
Yeah, well, this topic is a little different than most of the topics that I’ve done in the past. It seems like every May, June, July, I’m asked by the media to talk about networking as it relates to students who have graduated from college. And I’ve never really done a podcast on that topic. And I know this doesn’t really apply to all members, but a lot of members have children.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan: 
And who knows, maybe there are a number of members that are employees; we do have members that are employees and looking for work. And so I’m asked often by the media to talk about networking for jobs. And so that’s what we’re gonna talk about today.

Priscilla:
Okay, that sounds great.

Ivan:
Yeah, hopefully members will find it of some value. Actually, there are two or three things in here that apply to anyone whether they’re looking for a job or not. But the truth is, the data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics in the U.S. has found that the unemployment rate [for graduates] recently, as of this recording, has increased substantially. And it’s larger than the unemployment rate as it sits for the average person. So this year, almost more than any, it’s kind of a challenge for college students to get a job. But it’s always a challenge. My experience is — the media keeps asking me to do this every year, because it’s always an issue. Some issues are bigger than others. So anyway, I have six steps to networking for a new job. And let me share those and as I share them all, I’ll talk about where it’s also relevant as a BNI member. The first is certainly relevant as a BNI member.

Number one is cultivate a confident mindset. Desperation is not referable. So you’ve got to approach networking with confidence rather than hopelessness. You know, you will be depending on your network, to vouch for you and speak highly of you in hiring you. And so you want to come across as confident. That’s certainly true. As a BNI member, you have to have a confident mindset because people don’t want to refer someone who’s desperate, whether you’re looking for a job or you’re looking for referrals.

Ivan:
The second, I think, also applies to BNI members. And you know, the more I look at this the more I realize, a lot of this really does apply to BNI members. Number two definitely does. Clean up your online presence. It’s really important that you ensure your social media accounts project a professional image. Potential employers review online profiles to make sure that they reflect positively if they’re going to hire you. So in BNI, years ago, I told my HR department ‘Do social media searches on the people before you hire them.’ Oh, my goodness, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
There were some that were shocking. One in particular was a guy who we were thinking about hiring. I mean, I didn’t say check every person, I said check every person who were really looking at closely. And there was one guy we were looking at closely and every other post had the ‘F bomb’ thrown into it.

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
And he had, he was incredibly political in his positions. Aggressively political, and in BNI, you know, we try really hard to stay out of politics and religion. And he was aggressive about his stances and he was angry. And he was posting stuff that was constantly angry. And the truth is, that’s not the culture that I want to reinforce in my organization. And so we didn’t hire him.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan: 
Well, I think the same applies, certainly, in BNI. You know, you want to make your online presence be one that is open and comfortable for people. I have always said, stay out of politics and religion on what you do publicly. And some people are really angry about that. And the irony, Priscilla, and I know I’ve talked to you about this, is I have a bachelor’s degree in political science.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh.

Ivan:
You know, I worked for the government when I was a young man; I thought I might go into politics. I have no problem with politics.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
I have no problem with religion. The thing is, it tends to be divisive if you want to generate referrals. And it’s definitely divisive, politics are divisive, if you want to get hired somewhere. So, those first couple of things apply to BNI members as well.

The third, you know, is something important, I think, within a BNI member, but really important if you’re looking for a job. And that is to categorize your contacts. We want to take a look at all the contacts you have in your database and look at them and assess whether they are one of three categories: active, passive, or dormant. Active is where you could just pick up the phone and ask somebody for assistance; you have a relationship with them. Passive is something where, you know, you probably need to set an appointment, to reconnect with them, or do a one-to-one with them because you haven’t really stayed in touch. And dormant is where you’re reconnecting with somebody that you haven’t talked to in years. I mean, we all have that. I have people in high school and college that I haven’t talked to in years, and they become dormant relationships.

Now, in looking for a job, and this is really different than looking for referrals in BNI — massive difference, is Granovetter’s article on ‘The strength of weak ties’, the strength of weak ties. In BNI, what you want are strong ties to get referrals. But when you’re looking for a job, weak ties can actually be pretty good. Somebody that you barely know is more than willing to refer you to someone who’s looking for a job because that person has to do their due diligence. So the concept of the strength of weak ties works outside BNI – looking for employment. It doesn’t work so well inside BNI.

Priscilla:
Mm-hmm.

Ivan:
And number four is to leverage existing relationships. So go back to those active, I said active passive, dormant, go back to those active relationships, and really connect with those people and communicate with them that you’re looking for a job; that you’ve just graduated, LinkedIn is actually really good for that. I found LinkedIn to not be so good for referral marketing for a business, but it can be very good for referral marketing for a position or job.

I think the fifth item is also relevant in BNI, and that is to tap into diverse resources. You want to expand your network beyond your immediate circles and connections. Diversity in a network is powerful. If you want to build a powerful personal network, it’s got to be diverse, because networks are by nature cluster-like. People tend to hang out with people like themselves. And so if you have contacts that connect you to other clusters of individuals, then your network is more powerful. So you want to tap into diverse resources when you’re looking for work, or when you’re looking for referrals; that works really well for BNI.

And the last one also works well for BNI. And that is to network in person; engage in face-to-face networking by visiting associations. If you’re looking for work, [visit] associations in an industry that you’re interested in. So you go to associations like that, and you network with people that are in the business that you want to go into. And of course, with BNI networking in person is really common. Still the majority of our chapters meet in person or are hybrid where they’re meeting in person occasionally.

Ivan:
So those are the six steps to looking for employment, if you are. And I know this is a topic that’s not terribly relevant in BNI, but some of the key points are relevant. And you might have people that you know that are looking for work and pass on this podcast to them. I’m about to give you a bonus point – number seven.

Priscilla: (chuckling)
Okay.

Ivan:
And I do this when I do media interviews and they love the bonus point. Here’s the bonus point. When you’re looking for work, particularly in a entry level position, which again many BNI members wouldn’t be but you might have children who are, suggest that you do a working interview.

In other words, when you’re talking to the HR person or the person who’s doing the interview, if it’s a job that you really want, say to them, “Hey, I’d be happy to do a working interview with you, if you would like.” And most people don’t know what that is. And so they’ll probably say, “What is that?” And then you say, “Bring me in for a day or two, to do some project or some work so I can show you what I’m capable of, and then consider hiring me.” And you say to them, “Look, you can bring me in, I’ll work for free.” Now, most of the time, they won’t do that because there are legal issues, they gotta pay you something. But that aside, it’s amazing how often companies will go, “Well, that’s a really creative idea. Okay.”

And you know, Priscilla, I have taught at University for many years. And I’ve been recommending this to students for many years, and I’ve had students come back to me and say, “Thank you for that suggestion. I got a job based on suggesting a working interview.”

Even one of my children, and your children don’t always follow your advice, but my eldest did when I suggested the working interview, and she did it with a company that she wanted to go to work for, and they said, “Well, we’ve got an event coming up this week. Would you be willing to come in and do photography for the event? Let’s see how you do.” She went in, she did the photography. She took photos, it was great event. And they’re like, “You were really good. Yes, we’ll hire you.” And they hired her. And she worked there for years.

Priscilla:
Wow, that’s great.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s amazing. So the working interview concept is a great idea. And if you’re a BNI member, I’d love to hear if you think some kind of working interview strategy would work as a BNI referral. I’d love to hear what you would think about that. Otherwise, a lot of this content is you know, I think really good for BNI members and their children, and I hope you use it in that way. That’s everything I have for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay great. Thanks, Ivan.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: This week, Dr. Misner provides six networking tips for college students, recent graduates and anyone else looking for a job.

title.rendered: Episode 862: The Success Guidebook

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Elizabeth Hamilton-Guarino joins the podcast to explain that there’s more to success than meets the eye. She wrote The Success Guidebook to help people redefine success for themselves, personally and professionally. (BNI is mentioned in Chapter 6.)

Moments matter, because we’re not entitled to indefinite time on this planet. Dream big about how you want to spend them. If you can’t see it for yourself, other people aren’t going to see it either. Make sure you’re out there communicating your goals and your dreams to other people, as well as yourself, so that people can show up and help you.

Links

  • Visit Elizabeth’s website, The Best Ever You.
  • Get your copy of The Success Guidebook.

Complete Transcript of Episode 862

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hey, you know, I just got back from Charlotte, North Carolina, where we announced our new CEO to the organization. That’s the fifth CEO in BNI. And I’m really excited to have this individual on board. This week, I’m home and we’re starting the Cheers to 40 Years program, which is a little early. We have our 40th anniversary in January, but we’re kind of ramping up to it. So I’m really happy to do some Cheers to 40 Years programs.

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
Yeah. So today I have on my podcast, a longtime friend, Elizabeth Hamilton-Guarino, and Elizabeth, I’ve been writing for her www.besteveryou.com website for a few years now. And she has come out with a book called The Success Guidebook – How to Visualize, Actualize, and Amplify You. I’m in there, and BNI is mentioned in there, in chapter six. Everybody, pick up a copy of the book, I recommend it. And where she talks about networking in chapter six, she quotes me and she talks about BNI.

She is the founder of The Best Ever You Network and co-founder of Compliance4. And through these companies, she has helped individuals and organizations around the world be their best to achieve world-class excellence. And I can second that. I watched her operate over the years and she’s amazing. She’s also the author of an award-winning personal development book series: PERCOLATE – Let Your Best Self Filter Through and of course, The Guidebooks (Change and now Success). Elizabeth, thank you for being on BNI Podcast.

Elizabeth:
Thank you for having me. It’s such a joy always to be around you and learn from you. I feel like a lot of the things that I’m now doing in my life are in part because of your wisdom and your friendship over the years. So I’m always grateful for you.

Ivan:
Well, you’re very kind. Let me ask you the first question. And I can’t believe you shared this with me and said it’s okay to share on the podcast. You’re 54.

Elizabeth:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And a mom of four sons. So you clearly were married very early. Well, okay, so you’ve been married 25 years. You even gave that information; I can’t believe it.

Elizabeth:
I’ve been married twice, actually.

Ivan:
All right, fair enough. I get that. I totally get that. But now at 54 you’re publishing books. So talk about that. How could you be able to start doing things like that in the last third of one’s life is amazing. So tell me about that.

Elizabeth:
Yeah, you know, it’s really fun to be able to publish books. And I do add in there “at my age” sometimes, because, you know, I think age is what it is. And I think we’ve all got dreams, and it’s always been a dream of mine since I was, you know, all the way back in kindergarten, to be an author and publish books. And I don’t think I knew how it was going to take shape and take hold until, you know, I hit probably about 40 – 45. And I’m like, “Aha, there it is. I know what I’m going to do.” And here we are; it’s a joy. I’ve got The Change Guidebook, The Success Guidebook — both of them you’re in. You were a contributor in The Change Guidebook. And in the new one, I dedicate chapter six to you, like you were mentioning. And it’s just a joy.

The kids are 22, 24, 26, and 28. And we’ve got two graduating from Georgetown University next week with their master’s degrees. And I kind of think it’s my turn. But I started Best Ever You way back when, when the littlest one went into first grade. I thought, you know, I’m gonna get my suit on and go out the door, and I was the vice president of a financial services firm. And in about six months into that, I went, you know what, this is not what I want to be doing.

Ivan: (laughing)

Elizabeth:
And I was really like, “I don’t want to do this.” And I felt like I couldn’t do both. I couldn’t like be a mom and work at the same time and do both things great. So I closed my office door and wrote “Best Ever You, Best Ever Me.” And when Best Ever You took hold, I immediately quit my job. I started the Best Ever You Network. And people thought I was crazy.

Ivan:
Yeah, well, people thought I was crazy when I started BNI, too. So I wouldn’t be too troubled by that. I think it’s really powerful. At a certain point in your career, you know, not early in your career, but later in your career, to be able to make a lane change based on what you want to do, not just what you have to do, but what you want to do. I think there’s a lot of power in that. And I’m very impressed by that.

Elizabeth:
You know, I love what you just said, that’s a really good point. Because up to that point, most of me was in the financial services industry with compliance and training and mutual funds and all that stuff.  Which we still do, because my husband is a federal securities attorney, and we own Compliance4. But you know, it wasn’t exactly for me. After becoming a mom and having all these things and like, you know, there’s a lot that goes into, even helping young people be their best. There’s mindset, there’s action, there’s dreams, there’s all these things. So there’s a lot of learning that went on in our house. Actually, our sons were raised with #gratitude on the refrigerator and things. So that comes back in my face. If I’m ever grumpy, they look at the sign and go “Hashtag gratitude, Mom.”

Ivan:
Oh, that’s funny. That’s funny. I hate when your kids use your own words against you for something. It’s hard to argue with that. All right, so I got a couple of questions for you. Tell us about redefining success. That’s one of the things that you talk about.

Elizabeth:
You know, I think we’re at a point where a lot of things are needing to be updated. And success to me isn’t, you know… it’s great to have money and all these things. But it’s not all about that. It’s not all about every dollar and all the analytics and data and things like that. There’s so much more to success than meets the eye. And I think it’s time for us to sort of take a look at that and help people define success for themselves personally and professionally. Either one, both, whatever they want to do, but so that, you know, people don’t walk around feeling like they’re not enough, or they suck, or sad, or whatever. I think a lot of people walk around like that. I know, I certainly have had a lot of moments where I’ve walked around like that. I thought, you know, I think we gotta get a grip on this so people feel good.

Ivan:
Yeah, no, I agree completely. And, you know, my experience with my late wife was that she felt less than, because she has spent most of her life raising our children and then went into work. And I was like, when you raise great kids, you should not feel bad about that. And, you know, as most people know, my wife passed away a few years ago, I got remarried. And Jody looks at what I’m doing and is like, “I’m not as successful as you are.” I’m like, “Yeah, you are.” Your success is how you measure it, which is exactly what you’re saying. It’s how you measure it. And Jody raised three great kids and she, you know, supports the Jack Canfield organization. And she supports (chuckling) Ivan Misner too, which isn’t bad.

Elizabeth:
And me too. I mean, I wouldn’t have… like Jack Canfield endorsed the books. I wouldn’t have that without her. I love Jody. Jody, you’re the best.

Ivan:
Yeah. Yeah. And so it really is about defining success as to what brings you joy. What works for you. For me, it’s not about money. It’s not about fame. It’s about… for me, it’s about great experiences in life and what brings me joy. And it sounds like you have a similar philosophy on that.

Elizabeth:
And how I learned it is, you know, a lot of different things over the years. But one big part is, I’ve been resuscitated a couple of times from food allergic reactions. And when you realize you’re not entitled to time, I think things shift a little bit.

Ivan:
Yeah, that was the last thing I wanted to talk to you about was — we’re not entitled to time. Talk about that.

Elizabeth:
Yeah, we definitely aren’t entitled to time. And so when you think about that concept, it really makes you go, okay, my moments really, really, really matter. Well, how am I going to spend them? And I think when you take a look at, take a look at that, you can throw in enoughness in there and some gratitude and really benchmark success in a different way.

Ivan:
Yeah, in one of my books, Who’s in Your Room, we talk about the breaths that you have left in life. And that it’s important to embrace those breaths that you have left as much as possible. You know, sometimes you got to do what you got to do, but you really want to try to move towards doing the things that you want. So you’ve got this great new book. Tell us a little bit about it before we wrap up, and where people can get it.

Elizabeth:
Well, it’s a masterclass. So it’s three parts. It’s narrative from me, it’s contributor stories, and exercises. And so the entire thing is a masterclass with this certification. And people can get the book wherever books are sold. It’s an eBook, a paperback, and an audiobook.

Ivan:
Outstanding. And for everyone listening, BNI is mentioned in this book in chapter six; I’m quoted in this book. You got to pick up a copy of it so you can see what Elizabeth has to say about BNI. I’ll give you a hint, it’s good. So pick up the book, check it out. It’s great content for a BNI chapter. Elizabeth, anything that you’d like to wrap up with, before we end?

Elizabeth:
Just I think that, you know, keep your dreams alive. Dream big. And if you can, you know, “See it, be it, do it,” is kind of the theme in the book. And I think that if you can’t see it for yourself, other people aren’t going to see it either. So make sure you’re out there communicating your goals, your dreams, and so forth to other people, as well as yourself, so that people can show up and help you too.

Ivan:
I like the Dream Big concept. I just did a presentation on that. And it confuses people when I say this at first, but I think it takes as much effort to dream big as it does to dream small.

Elizabeth:
Uh-huh. Yeah.

Ivan:
And I say that because people that I know that dream small, are always talking about they’re not enough. They had a difficult childhood, they had this, they had that. And there’s all these reasons why they’re dreaming small. And that takes as much emotional energy as it does to just dream big. And to believe in yourself. And so I love concepts like what you’re describing, and I do recommend your book.

Elizabeth:
Thank you.

Ivan:
Elizabeth, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast. I appreciate you.

Elizabeth:
Thank you. I’m so grateful for you. And thank you very much for having me and all your support over the years.

Ivan:
Yeah, my pleasure. Anyone that wants to read some of my articles, and many, many, many other articles by many other people go to www.BestEverYou.com. Of course, check out SuccessGuidebook.com. Thank you, Elizabeth. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, that was just great. Thank you so much to both of you for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Podcast guest Elizabeth Hamilton-Guarino invites BNI members to redefine success, dream big, and communicate their goals and dreams.

title.rendered: Episode 861: Five Ways to Encourage Employees to Network (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 132, originally released on November 25, 2009. Links and offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

Many BNI members have employees, and we almost never think about them as a source of referrals for our businesses. But building a business through word of mouth is everyone’s job. Here are five tips to develop a networking mindset in your staff.

  1. Include networking in the job description for every employee.
  2. Establish clear and reasonable expectations.
  3. Teach your staff how to network effectively for the company—bring them to BNI.
  4. Motivate your staff to bring referrals to the company through an incentive program.
  5. Be sure your staff sees you practicing your networking skills.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcription of BNI Podcast Episode 132

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I am joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
Doing great, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
What do you have to share with us today?

Ivan:
Well, today I’m going to talk about, I think, an interesting topic, Five Ways to Encourage Employees to Network. Many of our BNI members have employees, and I’m not talking about sales representatives, but just employees; they may be clerical, they may be administrative people. And we almost never think about them as opportunities for giving referrals to our company. I think we focus so much on bringing in new business ourselves, members in particular, because members tend to be either the owner of the business or the salesperson or professional running the business, and so they kind of overlook the support staff as a source of referrals. I think that building your word-of-mouth for your business is not the responsibility of yours to do marketing or sales, or just the sales department of a company if you’re in a larger organization.

I think everybody within a company can be a salesperson or a referral marketer for the business, so that’s what I wanted to talk about today. I wanted to give five tips on how to engage your staff in the networking process.

Priscilla:
Okay, great! What are they?

Ivan:
Here’s the first one. Include networking in the job description for each and every employee.

Priscilla:
Well, that’s a good one.

Ivan:
Here’s another idea. Have some of your administrative people come with you to a BNI meeting so they can see what this thing is all about. It’s really interesting. I was at a BNI meeting at a big event that I did just recently, and I had a woman come up to me. I thought it was so cool.
