She said, “I love when my dad travels.” She works for her dad, she’s an employee, administrator. “I love when my dad travels because I get to come to these meetings.” That’s what she said, and I thought that was great. She said, “I love coming to BNI meetings, and it was really an eye opener for me to meet the people that he had been talking about, to see the professions that he had been talking about.” And that, I think, applies to any company. If you’re a member, get your administrative team one at a time, you don’t have to bring them all in one day, but bring them over a course of a year so they can see BNI and experience it. And it’s a great way to teach them; experience is a great teacher. Teach them about the BNI program.

Okay, so here’s number 4. Motivate your staff to bring referrals to the company. My wife once worked for a business owner who incorporated monetary bonuses into her word-of-mouth marketing expectations. So for every new customer or client that she brought into the business, she was given a bonus. It was a real win-win arrangement for the company, and as each new client brought in revenue well above that bonus amount, it benefited everybody, but yet, she was rewarded for her efforts. And I think having some kind of referral incentive for staff, we think about referral incentives for clients. If a client refers somebody to us, we give them some kind of bonus or cash incentive or a gift or something, and I think we should definitely being doing the same thing for our own employees.

Having a bonus system in place made it obvious to my wife that she should be attending Chamber meetings with or without the boss and developing connections in the community, passing out cards for the company, and she got these really nice bonuses and had an opportunity to meet many people. By the way, she joined BNI because of that, and that’s how I met her, so it had some other benefits along the way as well.

Priscilla:
It worked well for you!

Ivan:
It worked out really well for me! But I think it’s something that works out for a lot of people, and I recommend, you know, give some kind of incentive for the people in the organization that are bringing referrals to your organization. And it’s one area that most companies don’t even think about. They think about everybody else, the clients, prospective clients, but they don’t think about rewarding their own staff for bring in referrals, and it’s a natural.

So here’s the fifth one, the last one. Be sure your staff sees you practicing your networking skills. Oftentimes, we don’t, as entrepreneurs or salespeople, we don’t share with our staff the amount of time and energy we put into building or maintaining our business through word of mouth. I’ve always felt very strongly about this point. If I’m going to expect my staff to do something, I need to motivate and reward them, and I need to show them how to do it; I have walk the talk and show them what I’m doing.

And one way to do this – of course, you want to track how much business you’re doing so that you can share that with people so that they see that your efforts are, in fact, making a difference for your business. And when you’re going out, oftentimes, I think companies, they say they’re going to a Chamber mixer, and employees think, “Yeah, the boss is going out to another party,” and listen, people are really good at networking. That’s not what it’s about at all. It’s about starting to make connections and build those relationships. So inviting your staff to these Chamber functions, showing them how – don’t just have them there and let them loose. Talk to them. Tell them what you’re doing. Teach them what you have learned. We have had so much content in these podcasts and in my books that teaches people how to work the network. Share that with your employees so they can see that it’s not just social, but it’s a way of generating business through your networking efforts.

So those are the five things that I would recommend to encourage your employees to network, administrative staff, not just salespeople, but the people who work for you and help support you and your organization.

  • Include a job description for everyone.
  • Have clear and reasonable expectations.
  • Teach your staff how to do it.
  • Motivate your staff to bring in referrals through some kind of incentive.
  • And be sure your staff sees you practicing the networking skills that we’re talking about.

And I think if people do that, they’re really going to add a whole new aspect to their results in networking.

Priscilla:
Well, that’s great. Ivan, I think that also, you could reward your staff with tickets to Chamber functions, like sometimes they have, I know in Berkeley, they have something called the Crush Festival, which is a really nice event, a wine tasting event. And you could offer tickets to your staff.

Ivan:
You know, that’s a great idea. For those people who have joined you and come out to Chamber functions, I think it’s a great idea. You said, “Hey, thanks for coming to those Chamber functions this year. Here’s a couple of tickets to some event that’s going on, if you’d like them.” Great idea.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Okay.

Ivan:
Well, thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You’re so welcome, and thank you, Ivan.

Well, I think that’s it for this week, and I’d just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you’ll join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Building a business through word of mouth is everyone’s job. Here are five tips to develop a networking mindset in your staff.

title.rendered: Episode 860: CEUs, Testimonials, Presentations, and TYFCB (Ask Ivan)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week Dr. Misner answers member questions about four important elements of BNI: Continuing Education Units (CEUs), Testimonials, Presentations, and TYFCB. Chapter leadership teams and education coordinators should pay special attention to the answers.

Continuing Education Units (CEUs)

CEUs apply to anybody who’s learning things. That definitely includes listening to podcast episodes and recorded webinars.

Testimonials

Testimonials are normally delivered verbally during the weekly meeting, but you can give anyone a written testimonial anytime. Chapters with more than 50 members often ask members to give written testimonials because of meeting time constraints.

Weekly Presentations

Weekly presentations are not commercials. They are presentations designed to teach other members how to refer you.

Thank You for Closed Business (TYFCB)

National reporting of TYCB is based on the numbers that members give us via BNI Connect. According to those, there are 100 countries in the world that have a lower annual GDP than what BNI does in Thank You for Closed Business each year.

When in doubt, don’t make things up. Ask Ivan.

Complete Transcript of Episode 860

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Charlotte, North Carolina, this week at the BNI headquarters, BNI Global Office for a Board Meeting and to meet with staff.

Priscilla:  
Okay, great. And what is the topic today?

Ivan:
Well, today is Ask Ivan. I’ve been asked a number of questions over the last few months, and I’ve been kind of adding them up and decided to do one session on four topics. So I’ve got a lot of topics to cover in a short period of time, but that I think are pretty important topics.

The first one is CEUs [Continuing Education Units]. And I had somebody reach out to me and said, “You know, according to [BNI] Podcast number 331, for every five podcasts or webinars that they listen to, they should get a point towards a CEU.” And their local director said no, no. Only live presentations, live trainings, qualify for CEUs. Wrong. Dead wrong. Totally wrong. Why are we making this stuff up? Stop making stuff up, you guys. CEUs apply to anybody who’s learning things. Podcasts, of ALL the things, [BNI] Podcasts are one of the most important things to recognize for people getting CEUs. It’s amazing to me, Priscilla, how people just make things even more complicated. “Well, look–let’s make more rules.”

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
“No, you can’t get CEUs for podcasts; it’s got to be for live training.” Where did we get that? Not from me. [BNI] Podcasts are absolutely part of the CEU program. If you want continuing education units, listen to these podcasts, they’re very important. And so what I’d really love for regions to do is to stop making things up. Which is, by the way, the topic of several of the other things that I want to talk about today. So [BNI] podcasts qualify for CEUs. This is the final answer–to go to Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?–this is my final answer. CEUs count–podcasts count for CEUs.

Second, testimonials. So I got a question from somebody else who says they observed that their chapters had too many conditions put upon passing a testimonial. It had to be in writing, it had to be a full moon on a Tuesday, it had to be…. whatever–all these requirements to pass a testimonial. Okay. Generally speaking, testimonials can be verbal, and they can be at a BNI meeting. They don’t have to be in writing. That said, listen to the rest of my answer.

There are some chapters that are so large — 60, 80, 100 members, 150 Members, where doing verbal testimonials is just not possible. So with that exception, testimonials can be given anytime and they can be given verbally. Stop making stuff up. If somebody’s got a testimonial for another member, talk about it. With the exception of the size of the group — the devil is in the details on some of this stuff and I get that. With that exception aside, yeah, give testimonials verbally. There’s nothing wrong with that. Is there anything wrong with doing it in writing? No, nothing wrong with doing it in writing. That’s great. You know, the more options to give testimonials for the business that someone’s done, the better. But I never made up this rule that it’s got to be in writing. That said, the larger groups — I get it where they want, you know, it in writing because you can’t do it all verbally. Does that make sense, Priscilla, about testimonials?

Priscilla:
Yeah, totally. Yeah.

Ivan:
Okay. The third is what I’m seeing with some chapters calling their Weekly Presentations, their “weekly commercials.” Where did you get this stuff from?

Priscilla: (chuckling) 
TV.

Ivan:
Yes, it’s not BNI. We don’t do weekly commercials in BNI. We do weekly presentations and our presentation should be about educating our salesforce, educating our referral sources. It’s not called a weekly commercial. If that’s in your manual, I want you to pick up the phone and call BNI Global Headquarters immediately and say, “Hey, this is in our manual.” It shouldn’t be there. Because it’s not a weekly commercial, it is not a commercial. It is a weekly presentation to EDUCATE, educate people how to refer you. So please stop calling them weekly commercials for those regions that are doing that. Stop calling them weekly commercials. They’re weekly presentations to teach people how to refer you. That’s the third topic.

The fourth topic, Thank You for Closed Business. This is an important one. You know, in the last 12 months from the date of this recording, we’ve passed 15.4 million referrals and generated 24.7 billion, that’s billion with a “B,” 24.7 billion US dollars in Thank You for Closed Business for our members. 24.7 billion US dollars! Based on United Nations estimates of GDP [Gross Domestic Product], there would be 100 countries in the world, Priscilla, 100 countries in the world with a lower GDP than what BNI generated in Thank You for Closed Business in the last 12 months from this date past 12 months ago. That’s amazing to me, we’re making a huge difference in communities all around the world.

And I was asked to bring this topic up because of the formula that I used to use years ago. Back in 1993, I published my doctoral dissertation, which is about the impact of business networks on local communities. And I created a formula based on the amount of business that was being generated in BNI. Now I published that [in] ’93, it was based on research that went up through the end of 1991. So for the first six years of BNI, it took a look at the amount of referrals that are passed and the value of those referrals. And it was based on a statistical stratified random sample of members. And I felt very confident in the number. And so we published that for many years up until BNI Connect really became active in 2011, and we were able to get firm numbers based on what [BNI] members report. Now here’s the interesting thing, Priscilla. When we started getting firm numbers based on BNI Connect and the Thank You for Closed Business, do you think my formula was high or low? Do you think I overshot or undershot the numbers?

Priscilla:
I’m sure that you undershot them.

Ivan:
I way undershot, I was shocked! It was at least 30 or 40% lower than the real number. And it hit me when I finished my doctoral dissertation and the research in the early 90s, when BNI was only six years old, and I was looking at chapters that were 1, 2, 3, as many as six-years old. But by 2011, we had chapters that were 20 years old, almost 30 years old. And they pass a lot more referrals. And they generate a whole lot more referrals for members. And I didn’t realize that that has a huge impact on the Thank You for Closed Business. And so when we started reporting it in BNI Connect, the Thank You for Closed Business numbers were way higher than my formula which I used as part of my doctoral dissertation. And they were higher because the longer people are in BNI, the more referrals they generate.

Priscilla:
Mmm, hmm.

Ivan:
And I had somebody asked me about my dissertation recently and my formula, and so I thought I would bring it up in this Ask Ivan session today.

So just to review, as we wrap up, BNI Podcasts count for CEUs. Don’t let anyone tell you they don’t; they’re wrong. They do.

Testimonials should be given any way you want, with the caveat of really, really big chapters probably need to do it in writing, and I get that. But generally speaking, testimonials in a chapter, just give them – that’s the key. Testimonials are important.

It’s called Weekly Presentations, not a commercial. It’s not a commercial. It’s an educational moment.

And Thank You for Closed Business is based on the numbers that members give us. And based on the numbers that members give us, there are 100 countries in the world that have a lower GDP than what BNI has done in Thank You for Closed Business. And that makes me so incredibly proud of what this organization has become. And that’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s great. Ivan, thank you so much. We haven’t done an Ask Ivan for a long time. And this was a great one.

Ivan:
Yeah, I had a few topics in there and some will be controversial. We’ll see how they play out, but I feel strongly about all of them. Thank you, Priscilla. I love what you do.

Priscilla:
Okay, you are so welcome, Ivan. Okay, then. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

This entry is part 32 of 33 in the series Ask Ivan

Dr. Misner answers member questions about Continuing Education Units (CEUs), Testimonials, Presentations, and TYFCB.

title.rendered: Episode 859: Voracious Podcast Consumption

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Terry Donaldson from BNI Mountain House Power Partners–aka Sparkles the Clown–joins the podcast this week to share what she learned from listening to 700 episodes of The Official BNI Podcast in her first year of membership.

  • Seek out and welcome BNI members from unusual professions.
  • You won’t know what another member really does without doing One-to-Ones.
  • The podcasts are an incredible educational tool. Keep a podcast episode scorecard and listen to all of them.
  • Other members of your chapter can mentor you and show you how things work.

Links and Resources for Episode 859

  • Download Terry’s Podcast Listening Scorecard as a PDF
  • Download the Podcast Listening Scorecard as an Excel Spreadsheet
  • Listen to Episode 206, “You Never Know Who They Know”
  • Visit Terry’s website, Zany Entertainment

Complete Transcript of Episode 859

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at home in Austin, Texas this week and I have a guest on our podcast, Terry “Sparkles” Donaldson, aka Sparkles Delight the Clown, who is a BNI member. I love professions like this that are, you know, it’s not the normal profession that you see in BNI. It’s something unique and I think that is incredible. I love seeing professions like this. Terry became a professional clown 27 years ago, she was probably 16 at that point.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
Prior to that, she had over 50 different employers before she figured out what she really wanted to do when she grew up and at 47 she had her epiphany! Still clowning around at 74, she’s now 74, Sparkles has been to over 5339 events. Terry’s mom, who will be 101 years old, very soon here, sets an example for lifelong learning. By the end of her first year of BNI membership, she did listen to over 700, Official BNI Podcasts. 700! And she created a chart to keep track and we’re going to put that Excel spreadsheet Chart in this podcast, if at all possible. Terry, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Terry:
Thank you, Dr. Misner. It is such an honor to be here, able to talk to you and talk about how much I have gotten out of your podcasts.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. I appreciate that. And please call me Ivan.

Terry:
Oh, okay.

Ivan:
I appreciate that.

Terry:
Thank you, Ivan. I’m actually up to 825 of your podcasts. And in the previous… you have just explained how I can get to the rest of them. So pretty soon I will have all of them listened to.

Ivan: 
Alright, well, there’s gonna be a test next week.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
There’ll be no test. It’s funny to me, because I mean, people will come to me and talk about podcast number, you know, 150, and start describing it to me, and I’m like, “I don’t even I don’t even remember what that podcast was. What is that?” And so it’s amazing to me how many members have really immersed themselves in the podcasts and not only know, the topics, which I know, but the numbers, which I don’t remember. It’s incredible. And so I appreciate you doing this. And this is what actually interested me, was Priscilla mentioned that you have this Excel, I think it’s an Excel, spreadsheet of all the podcasts and the topics, which I think is incredible. And I wish more members were half as engaged in the process of lifelong learning as you clearly have been with these podcasts.

Terry:
Thank you. I had been substituting for BNI for over a dozen years. And I only knew that you had a 90-minute meeting and that you had One-to-Ones. I never knew about the wealth of information available to members. So I didn’t know that until I joined 14 months ago and they said, “Oh yeah, by the way, you can listen to podcasts.” It has just been so exciting for my personal growth and my business growth, to be able to listen to these. So thank you.

Ivan:
Well, as much as I’m not crazy about substituting for a decade, I do love that once you joined, you immersed yourself in the educational content. Look it’s second to none in BNI. No networking organization in the world provides the kind of educational content that BNI provides to its members through BNI Podcast, through my blog, IvanMisner.com, through my social media, and most importantly, through Business Builders online. There is just so much content that helps people build their business through referrals. So I’m glad you’ve engaged in it. I appreciate that.

Terry:
Thank you. One of the things I wanted to change in the title other than just “Voracious Podcast Consumption” – lessons learned from a new member. I think I’d like to share a couple things that I think will help your listeners. One of them, in the times of all those years that I did was a substitute. Nobody, no membership director ever said, we’re looking for a clown or an entertainer. They would say, “Oh, we need a CPA, or refer a lawyer, pest control,” they would list all of these standard kinds of categories.

Ivan:
Professions, yeah.

Terry:
But they would never say my specific category.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Terry:
So I figured, oh, I don’t belong in BNI because they never mentioned it.

Ivan:
Well, you bring up a good point. I mean, it’s very easy to focus on the common professions in BNI. But I think when somebody comes to visit in an unusual profession, it’s important for us as members to invite that person. And I’ve had some very unusual professions in BNI, including a pet shipper.

Terry:
Wow.

Ivan:
That was their profession; they shipped pets around the world. I was like, wow, okay. Somebody said to them, you should join, and they did. And they said they got a lot of business out of it. Another one that I thought was just crazy was goat lawn care.

Terry:
Oh, yes. Yeah!

Ivan:
Right. I saw that in France, where this company, this member, had goats that they would bring in to places that had hilly land where lawnmowers couldn’t safely go. They would bring in goats to eat all the grass and they were a great member. And they got a lot of business. And sometimes we, we see professionals like that we forget to ask them to join, to say, “Hey, look, we think we could help you.” And I think that’s really important. I’m glad somebody finally asked you.

Terry:
Actually, my girlfriend, who was a face painter, talked me into it, because she was getting so much out of her leadership. She was in a chapter in the Sacramento [California] area and after the COVID, locked down, they didn’t consider clowns as essential. What we know now is for people’s mental health, they actually needed more fun and joy and laughter. But so it was when people thought that my husband and I had retired, because they didn’t see us for a couple years, that was the inspiration for me to join BNI finally.

Ivan:
Well, I’m very glad you did, and I couldn’t agree more. You know, during COVID, a lot of the things that I published were — particularly in BNI podcast — and you’ll see there’s a number, I think, five Special Episodes I did. They were just me talking into the microphone, which was, you know, micro dose the news and the negativity that’s out there and focus on the positive, because the positive is what’s going to get you through these difficult times. And so, you know, I’m a big believer in focusing on positive and fun and, you know, good comedy and interesting things, rather than negative and dark. You know, focus on the light, not the dark when things are challenging.

Terry:
Amen.

Ivan:
Yeah, go ahead.

Terry:
Well, another lesson was, when I first started, I did a One-to-One and I didn’t know about the G.A.I.N.S. profile, scheduling the amount of time, you know, doing research beforehand on the bio, or the profile. And I’m in a One-to-One with this guy and I’m asking questions, and I’m a really good listener. And at an hour, he said, he had to go. I never had an opportunity to talk or share anything. And he had the audacity to say, “I know what you do.”

Ivan:
Mmmm.

Terry:
Oh, wow.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s bad. So one of the things that I’ve changed is the way I recommend the G.A.I.N.S. exchange over the years. I used to say, you know, have one person do the goals, accomplishments, interests, networks and skills, and then the other person. But I’ve changed that over the last few years. I’ve said, one person should talk about their goals, and then the next person talks about their goals. Then one person should talk about their accomplishments and the next person. And that way, you’re bouncing back and forth, so that you don’t get somebody to say something so lame as “I know what you do.”

Terry:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Because they don’t know what you do. You know, even in a profession like a florist… if a florist said, “Well, you know what I do.” Well, no, we don’t know what you do, not completely. Do you do weddings? Do you know, can you take on accounts where people can bill to their account? What kind of specialty services do you offer? And so I mean, there’s a lot in any profession that you just don’t know unless you hear from them. And certainly in an entertainment business like yours, that’s the case.

Terry:
Yeah. Well, that is actually a segue to one of my favorite podcasts of yours. You won’t remember it by number 206. But it’s the one “You Never Know Who They Know.”

Ivan:
Yeah.

Terry:
And that was a woman who owned a small gift basket business and she offered to help the project management consultant who did business with large manufacturers. And he haughtily said he didn’t see any possible way she could help him. Do you remember this one?

Ivan:
Oh, yeah, Episode 206. I do. Well, I remember because you sent me the notes on it. I do remember the podcast, though. And it was a very powerful message.

Terry:
Yeah. Because he essentially… he walked away. Said, “Thanks. But no, thanks.” But he didn’t understand that she had good clients in the manufacturing businesses, and her father-in-law owned the biggest manufacturing company in town.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Terry:
But he was rude and he was dismissive.

Ivan:
Yeah. And you know, when people are rude and dismissive, you don’t want to refer them. Because you know, when you give a referral, you give a little bit of your reputation away. If it’s a good referral, it enhances your reputation. If it’s a bad one, it hurts your reputation.

Terry:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Well, listen, we are almost out of time. It’s been awesome, Terry, having you on. Do you have any closing thoughts before we wrap up?

Terry:
Yes, I do. One of the things about my clown business is when I do a silly show with magic, I pretend I’m “dumb.” Because I’m wanting the children to participate; I’m wanting them to ask questions. I’m wanting them to learn how to express themselves differently. And, like, for example, I do a jump rope thing, where I don’t know how to jump rope, while the kids are calling me out to do things like over your head, and I throw the jump rope over my head; letting go of it under my feet, I step on it, you know, so I am engaging the kids to express themselves.

And I actually presented this little skit at a BNI Regional meeting and Martin had said, this is a perfect way, as an example of how you have to keep saying things differently, because people learn it, and then they express themselves differently. And when you have a mentor in BNI who’s helping you, they can show you how to jump rope. So you don’t have to learn from everybody telling you ways that you don’t understand how to do it. But you have a BNI person who’s already gone through the ropes, has the education to save you lots of time, effort, money, energy, when if you joined BNI, they can help you get going to hit the ground running faster.

Ivan:
Well, that’s very true. And I really appreciate all that you do for BNI. And [I’d] love to see that Excel spreadsheet and we’ll put it in this podcast if we can.

Terry:
It’s actually not Excel. It’s just a piece of paper I got offline. That was a chart that was numbered one through 1000.

Ivan:
Okay, I look forward to seeing that. If we can, we’ll put it in this podcast. So, your website is www.zanyentertainment.com, www.zanyentertainment.com. Anyone that’s interested, go there. Terry, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast and thank you for your participation in BNI.

Terry:
Oh thank you, Ivan. I appreciate being on.

Ivan:
All right.

Terry:
Thank you so much.

Priscilla:
Well, that’s it for this week. Thank you both for such great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Terry Donaldson aka Sparkles the Clown shares what she learned from listening to 700 episodes of the podcast during her first year in BNI.

title.rendered: Episode 858: The Makeover of Your Weekly Ask

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jim Meadows from the BNI Business Scouts chapter in Kansas City shares how he turned a blasé, general, and repetitive weekly presentation into a 50-week sales training curriculum for his chapter–all thanks to BNI Podcast Episode 80: Specialists Win More Referrals.

The more specific you can be about who makes a good referral and why, the easier it is for other members to think of potential referrals. The moment even one chapter member gives a unique, specific weekly ask, you can actually see people starting to perk up. Getting specific doesn’t just generate more referrals: it makes the whole meeting more exciting.

If you need help with or inspiration for your weekly presentation, this is only one of many episodes that can help you. Go to https://www.bnipodcast.com/category/presentations/ to find more.

Complete Transcript of Episode 858

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Las Vegas [Nevada, USA]. This week, we have our US Conference 2024, our first BNI conference ever was in 1990. So we’ve been doing this for three and a half decades, and I’ve come to a lot of conferences. This is the third one in Las Vegas. A lot of people don’t know that. But it’s the third one in Las Vegas. And it’s a great place to have a conference.

Priscilla:
Is it a lot of fun there when you’re doing the conference?

Ivan:
Yes. Well, I see the inside of hotels…

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
…and a lot of the restaurants, and they don’t let me out, Priscilla. Whenever I start to go towards the front door, there’s somebody there saying, “No, go back. You need to meet with people.” I really don’t get out much. I am getting there a day or two early this week so that I can, you know, see a show. But other than that, I don’t get out much.

Priscilla:
Okay, so who do you have with us today?

Ivan:
I have a guest today, James Meadows. James goes by Jim. And the topic today is the Astounding Makeover of My Weekly Ask for him. And we want to apply this to all of you who are listening. Jim is in his fourth year as a BNI member in the Business Scouts Chapter in Kansas City, Missouri, [USA] where he’s the [BNI Chapter] Education Coordinator. That would be the position I would love most in a chapter is Education Coordinator. Jim earned his MBA and Six Sigma Green Belt certification, which Six Sigma Green Belt won’t help you in a dark alley, but it will help you in business a great deal. His four-decade career with several major corporations includes positions in chemical research, corporate training, HR, quality assurance, leadership development, change management – that’s an important one, particularly in this day and age. Currently, Jim works with clients nationwide in credit card payment processing. He’s raised one boy and two girls, him and his wife Kathy are now happily empty nesters. I relate to that one, Jim, a lot. Welcome to BNI Podcast.

Jim:
My privilege and pleasure to be here, Ivan. Thank you.

Ivan:
So why you’re talking about your makeover of your weekly ask, your weekly presentation. And one of the things that is important to you is something that I think is important. And that’s the idea that specific is terrific. And that it can be difficult for BNI members to execute in a weekly ask. So talk to us about that.

Jim:
Certainly. You know, as BNI members, we really want to share information with our chapters so that we can help them identify high quality referrals for us. Human nature being what it is, and I have a part of human nature in me for sure, we typically kind of go lazy, simple, and general. So by me saying something like, “Any business that takes credit cards is a great referral for me,” we mentally convince our brain that that request actually covers every possible situation in existence, precisely because it’s so broad and big, right?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Jim:
Unfortunately, when we make that request, that blasé, monotonous, general, boring request, it doesn’t give the audience anything specific to think about, remember, to prompt them later on that week with a possible referral.

Ivan:
Yes. Okay, can I just say, Jim, I’m really glad you said ‘boring’ and, you know, ‘not very exciting.’ I couldn’t say that stuff; people would get annoyed with me. So I’m glad you’re saying it. But I completely agree. You gotta get laser specific in what you’re talking about. Otherwise, people just kind of doze off as you’re presenting.

Jim:
Oh, absolutely. You know, here’s what happens. Sometimes we say to ourselves, at least I always had, “Well, you know, I could stand up and give a very specific, unique weekly ask, but there’s so many other things I do for all of my clients. And if I stand up and give a real specific ask, I’m robbing my chapter of hearing about the 99.9% other great things I do.” But of course, I was dead wrong in thinking that, Ivan.

Ivan: 
Yeah, because you can’t do it all in a minute or 30 seconds or you know, each chapter has a different amount of time that they can do their weekly presentation. And you can’t do that in just 30 seconds or a minute, you need more time. And so that’s why being really specific over a period of a year gives a lot of content for people. But most people don’t get that and it takes a little while for them to understand it.

Jim:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
So how is the weekly ask portion of the meeting… how is it extra exciting when everyone is delivering a unique, specific request?

Jim:
Well, going back to the boring and monotonous, Ivan, you know, you can almost see your chapter members mentally checking out…

Ivan:
Yeah.

Jim:
As each chapter member gives a blasé, general, monotonous, boring, weekly ask. It practically puts people to sleep. However, I’ve noticed the moment even one chapter member gives a unique, specific weekly ask, you can actually see people starting to perk up. They’re thinking, hey, something I can finally sink my teeth into. Many people are actually taking notes during those weekly specific asks. I know I do, because I want to be able to help those chapter members. So yeah, it makes the meeting much more exciting and engaging.

Ivan:
Yeah. Now you’re calling it a “weekly ask” and an ask is fine. I’m okay with that. It’s good to ask for referrals. But also, to me, it’s also weekly education. To me, the best members understand that you’re trying to train a sales force, not close a sale. And so it’s about teaching people how to refer you. Would you not agree with that?

Jim:
I completely agree with that. It was when I listened to BNI Podcast number 80 multiple times it finally dawned on me, you know what, I’ve got to study this material and kind of reprogram my brain into realizing that by taking that time and energy to deliver a weekly, specific ask each week, over the course of a year, my sales and marketing team has a feast, because I’ve served up a bite size, specific ask week by week by week, over the course of a year.

Ivan:
And you said you listened to podcast number 80 and I love that, because we’re in the eight hundreds now. And so I love that you went back and listened to podcast number 80. There’s a lot of great content in some of those older podcasts.

Jim:
There really is. When I was brand new to BNI, I literally said look, I’ve got to start all the way back with podcast number one, and just start cycling through them. And I’m in the middle of my fourth year of BNI membership, and I can tell you right now, hands down, I have easily cycled through all 800+ podcasts probably a dozen times or so.

Ivan:
Oh my goodness — wow! I’m impressed. That’s impressive. You know, I find it interesting when I go to different countries, and people started saying, oh, podcast number 173 where you are talking about whatever… I’m like, wow. What was that podcast again? You know, I don’t have them memorized. But so many members really have immersed themselves in it like you have. And the truth is, it’s all part of the core value of Lifelong Learning, you know, continuing to improve your skill set.

Jim:
Ivan, specific, you say it all the time — specific is terrific. Can I give you a couple of quick examples?

Ivan:
Yes.

Jim:
One day, our chapter’s Moving and Storage guy stood up and said, “I’m looking for an estate planning attorney, because these are the people that often face client and family situations in which somebody is moving into an assisted living facility, or some other kind of a relocation.” When he said that, I instantly was able to give him a referral to a very fine estate planning attorney in my contact sphere. They now have a very productive working relationship. I never would have been able to generate that particular referral unless he had been that specific.

And then I had another case where one of our chapter members, our lady who handles supplemental health insurance, she stood up and said, “Hey, I’m looking for chiropractors, I am building a database of chiropractors nationwide and I want to be able to have them in my database, so I can refer them as viable treatment options for my clients when they’re injured.” Well, coincidentally, I had done some consulting with chiropractors earlier, and I instantly began generating referrals for her. And over the course of a week I gave her 10 referrals, but again, it never would have happened if she had not been specific.

Ivan:
Yeah, and that’s counterintuitive. You know, most people think you got to give the big picture, tell everybody you know everything you do. But the truth is that doesn’t work nearly as well as being incredibly specific because people are on their own able to pull back and say, “Well, what about this as a possible referral or what about that as a possible referral?” But they have a difficult time looping into the specificity unless a member gives that to them. And that’s really where the specific is terrific came from for me many, many years ago.

Jim:
You’re absolutely correct. And something I think we have to remind ourselves of, is, when you’re brand new to BNI, you have to go through a pretty steep learning curve, at least it was that way for me. But even as a new member, I sensed that my weekly ask was very challenging to me and I struggled with that. But I also observed in other chapters, everybody struggled with their weekly ask. Very few people made it highly specific and unique each week. And we really need to remind ourselves in BNI, that all of us need help and coaching and development so that we can fine tune how we approach that weekly ask. But I guarantee you, the effort is worth it.

Ivan:
Yeah, it is. And again, for everybody, we call it the weekly presentation. But there’s nothing wrong with making an ask and explaining what it is that you’re looking for, and being really, really particular. So before we wrap up, Jim, any last thoughts or comments, before we wrap up?

Jim:
I would say to go to BNI Podcast number 80. And Ivan, you laid out an awesome plan for building a 50-week curriculum. You told us the generic approach on how to do it. You got specific at the right places. And I took that and I sort of templated that over my whole industry and how I do my business. And I was able to build my 50-week curriculum fabulously by following podcast 80. And I can’t thank you and Priscilla enough for that.

Ivan:
Well, I really appreciate you being on BNI Podcast today. For anyone interested in more information on Jim and what he does, go to his business blog, which is blog.reliableinsights.com That’s blog.reliableinsights.com. Jim, thank you so much for being on the BNI Podcast. I appreciate you.

Jim:
Thank you.

Ivan:
Over to you Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, that was just great. Thank you so much to both of you for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Guest Jim Meadows shares how he turned a blasé, general, and repetitive weekly presentation into a 50-week sales training curriculum.

title.rendered: Episode 857: Networking—It’s More Than Just Talking Business (Classic)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This is a rebroadcast of Episode 126, first published in 2009. Links and offers may have expired.

A referral relationship is more than just doing business. You need to find common ground on a personal level. One exercise that can help accomplish this in a BNI chapter is the GAINS Exchange, discussed in Episode 5 of this podcast.

Even though many members are reluctant to do this exercise, it has tremendous results.

Two guys who had barely spoken to each other in their first year in one chapter formed a relationship over their shared interest in soccer and began passing business to each other as a result.

Ivan encourages Priscilla to go back to her BNI chapter and propose a new GAINS Exchange exercise.

See Business by Referral for more on this topic.

Brought to you by Networking Now. [Link to expired discount code removed.]

Complete Transcription of BNI Podcast Episode 126/857

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I am joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan.

Ivan:
Hi, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
What’s going on? How are you?

Ivan:
I’m doing great. Today we’re going to be talking about, I think, a subject that, when I started BNI, I don’t think I fully understood how important this was.

Priscilla:
Great. What is it you’re going to share with us, then?

Ivan:
Well, the fact that networking is more than just talking business, and when I started BNI, my primary focus was, “Hey, it’s all about business; let’s focus on business.” I mean, even in the introduction of the weekly speaker, I talked about the fact that I trained people to talk about their education, talk about their background, talk about their experience. And I really didn’t suggest that you talk about some of your personal interests. And that, I found over the years, was a mistake. People often think that networking is talking about business and exchanging cards, and that’s definitely part of it, but it’s not all of it.

In a networking group, you should talk about more than just business with people. A referral relationship is more than just, “I do business; you do business; let’s do business.” A much better approach is when you find common ground on a personal level and then make these connections and build business with other people.

I think the longer I’ve been involved in networking, the more I’ve seen the power of those personal interests making connections with others. Networking is really about building those personal relationships, and if you remove the personal from the equation, you limit that amount of business that can happen.

I talk about this at length in a podcast a long time ago. The listeners might want to go back to that podcast. It’s podcast number 5, podcast number 5, 005, and I talk about the gains exchange. One BNI group that I worked with, I introduced the GAINS Exchange before it came out in my book, Business by Referral. The GAINS Exchange is an exercise that’s available in BNI. Any member can get it. It’s in Business by Referral, but go to your director and get the form if you want to try it out. It stands for Goals, Accomplishments, Interests, Networks, and Skills. The idea is that you have people share their personal and professional information about themselves in those areas, Goals, Accomplishments, Interests, Networks, and Skills.

Well, two of the participants, when I tested this out years ago, had not done any business with each other, and they’d been in the chapter for a year, hadn’t done any business with each other, really didn’t make a connection at all. It wasn’t that they didn’t like each other; they just didn’t have anything in common; their businesses were really different. And they found out by doing this GAINS Exchange – which, by the way, they dragged their feet, kicking and screaming into this exercise, did not want to do it. Once they did it, they found out that they were both coaches for their sons’ soccer teams, or football teams, as it’s called in some countries. And they quickly became close friends and started helping each other conduct certain aspects of the soccer practices. And they would share coaching techniques with each other, and eventually, they began to scout other teams for each other, so that they would scout the other team and report back what a team was like.

Guess what? After they started doing this for each other on a personal level, within a few months, they started passing business to each other. They began referring business to each other. Two guys who had barely spoken to each other in the first year, because they had so little in common, ended up doing business with each other because they built a relationship over soccer, over football. Go figure! Who would have thought that? I certainly didn’t. But when I saw that done, that nailed it for me, and I knew that that technique had to be put in Business by Referral, and that’s where it was first introduced, was in Business by Referral.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I think that’s a tremendous tool. I mean, knowing the other person on a personal level is exactly what’s it’s all about, I think.

Ivan:
That’s where you kind of make connections with people. If you can find a common interest and start with that, we can make connections and have a very good chance at turning into business. And I really recommend that BNI members experiment with this. I’ve seen chapters where they’ve had every single member complete a GAINS Exchange and come back to the chapter with 30 copies, or however many members were in the chapter, and hand that out to everybody.

I’ve even seen some chapters that have a running three-ring binder so that when a new member joins, they get a binder of all of the members GAINS Exchange, so they, at a glance, get information about that person’s Goals, Accomplishments, Interests, Networks, and Skills. I’ve even seen where maybe they don’t do it to that level of detail; I’ve seen chapters where that week’s speaker also completes a GAINS Exchange. Besides the speaker information for the secretary/treasurer, they also complete a GAINS Exchange. And the member makes 30 copies, or however many for the members, and passes that out at the beginning of the meeting to say, “Hey, here’s who our speaker is going to be later today. Glance at it so you have a little bit of background about this person when he or she speaks.” And it’s really interesting to see how personal interests, whether it be something like soccer or sewing or cooking or wine or chess or anything, between two people makes a personal connection, which makes them feel closer and more connected, which leads to business.

Priscilla:
That’s totally true. I remember when I had to fill mine out, because that was a requirement of our chapter, it doesn’t seem to be right, but it was when I first joined, and I was so stressed about it.

Ivan:
Why so? Why were you stressed out about it?

Priscilla:
Well, it seemed like it just took forever, and I was just agonizing over the format and putting it together. And I remember I stayed up really late doing it and getting it to print right. And it was just like a job, but it was a good thing. I was glad I’d done it.

Ivan:
Well, certainly, spending time and effort in doing it accurately is important, but I’m a real believer of the concept of – Tom Peters talked about it in In Search of Excellence – do it, fix it, try it. Do it, fix it, try it. Do something, lap it around a little bit, see how it works out in the marketplace, fix it, and then try it again. So take that GAINS Exchange that you write and put it out there, see what kind of response you get from people. And then bring it back in and redo it, hang onto it. Redo it; maybe do it digitally. And then every year or so, you revise it and redistribute it. And as you get it out there and you see what seems to connect with people and what doesn’t, then you emphasize some of those things and try it again.

It’s not a one-of-a-kind of deal; it’s a thing that the chapter should do regularly. So one of the things I’d suggest is go back to your chapter and say, “Hey, we haven’t done this for a while. Let’s listen to this podcast, and then let’s all incorporate the GAINS Exchange in some way that makes sense for us over the next two months.”

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
All right. So are you going to report back and let me know how that goes in a couple months?

Priscilla:
Okay. I just had an idea where if you had the person next to you with your GAINS Exchange and then they announce to the group some unusual fact or some unusual thing you were involved with, and then you kind of went around the table, that might make it fun.

Ivan:
It does make it fun. I’ve seen that done. I’ve also seen it where members had to do a one-to-one with somebody else, and they gave them two or three weeks to do it. You’ve got to do it one to one; you’ve got to take a half hour, do a one-to-one with somebody else, minimum half hour one-to-one with somebody else. And then, four weeks from now, everybody is going to stand up and they’re going to do a 60 second presentation on the other person and what they really found interesting about them from the GAINS Exchange.

Priscilla:
Okay. There’s a great idea.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. I love it. Give them enough time; give them three weeks or so. That gives everybody a chance to meet; they do their one-to-one; and then they meet four weeks from then. And I would stand up and I would say what I had learned that was really interesting about you from what you did in the GAINS Exchange and what I read. It’s a great technique. I highly recommend it, and it’s a great way to implement this concept.

Priscilla:
Okay. I’m going to take that one back.

Ivan:
Okay. And you’re going to report back in a few months as to how it’s gone, right?

Priscilla:
That’s right!

Ivan:
Okay, good.

Priscilla:
All right. Well, thank you, Dr. Misner. I think we’re kind of out of time today.

Ivan:
Yup! I do have a special offer on NetworkingNow.

[Note added April 16, 2024: This offer has expired.]

Priscilla:
Oh, okay.

Ivan:
At the end of all of these podcasts, we talk about NetworkingNow and the downloadables. If you’re a BNI member and you would like to be involved in telebridges with me, every month I do a telebridge called the Networking Café, which is part of the NetworkingNow program. And NetworkingNow has tons of downloadable material, videos, audios, PDFs; there’s even some of my books. And so I’m going to give you a password right now that you can type in this password and get six months free in NetworkingNow, which is worth almost $70.

So the password is – it’s one word – freesixmonths. Spell it out; no number. So it’s F-R-E-E-S-I-X-M-O-N-T-H-S, freesixmonths, no spaces. That’s the password.

So when you go to NetworkingNow, you sign up, you punch that in, and you absolutely, positively, unequivocally will get six months free. And if you want to renew, then you can renew; or you can cancel it at six months, and you’ll never be charged anything.

But you’ll have access to tons of downloadable content, and you’ll get an e-mail from us every month saying, “Here’s when the Networking Café next one is, and you’re welcome to join Ivan, and here’s the subject.”

Priscilla:
And how does that work? I know we’re almost out of time, but what does it mean that you’re on this telebridge?

Ivan:
Well, I do a telebridge on subjects that – imagine that I did this topic that networking is more about talking business, but this is a podcast that is one-way communication. You can post a message, which is great. But what if you had a question? The same with the telebridge is that we open them up; we open up the lines and say, “Okay, what questions do you have?” And so it’s really a dialog with anybody as opposed to just a podcast or a one-way communication. If you have questions, you can chat with me directly on these telebridges which are done through NetworkingNow.

Priscilla:
That sounds great. Okay, well, thank you, Dr. Misner.

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I just want remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you’ll join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: A referral relationship is more than just doing business. You need to find common ground on a personal level. The GAINS Exchange can help.

title.rendered: Episode 856: A reason, a Season, a Lifetime

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Understanding that we encounter people in our lives for a reason, a season, or a lifetime allows us to navigate relationships with clarity, and purposefulness, and grace. Embracing the aspects of connections that we may have enables us to treasure each encounter and acknowledge the value and significance of the relationship regardless of its duration. It’s important to recognize those people that we’ve spent time with and created amazing life experiences with in BNI.

Complete Transcript of Episode 856

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am leaving pretty soon for the conference, the [BNI] US and Canadian National Conference in Las Vegas this year.

Priscilla:
Hmm, fun.

Ivan:
Yeah, I love going to BNI conferences. It’s always great to see so many members and directors.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Well, can you explain this wonderful title and tell us what it’s about?

Ivan:
I can and it’s all about relationships. You know, our connections are tapestries that are woven with the threads of emotions, experiences, memories. Throughout our lives, we come across individuals who leave a lasting impact on our life, on our journey. Some relationships endure for a lifetime, while others are transient, and they serve a purpose during a phase of our life.

The concept here is that we meet people for reasons, be it to learn something, experience personal growth, or even to challenge our perspectives and offer insights into the differing connections that we have. There are moments in life when certain individuals enter our lives seemingly by chance or by fate. And these encounters often occur at crossroads, acting as catalysts for some type of transformational progress. And it could be a mentor who guides us through obstacles, a friend who provides comfort during a challenging time, or even an encounter with a stranger that reshapes our perspective on life.

For example, think about that colleague who may have supportively pushed you beyond your comfort zone by challenging your ideas and encouraging some kind of innovation. And although this relationship may not last forever, the knowledge and the skills honed during that interaction, hold value throughout your life. Similarly, friendships formed during periods of your life, such as college roommates, or travel companions. College roommates in particular are very unusual for me, but college roommates or travel companions, they might be fleeting in duration, but they can leave lasting impressions.

So let’s talk for a moment about relationships for a season. Like the changing seasons, the dynamics of our relationships also change. And some connections are formed based on shared experiences, common interests, or being in the proximity of each other. That’s called proxemics. These connections can be incredibly meaningful, though they’re often just temporary in nature, and we refer to them as relationships because they’re intense, but they might have a built-in impermanence. That is, it might not be something that will be a long-term or lifelong relationship.

Think about these as friendships that we formed during phases of our life like childhood playmates, buddies from summer camp, or colleagues at one of our first jobs. While these bonds can be pretty deep and profound, circumstances often dictate how long they last. And as life moves forward and priorities shift, and geographical distances increase, it leads to you know, drifts in these relationships. Nevertheless, the memories we create together during these seasons, stay with us forever and shape who we are.

And then there are connections that last a lifetime. And amidst the nature of relationships that there are, you can select a few that withstand the test of time. And these connections can grow deeper with each passing year. I’ve got several of those that have lasted pretty much my entire life, certainly from high school until today. And lifetime connections give us a sense of belongingness while providing stability and emotional nourishment throughout our journey. And they bear witness to our victories and our hardships, providing support and understanding.

And these relationships really necessitate effort, communication, compromise. And you have to avoid things like benign neglect in order to have those lifetime relationships where you make sure to stay connected with somebody over a long period of time. And I think that no matter what kind of relationship that you may have, a relationship for a reason, a season or a lifetime… And by the way, lifetime, we have many BNI members who have been in BNI, for 20 or 30 years. We’ve got a couple that have been in business in BNI, for 39 years as of today’s date — that’s a lifetime relationship that they have with many, many people.

And whether it’s lifetime or seasonal, or for a reason, understanding that we encounter people in our lives for a reason, a season, or a lifetime, allows us to navigate relationships with clarity, and purposefulness, and grace. And embracing the aspects of connections that we may have enables us to value them for what they are – opportunities for personal development, learning experiences, enrichment. And by realizing the worth of these relationships, we really cherish, I think, the moments that we share, rather than kind of bemoaning their inevitable conclusion.

On one hand, nurturing connections requires investment of time and energy as well as patience and vulnerability. And by prioritizing these relationships in our lives, while setting boundaries and maintaining communication channels, we cultivate bonds that withstand the trials of time. And embracing the patterns of relationships, fosters resilience, and adaptability and gratitude. The concept that we might come across individuals for a short period, or a long period throughout our lives, I think provides insights.

And it’s really, you know, it’s worth thinking about in BNI, because we meet people who will come and maybe go in BNI, but those who stay for a long time are there for seasons or for a lifetime. And they’re built into sort of this intricate fabric of the relationships that you have. And whether these connections are fleeting or long lasting, each one serves a purpose in shaping our experiences and our perspectives as to who we are as individuals. And by embracing the nature of some relationships, and nurturing those, and that’s important nurturing those that last a lifetime, and by doing that, we foster sort of genuine connections that really enhance our lives. And therefore, we navigate the complexities of connecting with people throughout our lives.

And I think that allows us to treasure each encounter and acknowledge the value and significance of the relationship regardless of its duration. And I have certainly seen that in BNI. You know, running it now for almost 40 years, there are people that have come into my life and been there a long time; some that came in and left quickly, and some that have been around for decades. Priscilla, you’re one of them; you’ve been around in my life for decades now.

Priscilla:
Yeah. I know.

Ivan:
And I appreciate you and I appreciate the relationship that we have. And I think it’s important in BNI to recognize those people that we’ve spent time with and created amazing life experiences with. And that’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s a beautiful message, Ivan.

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla:
Really, really nice.

Ivan:
Thanks, I appreciate it. And I think we all as BNI members should give some thought to the people that we have a long-term relationship with in BNI, or in our referral relationship. And you know, I’m a big fan of gratitude and showing gratitude to those people for the relationship that you have is always important.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Such a good idea, Ivan. Before we go, I just have to say how much I value being on this podcast with you and getting the chance to speak with you every month and every week, and it’s really been a wonderful experience.

Ivan:
Well, it has for me too, and everywhere I go around the world, people repeat what you say to me every week. “Hi Ivan, how are you and where are you?”

Priscilla:
Right. (chuckling)

Ivan:
I can’t tell you how many people say that to me.

Priscilla:
That’s so fun. I love it.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s your line. Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, you’re so welcome, Ivan. Well, that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Whether we know people for a reason, a season, or a lifetime, it’s important to recognize our relationships in BNI.

title.rendered: Episode 855: Referrals Through Customer Appreciation

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Guest Michael Ann Dillinger explains how to use your customer appreciation program to refer your customers to members of your BNI chapter.

Not sure how you can provide referrals to businesses not related to your own? Instead of ordering customer appreciation gifts from a catalog, have your fellow BNI members provide them. The referring member pays for the gift, and the referred member collects customer feedback for their referral partner while doing so. Both members increase their chances of repeat business and potential referrals from those customers.

If you are having a hard time referring to someone, do a one-to-one with them to find areas where you overlap. What do they have coming up? What events do they have coming up? What issues do they have in their business? Find out how you can help with those things, and be sure to tell them how to refer you in turn.

Learn more about Michael Ann’s skin care line for men and women at https://michaelann.truaurabeauty.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 855

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in the Austin area this week doing a writing retreat. My publisher is putting me in a small room with a typewriter and told me to write, so that’s what I’m doing this week.

Priscilla: (laughing)
Okay, that sounds good. So who do you have today for us?

Ivan:
I have another guest today, Michael Ann Dillinger. Michael Ann and her husband were on- the-road truck drivers for a number of years where they went all across America in the days before GPS and cell phones. Michael has been in direct sales in the skincare business since 1998. And she has been in BNI as a member for 13 years. She’s now a Chapter Success Coach. And she has lots of interests including quilting, the violin, volunteering, and acting as an extra in TV or movies. I want to hear about that off recording, Michael, tell me more about that. And Michael, you and I have something in common. We were both named after our fathers. I was named after my dad, Ivan. And you are named after your dad, Michael. And they threw an “Ann” on to feminize the name. Pretty cool. Good. That’s a great story.

Michael Ann:
Thank you. Yes. Nice southern name, Michael Ann.

Ivan:
Michael Ann. Well, listen, you’ve got an interesting topic that I really wanted to share on the podcast. And that is a customer appreciation, and particularly customer appreciation with companies or businesses in BNI that have nothing to do with what you do. And so, I’ll just let you tell your story, because it’s a really good one.

Michael Ann:
Okay, well, I’ve been in BNI for about a year, and we had a plumber come into the group named Tim. And Tim was already doing customer appreciation with his clients. But he came into BNI and he just was such a giver; he wanted to give everybody referrals. But he got to me and he was a little bit stumped. He did not know how a plumbing company could refer business to a skincare and makeup salesperson in the group. So he kind of looked at what he was already doing, and he hit upon the customer appreciation. I think at the time they were mailing out tins of cookies to their customers, which was nice.

But he knew that if he had me take a gift, and I just do typically bath and body items for my line, which can work for men or women, and he knew if he could use me for that not only would he be referring to me, in helping out with BNI with referrals and doing his part, as far as that goes, he also knew that there would be a personal touch. So instead of that customer getting something in the mail, they get an actual phone call from me and a personal delivery. So the way we do that is he just would give me the information of the client. I call the client up, I tell them I have a customer appreciation gift for them, make arrangements to drop it by, then while I’m on the phone with them, I ask open-ended questions about this service [with the plumbing company]. So tell me about the communication. How did you like the end result? So ask open-ended questions. And I’m able to write down feedback, which is a valuable piece of information for the plumber to have. So if he needs to go back and follow up or correct anything then he can do that. And I’m also able to ask for that Google review that Google link.

So it provides more of a service to him because he’s got a live person doing this feedback and this customer appreciation rather than just sending a gift in the mail. So it just is a way to add a little bit more of a ‘wow’ factor with his customers and it ended up being a really good, just a really good partnership with a plumber and a skincare lady in the group and it was just an unusual connection that you would not have thought about otherwise.

Ivan:
So, how many years did you do this? You know, have this relationship where you’re basically giving gifts for the plumber?

Michael Ann:
I am still doing gifts for him, and it’s been over 10 years.

Ivan:
Wow.

Michael Ann:
I’ve also added some other trades as well — HVAC, home repair, painter — there’s lots of other trades that have used me. But it doesn’t have to be a trade. Even every once in a while, I’ll have a realtor reach out or someone like that, just anybody that wants to thank a customer can call me up and tell me what they’ve got in mind. And then I put something together, and I take it and provide the feedback. So it’s a way for them to provide that extra service without having to hire an employee, because they can truly call me for just one gift. It doesn’t have to be 10 gifts a month, it can just be whatever they need, they can hire me on an as-needed basis for that.

Ivan:
Yeah. And you not only provide the gift, but then you can provide them feedback on how things went, which is a real value add.

Michael Ann:
Yes, you’re right. And I don’t charge anything for the service. I don’t charge anything for the phone call the feedback, the delivery, or even the gift wrapping. They just tell me how much they want to spend on the gift and I make my money through the products. So I do not charge an additional fee for anything else other than just what the cost of the product is.

Ivan:
That’s impressive. Now, off air, I heard you talking to Priscilla about how does this work with men getting a gift? Because your products are normally associated with women. So talk about that a little bit. I found it interesting.

Michael Ann:
Yeah, that’s a great question. And typically the bath and body line that I have is more of a citrus scent. So it’s not feminine, it’s not floral. Men love bodywash. And our hand cream has Tasmanian pepper extract in it, so it’s good for red, chapped skin. So it’s great for men, especially men that work outside or work with their hands; it can be really good for that. So I use just the generic bath and body products. It typically can be used by men or women. And if the man doesn’t want it, he can always give it to his wife or mother, whatever.

Ivan: (chuckling)
Very true. Well, listen, I love this concept of, you know, appreciation gifts. I’ve been a big fan of doing some kind of thank you for referrals or for doing business for a long time. I think that works way better than you know giving a discount. Providing something of value add or a gift, like what you’re doing, is definitely the way to go. Anything else that you’d like to add?

Michael Ann:
No, I agree. I think anytime you can do that as a value-added service. I don’t even mind shipping stuff, you know, I have people that have customers that aren’t right in my region. So I still gift wrap it and make it look really cute and just add that personal touch. I add a little note in there that says, “Thank you from___” and then the name of the business that sent it to them. I do include my card in there as well, but there’s no sales pitch other than that. But people are usually… it’s like the icing on the cake. Because normally, 99% of the time the customer is happy with the work and that’s usually enough. And so when I call, it’s unexpected, and I often hear things like, “Oh my goodness. They didn’t have to do that. That was so nice of them.” So not only were they already happy because of the good job, but now they’re really going to remember that business because on top of everything else, they got a gift. And it’s usually a very well received… they’re very happy to get it.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. Related, but not exactly the same topic — you talk a little bit about a member — if they’re having trouble referring — to brainstorm with them about what needs they have in your business. You want to talk about that before we wrap up?

Michael Ann:
I would love to. So if you are having a hard time referring to someone, I just recommend do a one-to-one with them, and find areas where you overlap. What do they have coming up? What events do they have coming up? What issues do they have in their business? And see if that is maybe something that you can help them with. Also, give them the words to say. I don’t expect anyone to go out and sell my product for me. But if I know who they’re going to be talking to, I can at least tell them what to say. So that they can at least open the door and get their permission to call. So you know you can kind of come up with your own Power Team.

My chapter has started a Personal Services Power Team: Personal Services Problem Solvers. And we’re a group of eight that basically will come to your home and provide a service. And we’ve got some unique businesses that fit together in that as well, everything from a pet sitter, to travel agent, to a dryer vent – a person who will come clean your dryer vents. And so we just kind of decided, you know what? We fit together because we’re targeting the same person, basically the homeowner, a residential person, and we come to your home and provide a service. So we kind of brainstormed and came up with a Power Team.

Ivan:
What I love about this discussion with you, Michael Ann, is that you’re talking about things that kind of open up your mindset as to the possibilities of how you can get connected with people and get referrals for people. And it’s this kind of creativity that always blows me away in really successful groups, because they’re finding ways to find opportunities to do business.

Michael Ann:
I agree.

Ivan:
Well, good. Any last thought, before we wrap up?

Michael Ann:
I just appreciate your time. And thank you for allowing me to share.

Ivan:
Well, Michael Ann, thank you very much for being on the BNI Podcast. I really appreciate you being here. And I appreciate that story. And I think there’s a lot that, those of you who are listening, a lot to learn from this. Creativity creates opportunities. And that is exactly what Michael Ann in this [BNI] chapter has done. So thank you so much. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Wow, thank you both so much. That was a great podcast.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
