title.rendered: Episode 854: The World’s Best-Known Marketing Secret

content.rendered:
Synopsis

It’s been 30 years since The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret was first published in 1994. Mike Macedonio, co-author of the fourth edition, joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about why the book is still relevant today.

The Fourth Edition remains the most comprehensive book Dr. Misner has been involved with on networking, because it incorporates material from other BNI books that had appeared after the first edition, including the VCP Process®, The 10 Commandments of Networking a Mixer, and the Networking Scorecard.

Get your copy of The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret: Building Your Business by Referral on Amazon.

Complete Transcript of Episode 854

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
This week, I am headed to an area outside of Austin to stay at a… I’m going to do some “glamping.” You know, stay at a really nice camp that’s definitely first class — it’s really a chateau, but it’s going to be to see the solar eclipse. And it’s a Virgin Galactic program. Virgin Galactic is putting together a group of people to watch the solar eclipse that’s going across the United States.

Priscilla:
Wow, that really sounds like fun.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s the second one that I’ve had a chance to see. The first was on my way to Antarctica. So, that’ll be fun.

Priscilla:
That sounds great. And what do you have today for us?

Ivan:
I have a guest again, he was our guest last week. He’s been on six times now. Mike Macedonio, who is a [BNI USA] Executive Director in the [California] San Francisco Bay Area. He’s been a member and director of BNI for over 27 years. If you heard the introduction last week, he was switched to birth, which I still want to hear the answer to at some point, Mike, not today. He’s been struck by lightning and bitten on the face by a Bengal tiger, which we talked about last week. Mike, thanks for coming back to the BNI Podcast.

Mike:
Thank you for having me, Ivan.

Ivan:
So Mike, you are my co-author on The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret, as well as Truth or Delusion. You became a New York Times best-selling author along with me on the book Truth or Delusion, as I recall. And The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret, Fourth Edition, you co-authored with me. The book first came out in 1994. What I’d like to do today is maybe touch upon a couple of the things that we think are still… well, I think most of it is still valuable today. But the book came out 30 years ago, this year 1994, the First Edition came out. We are now on our Fourth Edition that I did with you, Mike.

And you know, what’s interesting is this book was rejected by over 40 publishers back in the 1990s. And finally, I got it published. This is back in the day when you absolutely had to have a publisher in order to have a book. Today, through Amazon, it’s pretty easy to publish. But I had 40 rejections and I kept a spreadsheet of all those rejections. I ran across it a couple of years ago, and I put it on a blog of mine. But I had 40 rejections before this book was accepted by a publisher and has now sold several hundred thousand copies and has been translated into multiple languages. And you are my co-author on the Fourth Edition, Mike. Let me ask you a question. What stands out most to you, from The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret that is really relevant still today?

Mike:
Well, I’ll tell you on multiple levels, I would say that this is a book that probably changed my life or the way I do business. And one of the first ways that happened was this book was picked up by a gentleman in Cape Cod, you know, who started the BNI in that area, who I then got introduced to, who then introduced me to being a [BNI] Director, and then the rest is history.

But also in terms of the content. You know, when I first got involved with BNI, my addictive personality really had me hungry for all of this information that you were writing about and teaching about. And, you know, between the book and the training programs that you and others helped create, Certified Networker, that just blew me away.

Ivan:
Yeah, well you were an integral part in the creation of Certified Networker.

Mike:
I would like to say I was a very eager student.

Ivan:
Yeah, but you also added an immense amount of content to that.

Mike:
I appreciate that. But again, I will credit that for really changing my perspective on marketing your business, and going away from being that self-sufficient, independent, dog-eat-dog mentality to the power of collaboration. You know, the power of Givers Gain, and that was a game changer for me. It absolutely was a game changer from that point on.

Ivan:
So I think this book is probably the most comprehensive book that I’ve been involved with on networking, because by the time you and I did the Fourth Edition, we really added a lot of content that I had in some other books. So it’s, it’s probably one of the most comprehensive. And I know the VCP Process® is something that you definitely want to talk about. But I want to mention “The 10 Commandments of Networking a Mixer,” which is chapter number eight in World’s Best Known Marketing Secret. That was the first article I ever wrote, ever, on networking was “The 10 Commandments of Networking a Mixer,” and it was picked up by a magazine or two. And it was the very first piece I ever wrote. I probably wrote it in 1985, it was published in 1986 as a standalone article, but ended up in the book by 1994. What would you like to point out from the book, Mike. Do you want to start with VCP?

Mike:
Yeah, I think the VCP Process is relevant then as it is now. It’s about the relationship phases we go through in developing that referral relationship.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mike:
From visibility to credibility and profitability. And truly understanding where we stand within that continuum is really the formula for referral success. And then what you also have in there is the networking scorecard. And I love this as a tool…

Ivan:
Before we get to the scorecard, because I want to talk about that. But before we get to that, I know you have a big issue about VCP not being a formula, but a process.

Mike:
Oh, yes.

Ivan:
It is NOT a formula. It’s a process. Do you want to speak briefly on that? And then talk about the scorecard.

Mike:
I think sometimes people will redefine what VCP means. I’ve heard people say visibility plus credibility equals profitability. Nothing could be further from the truth. They are, like you mentioned, a continuum of that relationship that you don’t skip. And profitability is a high level referral relationship. It’s not a transaction. It’s a referral process, not a sales process.

Ivan:
Right. And it’s not the end of a formula; it’s the end of a process, which people need to understand. Because you can have visibility and credibility, but not hit profitability. So it’s the process of getting there. Tell me about the scorecard.

Mike:
Well, what I love about the scorecard is a tool to measure your activities. But more specifically, it’s a measurement of “giving” activities. Right? These are all the activities we do with our network that contribute to our social capital; contribute sort of deposits in those referral relationships, which directly correlate to moving those relationships from visibility, to credibility, to profitability. You know, and there are simple things that still apply today. Thank you notes, appreciation gifts, connecting, referring other people. I just love that as a tool for us to be on track, to make sure we’re very deliberate about our networking activities.

Ivan:
So, do you know where the scorecard concept came from for me?

Mike:
For some reason, in the back of my mind, I want to think that it came from what used to be a cold calling tool.

Ivan:
Yeah, it was used substantially for cold calling. But it was used also for networking. And it’s the “one-card system” which was used by insurance agents. And I used to have a relationship with a woman who was an insurance agent. And I learned the one-card system, which did involve a lot of cold calling, but it also involved networking and other things. And I thought, well, if you can measure your cold calling success, why can’t you measure on your networking success? And so the scorecard came out of what’s, in the insurance industry, called the one-card system.

Mike:
You know, Ivan, like I mentioned, we’ve developed training programs off of this. It’s interesting, when I watch clients using the networking scorecard, you know, their points go up. And immediately after that, what happens is nothing. There’s actually a lag of time. But what ends up happening after that lag, is an increase in referrals. And then from that increase of referrals, there’s a lag before it actually turns into business, such as the sales cycle.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Mike:
Sometimes with the trap we fall into is we do all of these giving activities, we generate referrals, we get these bids, this business. So what do we start doing? Less giving activities.

Ivan:
Right.

Mike:
And it’s interesting how, you know what happens when we stop doing these activities? Nothing? Because again, there’s a lag; and then we notice our referrals drop off.

Ivan:
Well, I think you were the one that first mentioned to me that BNI is a little bit like a gym. You know, when you work out and you get really healthy and fit, the last thing you want to do is stop working out and staying healthy and fit. You want to continue to do it, which is the same here is you continue to do until you start to get the results. Or you’re filling a pipeline where it takes time for the results to come out of the pipe, but you’ve got to keep working it.

Mike:
Yep, it is. So many times we hear people say, “I’m too busy.” So you’re going to turn off your marketing? You’re going to leave the network?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mike:
You don’t stop going to the gym just because you’re in shape.

Ivan:
Yeah, look, the “I’m too busy” is the lamest excuse I’ve ever heard for networking. It’s like, you’re too busy to do marketing? I mean, networking is about marketing. And, you know, I understand that if a business is scaling and the company is growing, the person who is the owner may not be the member anymore, but they need to send their sales reps. Because if you want the pipeline to stay filled, then you send your sales reps. You don’t stop doing the things that made you successful. That’s just so incredibly obvious to me.

Mike:
100%.

Ivan:
Any last thoughts on The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret before I wrap up?

Mike:
Nothing other than I highly encourage anyone that wants to develop their own referral marketing plan, this book is a step-by-step chapter-by-chapter to do exactly that. It is on Audible so if you don’t like to read, you can do that. I guess the book is still in print because my golf pro came to me a number of weeks ago and asked me to sign the book for him. So I highly recommend you take a look at The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret, in creating your referral marketing plan.

Ivan:
This book IS still in print, and it is on Kindle, and it is on Audible. And it is written by myself and Mike Macedonio. The Fourth Edition subtitle is called: Building Your Business by Referral. We’ll make sure to have a link here for the book. I think this is probably the most comprehensive book that I’ve done. I did it with Mike. I think it’s the most comprehensive book I’ve done on networking, to [this] date. So I would invite you to check it out on Amazon. Mike, thanks again for being on BNI Podcast, your seventh visit to the podcast.

Mike:
Thank you, Ivan.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, thank you both so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret was first published in 1994. Ivan Misner and Mike Macedonio tell us why the book is still relevant.

title.rendered: Episode 853: Six Degrees of Separation

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Ivan Misner wrote The 29% Solution: 52 Weekly Networking Success Strategies when he learned that only 29% of people are actually connected to strangers by six degrees, and outlined networking strategies to make sure you’re part of the 29%.

After reading the book, Mike Macedonio discovered that more than half of the successful connections in Stanley Milgram’s small-world experiment were made through just three people.

Together, they discuss ways BNI members can become super-connectors and ways that BNI can recognize members for those efforts.

Ask yourself: are you a connector? And if so, how are you connecting and who you connecting? Are you connected to connectors? Share your ideas for becoming a phenomenal connector in the comments below.

Get your copy of The 29% Solution: 52 Weekly Networking Success Strategies.

Complete Transcript of Episode 853

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I am actually on my way to Los Angeles to do an event down in Orange County to speak to some [BNI] directors that are in Orange County this week.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. And who do you have on board with us today?

Ivan:
Well, we have a guest, Mike Macedonio. Mike is a New York Times bestselling author, a speaker, a future astronaut with Virgin Galactic; he and I are both in that program. He’s Executive Director for BNI in the San Francisco Bay Area. And in this role, he helps thousands of local business owners build their business through the BNI program. And he’s been part of BNI for 27 years now. Something that a lot of people don’t know about Mike, other than the fact that he’s been on BNI podcast five times now – episode 187, 188, 381, 530, and 603. And besides that, and even I didn’t know some of these things, he was switched at birth. Mike, not on the podcast, but off podcast you gotta tell me that story. [He’s been] struck by lightning and bitten in the face by a Bengal tiger. The tiger actually doesn’t surprise me because I know a little bit about some of your interests. But I didn’t know you were bitten in the face. Mike Macedonio, welcome to BNI Podcast, again.

Mike:
Oh, thank you, Ivan. And yes, they’re all true, by the way. I was switched at birth, struck by lightning, and bitten in the face by a Bengal tiger. Here’s something you may not know, Ivan. I was bitten in the face by a Bengal tiger during a BNI meeting.

Ivan:
Okay. I didn’t know it was during a BNI meeting.

Mike:
Yeah, it was a Zoom meeting. And I was caring for a cub that had been rescued and I thought people on the Zoom meeting would like to see a cub. I picked it up. Tigers do not like to be picked up, so he gave me a little snip on the cheek. I didn’t even recognize it. But my wife, Dawn, was telling me to wipe the blood, wipe it off.

Ivan: (chuckling)
All right, well, that’s a first. There’ve been a lot of things that have happened in BNI meetings, but I didn’t know of anybody being bitten on the face by a tiger during a BNI meeting.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
So, you want to talk about six degrees of separation. And I love this topic. And it’s a myth. So, tell me why is this a topic of interest to you, Mike?

Mike:
Well, starting with your book, The 29% Solution, when you reveal that it is a myth, and maybe you can tell us a little bit about the study that was done by Milgram, and why you classify this as a myth.

Ivan:
Yeah, so I did The 29% Solution because I was going to write an article on the six degrees of separation, how we’re all separated by everyone else in the world by six degrees. And as I did a deep dive in the research, I found out that it was just absolutely not true. That Milgram had done this study of several hundred people where they had to get a packet of material from someone in the Midwest in the U.S. to a stockbroker in Boston. And he discovered that it did, in fact, take about six steps to get from the people in the Midwest to the person in Boston. Now, this is before “Monsieur Google” where you can just look anybody up and figure out where they’re at. This was done back in the 1960s. And it did take six steps.

The problem was only 29% of the participants ever got it through to the person that it was supposed to go to; 71% never found the person. So, the media picked up on this idea of six degrees of separation and they said we’re all separated by six degrees, when in fact, it’s just not true. Only less than a third of the people in the survey were separated by six degrees; other people could never find the person. So my idea with The 29% Solution book was — how do you become part of the 29%? How do you become one of the people that Milgram found was connected to others by six degrees or six steps?

Mike:
And you know, when we hear that, there’s these, “Oh, no, we’re not all connected by six degrees.” And like you said, there can be a plan to be part of the 29%. What I found interesting in the study that Milgram had done, was there’s some information in there about the connectors that made it successful. And in short, what he found was when the packets were going from Omaha, Nebraska, to Sharon, Massachusetts, or Boston depending if it was his home or his business, that not all degrees of separation are equal. And I thought that was kind of fascinating, that even when the thing did succeed, he found some asymmetrical patterns.

Now, this is very interesting. Twenty-four of the letters reached the stockbroker’s home in Sharon, Massachusetts. Of those twenty-four, sixteen came from the same person, a clothing merchant – interesting. So, 16 out of 20 came through the same person, the balance of the letters that made to his office in Boston, the majority of those came through two other men. Okay, so overall, half of the responses that came back to the stockbroker were delivered to him by the same three people. And that’s when he talks about not all degrees are equal.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mike:
You know, when I think about that, it’s like, you’re right. Okay, getting connected and being connected happens to those special few. Okay, they’re known as the connectors. And we see that in networks like BNI, we see that in social networks; there are certain people that tend to be the connectors of their networks, and bring people in.

Ivan:
Yeah, listen, you bring up a good point. And I really didn’t talk about that in the book, I wasn’t aware of it. Just a handful of people were the ones that actually got the packet to the individual and they are connectors. And that’s what we can be in BNI. We can become those kinds of connectors.

Mike:
And, you know, when I look at that, I say okay, so how do I use that? How do I use this concept of these special few and the connectors? And certainly, The 29% Solution gives you a week-by-week way of how you can do that. And ultimately, what we want to do is, we want to be a connector. We want to be a connector, and we want to be connected to connectors. You know, and again, we look at BNI — How am I a connector? Well, when I bring a visitor, I’m connecting people I know to people I know. When I bring people into the network and sponsor them, okay, I’m being a connector. And I can also look, particularly with all of BNI’s tracking, it gives me all the information I need to be able to research — well, who were the other connectors? Who are the other people bringing people to the table — in visitors, in sponsorship?

Ivan:
So how could we inspire more connectors in BNI?

Mike:
That’s a great question. I think it starts with awareness. I think with this conversation you’re having right now that 29% of the people are the special few, and the other 71% can’t find their way around.

Ivan:
I think they can’t find their car keys.

Mike:
Yeah. I think starting the awareness in this conversation is a great way. And then the second way is well, how do I do it? You know, how do I introduce those people? What do I say? And I think one of the biggest things we can do inside of BNI is recognize people for doing it. Make a real big deal when people can successfully bring visitors or if they ultimately sponsor someone inside of BNI. You know, they’ve just brought an opportunity for any one of the people in their networks to have a tremendous relationship and referral source.

Ivan:
Yeah. Yeah, I think that recognition of bringing somebody in is important. You know, there are two levers that we can pull in BNI that increase retention. One is attendance at the Member Success Program [MSP] training. And then the other is a new member sponsoring another new member. And it was that second one that really confused me when I saw the data, but the more I thought about it, the more it made sense that if you bring in a friend, you don’t want to leave because you’ve got a friend that you have brought in. And so it’s really important, I think, for us to recognize those individuals, who particularly within their first year, sponsor someone into BNI, because the chances of them staying — them the sponsor, staying for a second, third, or fourth year goes up dramatically.

Mike:
And so what you have there is you have someone staying longer, so that’s a referral source established in the chapter. You have somebody else they brought in, and you’ve also shown them how to be a connector.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mike:
Well, now that they’ve done it once, they’re doing it again and it’s probably going to happen again and again and again.

Ivan:
Yeah, excellent. Well, what else… any other thoughts or ideas about six degrees and The 29% Solution?

Mike:
Now the one thing I would put out there to anyone that’s listening to this topic is, you know, evaluate yourself. You know, are you a connector? And if so, how are you connecting and who you connecting? Are you connected to connectors? And then finally, ask yourself, How can I be a better connector? How can I be one of those special few in the six degrees of separation and become a phenomenal connector?

Ivan:
Yeah, great suggestions. And for anyone who’s interested in the book, The 29% Solution, it is still available. It’s out of print… I think it’s out of print, it might still be available, but it’s definitely available on audiobook and on Kindle. And there’s some great content there. “52 Weekly Networking Success Strategies” is the subtitle of the book, and I invite you to go check it out. We’ll make sure and put a link in here. Any last thought, Mike, before we wrap up?

Mike:
No. I can’t recommend that book enough. I think it’s a great way to be part of the six degrees of separation or The 29% Solution. The second book I would also recommend is “The Tipping Point.” “The Tipping Point” also gets into the conversation of six degrees of separation.

Ivan:
Yeah, and there’s probably another book that you’d recommend, and we’re going to talk about that next week, called The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret.

Mike:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
All right. Mike, thanks for being on BNI Podcast, yet once again.

Mike:
Privilege.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

title.rendered: Episode 852: “Take Off Your Bib” (Classic)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week Dr. Misner brings us a great metaphor from a BNI director in South Dakota and a BNI member named Bill:

Networking will never work if you’re here for yourself. You need to take off your bib, put on your apron, and learn to serve other people.

Here are 12 ways to do that.

  1. Provide referrals.
  2. Make introductions.
  3. Arrange a meeting.
  4. Invite people to meetings.
  5. Endorse people’s products and services.
  6. Display their literature in your office.
  7. Distribute their information.
  8. Announce their events.
  9. Nominate people for awards.
  10. Follow-up on any referrals they give you.
  11. Serve as a sponsor.
  12. Publish information.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcription of BNI Podcast Episode 852/125

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from the beautiful Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I am joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan.

Ivan:
Hi, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
What do you have to share with us today?

Ivan:
Oh, I’ve got a great metaphor. I just love this. A few weeks ago, I received a message from a BNI director in the United States, and it read, “Ivan, I’m working on a chapter kick-off in South Dakota, and I had a salesman named Bill sit through the whole meeting, then not get up to leave when I ended it. And he sat there gazing into space.” He said, “Bill’s a 30-year veteran in the sales business.” And I asked him if I could help him, and he said, “You know, this whole networking thing will never work if I’m here for myself.”

Now, he just sat through, basically, a kick-off introduction, this is what it takes to kick off a successful BNI chapter. And he watches this meeting, and he tells the director, “This networking thing will never work if I’m here for myself. I need to take off my bib and put on my apron and learn to serve these people.”

I thought wow! Wow! If that’s not the concept of Givers Gain, an incredible summary of the concept of Givers Gain, I don’t know what is. Take off your bib and put on your apron. Stop coming to networking events looking for ways to feed yourself and look for ways to help others, to serve others.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
There’s really not much I add to that sentiment. I think truly effective networking is about taking off your bib and putting on your apron. It’s about helping other people succeed through the activities that go along with that process and you build your business and you prosper.

I just want to thank the director and the member. I think it’s an incredible metaphor to live by in BNI.

Priscilla:
I agree, and I was wondering, do you have any ideas on how to implement that?

Ivan:
Absolutely. I’ve written a little bit about ways that others can promote your business. And so let’s flip that on its head. Here are 12 simple ways that you can help other people in their business. I’m going to give you 12 things real quick.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
The first, of course, is provide referrals. BNI, that’s really important, is you’ll look for referrals for other people. But there’s more than that.

You can introduce people to prospects. Make introductions to other people, number 2.

Number 3, arrange a meeting, set up a meeting where you’re not just introducing two people via e-mail, which is one way, but you actually set a meeting to connect with these people.

4, invite people to attend meetings with you. That’s a great way. You go to a meeting with somebody else and you promote them. Say, “Here’s my friend. They’re really good at such-and-such.”

Endorse people’s products and services, do a testimonial letter. I do endorsements for books every month. I probably do two or three a month, and I keep doing them because it puts my name out there as an expert, and I think doing endorsements for other people’s products or services is a great way to put your name out there, but also to help put others’.

Display their literature and products in your offices or homes, particularly offices. If you have an opportunity to display other people’s material, I think that’s great.

Distribute their information. If you’re sending out a newsletter, include some information from somebody else.

Make an announcement. If you’re attending meetings or speaking groups and, let’s say, somebody’s got an event going on, promote them. Say, “I’ve got a good friend that’s doing an event locally.”

Here’s an unusual one. Number 9, nominate people for recognition and awards.

Priscilla:
That’s a great one.

Ivan:
Nominate them for recognition and awards. A lot of times there’s local organizations that recognize business people one way or another, and I think that’s a great way. And it could even be nominating for their community service. That’s a great one.

Here’s 10. Make sure to follow up with referrals that have been given to you. You want prospects to be really, really well taken care of. So when somebody gives you a referral, you do a better job for them than you do for anyone else. Believe it or not, that serves them, because when you give a referral, you give a little bit of your reputation away. When you give a good referral, it enhances your reputation. So if somebody decides to give you a referral, you better do a better job than normal; you better to the best you possibly can, because that makes them look good.

Serve as a sponsor, if you have an opportunity. Number 11, serve as a sponsor. If you can fund or sponsor a program for somebody else, I think that’s excellent.

And one more. Publish information. If you write, if you have a newsletter, if you have a blog, do any writing at all, those people that you’ve got a relationship with, make sure to recommend them. Look at BNI SuccessNet. We have a section on SuccessNet that says “BNI Recommends.” Here are products, books, services for people that have supported me, that have supported BNI, and we try to reciprocate and do that same for them through that section.

So there’s more. This is just some real simple ideas that you can apply, 12 simple ideas on how you can take off your bib and put on your apron and serve others more effectively in BNI.

Priscilla:
Those are great. I especially like the idea of giving people recommendations on testimonials. I think they’re so powerful in the meeting. You just can’t say enough about somebody, and they can’t say it about themselves, so I think that’s a great one.

Ivan:
Well, it’s very true, and you bring up a good point. It’s never as effective, you talking about yourself as having somebody else talk about you. It’s one of the reasons why, whenever I do a speaking engagement, I never open up by giving my background. Sometimes organizations say, “Talk a little bit about yourself so we know more about you.” Never, I never ever do that. I have a set bio and somebody else reads that bio, because if somebody else says, “He’s done this; he’s done that; he’s done this,” it’s much more impressive than you talking about yourself.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
So the idea of an endorsement is, I think, really valuable. You’re right.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Well, great. I think those are wonderful ideas.

Ivan:
I do, too, and I want thank, again, the director from South Dakota and the member, because I think it’s a wonderful metaphor that we can all use, take off your bib and put on your apron. Great concept.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you, Dr. Misner.

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla:
Well, I just want remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you’ll join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Networking will never work if you’re here for yourself. You need to take off your bib, put on your apron, and learn to serve other people.

title.rendered: Episode 851: The Benefits and Drawbacks to Co-Creation

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week, Dr. Misner shares the top 7 benefits and top 7 drawbacks and potential pitfalls of co-creation, based on a survey of 4200 respondents for The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success.

The Top 7 Benefits of Co-Creation

  1. The diversity of ideas.
  2. Learning from each other.
  3. Stronger relationships.
  4. Seeing areas of the business you don’t normally see.
  5. More empowered teams.
  6. Increased quality of creativity.
  7. Shared resources.

The Top 7 Drawbacks of Co-Creation

  1. Personality conflicts.
  2. Dealing with egos.
  3. Poor communication.
  4. People who don’t pull their weight.
  5. Lack of agreement on who makes the final decision.
  6. Individuals hijacking the direction of the project.
  7. Non-aligned vision for the project.

Complete Transcript of Episode 851

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I’m in Austin sort of this week, but I’m also doing a virtual book tour, which means I’m talking about my latest book, The 3rd Paradigm, in a virtual way. And I kind of came across it in a very… the idea, in a very interesting way.

A friend of mine, Kian Gohar, who’s a member of TLC [Transformational Leadership Council], is working on a project where he’s having [sic] brainstorming with AI, like a thought participant. And so he asked me if he could do that with me, and I said, “Yes.” And he asked me a few questions, and he said, “Well, is there anything that you’re working on that you’d like to talk to the AI about?” And so he’s got voice activation with it. So I said yes. And I said to the AI what “The 3rd Paradigm” was all about, the book, and how it worked. And I said, “Now, what can I do to help get the word out about this book that isn’t done, that isn’t very traditional?”

And the AI spoke back to me and said, “Well, Ivan, have you thought about doing a virtual book tour? A virtual book tour is where you can take sections of the book and talk about them, you know, every month and so you’re going in deep over a period of time, and you invite people to come.” And I’m like, “Oh, my goodness, No, I never thought of that.”

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
And so I’m doing a virtual book tour this week, because Chat GPT, as a thought participant, suggested it to me.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh, that’s great.

Ivan:
What a great way to use AI, I think.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh.

Ivan:
So, the benefits and drawbacks to co-creation, and co-creation, an example of it is crowdsourcing. Another example, BNI was way ahead of its time with co-creation, because I created the Board of Advisors back in 1986. And the Board of Advisors either approved or created every single member policy that exists today; the Board of Advisors did it. So we were way ahead of our time with co-creation. And it’s worked for me very effectively, and if you find this topic at all interesting pick up a copy of the book, there’s definitely going to be a link in the podcast.

We had wide distribution of gender who participated [in a survey], both men and women, reasonably equal, the spread on the age of the respondents was almost a bell curve, like the majority being 40s to 60. Less people under 39 and over 60s; it’s pretty close to a bell curve. But the part that I think is most important, and we spend the most time on in the book, are the top seven benefits of co-creation and the top seven drawbacks. And so that’s what I’m going to touch upon here today.

If you want to have a co-creative process in your business, these are the benefits. These are the reasons why you should do it. And I think I mentioned in the previous podcast, we surveyed over 4,000 it was 4,200 respondents, and we had almost 2,000 open-ended, actually 1,000 open-ended comments, with 1,900 people talking about leadership.

And so let’s talk about what are the top seven benefits, and then I’m going to jump into the drawbacks. The number one benefit for co-creation is the diversity of ideas. And you know, I’ve talked about diversity being important in your network for years because it connects you to other clusters of people. But here in this book it was the number one topic in the survey, because the diversity of ideas fills skill gaps for more well-rounded, for a more well-rounded solution to the challenge that you’ve got. Diversity of positions resulted in good input into the project. These are actually… I’m reading some exact quotes from the 1,000 comments that were made in the book. Another person said a diverse group of followers, leaders, thinkers, and workers help with a project.

The second highest was learning from each other. And people said things like, you know, the power of brainstorming, fresh ideas. There was one story in the book, an incredible story. He said at a large supermarket organization team came up with a simple idea of placing a rubber stopper on the racks, that would keep the merchandise pulled forward to give a look a full inventory, give an impression of full inventory. And it established a minimum number for inventory before reorder, and they were able to reduce the amount of inventory by 48%. It was millions of dollars that they saved with no negative effect on sales. That is a classic example of how co-creation works.

Stronger relationships was the third benefit; synergies are created. The concept of using teams to create and innovate resulted in significantly stronger buy-in from people. Fourth is seeing areas of the business that you don’t see. This is an important one. Team members come in with different areas of expertise. And if you want to have innovation, you got to let everyone speak up and you got to listen to all the different perspectives. You know, sometimes the field people don’t think that the tech people should be giving input, but you need the tech people to give input. And the tech people think the field people are too bossy. And so you know, there’s this natural challenge, but you’ve got to overcome that challenge and make sure everyone speaks. And by looking at the problem from all angles, you’re able to do that.

Ivan:
Five is more empowered teams, and a sense of ownership. You get alignment, vested interest. You get sales team and administration working together eventually.

Six is increased quality of creativity, which is a really important one. What is that old phrase – many hands make short work, or something like that. And that’s the case here, is that you’ve got a lot of people putting in ideas, you really get a lot of quality. People come up with different ideas for ways, really to reach the same objective.

And the last one is shared resources. Sharing knowledge with people with fewer resources is really valuable. Pooling various skills is valuable. Bringing additional resources to a project increases the client’s confidence when you have a lot of people working on it. So those are the top seven benefits of co-creation.

Let’s talk briefly about the top seven drawbacks because you gotta be prepared. If you want to be a great leader and be co-creative, you got to be prepared to deal with the drawbacks. And number one was personality conflicts. Number one thing: disputes based on personality conflicts, and they slow the co-creative process. Competition creates chaos in the process. And so, you know, leaders gotta learn how to manage conflicts that might be in the team.

The second one is dealing with egos. And that’s different than personality conflicts. Personality conflicts are two or three people who just don’t get along. Dealing with egos is all by yourself. You know, it’s somebody whose ego enters the room before they do. And, you know, they kind of take charge. And, you know, one person wrote, “Some people love to talk, and their favorite subject is themselves.”

Priscilla: [laughing]

Ivan:
That was a direct quote from one of the respondents. Number three is poor communication. A lot of talking, not a lot of listening. That’s a drawback. And so a really good leader, and I talked about the importance of leadership and co-creation last week, a really good leader makes sure that everyone is part of the communication process.

Number four is people who don’t pull their weight. People that have abdicated their responsibilities or dumped obligations onto other people. Anyone who has gone to college and has done group projects, you always have one student that doesn’t pull their weight. And that sometimes happens in co-creation. And so you need to make sure that everyone on that team is pulling their weight or you replace them.

Number five is lack of agreement on who makes the final decision. That’s important. Somebody’s got to make a final decision. Yeah, co-creation is about crowdsourcing. Wikipedia is a great example of crowdsourcing. But guess what? Somebody makes an opinion on Wikipedia. Someone takes stuff down or puts stuff up. So there is somebody who has the final yea or nay, but that somebody really should let people run the show. And they should get involved really towards the end in making final decisions.

Ivan:
Number six is individuals hijacking the direction of the project. You know, sometimes people have ulterior motives and they take the project in a wrong direction.

And number seven in the book is a non-aligned vision for a project where people think they’re doing something different than other people think. And so everybody’s got to be on the same page, in terms of where this project is going.

And data like this, data like I’ve just shared in this podcast, helps examine a subjective process in a more objective way. Because you get real life data on the things that work and the things that you want to look out for when you’re trying to do co-creation. Does that make sense, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Yeah, it does.

Ivan:
Yeah, and if you can get everyone rowing in the same direction, at the same time, with the same purpose, magic happens. And we see that, we certainly see that in BNI with everyone, in good chapters, everyone rowing in the same direction, taking the chapter, moving the chapter forward.

As a matter of fact, the end of [the book] Givers Gain, I say, “If all the people in an organization role in the same direction, that organization can dominate any industry, in any market, against any competition, at any time.” BNI dominated its industry in almost every market against all the competition for almost four decades. And that’s happened because of a shared vision, and a co-creation of that vision. That’s it for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That was great. Thank you, Ivan.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: This week, Dr. Misner shares the top 7 benefits and top 7 drawbacks and potential pitfalls of co-creation.

title.rendered: Episode 850: How Great Leaders Communicate Their Vision

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The key to being a great leader is to hold the vision and not the obstacles. Co-creative leaders are like conductors of the symphony. They maintain the big picture of the overriding objective while allowing everyone to add their own unique contributions. Here’s how you can become a great leader:

  • Identify team members’ strengths.
  • Create a safe environment for team members to speak up.
  • Get input and build consensus about goals.
  • Leverage contextual intelligence.
  • Adapt as circumstances change.
  • Identify potential challenges.
  • Ask your team good questions.
  • Relinquish control and coach others to be their best.
  • Set an example for the team to follow.
  • Resist procrastination and work through the mess.
  • Don’t be afraid to make mistakes.

Today’s topic comes from The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success. Get your copy on Amazon today!

Complete Transcript of Episode 850

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to the official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am back in Austin, Texas, talking to everyone from my home office. And this week, I’m doing a live Founder’s Education series online. And hopefully, members from around the world can participate.

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
So today, you know, I promised that I would be talking about parts of my latest book, The 3rd Paradigm, the subtitle is “A Radical Shift to Greater Success.” And there’s an important section in there about how great leaders communicate their vision. And so that’s what I’m gonna talk about today. It’s… the material is right out of the book. And I think, you know, we’re all leaders in some way; most of us have employees. But you know, you’re a leader; you can be a leader in your business, certainly with your clients, with customers. So, to expand your leadership capacity, it’s important to have buy-in and consensus and to move projects forward, despite challenges that might arise.

And the book is about co-creation and co-creation involves knowing how to collaborate in a way that gets the best out of your partners and yourself. And to get buy-in and consensus as a leader and to move projects forward despite the challenges that may arise. And I said in a previous podcast, you know, hold the vision, not the obstacles. And that’s an important theme in the book because there are always obstacles. There were obstacles when I started BNI, there were obstacles when we became international, there were obstacles during COVID, there’s obstacles today, there will be obstacles tomorrow. But the key to be a really great leader is to hold the vision and not the obstacles. That means that the leader is basically a vision champion.

So the real-world application of any concept is critical if you want to truly impact people and the people who work for you and the people who work with you. So it takes a special type of leader to execute on this idea of co-creation. And again, an example of co-creation, if you haven’t heard the previous podcast, crowdsourcing is a great example, think-tank work. And I talk about many different types of co-creation.

So leading a co-creative process has to begin really by displaying a quiet confidence in your ability and in everyone else’s ability. Co-creative leaders are somewhat like conductors of the symphony. Their role is to unify the performers, set the tempo, keep the orchestra playing in time, and in sync. And this is where they are the vision champions. They maintain the big picture of the overriding objective while allowing everyone to add their own unique contributions to the crowdsourced result.

You know, as the world becomes ever more complex and interconnected, the traditional top-down approach to leadership is really not effective. And we saw that in the first paradigm about production and competition, and started to see that relaxed with the second paradigm where it was about cooperation. But co-creation is more circular, I would say, than it is a pyramid.

To create lasting change, leaders really learn how to co-create with their teams by identifying team members’ strengths. Creating a safe environment — that’s important, where everyone feels heard and respected. Everyone’s got to have a chance to talk and leverage those abilities to achieve a collective success.

Now, there are a lot of ways leaders can hold the vision of a project or in a business. And it begins with leaders being really clear about what they want to achieve and why it’s important; getting the input on that, as well. You can’t just from the top down say, “This is what we’re going to achieve.” You’ve got to get buy-in. So they have to be open to input from all the team members and only then can the team reach a consensus and move forward together, as always, in alignment with an overarching vision.

You’ve got to also be able to leverage contextual intelligence. And I know I’ve talked about contextual intelligence before. I first learned that from Warren Bennis, who was in his day, the leading expert on leadership and I studied under him at USC. And it’s an intelligence in terms of understanding the context of the situation, and every context is a little different, people are different. And so you’ve got to learn how to work with them.

So, leaders have to be able to adapt their plans and strategies as circumstances change. I mean, we certainly did in BNI when COVID hit and we transitioned almost 10,000 chapters, pretty much overnight, to online. So leaders have to be able to identify potential challenges and saboteurs — people who might want to undermine the process, and they have to manage that effectively. You do that by asking questions; leaders can really get a lot of great value from co-creative teams simply by asking good questions. You know, when do you want to get this done? When do we want to make this decision? What is our ultimate goal for this project? What’s stopping us from achieving success? By routinely asking good questions, you have the effect of reinforcing consensus and ensuring a cohesive vision among stakeholders. 

I tell a story in the book about “Richard,” we’ve talked about who “Richard” is. And there’s an example where there’s an argument going on, disagreement in an agreeable way, but a disagreement and someone said to the IT person who was running that meeting, “You know, you’ve got to shut this down, they really disagree.” And the IT person said, “No, this is good. This is good. Let them talk. They’ll hash it out. They’ll come to a consensus.” And they did. And that’s great leadership.

Sometimes, you gotta lead from behind, but with guardrails. You’ve got guardrails; stay in bounds, but you lead from behind. Sometimes leading a co-creative process can seem like your team is running the plays, they’re running the field, and you’re just running behind them. And I actually had someone say this to me once, “It’s got to be like everybody is running down a field or running down a track and you’re running behind them, Ivan, and you’re moving your hands and say, “No, no, you’re out of bounds. You’re out of bounds, move in bounds. You, over here on the left, you’re out of bounds move in.” And it is like that a little bit. But when everyone is running, and you’ve got these guardrails up, they’re achieving success, and they’re moving you in a direction that is going to be way more successful than trying to do this by yourself.

So by coaching and cultivating your team, leaders have to relinquish control and focus instead on coaching others to perform their best. And that’s why it’s important to identify team members’ strengths, and the leader can then position them to contribute to the group. Communication, clear and effective communication is necessary amongst all the stakeholders. And I talked in the book about communication saturation, which when there’s a challenge, you just saturate the community with information so they know what’s going on. You know you’ve done a good job of that when people stop barking, stop complaining, and to some extent, stop coming to the meetings that you might have, because they know what’s going on. They’re good, then things calm down.

Above all, the leader has got to be an example to others. They have to be open minded, respectful, professional, non-judgmental, humble, transparent, appreciative, that’s a lot of things that a leader needs to do. But these are the things that make a great leader. More than that, their actions have to embody the project’s vision, the mutually desired outcome. Otherwise, they’ll drag the whole process down.

We did an open-ended survey for this book. We had four thousand people; four thousand people responded. And what was amazing is it contained 1,945 mentions of the word “leader.” So of 4,000 people, we had almost 2,000 talk about leadership being important in co-creation. And so obviously, leadership is an important part of the process.

I know we’re running out of time but let me give you just a few more things and we’ll wrap up.  Things to really look out for is procrastination; that co-creation is dynamic, it’s messy — it is both a muddy mess, and a thing of beauty, and it holds both spots simultaneously. So don’t succumb to the mess, work through it.

A lack of transparency can be a problem; co-creation has got to have a lot of transparency. Perfectionism can be a problem with co-creation. We quoted LinkedIn co-founder, Reed Hoffman, who said, “If you’re not embarrassed by the first version of your product, you’ve launched too late.” So it’s okay to have some mistakes.

And lack of humility, that’s a problem. And I’ve said this before on BNI podcast, humble people don’t think less of themselves, they just think of themselves less. And the lack of humility can be a real problem in the process of co-creation. Leaders not only hold the vision of the co-creative project, but they have to hold themselves accountable. And recognizing and dealing with these barriers is a good place to start. I highly recommend the book for anyone who wants to learn a little bit about co-creation and a large portion of the book is how to deal with conflict. I don’t think I’ve talked about that section of the book; I need to do that down the road. But how to deal with conflict is one of them. That’s it for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Oh, that was great. I think it was very enjoyable and great information. Well, that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Learn how you can become a great leader by holding the vision of your objective and coaching your team to success.

title.rendered: Episode 849: The Intangibles of BNI

content.rendered:
Synopsis

People join BNI for its tangible benefits and stay in BNI for both tangible and intangible benefits. This week, Dr. Ben Reebs from the Portland Business Advantage chapter joins the podcast to share 4 intangible benefits of being a BNI member.

  1. Strategic advice
  2. Financial leverage
  3. Cost savings
  4. Diverse cross-industry support

Intangibles have a substantial cash value, but it doesn’t necessarily show up in Thank You For Closed Business. Remember those intangibles at membership renewal time.

Many of these intangibles result from conducting regular one-to-ones with other members of your BNI chapter. Learn more about one-to-ones in Episode 570: More One-to-Ones Equals More Referrals.

Complete Transcript of Episode 849

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, I’m having fun this week in Napa Valley. It’s one of my happy places to go. I love the wineries there.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. Okay, that’s my part of the world. And who do you have with us today?

Ivan:
It is, yeah it’s a great part. So yeah, I have a guest today, Dr. Ben Reebs. He’s an award-winning Naturopathic Physician. He’s founder of the Portland Clinic of Natural Health, and the author of The Serpent and The Butterfly: The Seven Laws of Healing. He’s recognized as a leading world expert using integrative medicine to treat complex chronic disease. He’s been a member of BNI for seven years; he holds the Power of One, is a Gold Club member, and proudly holds the naturopathic doctor seat in the largest chapter that’s ever existed on the [U.S.] West Coast, Portland Business Advantage. He currently lives in Portland, Oregon with his wife, Maria, and 2-year-old son, River. Dr Reebs, Ben, it’s really nice to have you on BNI Podcast.

Ben:
Thank you so much for having me, Ivan. It’s an honor to be here.

Ivan:
Well, I love your topic; I think it’s great. It is about the intangibles of BNI. So, what do you mean by that?

Ben:
Yeah, so you know we join BNI for the tangibles, for the referrals. The Thank You for Closed Business, to grow our business, and we’ve all heard that our network is our net worth, and you know, we know that the whole is greater than the sum of its parts. And what I’ve found in being in BNI now for 7 years, is that we come for the tangibles but in my opinion, it’s the intangibles that are equally important, if not more important, and keep us around. And the benefits of the intangibles can really be experienced the larger a chapter is and the more diverse the business professional classifications are in the group.

Ivan:
Yeah, I agree. By the way, I didn’t say this after the bio, but I’m a big believer in natural medicine. So, it’s great to have you as part of the organization. Our surveys show what you’re describing is exactly the case. People join for the referrals, but they stay for the referrals AND the relationships. So both are really important. There are five different things that you wanted to talk about. One is strategic advice. Do you want to discuss that?

Ben:
Yeah, so strategic advice as a revenue enhancer. I think it was last year, I was meeting with a bankruptcy attorney in my group. And he’s done about 40,000 bankruptcies, more than any attorney in the state of Oregon. And he said, “You know, Ben, I think you qualify for the Employee Retention Credit, the ERC.” And I said, “No, my CPA told me I don’t qualify.” And he said, “Well, you’re a medical provider, and you had employees in 2020 and 2021. You might want to look into that.” And we’re just having a One-to-One [meeting]. And so I looked into it; it took me about 10 minutes to apply. And three months later, I got like $20,000 or more in checks from the IRS. So strategic advice really can make a difference in BNI.

Ivan:
That’s incredible. That’s an amazing story and very impressive. And you’re right, it’s not just the referrals. Sometimes the advice can increase your business in some way or another, or provide value like he did. All right, talk about financial leverage.

Ben:
Yeah. So a few years ago, I had a lease-to-buy agreement with my landlord. And it came time, I was ready to purchase my commercial property for my clinic and I needed financing. And so I went to the small business banker in my group, and he referred me to an SBA [Small Business Administration] specializing bank in San Diego. And they got me incredible terms with only 10% down, about 6% interest, and it was a half million-dollar loan. And I also qualified for the Cares Relief Act. And so I got my first three mortgages covered, and this is all just through a BNI referral that wasn’t technically for Thank You for Closed Business.

Ivan:
Yeah. Yeah, by the way, I had a similar story with one of my BNI offices and one of the very first ones, I got an SBA loan, and it allowed us to grow. Talk about cost savings.

Ben:
This one’s my favorite. It was probably four or five years ago, I was having a One-to-One with the computer repair tech in my group. And he said, “You know, Ben, I just made a house call and fixed a woman’s computer. She happens to be a medical biller, and she’s looking for business. Could I connect her with you?” And I said, “Well, you know, I just so happen to be looking for a medical biller, because I’m not very happy with the one I have right now.” So he connected me and I hired her. And immediately, all of the reimbursements I was getting went up about 20%. And she’s now been with me for about five years, and she’s literally made me hundreds of thousands of dollars. Just from having a One-to-One I was connected to her. So it was just incredible.

Ivan:
You know, you’ve mentioned One-to-Ones a couple of times, and we’ve talked about this in previous podcasts, look up the podcast number, or maybe we can put the link in here. [BNI Podcast Episode 570] There’s a direct link between people who do regular One-to-Ones and people who don’t, and [it’s an] example of a direct link for increased business and referrals. So I’m glad that you’re talking about that, because I think it’s really powerful. Diverse cross industry support — talk to me about that.

Ben:
Yeah. So this, you know, there’s a story here. Recently, it was in September, my wife launched a medi-spa. And we hired the event planner in our BNI group to do our grand opening. And she did an incredible job, and we had about 100 local business owners come through, and about half of them were from BNI and the other half were from the local chamber. And it was just an incredible group celebrating community, you know, that wouldn’t have been possible without BNI.

And then the following month, my wife had a Small Business Saturday, and we had several of the members in our BNI group, you know, featuring their products and services. And it just went off really well. And it was all just because of BNI. We couldn’t have done this without BNI and it just really illustrates this diverse cross-industry support that is possible in BNI.

Ivan:
Did you say it was done at her office or your office?

Ben:
Yes, it was done at her medi-spa, which has since merged with my clinic. So we’re now in the same office.

Ivan:
You know, that’s something I haven’t talked about much if at all on podcasts. I think it’s a great idea to do a chamber event at your office if it’s large enough, or a BNI event at your office if it’s large enough, because you not only get a chance to show people what you do, they see where you’re at. It’s a great connection. So that was a smart move. You had one story that you wanted to share, and we’re almost out of time, but you have a story that you wanted to share about the concept of the intangibles of being in BNI.

Ben:
I actually already shared it. It is the story I just shared about finding the medical biller. And you know, I just want to say that these intangibles add up to probably a million dollars, but they don’t necessarily show up in Thank You for Closed Business. But I think that BNI members really need to factor this in, you know, when they’re considering membership renewal, as well as new applicants.

Ivan:
Yeah, you’re absolutely right. And, you know, I’ve seen people who have said, well, you know, I’ve got these relationships, those relationships will continue if I leave, and then they leave. And I’ve seen people come back saying, “You know, those relationships didn’t continue.” And so in one case, the person’s position was filled. But when the person who filled it left, the original person came back and said, “Yeah, I’m not leaving now. There’s so much benefit besides referrals that I got out of this.” So, Ben, any closing thoughts that you have from your years of experience?

Ben:
I’m just wondering if you have an example of an intangible that you experienced?

Ivan:
Well, there have been so many intangibles, including, you know, meeting my late wife, through BNI. We were married 31 years. And meeting Jody was a referral that came about because of something I did in BNI. Jody works for Jack Canfield, who wrote “Chicken Soup for the Soul” and because of something I did for BNI, I was invited to join TLC, the Transformational Leadership Council and someone in TLC is a partner with Jack and referred Jody to me. So I’ve gotten married a couple of times, with BNI. (chuckling) That’s pretty good.

Ben:
Wow!

Ivan:
Priscilla, did we ever talk about the gentleman that tried to adopt the kids in Kazakhstan?

Priscilla:
No, I don’t ever remember that.

Ivan:
Okay, so I don’t have enough time to give the whole story. I’ll do it on a future one. But, a BNI member couldn’t stand up and speak in front of people, just could not do it. But he had to go to Kazakhstan to talk to the judge about expediting an adoption for two, I know I’ve been saying boys, but it turns out there are two twin girls. One had a heart condition. And he had to talk to a judge, and he said he was afraid to stand up and talk to his BNI members at first, but he stood in another country and talked to a judge and convinced him to release the children early and it saved their life. So doesn’t get a whole lot better than that. That was pretty impressive.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Well, Ben, your website is www.portlandclinicofnaturalhealth.com. That’s portlandclinicofnaturalhealth.com. I love seeing doctors in BNI; I particularly love seeing naturopathic doctors in BNI. So I wish you continued success in the organization and I thank you for being part of it.

Ben:
Thank you so much.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you so much. That was some wonderful information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
