title.rendered: Episode 848: Want Referrals? Don’t Bore Me to Tears (Classic)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Charlie Lawson, “unnatural networker” and BNI national director for the UK and Ireland, joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about storytelling, and tells a great story in the process.

What sells your services is not listing what you offer. To give your networking contacts the tools to find you referrals, you need two things:

  1. How does the client feel before you do the work? What’s the situation?
  2. How does the client feel after you do the work?

It’s not important to describe the process. Create an emotional connection between the person you’re talking about and the client. To get an engaging story, tell it from the client’s point of view. Facts tell, stories sell.

To read more of Dr. Misner’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 848/588

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio from Berkeley, CA. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. I have a good friend that I’m interviewing with today, Charlie Lawson. Charlie is the Co-National Director of BNI in the UK and Ireland. He is really proud to be a part of developing people, businesses to change lives and enrich communities. But what I think is really interesting is that despite running BNI in the United Kingdom and Ireland, Charlie is, by his own definition, an unnatural networker. He says that most experts talk about networking as though it is easy to talk to strangers, but Charlie is uncomfortable talking to strangers. He helps other unnatural networkers gain confidence by putting networking across the point of view of someone who would rather avoid it completely.

Charlie, it is really a pleasure to have you on BNI Podcast.

Charlie:
Thanks for having me on. I am definitely an unnatural networker, and if I can avoid talking to strangers and chat with my friends and mates instead, then that is much easier. But yeah, it is a nice angle. It is a elephant in the room when I do whisper it.

Ivan:
Yeah, and you know with BNI it’s all about relationships so it is a little bit easier. Your topic for today is “want referrals- don’t bore me to tears.” And I love that title, it is a great title. It’s really about storytelling. So, tell us about storytelling. Why is it so important? It is one of the pieces that we don’t talk enough about, so I’m really happy to have you on. Charlie, go ahead.

Charlie:
I urge everyone to look back at their last chapter meeting and think back on their weekly presentation, were they all captivating and interesting? Did they grab your attention? Some of them I’m sure, but I would imagine not all of them were. If you think back to any networking convention, BNI meeting or somewhere else, how were the conversations there? Were they all engaging? Did they all grab you? Or did you actually find yourself asking, “really do I have to carry on this conversation? Or will I have to be bored to tears?” I think that for me, being an unnatural networker, it’s hard enough for me to talk to strangers in the first place. If I think that I’m boring them to tears, I am very conscious that I want them to be engaged in the conversation. I want to be liked. If an introvert wants to make it, you have got to make it as easy a possible. So, I want to know that people are really listening, and I found that stories are a much better way to do that.

Since we are talking about storytelling, I will share a quick story about Dena. Dena was a BNI member in Yorkshire, in the north of England, and she got a call on a February afternoon at about 5 o’clock- if you have ever been to Yorkshire in February you know by 5 o’clock that it is cold, dark and it was also raining that day. So when Dena answered the phone, she could tell that there was a lady on the other end of the line who was obviously quite upset from the commotion. Dena could hear kids running around and making noise, there was a lot going on. And she said, “What’s going on? How can I help?” Dena ran a listing agency, so she was selling properties to get people put in accommodations- not buying properties but more of a short term, not just an overnight accommodation. What the lady was trying to do- she said, “I’ve got a real problem.” Turns out that she had been chucked out of the house by her partner. Her partner had literally said, “You gotta go to find somewhere else to live.” And left her with the kids. And she was calling Dena to see if there was anything she could do. She said, “I know this doesn’t usually happen at short notice. I know there’s usually contracts and other things of that sort all depending of the availability of the place, but I need somewhere to stay tonight.” Dena managed to source her a property. Not only that, she was able to, in the short term circumvent the process and say, “Hey, look, let’s just get you in the property and we will work out the details tomorrow-“ when it was possible to do it. Not only that, right as she was about to hang up the phone and say, “Look, here’s the details, just come to the property.” She said, “Hang on, where are you?” Turns out that lady had literally been chucked out by her ex partner and was standing out on the roadside with her kids in tow, with a couple of suitcases and boxes, and she had nowhere to go. She had no car. She had no way to get there. So Dena got in her car and said, “I am going to come and pick you up.” She picked them up and took them to the place where they managed to sort everything out. My question for anyone is, would you refer business to Dena?

And I can imagine the answer for most people would be, “Yeah, what an amazing service she offered. She was able to do something short notice- that she may not always be able to do, but she was kind, compassionate, caring and she wanted to help.” What Dena realized was that they were in a real grave situation there and she could make a difference for this lady.

Ivan:
Let me swoop in for just a second here, Charlie. That’s a great story and I want to break it down slightly. What Dena did was establish the setting- and I gotta be honest, what you did with Dena’s story was establish the setting, laid out the crisis and then you described the solution that she gave and how she was able to help and serve that potential customer. That is what makes a great story. Nobody in that room would be bored to tears! I can’t imagine anyone can be bored listening to this podcast. That is. A great example of a great story.

Please go on, Charlie.

Charlie:
When you hear weekly presentations, you hear networking conversations, and it’s often not like that though. It is often- take the listing agent again. A listing agent might be saying at the weekly presentations or networking conversations, “Well I can offer short term leases and last minute solutions. I’ve got a great range of services. I can offer inventory services. I can do this and that.” That doesn’t sell the services of the listing agent. What sells the services of the listing agent is the fact that we know we’d be comfortable referring her. And that is where storytelling happens. It’s not just for making it more engaging and interesting to hear, even though you enjoy what you hear more, it actually makes us do something with it. It becomes memorable. And when it comes memorable, it becomes the sort of thing we bring back up in a future conversation. And next time someone asks me about listing agents, who am I going to think about? I’m going to think about Dena because I have heard that powerful story.

And then I’ll start to break it down and start to think of what’s the difference there. If I am listing off my products and services, effectively that’s just not the information we need to make referrals. We are assuming here that we’ve got a good relationship. Once we’ve got a good relationship- you know that is a prerequisite for networking obviously, you have got to have a relationship there. But once we’ve got a relationship, we want to give our networking contacts the tools to find those referrals. And just listing our products and services doesn’t work. What we really need to figure out is how did the client feel before we did the work? What’s the situation they are in, and how are they feeling about life? You know, generally in that situation, there is going to be some level of emotion, so be careful here. Often when we think of emotion we think of sadness because that’s an obvious emotion, but there are all sorts of emotions; worry, anxious, anger, happiness- all sorts of different emotions. Let’s take Dena’s client for an example. How was she feeling? Well, she was feeling scared, very worried, she’s got kids running around. Imagine how she is feeling. That’s a horrible situation to be in. And what we have got to try to do is when we are telling our stories, is putting it across from the client’s point of view so their emotion comes through. That’s when you get the visceral connection to a story. Even though we may not have been in that situation ourselves and of course that is quite an extreme story, but it’s the visceral connection that makes us feel and be like, “That poor lady! What happened? How does the story end?” Then we don’t need to hear what Dena actually did. In terms of the due process, she actually did get the lady into a flat- but we do not really care about that. What we really care after that is, how did the person feel afterwards?

I’ll refer to my friend Andy Bounds. He’s spoken at BNI events in the UK, Ireland and other countries as well. He is a sales guy and messaging guy, and talks about the ‘after’. You know, you don’t buy a product for the product. You buy a product for what the product gives you. I’ll try to give an example: You don’t buy a pair of glasses for the glasses, you buy them for sight. The sight is the ‘after’ that we like. So, the ‘after’ in this case is how did the lady feel afterwards? What was the situation’s result? You can then feel comfortable to refer them.

When you are focusing on stories in your weekly presentation and your networking conversation, think about it from the client’s point of view, not your point of view. When you are thinking from your point of view, you are just sort of trying to say what you did. Imagine if I told Dena’s story like, “I received a call and I realized I had to get somewhere last minute, and I had to look up a list of accommodations to find this out…“ It’s just not that interesting. When it’s from a clients point of view, we get an engaging story. We don’t bore people to tears. Also, people start to do something with it.

Ivan:
Well, we are almost out of time, and I want to give you a moment to wrap up. Before I do, I just want to repeat for everyone who is listening, two key factors that Charlie just shared for everyone. Number one, in your networking meeting, how do they feel before you turned up? In your presentation you want to describe how people felt before you turned up. And then, you want to talk about how they feel after you did the work. Those two things are key and I am really happy that you are on this podcast to share with us, Charlie.

Any closing thoughts to leave us with before we wrap up?

Charlie:
Yeah, the moral of the story is that I think storytelling is more interesting, memorable, and crucially more referable. And that is why we go to networking events and BNI because we want to build our business through referrals. We’ve got to give our fellow members the tools to find those referrals, and I think storytelling is a great way to do it. Plus, it won’t bore people to tears when it comes to your networking.

Ivan:
I love it, Charlie. Everyone who is listening to this, you have to share this with members of your chapter. Education Coordinators, this is a great educational slot. Share this content with your BNI chapters. Remember, facts tell, stories sell. And stories are what give that visceral feel that you were talking about, Charlie.

Thanks so much for being on the BNI podcast. It was a pleasure having you on.

Charlie:
It was my pleasure.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well thank you both very much. I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: To give your networking contacts the tools to find you referrals, tell the story from the client’s point of view. Facts tell; stories sell.

title.rendered: Episode 847: The Four Knows to Success

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Before you begin collaborating with outside companies and individuals, you need to have a solid understanding of your own operation. In The 3rd Paradigm, Dr. Misner and his co-authors call that understanding The Four Knows:

  1. Know the right focus; know what to focus on.
  2. Know the right process; know what process to use.
  3. Know the right communication.
  4. Know the right execution.

Everyone needs to be focused on a mutually desired outcome, a shared WHY. The better communication you have with the people who work with you, the more successful you’re going to be in the process and in your company. Saturate the team with transparent communication methodologies in order to ensure that everyone knows what is going on with the project. And above all, hold the vision, not the obstacles.

More from The 3rd Paradigm

  • Get your copy of The 3rd Paradigm on Amazon.
  • Listen to Episode 843: The New Force Driving Business Success.
  • Listen to Episode 845: Boost Creativity, Community, and Sales.

Complete Transcript of Episode 847

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m in Austin today, but I’m headed to Southern California. So I’ll be on the road again this week.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. All right. So what do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
I’m going to talk a little bit more about some of the concepts out of “The 3rd Paradigm” book. And this topic is called the Four Knows to Success. And that’s “knows,” K-N-O-W-S. So there are Four Knows, or pillars, that form a solid foundation for business success as it relates to co-creation.

Co-creation, as I’ve talked about in a couple of other podcasts, is a process in which several parties come together to produce goods or outcomes that serve a common goal. And in “The 3rd Paradigm,” Dr. Heidi Giusto, Dawa Phillips, and I outline five types of co-creation that can result in success for businesses. And I discussed them in a previous episode; I’ll mention them again later. But before you begin collaborating with outside companies and individuals, we stress that it’s really important for you to have a solid understanding of your own operation.

And we call these pillars The Four Knows. I’m going to explain why each is so critical to success. No matter which of the types of co-creation that you use, which we talked about a couple of episodes ago, you’ll need to have all four of the following pillars in place in order to succeed in co-creation, and in building a successful business. These are The Four Knows:

  1. You got to know the right focus; know what to focus on.
  2. Know the right process; know what process to use.
  3. Know the right communication. And
  4. Know the right execution.

Let’s start with the right focus. Here, it’s about establishing a mutually desired outcome within the organization, in your operation. Here, everyone involved in the co-creative process needs to be rowing in unison and in the same direction. And this means that everyone needs to be focused on a mutually desired outcome. Disaster absolutely strikes when people fail to work together. And we conducted that survey of 4200 business professionals, and it was really funny — one respondent shared how ineffective teamwork actually derailed their leader’s attempt to market an event. And they said, and I’m quoting them here, “We had not been able to communicate the WHY to them or get their views to make the event a part of the entire team.” Which is ironic because one of our previous podcasts was about understanding your WHY, and that’s an important piece of understanding the right focus.

So focusing on mutually desired outcomes requires that all the team members have a shared WHY. Co-creation leaders can guide their team through a series of questions that will help them work together efficiently and effectively and creatively even if they’re composed of diverse stakeholders. We certainly see that in BNI. You know, our WHY in BNI is to build relationships with one another and to generate referrals for each other. So this process will help identify the right focus. The outcome can’t be vaguely defined; it has to be clear. BNI is clear: We help people create referrals for life; we are a referral organization.

And that’s what you have to do in any business. And when it is, the co-creation stakeholders will have the necessary shared purpose and intention. Then once the mutually desired outcome is established, teams can accomplish really unbelievable things. One survey respondent shared this sentiment, quoting them, “If the right people, with the same goals, lifting, encouraging, and working together with the same commitment to achieve TOGETHER are in place, wonders can happen.” And gosh, if we don’t see that in BNI, I don’t know where you see that. You get the right people with the right goals lifting together and encouraging each, it’s miraculous, what you see happen in really successful BNI chapters.

The next is the right process. And here we’re talking about implementing a framework. So you have to identify the right process for a co-creative project to ensure that the stakeholders can work efficiently and effectively. It’s not enough to have a team that’s committed to a mutually desired outcome, without an underlying process guiding the mission. And if you don’t do that, frustrations mount.

BNI has a process; we follow that process. And it’s that process that helps us become successful and helps us generate millions and millions of referrals, billions of US dollars in business, by having a process and following it. So the process of choosing the framework begins by selecting the five types of co-creation that I previously introduced in a previous podcast. And those five are

  1. Think Tank/Brainstorm – that’s one;
  2. Crowdsourcing,
  3. Open Sourcing,
  4. Mass Customization,
  5. and User-Generated Content.

In BNI, we’ve substantially used the Think Tank/Brainstorming, and we’ve used Crowdsourcing in the organization.

Now, once the co-creation leaders determine the right type, then additional pragmatic considerations are important to understand the overarching framework of the process. So these include determining clear roles and responsibilities for team members. When people don’t have clear roles, they end up going down rabbit holes instead of focusing on the things that need to be done. And there also has to be accountability in place; we certainly see that in BNI. BNI only works when it’s a system of accountability that is in place. And I have said for many years, you know, you apply the rules more like Mandela than Atilla, but you’ve got to apply the rules. There has to be accountability.

Some of the survey respondents in the survey we did attested to the importance of having the right process. One person said that all the persons working on a project should be specifically responsible and accountable to some extent, and all the persons should be required to provide input. Again, very applicable within a BNI context. Others remarked on the importance of having a good framework for growth in place; again, something very important within BNI. And the need to be clear on the set tasks. That’s one of the reasons why we have a set agenda that we operate on every week. Other aspects are deadlines, and people being responsible to either follow up with them or to track the process.

The fourth is the right communication. The right communication is really important. And it’s important to ensure open communication. Open communication is crucial to a successful co-creation process. And this has to span employees, customers, vendors, and all of the company’s stakeholders. Without clear communication, co-creation will absolutely fail even if everyone involved is passionate and fully engaged in the project. Many survey respondents underscored the importance of having the right communication. Communication is vital. I can’t say this enough; the better communication you have with the people who work with you, the more successful you’re going to be in the process and in your company.

In companies where ideas are freely shared, a tremendous amount of growth happens. I’ve been in companies where people in charge didn’t want to hear any ideas that weren’t their own, so no growth happened there. The larger the organization, the more there is, sometimes, the lack of communication about strategy and goals, which often results in no direction. There is also a lack of communication about values. It’s one of the things that we’ve really done well in BNI is focus on the Core Values and repeat the Core Values starting with Givers Gain. And virtually everyone in every [BNI] country understands that.

One of the more memorable respondents in the survey said, “Everyone has to be singing off the same sheet of music!” Indeed, a chorus full of enthusiastic singers each giving it their all will have a miserable time if everyone isn’t performing the same song. So you’ve got to be performing off the same sheet of music.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
And this means you need to have a strategy of what I call, and we call it in the book, communication saturation. It’s an important concept — communication saturation. You literally saturate the team with transparent communication methodologies in order to ensure that everyone knows, or at least can know, what is going on with the project.

The last step is the right execution. You have to ensure knowledgeable leadership. Even with everything else in place – the right focus, the right process, the right communication – it will all be for naught if an organization lacks the right execution. So leaders of co-creation have to lay the groundwork for successful empowerment and execution within the team. With this final KNOW, leaders have to have a clear plan and capably execute it while empowering stakeholders. The entire process is at stake if the co-creative initiative falls short on execution. One survey respondent in the book quipped… (I love this.) “Just a joke from my university days: When I die, I’d like the people I did the group projects with to lower me into the grave, so they can let me down one more time.”

Pricilla: (chuckling)
Wow.

Ivan:
That’s awful and hysterical at the same time. But with the right execution, stakeholders’ morale, it soars; without it, disappointment abounds. And one of the most important concepts in the book is that you have to constantly Hold the vision, not the obstacles. You have to hold the vision, not the obstacles. There will be obstacles. But if you have the right focus, the right process, the right communication, and the right execution – if you have those critical pillars – you’re going to create a successful co-creative process. And I invite people to check out the book, “The 3rd Paradigm.” There’ll be a link here in the podcast for you to go and check it out personally. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. It’s a great follow up to your podcasts that we’ve released – number 845, I believe. Is that right?

Ivan:
Yeah, and I think the one before that, it might have been 843.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh.

Ivan:
I’m gonna have a couple more. I’m going to have at least two more where I talk about [it]. And I know it’s a little more academic than some of my stuff, but this can really make a big difference for people who implement it within their organization.

Priscilla:
It’s a great opportunity to find out what you’re like as a professor.

Ivan:
There you go. That’s true.

Priscilla: (chuckling) 
That’s true. Okay, great. Well, is that it for this week?

Ivan:
That’s it for this week.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thanks so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Before you begin collaborating with outside companies and individuals, you need a solid understanding of the Four Knows of your own operation.

title.rendered: Episode 846: Cracking the WHY Code

content.rendered:
Synopsis

When you talk about why you do what you do, you stand out from everyone else that does what you do. But in order to do that, you need to know what your WHY is. Dr. Gary Sanchez joins Dr. Misner this week to introduce the 9 WHYs. Everyone has all nine WHYs, but one is dominant in each of us.

The 9 WHYs

  1. Contribute
  2. Trust
  3. Make Sense
  4. Better Way
  5. Right Way
  6. Challenge
  7. Mastery
  8. Clarify
  9. Simplify

Resources

  • Watch Dr. Misner’s interview on the Beyond Your WHY Podcast.
  • Visit the WHY Institute to find your WHY operating system (WHY.os).

Complete Transcript of Episode 846

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m physically in Austin, Texas this week, my home. But I’m all over the world because this is International Networking Week® in 2024. So I’m doing events all over the world.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. And who do you have as a guest today?

Ivan:
I have a guest; I was on his podcast, and I’ve invited him to be on the BNI Podcast. His name is Dr. Gary Sanchez. He’s an international speaker, author, dentist, and inventor. He is known as “Dr. WHY”, uppercase W-H-Y, for discovering the 9 WHYs and developing the WHY Discovery Tool. He’s the author of the book, “The Power of the 9 WHYs: How People and Teams Get Clear, Stand Out and Play Bigger.” He earned his degree in biology from the University of Colorado in Boulder and his D.D.S., his dental degree, from University of Southern California. We have something in common, Dr. Sanchez. Your favorite saying is “There’s always a better way!” Welcome to the BNI Podcast, Gary.

Gary:
Thank you, Ivan. I appreciate it. I’m excited to be here.

Ivan:
Well, it’s good to have you here. And let’s jump right in. Talk to me about cracking the WHY code. What does that mean? And how would my BNI members benefit from understanding this?

Gary:
Great question. Well, so many of today’s gurus, well forever actually, people have been talking about this concept of Why. Why do I do what I do? Why am I here? You’ve heard it from Mark Twain that said, “The two most important days in your life are the day you were born and the day you find out why.” Most recently, Simon Sinek has talked quite a bit about it. But actually helping you discover your WHY is when it has value for you. And that’s kind of my story.

I was a dentist, like you mentioned, from USC. And the advice that I was given was – build a great product and people will come. So I spent 20 years building a great dental practice, only to still blend in with everybody else that said, “Yeah, I’m a dentist, too.” And that’s when I heard about the concept of WHY; saw Simon Sinek’s TED Talk like 61 million other people, read his book multiple times and said, “That’s what I’m missing.” I have a great ‘what,’ but I don’t know my WHY. So I became obsessed with that; called Simon. He and I spent about eight months together going back through my life looking for clues, trying to figure it out. And when I finally figured out that my WHY is to find a better way, and then share it, that’s when my life started to make a lot of sense to me.

And so in my own dental practice, we stopped talking about what we do – crowns, bridges, fillings – and we started talking about why we do what we do and what we believe. And that’s when my practice really took off. You know, when you talk about what you do you blend in with everybody else who does what you do. When you talk about why you do what you do, you stand out from everyone else that does what you do. But in order to do that, you need to know what your WHY is. And since my WHY is to find a better way and share it, I went back and figured out what Simon was trying to do and I made it better. And I started to help anybody that would let me, and I helped thousands of people. And in doing so, I started to see patterns and trends and similarities and figured out there’s only nine different WHYs.

Ivan:
Yeah, let’s talk about that. The Power of 9 WHYs; you did a book on that. So talk to us about the 9 WHYs, or some of them at least.

Gary:
Yeah. So, like in your case, your WHY is to contribute to a greater cause, right? Add value, have an impact in the lives of others. So you use your time, your money, your energy, your connections to push other people forward.

Ivan:
Can I just add to that? You know, I did a podcast with you and I’ll make sure that that link goes up for anybody who wants to see my WHY on Gary’s podcast.

Gary:
Yeah. And so one of the great things about that is once I figured out there was nine WHYs, I figured out that one of them is your WHY, one of them is your How, and one of them is your What. And so your message becomes even clearer when you know all three. So, can I use you as the example?

Ivan:
Of course you can.

Gary:
Okay, so if all you saw was Ivan’s picture, and you tried to figure out now who is this guy? And you would look at his hairstyle and his facial hair, and if he had any jewelry and what he’s wearing and his posture and glasses and things, and you try to figure out who he is. So imagine you’re at a networking event and you see him. You go, “Oh, let’s go talk to him,” and you try to figure out who he is. But you don’t really know. So you create a narrative around what you think and then that’s who he becomes.

But if I were to tell you that Ivan’s WHY is to contribute to a greater cause, like we mentioned; have an impact in the lives of others. HOW he does that is by simplifying things, making them easier to do. And ultimately, WHAT he brings is the right way to get results – the structure, the processes, the systems around getting predictable results. Now, you know so much more about him.

And we could dive into that if you want on the best way to connect with you, or somebody that has your WHY, How and What. We call that your WHY operating system, or WHY.os. And so that’s the next evolution of WHY. So we cracked the WHY code, we figured out that there’s only nine why’s but then we took it even deeper by creating your WHY operating system, your WHY.os, which is your why, your how, your what – and then developing your message around that. Does that make sense?

Ivan: 
It does. But give me some more of the nine. There’s several there that you gave me, but what are some more than nine?

Gary:
Okay, so the first one is Contribute – to contribute to a greater cause, add value, have an impact in the lives of others.

The second one is Trust – to create relationships based upon trust, to be the trusted source.

The third one is Make Sense – to make sense of the complex and challenging; they’re great problem solvers.

The fourth one is my WHY, which is to find a better way and share it.

The fifth one is Right Way – to do things the right way in order to get predictable results.

The sixth WHY is Challenge – to challenge the status quo and think differently.

The seventh WHY is Mastery – to seek mastery and understanding.

The eighth WHY is Clarify – to make things clear, crystal clear, and understandable.

And the last one is Simplify – to decrease complexity and make easier to do, easier to understand.

So those are the nine WHYs in a nutshell.

Ivan:
Everybody has at least one of those themes as their WHY. Is that what you’re saying?

Gary:
Everybody has all of them, but one of them is more dominant than the rest. And that’s the one that becomes your WHY.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Gary:
The second most important one to you is your HOW. And the third most is your WHAT. But the WHY.os. So what we haven’t talked about yet, Ivan, is that after doing this with many, many thousands of people, I got a lot of data. And with that data, I was able to develop the algorithm and write the software, where you can now discover your WHY.os in about eight minutes online, which is what you went through. But it actually goes in all nine levels deep. So it gives you a quite a bit of information on yourself.

Ivan:
And so that’s the WHY.os that you were talking about.

Gary:
Yeah, the WHY operating system, that’s the basis of your messaging. So if the question becomes why should I choose you over everybody else who does what you do? It all goes back to your WHY, your How and your What. And that’s why it’s so critical for you to know it. You know, once you know your WHY.os, then you’ll see clearly are you on the right track? Are you living your passion?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Gary:
Are you going in the right direction? And then you can build your messaging, marketing, and branding, all based on your WHY.os, so that you stand out from everybody else who does what you do.

Ivan:
So how do you use the WHY.os to network? Because as you know, BNI is a networking organization so that’s an important factor for my members.

Gary:
Great question. So first of all, it’s critical for you to know in terms of messaging yourself, like we just talked about, so you know who you are, and you’re able to message it. And it’ll answer the question, why should somebody choose you? But the second thing is when you know the WHY.os of your prospects, or your clients or the people that the other members in your BNI group, you know how to connect with them.

So, Tony Alessandra talks about the Platinum Rule, right? We all know the Golden Rule – treat others the way we want to be treated. But what if they don’t want to be treated the way you want to be treated? So then you use the Platinum Rule, which is treat others the way they want to be treated. Well, how you do that is by knowing their WHY operating system. So you’ll know the words that they need to hear in order to make a decision. You’ll know how to connect with them on a much deeper level so that you get to speak to them in the way they want to be spoken to. And it helps you move people forward.

The other thing that it does, Ivan, is it allows other people to know how to talk about you. So what’s great about referring other people is when you know what to say about them. Instead of, “Oh, yeah, they’re a great guy. They do a great job with their business; they’re really good at what they do.” Now, you’ll be able to talk about somebody in a way that’s authentic to who they are, and why they stand out from the others that do what they do.

So it’s a perfect tool to utilize when networking. It’s also a perfect tool to use when prospecting because it only takes eight minutes. So you can have your clients… you know, I don’t really meet with anybody until I know their WHY.os because what are we going to talk about? Right? How are your kids or things like that. Once you know their WHY.os, you’ll know exactly how to connect with them in a way that they want to be connected to.

Ivan:
It improves your personal messaging and allows connections to take place faster, yeah?

Gary:
You got it. Better connections faster.

Ivan:
Well, Gary, first of all, thanks for having me on your podcast. It was a pleasure having you on the BNI Podcast. How can people get more information about what you do?

Gary: Go to www.WHYinstitute.com. If you want to discover your WHY.os, just go to WHYinstitute.com. W-H-Y institute.com, and then click on the tab that says “Discover My WHY.os,” and it will take about eight minutes, but it’ll find your WHY, your How, your What, and create your personal message for you. I think you’ll find it fascinating. It really helps you answer the question: Why should I choose you?

Ivan:
Well, I found it fascinating, and I appreciate you being on BNI Podcast today, Gary. Thank you so much.

Gary:
Thank you, Ivan. I appreciate it.

Ivan:
All right, back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thanks so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
