excerpt.rendered: When you talk about why you do what you do, you stand out from everyone else that does what you do. Discover your 9 WHYs with Gary Sanchez.

title.rendered: Episode 845: Boost Creativity, Community, and Sales

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dr. Misner introduces the five types of co-creation discussed in The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success and explains what each of them brings to the table for like-minded entrepreneurs and business owners:

  • Brainstorming (“Think Tank”): Bringing people together to create a solution
  • Crowdsourcing: Creating together using web-based tools
  • Open Source: Inviting people to contribute to your data pool or software project
  • Mass Customization: Personalized products for everyone
  • User-Generated Content: Using knowledge or content others have made public online

Does your company make use of co-creations? What type of co-creation do you think would work best for your business? Share your answers in the comments.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 843: The New Force Driving Business Success.
  • Get your copy of The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success

Complete Transcript of Episode 845

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I just got back from North Carolina; I was at the BNI Global Headquarters. And it’s always good to go there and see all the employees and some of the leadership team people.

Priscilla:
I bet it’s really fun. So what do you have for us today?

Ivan:
Well, today I want to talk a little bit more about co-creation because I believe co-creation can boost creativity, community, and sales for any business. The question, of course, is what is co-creation? In Episode 843, I talked about the Three Paradigms. So go back and check out episode 843. I talked about competition, cooperation, and co-creation.

So over the next several weeks, I want to talk a little bit about co-creation a little further. I think it’s a very powerful tool for businesses. There’s really no aspect of your business that can’t be improved with the help of co-creation. And in our new book, “The 3rd Paradigm,” the subtitle is “A Radical Shift to Greater Success,” Dr. Heidi Giusto, Dawa Phillips and I outline the concept of co-creation in business. And co-creation is a big step, further than mere cooperation. It’s about bringing different parties together to actually produce, improve, or customize a product or service based on a mutually desired outcome.

So in this episode, I want to talk about the five types of co-creation, and what each of them brings to the table for like-minded entrepreneurs and business owners, that will involve a radical shift to greater success. Part of what makes co-creation so attractive is that it’s always a joint process and a group of people working together on a solution. And the involvement of other stakeholders in that solution really determines the form of co-creation you can, or that you want to, engage in. And like all frameworks, co-creation comes with its own set of limitations and challenges that you need to be aware of.

The drawbacks mentioned most often in our survey, and we did a survey of over 4,000 businesspeople, are personality conflicts and dealing with egos. Does that sound like a couple of issues that we see in BNI from time to time, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Yeah, absolutely.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s not unusual in they’re not unusual problems, particularly when there are multiple cooks in the kitchen. In the book, we highlight five types or frameworks of co-creation, that can be likened to five different paths, which can all lead to the destination you want. Now, which road you choose depends on which path you consider is most suitable for your journey. So, some types of co-creation are more popular and more familiar to the average person than others. And many types of co-creation happen in plain sight. But unless you’re really trained to perceive co-creation in action, it might look similar to some traditional business model.

So here are the five types of co-creation that we talked about in the book, “The 3rd Paradigm.” The first is think tank/brainstorming. And that’s where a group or a company brings together a consortium of people, experts, suppliers and/or partners to develop a new solution, a product or some service. In some instances, this even results in customers handling part of the production, like flat pack furniture that customers transport and assemble themselves. Self-scanning systems in supermarkets, self-serve ticket counters at airports… BNI really started this back in the mid-80s, with others, with our Board of Advisors creating all the policies that members live by today. [They] were all created by members. So members created policies in BNI.

The second is one that most people are probably pretty familiar with, and that’s crowdsourcing. And that’s where a large group of people often volunteers co-create, often for free, a product or a service by using web-based creative tools like Wikipedia, Kickstarter, those kinds of things. And then this type of co-creation can lead to an increased quality of creativity, which was also one of the benefits that was mentioned in the survey of 4,000 people that we did – increase quality of creativity.

The third is open source. And that’s where a group or company invites a large group of internal and external experts to tackle its innovation and the challenges related to that, or contribute to the data pool, like the Center for Open Science and ResearchGate, those are good examples. These can lead to what a lot of participants in our survey described as a shared sense of ownership and shared resources. And I don’t want you to underestimate those key factors, that those are really important in the development of co-creation within a company. And co-creation can happen, it doesn’t have to be a big company, it can be a small company. I mean, BNI, we started with this idea of co-creation, when I literally just had three, four or five employees at the most, and I’m talking about independent contractors. I probably had two employees, two part-time employees and three independent contractors when we started with this concept of co-creation.

The fourth is mass customization. That’s where a group or a company mass produces products that have been individually tailored to a customer’s wishes, like a T-shirt printed with your own photo, personalized Vans shoes, or customized luggage. Many participants in our survey, the 4,000 entrepreneurs, mentioned the diversity of ideas as a major benefit of co-creation.

And the fifth is user-generated content, user-generated content. And that’s where a group or a company uses knowledge and content that’s made public by people like something posted online. And there are all kinds of web tools to help you find very quickly information that others have posted online – customer feedback on blogs, forums, YouTube videos, social media platforms – these are all user-generated content. It’s another great example of gaining access to shared resources, which many in our survey listed as one of the advantages of co-creation.

Certainly, BNI has used user-generated content a great deal with members doing videos about their experience in BNI, and talking about how great their BNI chapter is. That’s a form of co-creation. So you can group these five types of co-creation into two different buckets to help you wrap your head around it. And those two buckets are kind of according to what role the group or company plays in the process and it’s about who steers the process. Is it the individual consumer steering the process? Or is it the company steering the process?

For example, it was the company steering the process with BNI’s Board of Advisors; I wanted members to create member policies. On the other hand, it’s the members who steer the process when it comes to user-generated content, like YouTube videos and whatnot, about BNI. So, co-creation can happen in those two big buckets, but there are five different ways that it can happen. So according to the kind of value that’s created this is whether your value is a standardized value that all customers can enjoy, co-creating a better product or a service, or a personalized value that’s individually tailored to each customer, co-creating a personalized product or service.

So co-creation, following the five types that I just mentioned, is about making something better or making something unique. If unique is the value proposition, even average products can find market success because they tap into the value of co-creation. And if it is making something better, even average products can still find market success by being improved with the help of customers and consumers. And this is really the power of co-creation. That’s why co-creation is continuing to rise in popularity among new and established businesses alike.

So the question, if you’re listening to this podcast, that you should be asking is what type of co-creation would work for you and your company? What would work best? Co-creation can absolutely unlock additional value in your value creation chain and make that value available for you and other people’s success. Wouldn’t it be really great to optimize the value that you provide? Because you trust and engage with others rather than keeping your value generation tied to an outdated business model like competition or even cooperation, the First and Second paradigms. And I think it’d be really meaningful to elevate your relationships and engage the talents, the knowledge, and skills of those who care most about what you do, your customers, your fellow BNI members, as well as consumers and stakeholders of your products or services.

So there’s no aspect of your business that can’t be touched and improved upon with the help of co-creation. Every part of every business can benefit from unlocking and harnessing the power of this concept to identify the value yet to be discovered within the organization and unleashed and scaled. Every business could achieve, I think, greater success by embracing the co-creation process and expanding the trust and investment that is generated with customers, consumers, and stakeholders alike.

So this concept is a little more academic, slightly more esoteric than some of my other topics, Priscilla. It’s, I think, a really powerful one that is going to be the wave of the future over time. And it’s one of the few concepts that really did start within the university system and is kind of emerging into the business world. Whereas most often, it’s the business world that finds this stuff and then starts [and then] the universities pay attention; it kind of happened in the opposite direction.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
That’s my message for today. And I do plan on having a couple of more topics on co-creation. I’ll make sure there’s a link put into this podcast below that will allow you to go to the book, “The 3rd Paradigm,” where you can read a whole lot more about the concept.

Priscilla:
Great.
Ivan:
That’s it for today.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, thank you. I think that’s really interesting, and I’m sure that the listeners are going to enjoy listening to that.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Discover the five types of co-creation and what each of them brings to the table for like-minded entrepreneurs and business owners.

title.rendered: Episode 844: Is Your Chapter on Fire, on Hold, or in a Hole? (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 480, first published on October 26, 2016. Links and offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

As we discussed in Episode 409, chapters go through cycles. Here’s an easy way to recognize and categorize chapter cycles.

On Fire

These chapters are energized, goal-oriented, and thriving. They’re generating a lot of referrals with a high value. They’re the biggest groups–or on their way to being it. Members of the leadership team attend all the required trainings, and more. BNI members in these chapters have completed their BNI Connect profiles and pass referrals every week. They conduct effective one-to-ones. And they have fun together.

On Hold

These chapters are still generating referrals and revenue, but they’re just going through the motions. They do the minimum and get average results. Sometimes these groups have been on fire in the past, but became complacent and started to slide. They’ve often given up on growth, and don’t follow all the suggestions of their regional directors and the BNI policies.

In a Hole

These chapters are struggling with attendance. They’re not holding members accountable. They have difficulty retaining members. These chapters focus on problems rather than solutions. They don’t follow the agenda and policies of BNI very well, and they often regard their regional director as an outsider rather than a resource.

Where does your group stand right now? If your group is on fire, congratulations. That’s how BNI is supposed to be. If your group is on hold, it’s not that hard to transition to being on fire. Even if you’re in a hole, you can break out of that, as long as the leadership team and the membership are committed to change.

The key to improvement is recognizing where you are. Why settle for mediocrity when excellence is an option?

Use the worksheet below to rate your chapter. Is your chapter is on hold or in a hole, contact your director for help.

Rate Your ChapterDownload

If you’ve moved your chapter from “In a Hole” to “On Fire,” share what you did in the comments.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 844/480

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
This week, I am in Houston and in Boston. You didn’t know that they were real close to each other, did you?

Priscilla:
No, I did not know that.

Ivan:
I am doing BNI things for both Houston and Boston. I’ll be traveling a little bit. I love the topic today.

Priscilla:
Olay. Help us with this one.

Ivan:
If your chapter has ever gone through a cycle and if your chapter has been around for a couple years, it has probably gone through the cycle. This is all about a life chapter cycle, so I would recommend you go to BNI podcast and do a search on chapter cycles. You will find some additional information on chapter cycles here. Plus, I just came up with these three phrases recently, and I think they perfectly identify where chapters are within the cycle process and it is so easy to remember and so easy to understand.

You walk into some chapters and they are on fire. They are energized, goal-oriented. They are thriving, and most importantly, they are referring high dollar value or high revenue value in thank you for closed business values, while others are basically on hold. They are just kind of going through the motions, doing the minimum, getting average results. Then there those others who are in a hole. They are struggling with attendance. They are not holding others accountable. They are not excited, and they are having difficulty maintaining a strong membership.

So if you are listening to this, what I would like for you to do is to stop for a moment and think for yourself. Where is your group right now relating to these three stages? Be honest. Where do you think your group stands?

If you think your group is on fire, then let me congratulate you. That was my vision for what a great BNI experience should be about.

If your group is on hold, trust me when I say that it doesn’t take a lot to move from this stage to on fire. I have seen many groups do that in a short period of time.

If your group is in a hole, don’t despair. It is going to be an incredible group. Believe me. I have seen it. The exciting thing is it is actually possible to leapfrog over the middle, from being in a hold to leapfrog to being on fire fairly quickly with the right membership team and full commitment from your members.

Just know that all groups go through this cycle. It happens; it’s okay. They key is to recognize the cycle and know when it is time for you to move out of one cycle and go to another.

Why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option? Excellence is an option in all BNI chapters all around the world. So let’s look at the types of groups real quickly here.

On fire groups- these are chapters that are not willing to settle for mediocrity. They are the biggest. They are the best. Or if they are not the biggest and the best, they are working toward and striving to be the biggest and the best, the most productive chapters for members to be in.

Their leadership teams are committed to BNI’s policies, guidelines and systems. Their members attend virtually all of the trainings offered by the local region. Often several times, you will find members attached things to their badges denoting all of the different roles they have played in the chapter- visitor host, education coordinator, leadership team, etc.

Most of the members of these groups have fully completed their BNI Connect profile on BNI Connect, including member testimonials, badges and CEUs. They are actively passing referrals online and reporting them to the chapter each week. The excitement level of chapters like this is electric.

Chapters in this phase just exude energy and positive attitude. They are likely to try to use the ideas that the Directors give. They effectively do 1-2-1s, that is a really important one. And they tend to operate in a really friendly environment. They have great culture, support and positivity. They don’t let their business obstruct their way of having fun and developing close relationships. They are really good to be around.

I really love on fire groups. On fire groups are what I meant to create when I started BNI.

On hold. On hold groups have become somewhat complacent. They have oftentimes been on fire and they lost that spark that made them successful or maybe they may be a smaller chapter who has begun to give up on growth and they have accepted status quo.

The chapter that is going through this phase tends to believe that they are okay. They’re okay. Not bad, no pressing problems. There is not that much that we need to do.

You know, the business they are getting and doing is okay. It may not be great but it is alright. They may take a few suggestions offered by the Director, but they usually dismiss a number of suggestions because they don’t think it applies to them. They know better. They tend to follow many but not all of the policies and basic structure of the meetings, but they have have lost or are losing their enthusiasm or spirit.

It was Jim Collins in Good to Great who said, “What is the enemy of good?” People would say, well, bad. That is not the answer. The enemy of great- correction. The question is “What is the enemy of great?” and most people answer “bad”. The answer to what is the enemy of great is good. Not bad. Good is the enemy of great.

You have heard people say it. “That is good enough.” “That will work.” Oftentimes, and certainly in BNI, good is the enemy of great. On hold is often good, but on fire is great.

Of course, I think you know where we are going with “in a hole.” Chapters is this phase tend to have numerous problems. It could be any one of these or all of them: attitudes, referrals, attendance. Almost always, attendance is lower than the groups in the other phases that I have discussed.

Unfortunately, most of the membership is focused on the problem and not on the solution. We talked about aligning ourselves with people who are either engines or anchors. The [engines] tend to align themselves with solutions. The chapters that tend to focus on problems instead of solutions tend to become experts on problems. That is where they live as a group instead of “How do we get to a solution?”

They tend not to follow the agenda, structure and policy very well. They tend to view their local Director as an outsider instead of a resource and a coach to support.

By the way, the process begins with identifying the phase that you are in and in contacting your Director for getting help to stay in on fire states, or help to on hold, or in a hole.

All chapters go through these phases, Priscilla. I know your chapter has. I have managed many, many chapters who have – the really successful chapters tend to spend more time in the on fire phase than in the other two phases.

I am going to include in this podcast. It is called the Rate Your Chapter Worksheet. If you do this and you determine that you are on hold or in a hole, I want you to contact your Director and get some suggestions on how you can help your group achieve excellence.

One way to kind of determine where you are at is have all of your members complete this chapter worksheet that I am going to include with this podcast.

We have thousands of groups now in dozens of countries. With that many groups, we have landed on the right combination of passion, structure, accountability and culture to make the groups work. Don’t reinvent the wheel, everyone. The process works really well for chapters with the tools and the systems that we provide.

So do a little assessment of your group, maybe direct them to complete this form that I am going to include here. I would love to hear your thoughts and then I will have a closing thought for you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Well, do you think that using that form is appropriate for an educational moment?

Ivan:
Absolutely. It’s a great assessment. It’s a self assessment tool. All of the members of the chapter just fill it out, but you know, you will need to- what I would do is prep everyone and tell them. “Next week, we are going to do this to rate your chapter. I will hand it out and we will take three to five minutes during the meeting. Have everyone collect that, if you would please. Then I am going to collect them and calculate the results.”

I think that it takes a little bit of work for the educational coordinator to do but it is really, really valuable. So yes, but I would prep them. I wouldn’t just throw it at them.

Priscilla:
Okay. Alright.

Ivan:
So we are probably out of time. Let me leave you with one last thought. There are two things, actually.

I would love to hear your thoughts about chapter cycles. Have you experienced them? And for those of you who were in a hold or on hold and are now on fire, people need to hear from you. They really need to hear from you. So if your group was on hold or in a hole, talk about what your group did to get on fire. I think that is probably as valuable or more valuable than my podcast, hearing it from members.

I think passion is really important to success, but as important as passion in to success, I really believe that passion alone does not produce results. Passion and commitment produce results. Take the passion that you have for BNI, attach some commitment to it and your chapter will go from on hold or in a hole to on fire faster than you thought possible.

That is the kind of organization that I was trying to start over 30 years ago when I started BNI.

Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Thank you so much, Dr. Misner. Okay, I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Is your BNI chapter On Fire, On Hold, or In a Hole? Use the Rate Your Chapter Worksheet to find out–and learn what you can to do be On Fire.

title.rendered: Episode 843: The New Force Driving Business Success

content.rendered:
Synopsis

We live in an age of sweeping conflict, widespread skepticism, and intense anxiety. The solution to today’s challenges lies in the 3rd Paradigm. The 1st Paradigm is competition. The 2nd Paradigm is cooperation. The 3rd Paradigm is co-creation.

In the age of co-creation, consumers want to work together with their favorite brands to ensure that products and services are adapted to meet their needs. In addition, many want to make sure that there’s a social cause related to the brand. BNI has been a leader in co-creation since 1986, and it has a social cause: children and education through the BNI Foundation.

Links

  • Get your copy of The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success.
  • Listen to Episode 835: A Radical Shift to Greater Success.
  • Visit the BNI Foundation website.

Complete Transcript of Episode 843

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in New Orleans this week for the Transformational Leadership Council (TLC), which was started by Jack Canfield. And of course, I’m going to meet with some local BNI people while I’m here.

Priscilla: 
Oh, boy, that’s such a fun city.

Ivan:
It is a fun city and a great place to buy art. I have bought most of my fine art in New Orleans.

Priscilla:
Oh, interesting. Okay, so what do you have for us today?

Ivan:
Well, I want to talk a little bit more about the book that recently came out, “The 3rd Paradigm,” the book I co-wrote. And I’m going to do several sessions on “The 3rd Paradigm” over the next few weeks. So the latest book, “The 3rd Paradigm,” discusses how businesses should harness the power of consumers who want to have a say in creating and improving the products and services that they buy. And so I want to expand on this concept here in this podcast.

Imagine a world where you can tackle the greatest, most challenging problems with a proven approach that leads to unmatched success. That’s the premise and the promise of the book that I co-wrote with Dr. Heidi Giusto and Dawa Phillips, who’s also a TLC member; I’m seeing him this week. You know, Priscilla, we live in an age of sweeping conflict, widespread skepticism, and intense anxiety. Contention feels pervasive. A balanced discourse is like a thing of the past, and pundits constantly tell us what’s wrong with society. I know I’ve said this in podcasts before, but people complain like it’s an Olympic event — and I checked this, it’s not.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
And we obsess over the massive problems that we see in the world. Negativity seems to be the norm. However, I really believe there’s hope. There’s an answer, and it starts with focusing on the solutions. When people focus on problems, they become world-class experts on “the problem.” But when they focus on solutions, they can become world-class experts on “the solution.” And we believe “the solution,” in part, to today’s massive challenges lies within the 3rd Paradigm.

Now as a reference point, a paradigm is a philosophical framework or discipline within which theories and laws are formulated. And we believe we’re entering the era of the 3rd Paradigm. And so let’s take you on a short journey through what we define as the three paradigms. Now I talked about these three paradigms briefly in episode number 835. I’m going to go into more detail here.

The 1st Paradigm is the era of competition. This paradigm was formulated within the framework of the laws of production by early pioneers of business thinking. Formal theories of management from the 1800s, like Frederick Taylor, the Gilbreths, Max Weber, and others who focused on issues like workflow, economic efficiency, labor productivity. This was the era of “scientific management,” which, interestingly enough, is the beginning of business theory; there really wasn’t much written about business the scientific management. That’s the 1st Paradigm.

The 1st Paradigm was so focused on productivity and competition that it failed to account for the needs of people in the workforce. Competition was about gaining something by defeating your rivals or establishing superiority over them. And it meant having a winner or a loser. I mean, can you imagine living in the production-oriented, competitive model today? Sixty-hour workweeks would be the norm, there would be few–if any–breaks, you’d be expected to work during much of your lunchtime. Forget about safety regulations. Children might be working. You know, in most “developed” nations, that now seems inconceivable, but in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, it was common throughout the world.

So the 2nd Paradigm is the era of cooperation. The paradigm evolved over time as people learned that a strictly production-oriented approach didn’t take into account the interaction of the people involved in the process. This era was about two or more people working together towards shared goals. And the focus on acting together for a common purpose started to evolve in the early 1960s. Around this time, MIT management professor Douglas McGregor published his groundbreaking book, “The Human Side of Enterprise” in 1960, which introduced the concepts of Theory X and Theory Y. And this is stuff I teach in my management… when I taught management theory at university, these are the kind of things that I talked about.

Now, McGregor’s framework highlights the motivating role of job satisfaction, and argues that people can do their work without constant direct supervision. And he insisted that people are one of the most valuable assets driving organizational success. And I know that’s like “obvious” now. But in the 1960s, this was new age; this was crazy that people were an important part of productivity in a business. So we may be puzzled by this because the idea does seem obvious to us, but they weren’t obvious at the start of the 2nd Paradigm. This was, in fact, revolutionary management thinking in its day.

Now, during the time of the 2nd Paradigm, management consultant Peter Drucker developed the MBO process (Management by Objective), where managers and employees could identify common goals and define their areas of responsibility. This is all part of cooperation. Today’s organizations are much more cooperative. Old command-and-control methods have largely been replaced by much more openness and transparency than what we had in the 1st Paradigm. So while the cooperative model, or collaborative model, was a massive improvement from the strictly competitive, production-oriented model, it was still lacking the full potential of the human experience.

And so in the 1980s, John Naisbitt wrote in his groundbreaking book, Megatrends, that humanity is moving towards a “high-tech, high-touch” society, saying that the more technologically advanced we became, the more important was to become highly connected to people. Now, what a lot of people don’t know is, I read this book in the early 1980s. This was the genesis of my thinking about BNI and how putting together a group of people to work together for a common goal of increasing referrals in a structured process. In part, the seed of that came from having read “Megatrends” and John Naisbitt, and it’s one of the reasons why I was able to get John to contribute to a book that I did called “Masters of Networking,” because he really was the person that had me start thinking about the power of getting people together.

And this was several years before I started BNI, getting people together to network effectively in a relationship, in a professional relationship.

Now, it’s really important to note that this transition from one paradigm to another doesn’t happen overnight. It emerges by evolving over time.

Ivan:
So let’s go to the 3rd Paradigm, which is what the book is about. The 3rd Paradigm is the era of co-creation. Now this concept begins where cooperation leaves off. The difference between cooperation and co-creation is the difference between working together and creating together. You may collaborate on a project, but you co-create products and services. Co-creation is a significant step beyond cooperation. It’s about bringing different parties together to actually produce, improve, or customize a product or service.

While some scholars recognized the theory of co-creation in the late 20th century, it was really in the 21st century that the internet brought the concept to the forefront. Crowdsourcing has become a critical tool for engagement. Waze — apps like Waze, the navigation app used by millions of people is a good example of this concept. The widespread application of digital technology has made customer empowerment a must.

So society is rapidly moving from a passive to a more participatory consumer culture. Co-creation plays a key role in this, since today’s consumers want a say in creating new products and services or improving existing ones. In an article in the early 2000s, the Harvard Business Review talks about “Co-opting Customer Competence,” and how customers are transitioning from being a passive audience to being a more active player-becoming “co-creators as well as consumers of value.” And in this age of co-creation… and I think BNI was one of the leading organizations on co-creation, because in 1986 I formed the Board of Advisors and the Board of Advisors, made up of [BNI] members, created every single policy that exists in this organization. They either created the policy or approved the policies that were in existence, and they created many of them. They altered many of them. And so BNI has, from its early days, been the leader in co-creation.

And I believe that, in the age of co-creation, consumers want to work together with their favorite brands to ensure that products and services are adapted to meet their needs. In addition, many want to make sure that there’s a social cause related to the brand. And for those of you who don’t know, BNI, we have a foundation, we try to talk about the Foundation as often as possible. The BNI Foundation supports children in education. If you do a search on BNI Podcast, in quotation marks “BNI Foundation,” you’ll find many podcasts that have been done over the years, including several with my late wife, who was the co-founder of the BNI Foundation with me. And BNI has a social cause, and our social cause is children and education through the BNI Foundation.

So organizations engage in co-creation because they wish to foster the buy-in of stakeholder interest and increase the value through innovation. And Priscilla, that’s what this book is all about. It’s about the 3rd paradigm or co-creation. And as I mentioned in a previous episode where I talked about the book, episode 835, we surveyed 4000 people, and the book really talks at length about what works with co-creation, what doesn’t work with co-creation. And you don’t have to be a big company to employ the concepts of co-creation You can be a small company and start to use the power of co-creation into the 21st century.

And so I invite all my listeners to take a look at my book, “The 3rd Paradigm,” it’s on Amazon and in bookstores. And if you get a copy, would you please leave a review? Reviews really do help promote books and this book certainly mentions BNI multiple times and it’s great PR for the organization. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla: 
Great. It’s so fascinating. Thank you, Ivan. Are we done? Do you have more to add?

Ivan: (chuckling)
If you have any questions, I have lots more to add, but that’s everything I’ve got for today.

Priscilla:
Okay, great.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: In the age of co-creation, consumers want to work together with brands to ensure that products and services meet their needs.

title.rendered: Episode 842: Creating a Chapter of Over 100 Members

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Shirley Towne from BNI South Carolina Midlands joins Dr. Misner this week to explain how to create a culture of growth to get your chapter to 100 members and beyond. Shirley’s own chapter of 152 people passed $21 million worth of business last year, an average of six figures per member.

  • Set performance standards for members, with 60 days written notice for a probation period.
  • Enforce the attendance policy and only renew members who show up.
  • Go beyond inviting visitors: ask each member to sponsor one new member every calendar year.

Complete Transcript of Episode 842

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m back from San Antonio from last week in Austin, Texas. However, I am doing a live event via Zoom in BNI Colombia. So I’m looking forward to talking to our members in Colombia this week.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. And who do you have this morning?

Ivan:
We have a guest this week, Shirley Towne. I’ve known Shirley for a long time. Shirley is the Executive Director of BNI Midlands of South Carolina [USA]. She has grown her region from 379 members to 1053 members in six years. She became a member in 2013. She was the 36th member of the chapter, which now has… this chapter now has 152 members. Oh my goodness! Shirley, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Shirley:
Thank you so much. Happy to be here.

Ivan:
Well listen. First of all, I’ll tell you right up front: Years ago, I would say, “Oh, chapters of 80 members, I think that’s probably the maximum you could get.” And then it was 100 members. And then, you know, I saw chapters that were over 100 members, and now we have chapters at 200 members. It’s a completely different paradigm, even for me.

And I say this because I know there are members listening to this going, “Oh, yeah, but we couldn’t do that.” I know there are definitely [BNI] Directors in the United States that say, “No, no, that can only be done in Asia and India.” They are totally wrong. Because you’re doing it right there in the Midlands Region of BNI. Well done, Shirley.

Shirley:
Thank you so much. We’re very proud of them.

Ivan:
I’m really proud of this chapter. I’m very, very impressed by that. So there’s a few things that we should talk about. The first is creating the culture of growth. So talk about that.

Shirley:
Yeah, so I think that was our first hurdle. I remember when we first started to grow, people would complain about it getting too big.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
And I remember doing this exercise where we had the last 10 members stand up, and they stood up. And then I said, “Raise your hand if you’ve received a referral from any of these 10 members.” And it was almost every single member. And I was like, “Why would we ever put a lid on this?” Like, imagine if we had capped it at 50 or 60? You know, why would we want to do that. And so it was just really changing their mindset to think that we could be more. 

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
But I’ll be honest with you, we never, ever had the vision of getting that big. Honestly, when I became President [of the chapter], our vision was to really add 10 members, so maybe we could shave the bottom 10 that weren’t performing. And as we set performance standards, we didn’t have to; like, they just rose to the occasion.

Ivan:
They rose. That’s impressive that they rose. But I want to get to the heart of the issue for me in terms of growth with a lot of chapters. They say what you said, which is “We won’t get to know each other”, “We won’t do well.” So just out of curiosity, Shirley, how much business has the chapter generated in the past 12 months? How much Thank You For Closed Business?

Shirley:
I just looked at this and it is $21 million. And some change, actually.

Ivan:
Alright, if you’re listening to this podcast, I want you to process that. 21 million US dollars in business in the last 12 months. You can’t do that with a chapter of 20 members. But you can do that with a chapter over 100 members, and that’s what they’re doing in your region, Shirley. I’m really just… that blows me away. I’m really proud. And that’s a HUGE number. And I don’t understand why members all around the world wouldn’t want to get a piece of that kind of business.

Shirley:
And you just said “a piece of that business” and I think that’s the thing that is so amazing, is when you look at that roster, and I literally can go down the roster and think about amazing stories of crazy, awesome referrals that they’ve given and received, but it’s not just a few power players. It’s all different levels and sizes of businesses referring crazy. My favorite member ever in that chapter was a jewelry lady who has since retired – 3/4 of a million dollars almost every single year she would give.

Ivan:
Wow! Yeah, well, you know, the average value for a seat in BNI has certainly changed over the years. It’s in the tens of thousands on average. But if I do the numbers right, this is six figures…

Shirley:
Yes.

Ivan:
…for the average value of a seat. Now, look, I get it, the average average is average. If your head is in the freezer and your feet are in the oven, on average you’re okay.


Shirley: (laughing)
Right.

Ivan:
Which is not the case. It’s average. But still, six figures?! That’s unbelievable. It’s incredible.

Shirley:
Well, our realtor just mentioned that he has hit over the $2 million mark in commissions.

Ivan:
In commissions, not sales, but commissions.

Shirley:
Commissions, correct.

Ivan:
Awesome.

Shirley:
Yeah, and he’s a huge part of the success of this chapter.

Ivan:
All right, there’s so much I want to talk about. One of the things that you had on your list is an intentional focus on sponsorship during the seven-months’ check-in. Talk to me about that.

Shirley:
Yeah, so you know, we talk so much about bringing in a visitor and this chapter has really changed the mindset to not talk about bringing a visitor but to sponsoring one [new member] within a calendar year. That’s always their goal, is to sponsor one in a calendar year. And so when we do those seven-month check-ins, you know, that’s something that we talk about. And that hasn’t always been the case; like it used to not be talked about. I remember there being a time when we were afraid to even talk about people bringing visitors.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
It was like, you know, I don’t know how many times I’ve heard this, Ivan. “You can be in the green and do everything and not bring a visitor.” And that’s like the one thing that everybody always says. You can do these other four things and not bring a visitor and still be in the green. I’m like, “Why do you choose that one thing?” Like, stop.

Ivan:
Yeah, right.

Shirley:
So that’s part of the mindset change is to stop saying things like that.

Ivan:
Yeah, I mean, you know, one of the things… there are a couple of levers that you can pull that really dramatically increase retention. One is going to Member Success Program training, and the other is bringing in a new member within the first year of membership. And that one surprised me. I’ve talked about it before, but that one surprised me. But it’s because when you bring in an associate or a friend, you want to stay. You want to be there. And so you increase retention by bringing in somebody into a chapter.

Shirley: 
I remember the first time you said that, and at first, it caught me exactly like you did. It was a little surprising to me. But then it wasn’t surprising when you really think about it. I mean, you bring your huge referral partner in, you’re not going to just quit after you bring them in.

Ivan:
Nope, not at all. Absolutely. All right, there’s so much more. You call them shareholder meetings, but they’re basically member meetings outside of the normal meeting. Talk to me about that.

Shirley:
Okay, in the very beginning, we felt like the culture was a little broken. And so we decided to have a shareholder meeting where we invite the chapter to meet after… you know, really, it’s in the evening hours, more like a happy hour kind of thing, and dinner. And we send out an anonymous survey where they fill it out and then we literally go over the answers to the survey. And so we did that very early on. And I remember there were six of us that showed up. This is when the chapter was about 40, six of us that showed up: three were on the leadership team, one guy thought it was just a happy hour didn’t know what he signed up for.

Ivan: (chuckling)

Shirley:
That guy was the most impactful guy. He said, “Oh, I didn’t know what this was.” He said, “But what I’ll tell you is I golf with a bunch of my buddies, and one of them belongs to this networking group. And we can’t golf during his time because like, he’ll get kicked out.” He said, “But you know, with us, like I can miss whenever and I won’t get kicked out. Because all I have to do is send a sub or… you know, we don’t kick anybody out because of attendance.” And when he said that, I was the President, when he said that, it really made me mad, but it was because I knew he was right. And so that day, we drew a line in the sand.

Ivan:
Good

Shirley:
It was our biggest takeaway. And we said we’re going to enforce the attendance policy starting today.

Ivan:
It makes a huge difference! I’ve been saying this for decades. It makes a gigantic difference in results.

Shirley:
That day, we said we were going to add ten [new members] so we could get rid of the bottom 10. We kicked out one guy, everybody took note; he was a high performer, but he was not coming to the meetings. He had a beach house, and he was headed to the beach early on Thursday. And we kicked him out and everybody changed and they rose to the occasion. We didn’t have to take anybody else out and that’s what started happening. We gained 10; we kept them and we… Like the performance standards just changed that day, it was all because of that guy. I’ll never forget him.

Ivan:
Can I just suggest that we change “We kicked them out” to “We opened their classification”? A little gentler.

Shirley: (laughing)
Absolutely. Sorry about that.

Ivan: (chuckling)
No, no, no, I get it, I totally get it. Alright, so I want to talk about how long it goes to do a meeting. And there’s so much more I want to hear from you. But the third topic you wanted to cover was setting performance standards. So do you want to touch upon that?

Shirley:
Yeah, so this chapter came to me and said, “Hey, we want to be a little bit better. And we want to set some performance standards.” And they came to me and said, “We want to put members on probation and have a plan to work with them, if they’re in the yellow for two consecutive months.”

Ivan:
Mmm.

Shirley: 
And if they’re in the red or the black, they wanted to be a little bit more aggressive to get him out of the red or the black within 30 days. And so I said, “Well, we’ve got to put this out there; we want to give everybody 60 days written notice before we implement this. And we did, and that’s when the chapter exploded, and probably went from 110 to 130. And I will be upfront with you, during the pause –  the great pause as you like to call it – we really lightened up on our standards. And when we went back to in-person we had to tighten up again. And I was scared; like we lost some people during that tightening up – on their own accord, because they didn’t like having to come back. Like they got used to being on Zoom. But the chapter rebounded from that so quickly and went right back up to 150.

Ivan:
Look, if you expect the best from people, you get it. And if you expect less than the best, you get it. The key is to apply the policies and the rules like you’re talking about – more like Mandela, than Attila. You know, it’s tough love, but you got to draw the line in the sand. Otherwise, you’re not going to achieve what you guys have achieved, which is just amazing to me; it blows my mind. I’m so impressed. We’re almost out of time, can you talk for just a minute or so about the fact that it does take longer to do a meeting of 150 people. It’s a little over two hours, officially an hour and a half. But you give them a little wiggle room in there, I think.

Shirley:
Officially an hour and 45 minutes of the exact meeting. But if you add on the Open Networking, you’re talking about a total of two hours that we expect them to be there. We do, just because it’s a lot… in the south, we feed people. So this is a lunch chapter in-person. So we open up the food line 30 minutes before you’re expected to be there. The culture of this chapter is they want to get there early. So they break bread together, like at tables with each other, before the meeting starts. So it is a little bit of a different culture with people wanting to get there 30 to 45 minutes before the meeting starts.

Ivan:
Well, Shirley, I’m just so impressed. I’m so proud of this chapter. And for anyone, anyplace in the world, that says, “You’re different in your country, we can’t do it here,” you’re clearly an example of the fact that it can be done everywhere. Not just Asia, not just Europe, not just South America. But in the US where BNI started. We can do it just like everyone else around the world. I’m so proud of you and proud of your region.

Shirley:
Thank you so much. I’m really proud of them as well.

Ivan:
Just before I hand it back to Priscilla, I just want to say again – $21 million in Thank You For Closed Business in the last 12 months. If that alone isn’t the argument to grow a chapter, I don’t know what is. Shirley, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast.

Shirley:
Thank you. It’s my honor.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Yeah, well, thank you both. That was fantastic.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Shirley Towne joins Dr. Misner this week to explain how to create a culture of growth to get your chapter to 100 members and beyond.

title.rendered: Episode 841: Member Retention

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Michael Mayer, BNI National Director for Germany-Austria, joins Dr. Misner to explain why caring about new members and building relationships with them is the key to member retention. It’s especially important to do get connected and stay connected in the first three months after a member joins BNI. Start by finding something in common with each new member and sharing something of yourself.

For more about sharing stories as a key part of member and chapter success, listen to Episode 308: 3+1=Member Success.

Complete Transcript of Episode 841

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan: 
Hi, Priscilla and Happy New Years, everybody. I am on vacation this week in San Antonio, Texas. I’ll tell you more about that in a few weeks. But I’m taking some time off this week.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. And who do you have as a guest today?

Ivan:
I have a longtime friend, the National Director for [BNI] Austria and Germany. He lives in Vienna. And he’s been involved in BNI since 2004. He is Michael Mayer. And Michael was in agricultural produce at one point; a recycling company, and then in 2004, got involved in BNI Vienna. Michael is married to Andrea since 2008. And he likes white wine, which is funny, Michael, because you always bring me red wine, which is okay with me. And you like riding motorcycles. You live in Vienna and Mannersdorf, I think is the correct pronunciation, you can correct me if I’m wrong. And your biggest wish is to be on vacation on Necker Island – that might be possible, and you never know. Michael Mayer, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Michael:
Thank you, Ivan for having me. It’s funny, when you talk about white wine, I like white wine. But I know exactly that you like red wine. And that’s why I bring you red wine. And this, I remember we had some very good times, and you have a very good choice when it comes to red wine. And I always enjoy having some good red wine with you.

Ivan:
You do and you always bring them to the international conventions. I look forward to that every year. Thanks. And we’ve been known to open up my wine cellar a few times when you guys have visited.

Michael:
That’s true. You’re very generous. And I have to say it was a great pleasure to taste his wine. It’s very good times in your house. Thank you.

Ivan:
Well, come on back to Austin. And you can see my new wine cellar. I don’t think you’ve seen this one.

Michael:
Oh, I’ve not seen this one. But thank you.

Ivan:
Yeah, we can fix that.

Michael:
Thank you.

Ivan:
So we’re talking about member retention. And there’s three things that you wanted to talk about. The first is the care, the care and feeding of members, basically. The care for members. Talk about that.

Michael:
It’s interesting, we are very good in BNI launching chapters. And I think we are very good in also converting visitors into members. And when it comes to 3+1, one is to hold members in our chapters–to retain them. And we always calculate percentages. And I realize that percentages can be misleading, because at the end of the day, what is the percentage? But if you look at absolute numbers, and you can see that I don’t know, out of a 500-member region, you lost 200 a year, or 250. It was 250 people who start building relationships and they enjoyed BNI. But they left us, you know, because whatever reason.

And it’s funny, when you look at these numbers, everybody who leaves BNI… it really hurts myself [sic]. So when it comes to retention, I was looking from this side where at the end of the day, we need to take care of our members and taking care is something where you have to put all your thoughts & emotions into that instead of just working a process. Don’t get me wrong, I think process is super important. But having this kind of leading thought of caring about people, caring about members, caring about entrepreneurs in BNI, I think it’s the initial thought when I talk about retention.

Ivan:
Yeah, well, let’s come back to that for a second. But I want to talk about the numbers that you mentioned. Because we found in a couple of surveys and I’ve mentioned this in previous podcasts, that a chapter is going to lose roughly 15 to 20% of its membership for reasons that it can’t control. There’s a health issue, the member moves, a member changes profession, the member retires, or the chapter let them go. There can be any number of reasons so if you lose 250 members in a region, somewhere around 50 of those are kind of out of the control of the chapter, other than letting them go. So it’s really the 200 that you want to try to do your best to hang on to. And that really means staying connected with them, which is your next point. Which is touch points with the member, particularly within the first year.

Michael:
Exactly. I mean, when people are joining BNI, it’s interesting — they come in to BNI meeting or to a visitor’s day, and they really, they really loved the energy of the meeting, they will really like the people there, they see business being passed. And it’s interesting, they want to join, and after they sign the application and they paid their dues, they get inducted into the chapter. But it’s interesting, when there is nobody there who holds their hand, or shows support or shows care, it can easily be that they’re not getting being part of the group, or they don’t know how this whole thing works. And sooner or very quickly, we lose these kind of people. So it’s very important that in the first three months, you need to stay in touch with the newest members.

We started to do like this as the first touch — when we invited every quarter of the year all new members with their life partners, to a networking evening in our office, because we think it’s very important to get to know them and, and really show them the vision of BNI. And that we are there for them, which probably cannot solve all the problems that they’re having. But we show that we care for them. And this evening is… I love this evening, it’s such a great evening, because you have people from all different chapters. And that’s why I think this is our first touch point what I think it’s super important to build relationship and show them that we care.

Ivan:
So one of the things I love about you talking about this subject, and you and I talked about this in the past, is that in Germany and Austria, for a number of years, you were very, very focused on the process, which is important. Don’t get me wrong: process, system, [is] very important. But at some point, you came to the realization that it was really about the relationships and the connections that people had. Do you want to talk for a moment about that?

Michael:
Happy to share it, I remember somebody created this kind of thing: passion, people, processes. And I’m a big believer that all three have to work together. But when it comes to BNI and relationship, it’s like you need to, you know, if you want to build relationship with somebody, and especially with new members, you need to find out firstly, what you have in common. If you find something that you have in common, you have a starting point for a conversation.

And secondly, if you want to build relationship, you need to share something from yourself; you need to share something, what other people are interested in. And if you do that at this first meeting, and I’m a very open person, because I learned in BNI that you need to be open to really connect with people, I share something from my life, and I try to find something in common with new members.

And it’s interesting, when you have this kind of two things, suddenly you have a connection. And suddenly this is not a… it’s not a business partner, suddenly, this is something more, you build a relationship and you have something in common, and he knows something from me, and he would open up as well. And we haven’t even talked about business.

It’s funny, then they get to know me and they run BNI in Vienna or Germany and Austria, and you know I make it personal; I tell them, “Guys, if you have any problems, call me, call our staff, we’re there for you.” So this evening, we serve champagne, we have some wine, we build relationships and find common things. And at the end of the day, they meet with other members from other chapters. And suddenly they’re not part of one chapter, they’re part of a community. You know, at the end of the day, I share the vision of BNI – changing the way the world does business. And suddenly, they’re like, “Oh, I’ve never thought about this. Oh, there’s something more behind. Oh, they really care.” And that’s the start. Where I think relationship is super important when it comes to retention.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think relationship is absolutely critical. And, you know, you bring up a couple of points of fact that when they see that the organization is global, that is eye-opening to members. And when they know that the local, the national office or regional office cares, that’s critical. That’s the cement that keeps members engaged, I think, in the process.

Michael:
You know what’s funny? You know, to be in BNI now for over 20 years and people still coming up to me this evening said, “So, what’s your real profession?” It’s so funny because, you know, we have now, you know, we crossed the 11,000 members mark a week ago, and we have record numbers of members in Germany and in Austria. And still, we have, you know, we have 65 franchised regions. It’s quite a big operation. I’m really happy to be part of that. And some members are coming up to me because they have never seen the big picture, “So what’s your job? What’s your real job?” And I will start laughing and say, “Guys, we are here to support you, we have a very big organization, there are over 300,000 members, and it’s a big thing.” And we show them how big it is. And if you don’t come together with the new members, you know, they need to be active themselves instead of just showing them where we are. And I’m very proud to be part of this organization, because I still love it after 20 years.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think a lot of members are surprised that there’s over 300,000 members in the organization. And there’s more than 10,000 people that work for the company, counting directors and employees. It’s a pretty big operation. We’re almost out of time, but you had one more topic that you wanted to touch upon. And that was recognition. Let’s touch upon that we’ve got about a minute or two.

Michael:
I remember coming to the first [BNI] Global Convention, it was in Los Angeles, and suddenly people were paying really deep respect for people who did something very good. And there was a standing ovation, like coming from a country where standing ovation is not existing in us, I just saw the power of recognition where people really feel that giving recognition is super important. And it starts off, when we’re on the local level. It’s super interesting, when you really say thank you to people, thank you for referral, thank you for the content. Thank you for listening to me. And I think this is part where you do it in a one-to-one and you really pick something super specific when you thank somebody. I think if you say you know you’re a great person, that’s nice.

But if you really being specific in recognition, saying, “Thank you for passing me three referrals in the last two months, that made a difference in my business. Out of the three referrals, I closed 100% of them. And they already ordered a second or third time. Thank you for passing me these three referrals in the last two months.” (or whatever). The more specific you are, the more that people appreciate really, the recognition. So I really changed completely my way of dealing with other people and saying things too often is not. I mean, you can say as often as possible, and being very specific here. Thank you. And I think on the chapter level, and it’s funny, we always thought in Germany, Austria, recognition is important. It’s the same thing like in every other country. It is yeah, people appreciate it so much.

Ivan:
You know, I read a piece many years ago by Frederick Hertzberg, where he talks about motivation. And one of the best ways to motivate people within an organization is recognition. And I recommend that piece. Frederick Hertzberg, in the Harvard Business Review, “How do you motivate people?” Recognition is absolutely critical and certainly is in BNI. Michael, I want to thank you for your many years of service in the BNI organization and everything you’re doing in Austria and Germany, and I appreciate you being on BNI Podcast today.

Michael:
Thank you very much, Ivan, for this chance. Thank you very much.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Michael Mayer explains why caring about new BNI members and building relationships with them is the key to member retention.
