title.rendered: Episode 840: The Value of Exit Interviews (Classic)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 230, first published on November 9, 2011. Links and offers may no longer be valid.

We mentioned exit interviews in Episode 222; in this episode, Dr. Misner explains how exit interviews came to BNI.

Many years ago a terrific BNI member, someone who really seemed to love BNI, quit unexpectedly. Dr. Misner designed an exit interview and found out that the real reason the member was leaving was because another member had behaved unethically. The membership committee confronted this other member and removed the person from the chapter, allowing the first member to stay.

Here are some of the questions in an exit interview:

  • What are your reasons for leaving?
  • Did you find that BNI was beneficial to you?
  • What did you like most about BNI?
  • Was there anything you disliked about BNI? If so, what was it and how would you change it?
  • Did you feel you were well-informed about the activities and benefits of BNI?
  • Did you meet for lunch with other members of the group? How often?

Take this podcast to your leadership team. And download your own copy of “The Significance and Value of the Exit Interview” below.

The Significance and Value of the Exit InterviewDownload

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 230/840

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I come to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. This week I am at the international BNI conference in Long Beach. I talked about it in last week’s podcast. We have hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of people coming here now. The conference starts tomorrow. We do this every year. It’s like going to a UN conference. It’s really an amazing experience. Members are welcome to come on the member’s day, which is on Friday every year. So just look at BNI.com in November of every year and if a BNI member anywhere in the world would like to come, you are welcome. We had somebody from Africa come last year.

Priscilla:
Wow. I know it’s very interesting to meet all of the people from all over the world. It gives you a real sense of how big the organization is.

Ivan:
It really does. No question about it. Today I am going to talk about the value of exit interviews. I thought I would start with a couple of things. First of all, I made reference to an exit interview in episode 222, Why Do People Leave? So if you have a chance and are listening to this podcast and you have not listened to episode 222, I think it goes hand in glove with this podcast. So take a look at that.

In that podcast, I talked about the fact that we started doing exit interviews a number of years ago, but I don’t think I gave the story about how they came about. So I thought I would open it up with this story as to how exit interviews began. What happened was that we had somebody quit a chapter and they were a great member. They seemed very, very happy. They really seemed to like the organization. So somebody said we should really ask them why. I said we should do an exit interview. They asked what that is. This is years ago, Priscilla, I couldn’t have had more than 40 chapters.

One of the kinds of things that I did as a management consultant was I designed exit interviews for companies. I said it was just a list of questions that you ask somebody when they leave. So I wrote this up for a specific member. I called him and said we are starting to do exit interviews. I didn’t want to say it was because he is leaving. That’s what I did. We were starting to do exit interviews. I just had a few questions. We asked why the person was leaving. I asked the question a couple of times in different ways. I found out, Priscilla, that the person was leaving because they were mad at another member.

Even though they said that business was really booming, business was great and they couldn’t handle it all, I found out that that wasn’t the real reason. The reason was that somebody else had kind of ripped him off in a chapter. That’s when I had an opportunity then to do some consulting, some mentoring. I said if they really wanted to leave because somebody has done something inappropriate? We should deal with that and not have you leave. He said he didn’t want to make a big deal out of it.

The thing about this guy was that I knew he was a real ethical person. I guilted him. I’ll be honest. I guilted him. I said, “If you leave, do you think this guy is going to do the same thine to someone else?” He said yes. I said, “ Then let’s do the right thing together and let me help you with the membership committee. Let’s bring this to the membership committee so that they know what happened and they can make a decision. The membership committee heard the issue and they removed the other person. So guess who stayed. The guy who was going to quit. It all started because of this exit interview.

In this podcast, I am going to review what the exit interview consists of. There is going to be a downloadable so that members can download this on their own, although this is available to leadership teams and directors. Any member can download this. I urge chapters to use this when you have people leaving. Not just anybody, but I recommend that you use it consistently in your chapter.

Priscilla:
I think it’s a great idea.

Ivan:
One of the things that we say is that the value of the exit interview is directors- but more than just directors, I think members can do this as well. I don’t recommend that it be any member. I recommend that it be the leadership team, somebody from the leadership team, or somebody from the membership committee. Anyone in authority who regularly performs exit interviews, there is a number of benefits. It allows the chapter to figure out if maybe this isn’t the right chapter.

Maybe a different chapter might work for them because of a personality conflict. It provides insight as to what a member’s impression is and how BNI can serve more effectively in terms of quality control. It can help chapters take ownership for the quality of their members and avert potential problems.

Exit interviews are a very powerful tool that I think are not utilized as much as they have been in the past in some reasons. I would urge you to have somebody in your group be responsible for doing exit interviews. Here is what the exit interview consists of. Then maybe, Priscilla, we can just chat for a few minutes if you have any questions.

The exit interview basically consists of you putting down who is interviewing, who you are interviewing and you ask them what is the reason for leaving? Do you mind telling me why you are leaving? What are the reason/s for leaving? Do you find that BNI is a benefit to your profession?

We found this in studies that we have done in the past that 20% of a chapter will leave for reasons that a chapter can’t control. I have talked about this in a previous podcast. Health problems, changing careers, moving- you are going to get 20% turnover, so if you have 40% turnover in your chapter, half of that is going to be for stuff that you can’t control. So when you ask if they feel it has been of benefit and they say yes, you find out maybe that they are leaving because of something that is really beyond your chapter’s control.

Ask them no matter what they said- whether they are leaving because they are unhappy or happy- what did you like most about BNI? Another question is was there anything that you disliked about BNI, and if so, what is it and how would you change it if you could? Did you feel that you were well informed on the benefits and activities offered to you by BNI?

Then there are five questions that are asked in the last question, sort of a yes or no. This gives you a sense of the kind of activity that the member engaged in, so if they say they didn’t get any business out of BNI, you can get a sense of whether they were engaged in the kind of activities that they needed to be in. For example, did you meet for lunch for other members of the group? Yes or no, and if so, roughly how many? Were you specific about expressing what a good referral for you was? Did you ask members if the referral you gave worked for them? Did you prepare your 60 second presentations? Did you feel you had good communication with your chapter and its members?

Really the idea is just to get people to open up and talk. Here’s and old- this isn’t in any other written material. I am going to give you and old technique as an old HR guy who has worked with human resources and studies and surveys. A concept called semantic differential questioning, when you basically ask the same question in different ways. Asking why somebody left using different words will oftentimes get different answers.

What was the reason for leaving? What was one of the most important factors in your decision to leave? You don’t do this one right after the other. You might do one in the beginning, throw another one in the middle and one at the very end. What’s the bottom line, Priscilla, on why you left? You only do that when you have somebody whom you think has another reason for leaving than what they are saying.

By using semantic differential questioning where you ask the same question in two or three different ways, I know it sounds silly, but you will get, many times, a different answer. When they say to you, you know what, you kind of asked that and they kind of answered that, then you are probably getting the answer that’s true. Again, I wouldn’t use that technique on everyone, but when you think you have someone who is not being completely candid for whatever reason, it’s a great way in an exit interview to get the bottom line, to get the information.

Knowledge is power. If you know why people are leaving, you can impact it. I highly recommend exit interviews, so go back and listen to episode 22 and put them together.

Priscilla:
Episode 222

Ivan:
Episode 222 will tie together with this episode. 22 was a long time ago.

Priscilla:
I want to just say a few things. One is that I think we definitely lost a very positive member due to something that was personal between members. He just did not want to deal with it. I think it’s really a shame because had perfect attendance, had been a member for years, and was a really great person. I think if we had done something like this, maybe we could have avoided it. He just felt really uncomfortable. He didn’t talk about what the reasoning was but it was definitely a conflict with another member. So I think it happens more often than you may think.

Ivan:
It does and people- oftentimes, there are a lot of reasons why they don’t talk about it. It may be that they just don’t want to cause waves. It might be that they don’t want to have a battle. It might be that they are just so frustrated that they don’t ever want to sit in the same room with the other person.

Priscilla:
I think it was that.

Ivan:
In a way, that is the wrong way because now you leave the person who created the problem in the chapter. Good people will do the right thing when you present it to them properly. Do you think that that person is going to do the same thing? They will often say yes. Then let’s do the right thing here and tell somebody about this.

Priscilla:
That’s a great idea. I am definitely going to take this back to my chapter and get it started because we have a very god chapter that is eight years old, but we are losing some of our newer members, so we have to figure out why.

Ivan:
That’s important. I recommend that everyone listening to this podcast, take this podcast to your leadership team. Tell your leadership team , “This is a podcast you need to listen to. I think this chapter should consider doing them.” Every chapter doesn’t need to do them. This is one of those techniques, Priscilla, where this is not BNI 101. This is BNI 401. If you don’t do this, it’s not the end of the world but if you have a really good chapter and you just want to make it better and you want to do some things- you’re already a good chapter and you want to get even better, this is a technique that really, really works.

Of course, it works on the chapters that are struggling as well, but it’s a little more complicated to deal with and it’s a little more commitment.

Priscilla:
Okay great, Ivan. I really like that.

Ivan:
Well, thanks a lot, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay I think that is it for this week. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner explains how exit interviews for departing members came to BNI. Take this podcast and the report to your leadership team.

title.rendered: Episode 839: Does BNI Work for Employees?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

BNI is not just for entrepreneurs. Employees bring valuable referrals to their employers. Even one employee’s participation in BNI can have great benefits for a local bank branch or retail location. If you’re an employee, talk to your supervisor about sponsoring your BNI membership.

By recognizing the potential of business networking and actively pursuing professional relationships, employees can unlock doors to new referral opportunities. Whether you’re self-employed or you work for someone, BNI can work very effectively for you.

Complete Transcript of Episode 839

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Austin two weeks in a row. Yay! Sometimes it’s good not to be traveling so much.

Priscilla:
Yeah, no kidding. So tell us what we’re doing today.

Ivan:
Well, we’re talking about “Does BNI work for employees?” And the short answer is yes, it absolutely does. It works great for employees. And I learned this, gosh — this story is probably 20, maybe 25 years old, but I’ve never told it in a podcast. Several, I mean many years ago, at least 20 to 25 years ago, I met a bank manager who was really attempting to persuade his supervisor that participation in a BNI chapter would get great results for his branch. And the supervisor reluctantly agreed to let him become a member on a trial basis. And the bank manager began getting referrals soon after joining the BNI group. And after several months, a fellow member gave him a particularly good referral; it was a man who was disgruntled with the level of service at his current bank. And the manager decided to visit the man at the bank branch. I’m sorry, the manager decided to visit the man at his company. So he went to the referral, a person’s company. And the man told the manager that he felt he was not getting personal service from his existing bank. And the manager assured him that his bank prided itself on great service.

Now this is an employee, okay; he didn’t own the bank, he was a bank manager. And he gave the man his personal mobile phone, and his home phone and told him if there’s ever a problem, he can reach out anytime a day, at home or at work. And if he was interested in you know, doing business at the branch, just let him know.

And so two days later, at 9am, the man, the referral, was standing at the bank door with several savings and checkbooks in hand, several savings account information and checkbooks in hand, and the branch manager met him at the door and thanked him for coming to the branch. And the man said he was really impressed with the way he was handled by the manager and that he had decided to transfer his accounts to the manager’s bank. And to the astonishment of the manager, the new customer handed over checking, savings, and money market accounts — now remember, this was almost 25 years ago — he handed over accounts that were worth over $950,000.

Priscilla: (chuckles)

Ivan:
And after everything was completed, the man told the manager how glad he was to be referred to him by their mutual friend, a BNI member. So a BNI member made that referral. So I first heard this story when my office at BNI headquarters started getting phone calls. It was in the next two days after that referral took place. And we’re getting phone calls from every branch manager in Southern California with this bank. And each of them wanted information about a local BNI chapter in their area. And I knew this bank manager and he told me that his boss, his regional manager, didn’t want him to join, but he did anyway. And then it turned out that when the bank manager got that $950,000 referral, his supervisor asked him at a tele-bridge, remember tele-bridges before Zoom, where everybody would get on the phone and talk. And he said, “Well, I got it from BNI, that organization that you weren’t sure about.” He’s like, “You got a $950,000 referral?” And he said, “Yeah, and I’ve gotten these other referrals, too. And this is why we’re doing so well in the branch.” And so the supervisor told all the other branch managers to go join a local BNI chapter. So we started getting all of these phone calls from branch managers because of this referral.

And the lesson here is that for those of you who are working as employees, like the managers [in] the story there, there’s a valuable lesson there. Persuade your supervisor. Convincing supervisors of the merits of business networking is really the first hurdle. And you know, I spoke to an individual who is eager to join a networking group but faced continued resistance from his boss, who cited budget constraints and said the company couldn’t pay for it. And, now this is a different person, and this salesman, this particular salesman, proposed a really interesting deal. He said he would pay for the membership, he, the salesperson, would pay for the membership, and if he secured two referrals resulting in sales within 30 days, would the company reimburse him? The boss said, “Oh yeah, sure, fine. If you come up with two sales, I’ll see to it that the company pays for the membership.” Well, guess what?

Priscilla:
What?

Ivan:
The salesman closed three sales and was working on four more by the end of the first couple of months and true to their agreement, the boss covered the initial membership and then paid for the renewal, acknowledging that there are incredibly tangible benefits derived from the salesman’s networking efforts. So there’s really two big stories here. One is, you know, if you’re an employee, getting your supervisor to approve a membership is great. And the other thing is, sometimes chapters… they just want entrepreneurs Well, the truth is, there are great employees for large organizations, who would make great members like the salesman that I mentioned, and the bank manager. The bank manager’s success and the subsequent organizational response really highlight the opportunity for a sort of a cultural shift within companies. The reluctance of the supervisor who’s initially hesitant about the networking idea, really transformed into a proactive encouragement for all of his branch managers to join a local BNI chapter. And you know, now we have many, many, many banks who did this, and that shift really started years ago. In today’s digital age, the landscape of networking has expanded beyond traditional in-person interactions, and virtual platforms and online communities, I think, provide avenues for connecting with professionals globally; and transcending geographic boundaries, particularly if you’re in a hybrid chapter or an online chapter. And of course, in-person networking, I believe, remains invaluable. And that is still what the majority of our organization is, is an in-person networking organization.

I think the lessons learned from this story is, and I’d love any of your thoughts, Priscilla, is that in this podcast, the lessons learned in this podcast extend beyond individual success, to really encompass organizational growth and cultural transformation within organizations. By recognizing the potential of business networking and actively pursuing professional relationships, employees, individuals with companies, can unlock doors to new referral opportunities. And so whether you’re self-employed, or you work for someone, BNI can work very effectively for you. And I think this is an important message for employees, and it’s also an important message for BNI members to hear that employees can make great members of a BNI chapter. Both of those are true stories, Priscilla. Any thoughts before we wrap up?

Priscilla:
Yes, I have a true story, because we had an a bank manager in our chapter 15 years ago, and I joined that bank and I’m still with that bank. Isn’t that crazy?

Ivan:
Oh wow, fantastic.

Priscilla: (laughing)
It worked on me.

Ivan:
Look, you know, the bank that I use here in Austin is a member of BNI. And I’ve been using them now for 10 years. And, you know, it was an employee of the bank that I started working with, and that employee has changed to somebody else. But, you know, still, the bank is still involved in BNI, and I’m happy to continue to bank with this branch. Well, that’s everything that I’ve got, Priscilla, unless you have anything else to add.

Priscilla:
I’m good. Thank you, Ivan.

Ivan:
Well, I thank you very much. And we’re coming up on the new year here, real soon.

Priscilla:
That’s true. Well, in case I don’t talk to you again before – Happy New Year.

Ivan:
Thank you very much; you, too, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 838: Diversity and Accessibility in BNI

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Claudia Thompson from the BNI New York CORE region explains how BNI chapters can better accommodate members with different kinds of disabilities.

  • Clear paths and increase lighting to help members with limited vision to navigate more easily.
  • Make sure tables are spaced evenly with room between them for walkers, wheelchairs, and service animals.
  • Enable captions on Zoom meetings and in PowerPoint presentations.
  • Face members with hearing loss directly when you speak to them so they can read your lips.
  • Minimize loud sounds, flashing lights, and other distractions. They make it especially difficult for people with ADD and autism to concentrate.
  • Make sure your meeting venue is wheelchair accessible, including the restrooms and your meeting room. (This is a legal requirement in the USA and many other countries.)
  • Always treat disabled people with the same respect as other members.

Complete Transcript of Episode 838

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi, Priscilla. I am actually in Austin this week. I’ve got a fundraiser for a charity, a nonprofit organization who is a member of BNI – Austin Child Guidance Center, and my late wife, Elisabeth, was really active with that organization. So we’re doing a little fundraiser for them this week here in Austin at my house.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great.

Ivan:
Yeah. So I have a guest this week. Her name is Claudia Thompson. Claudia has been on twice before, this is her third visit. And we’re going to be talking about diversity, equity and inclusion, and basically accessibility to BNI meetings. Claudia is the Managing Director for the [BNI] New York CORE regions. She’s been a BNI member for 20 years.

Priscilla:
Wow!

Ivan:
She had to join when she was 16; I’ve met Claudia — she had to have joined when she was 16 because I can’t believe she’s been a member 20 years. And she has served in multiple chapter leadership positions, and has been a Launch Director Consultant, an Area Director, Senior Launch Consultant, and currently is the Chair of the Diversity, Equity and Inclusion Panel at BNI. So we’ve got a few things we’re going to talk about here on an area that is clearly in her wheelhouse and her expertise. The first is something that I’ve seen from time to time, not often, but from time to time in BNI. And that is accommodating the visually impaired or hard of hearing members. Talk about that a little bit, Claudia.  And by the way, welcome back to the BNI Podcast.

Claudia:
Thank you, thank you so much for having me. Thanks for also saying I was 16 when I joined. That’s what I’m gonna walk away with from this conversation. So, obviously I have a passion for diversity for a long time. And I really tapped into this, particularly visually impaired and blind folks, because my mother has glaucoma, and is now learning how to navigate with being legally blind.

So there are a lot of things that we can do for specifically folks who are visually impaired. And so obviously, if a space allows animals, so my mother doesn’t have seeing eye dog, but a place that would do that, that would also cover service animals, for folks who need those. Clearing paths, right, I asked for this, like for people to clear paths like so they don’t have their bags down on the ground or computer bags in the way. For my mom, she can see very little but she has no periphery. So she can typically trip over things. So a lot of folks, whether they can see a little bit or not at all, clearing the paths during the meetings is really helpful. Making sure that everything is spaced evenly. Lighting is critical. She had mentioned this, too, like when there’s low or poor lighting, it’s very difficult for them to be able to navigate as well, if they do have some vision.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Claudia:
So yeah, so those are some of the key things as well. And then of course, enunciating clearly and concisely so they can understand. And spelling things out, if you will, not making assumptions because a lot of times people use their visual to understand and so when that sense is taken away, making sure that we spell things out literally for them.

Ivan:
I’ve also seen members, particularly with members who are visually impaired, where members will make sure to greet them as they come into the meeting and the person who’s visually impaired will hold on to their arm as they walk to the table or, you know, stand and visit with people. So whatever we can do to help somebody like that is always appreciated.

Claudia:
Yeah, 100%. Because if they don’t have a cane or a dog, people won’t always know. My mom started using the cane more to kind of let people know, “It looks like I can see well but I can’t.” And so she’ll grab onto someone’s arm to guide her, so that’s another really good one.

Ivan:
And for the hearing impaired. Did you talk about that already?

Claudia:
Well, no. But yeah, so we actually, in one of our DEI webinars, we interviewed a [BNI chapter] vice-president, who is down in Charlotte area, and she has 98% hearing loss. And so she said during COVID, during the lockdown, she really appreciated being on Zoom, because those who can, they can request to have captions, right?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Claudia:
Usually, typically, folks who have that. So as far as Zoom goes, both for blind and for those who are deaf or hard of hearing, it’s really helpful because there’s already tools that they can navigate on their own. However, if you’re at a meeting, being able to speak in front of the person, a lot of people can read lips, not making the assumption that they sign or read lips. Some people, she, this particular woman, did not sign at all. So that you know, making sure that whatever way that they can communicate, knowing that pretty early on, and then being in front of them when you’re speaking, not like turning your head away.

Ivan:
Right.

Claudia:
And then of course, being able to have caption use, even if people are presenting a PowerPoint in person, maybe they have captions, you know, available on the audio, visual. And then signage when applicable, any kind of, again, spelling things out, but in writing. Allowing an interpreter and I don’t know how this would work, I know my chapter would be more than accepting. But having someone maybe not have to pay the fee, maybe they pay for their meal. But having someone be able to attend each week, unless in fact, interpreter is their business. But if they need an interpreter there, like a sign language interpreter, that they will be able to also attend and accommodate that need.

Ivan:
I’ve seen chapters that have done that and allowed that. Another thing I’d recommend is the use of an app like Otter.ai, which immediately transcribes things in writing. So it would just need to be reasonably close, or, you know, centered. I found that app and I’m sure, you know, as this podcast, sits here for years, there’ll be other apps in addition to that. One other thing on the visually impaired, I know, one of my books has been done in Braille, “World’s Best Known Marketing Secret,” and of course, Audible is great. And most of my books are now done on Audible.

Claudia:
Oh, which is fantastic. I mean, even myself, I typically get books on Audible. I know that Perkins School for the Blind, which is in Massachusetts, actually will translate most books into Braille for folks.

Ivan:
Yeah, any books that I own the copyright on, I’m happy to give them the rights to translate it to Braille.

Claudia:
Yeah, that’s fantastic. Yeah.

Ivan:
So members with developmental delays, this is the second or technically the third category, challenges like autism. Do you want to talk about that briefly?

Claudia:
Yeah, absolutely. So we again, this was another topic that we’ve been getting a lot of requests on, particularly folks who might be on the spectrum. So they may not be developmentally delayed, in all cases, but they do deal with social challenges. So again, speaking clearly is key. The use of language in all of these cases is also really important. So not saying things like “stupid” or “dumb,” like saying, “Oh, I’m so dumb, I said that.” You’d be, you know, pretty mindful to not have that. And then also being mindful of sounds and flashing lights. So a lot of loud sounds can really cause disruption as well. So not being too loud, not saying things close by to them. And then also maybe not using slang, or, you know, phrases that might be hard to understand. So being really direct and trying to avoid things like small talk, sometimes, like not getting offended, as well, if they’re not receptive to small talk.

Ivan:
So I belong to an organization. I’ve mentioned it before, the TLC, the Transformational Leadership Council, and one of the members has a developmental challenge in terms of he has a hard time recognizing faces, which is a very unusual challenge. And so he just asked everyone, he was, you know, very clear with everybody about his challenge, he just asked everyone, when they see him just say, “Hi, this is Ivan.” And, you know, at some point, he recognizes a face, but it was helpful for him to get to remember people by just, you know, using their name or making sure to wear their nametag.

Claudia:
Yes, wearing name tags is everything. I know that that’s a big piece of it. So if you forget it, it may be a challenge. And again, people might take offense that “They couldn’t even remember me.” But of course, yeah, so keeping those in mind.

Ivan:
So we’re almost out of time. The third category is learning challenges. You want to talk briefly about that?

Claudia:
Yes, definitely. And I know that you have some insight into this as well. So for myself, actually, I have ADD or neurodivergent. And so a lot of times people will say things, and I have to stay focused. Now, if, again, however people manage that, whether it’s medication, whether it’s therapy, whether it’s, you know, who knows, there’s different tools I use, you know, myself, I use different tools, but for ways to understand that having patience with folks because you just don’t know what they might be dealing with, and how they process information when you’re sharing. So sometimes, something that’s not understood or written well in the newsletter, you know, or even on Business Builder, you know, I know that we’re reevaluating all of our tools. And hopefully, that’s something kept in mind with folks. And then, even particularly with specific learning challenges like dyslexia, you know, where the letters are funny to people, or they read things differently.

Ivan:
We need to wrap here in just a minute. I wanted to mention dyslexia. I’ve got a good friend who’s working on a book right now on dyslexia, his name is Jamie Waller, and he’s working on a book. And one of the people that I have met many times who’s dyslexic, and a billionaire, is Richard Branson. So you know, very successful people can have these kinds of learning challenges and yet be successful and be great BNI members.

Claudia:
Oh, that’s — wow.

Ivan:
Keep that in mind. Alright, I know, you’ve got one other thing, we’ve got about 15 seconds left, the accommodating visitors and members who are disabled?

Claudia:
Sure. So just thinking about venues; I come from, you mentioned, a [BNI chapter] launch background. And I remember one of the things that I would always keep at the forefront is: is this place accessible? Here in the States, we have, ADA, which is the American [with] Disabilities Act compliance. You know, is there a ramp or an elevator if there are steps. Is it easy to access for just anyone who might be – sometimes people have surgery and they are unable to be fully mobile. So thinking about that in mind, making sure that things are bright, you know, as well, again, the lighting is bright, making sure that it’s easy for people to get to, even getting rides as well, you know, being able to make sure that people have  accessibility in rides, if they’re not able to drive. Always remember to speak clearly, concisely. And then also keep in mind, of course, without bias or microaggressions. And then one big, big thing that we often see with those who are not able bodied, they’re often infanti[li]zed. So assuming that somebody’s ability or inability based on a disability that they may have. So keeping those things in mind when you’re at the meetings, but again, accessibility is definitely key.

Ivan:
Well, this is great information, Claudia. We can all learn from this and make our organization accessible to all businesspeople. And so I really appreciate you being on BNI Podcast again. Thank you.

Claudia:
Absolutely. Thank you.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

Okay, that’s perfect. Thank you so much for this great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Claudia Thompson from the BNI New York CORE region explains how BNI chapters can better accommodate members with disabilities.

title.rendered: Episode 837: Sponsoring 52 Members in 3½ Years

content.rendered:
Synopsis

“Visitor Queen” Christine Cheng from BNI’s Founding Region explains how she invited more than 200 visitors and sponsored 52 members into BNI–and you can, too.

  • When your chapter is looking to fill a space for a particular profession, post “In search of a <profession> that covers <location> and can take on referrals” to your social networks. Follow up with the recommendations and invite them to your BNI meeting.
  • Repeat the process in your Facebook and other groups. If you don’t get results in the groups you already belong to, look for groups for people in that profession.
  • Set your LinkedIn and other profiles to notify your network when you update your status. Invite people who respond to your BNI meeting.
  • Instead of deleting the marketing emails and LinkedIn messages from people who want to set up a sales meeting, invite them to your BNI meeting.
  • Use a script (see below) to make this process easy.

This process works both for chapters that meet exclusively in person and those that meet on Zoom.

Watch Christine’s 2022 BNI National Conference Presentation, “The Secret to Inviting 200 Visitors and Converting Them to BNI Members”.

Complete Transcript of Episode 837

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Southern California this week. My eldest daughter has got a little procedure and I want to be there and I’m going to see my grandson again. So I have a chance to see my grandson.

Priscilla:
Oh, how nice. Great.

Ivan:
I have a visitor this week. Her name is Christine Cheng, and she is a multiple laundromat owner, a realtor a marathoner, a half Ironman, a BNI Triple Diamond sponsor, a BNI Director Consultant of five years, and in three and a half years –  she’s been a member for, I believe, three and a half years and she was a presenter at the 2022 BNI [USA] National Conference and her topic was 200 visitors in one year. She has sponsored 52 members in three and a half years. Christine, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Christine:
Thank you, Dr. Misner.

Ivan:
Call me Ivan, Christine; it’s great to have you on. You’re known as the inviting queen, Visitor Queen, of the Founding Region, you’re in the very first region of BNI. And within three years, you invited hundreds of visitors to your chapter. And to date you’ve sponsored 52 members. Okay, how do you do that? I know members are out there going, “Are you kidding me? That’s not possible.” It’s possible. How do you do it?

Christine:
It is possible and it’s just like real estate – it’s a numbers game. The more people you reach out to, the more chances you will get a listing. My goal is to be in the elite club by the end of 2023, which is sponsoring two more members to BNI by the end of 2023. And is it doable? Of course it is.

Ivan:
That is amazing to me. Absolutely incredible. So, share with us the top three ways you invite visitors to your chapter meetings. I know there’s social media, general social media, LinkedIn, and email, texts, etc. So talk about those.

Christine:
So what I do is I create a post of a profession that you would want to fill in your chapter. I will use realtor as my example. In search of a realtor that covers Eastvale, California and can take on referrals. Notice I didn’t mention BNI or networking groups. I am asking for recommendations because everyone wants to help a friend out. I also share this post on different real estate groups I belong to. Now once you see your friends tagging their realtor friends, you message the realtor directly with the script that I created. “Hi, John, are you a realtor? And do you cover Eastvale, California?” Wait til they respond, and they will. They will respond ‘Yes.’ Then you say, “I belong to a chapter of business professionals. And we need a realtor that covers Eastvale to connect with, build relationships and trust with, and eventually refer business to. Are you available this Wednesday at 11:30am for a Zoom call?” and wait for them to respond. If they say, “Can you give me more info?” Well, we can respond with, “We are a group of business professionals. One loan officer, one business attorney, one escrow officer, one P&C insurance, one life insurance agent. Basically, one seat per profession, there’s no competition. The goal is to refer business to each other.” And on a side note, remember to include one profession that can refer business to them.

Now when they say, “Yes, I’m available.” You respond with, “Great. Please provide your email address, your cell phone, and a company name. I’ll register you for the Zoom link.” And you register them immediately. Once you’re done, you go back and respond “You’re all set. You should have received an email from BNI. No need to do anything. A member of my visitor host team will be calling or emailing you the day before to remind you. I’m looking forward to meeting you.” That’s it – simple and easy. Don’t overthink it, no need to vet them. The goal is to get them to visit the chapter. Now once they attend the meeting, BNI will sell itself, if they see the value. Now come the day before the meeting, I call each and every visitor to remind them of the meeting and to make sure they have the Zoom link. Whether they have the link or not, I text it to them, and with my business card. Many have said that because I call them, it shows that I care. Another way to do this is to once again think of a profession you want to fill in your chapter, or profession that you want to invite to hear the featured speaker. Now on Facebook, search that profession in the Groups page; I’ll use photographers as my example. Basically, my goal is to connect with all types of photographers in Southern California. Once you’re approved to be in the group, scroll through the posts. Some are real estate photographers, some are newborn, and some are maternity photographers. Some are wedding photographers, we can use them all in our chapter.

Another group to join is something you have in common with. For example, if you’re a mom, there’s so many mom groups out there. I belong to L.A. Mommies, Pasadena Mommies, and city groups like Chino Connects. So choose groups where members can get support from each other. Once you’re in the group scroll through the posts. They ask for all sorts of professions – event coordinator, realtors covering certain areas, termite company, property managers; perfect opportunity to message them and start the conversation. So the next time you’re scrolling Facebook, be on the lookout for professions you can invite to the weekly chapter meetings.


Ivan:
Listen, that’s really thorough. Is your group an online group exclusively? Or is it hybrid?

Christine:
It’s an online group exclusively.

Ivan:
So for those chapters, we still have majority of our chapters… about 32% of our organizations, at this moment, is hybrid – online and in-person, and completely online. 16% completely online, 16% hybrid. So a lot are meeting in-person. How would you change what you just described for an in-person group? Any thoughts? I’m throwing this at you as a surprise question, any changes?

Christine:
No changes whatsoever. It’s basically the same thing.

Ivan:
You just invite them to the morning meeting, as opposed to an online Zoom link?

Christine:
Correct.

Ivan:
Okay. All right. Well, that sounds pretty solid. And from this approach, you’ve gotten 52 members. And as I understand, it’s 52 members in your chapter and a couple of other chapters. Is that correct? Or is it just yours?

Christine:
That is correct. Other chapters, too.

Ivan: 
Other chapters, too. Wow, that’s really impressive. So, now I know you have some thoughts on LinkedIn as well. Talk about your LinkedIn approach.

Christine:
For LinkedIn, if you change your role to your role in the chapter, for example, Director, Consultant, President, Vice President – once you change that role, your contacts will get that notification, and they’re going to comment or message you. And you just start with the script again. It’s the same way, it works perfectly fine. And I’ve done that, and it works.

Ivan:
Interesting. So you just change your role, or I would assume you can add to your existing role, and get the same thing.

Christine:
Yes.

Ivan:
And all of your contacts will be messaged. I believe you get the option of saying, “Yeah let everyone know or don’t let everyone know,” is that correct?

Christine:
That’s correct.

Ivan:
So you want to say let everyone know. So then they see that and some people will reach out to you.

Christine:
Yes.

Ivan:
What an interesting idea. I really like that. I’ve never… let’s see, I’ve been doing this 39 years, I keep thinking I’ve heard it all, I haven’t heard that. That’s a great concept. Alright, so now, you’ve got the visitor registration. Give me some more. Who gets the texts and emails?

Christine:
If you receive a text or an email or a phone call from people soliciting their products and services.

Ivan:
Got it, yes.

Christine:
Now, before I would say, “I don’t want to waste your time, but I’m not interested.” And I hang up the phone, or I delete the email because I don’t have time for that. I already have a termite company I use. Now how about emails from companies around the holidays like Christmas and New Years? Rather than deleting them, respond back with the scripts. These are great opportunities to invite them to your weekly chapter meetings. And even if they don’t apply statistics show that an average visitor passes $1,500 to $2,000 in Thank You For Closed Business to BNI members.

Ivan:
Okay, so somebody that reaches out to you by email, and they’re basically spamming you or LinkedIn. I’m very active on LinkedIn, but I get spammed a lot on LinkedIn, you’re suggesting, you basically respond back to them with the script that you just gave earlier?

Christine:
That’s correct.

Ivan:
Okay, so I’ve seen variations of that and 100% – they work. Every person who has told me they take cold – it used to be cold call, somebody would call in, and I remember one guy saying that he told his Secretary to put every cold call through. He took them and he would say, “I’ll meet you next Wednesday at such and such a time.” You know, whatever time that meeting is, sometimes most of the time they are in the morning, but whatever time the meeting is, and they, you know, you’d say bring 30 or 40 business cards, and then people would show up. So use that approach or the script, which you just described. I think that’s brilliant.

Christine:
Yes. And when they try to sell me, I’ll say, why don’t you come to the meeting and market to 20+ members in my chapter? This way you can [reach more people].

Ivan:
Yeah, I love it. Any last comment before we wrap up?

Christine:
Yes, I was the one of the presenters at the 2022 [BNI USA] National Conference, and I created a video and I’d like to share it in the transcript.

Ivan:
Yeah, you can get it on the drive, a Google Doc. We’re gonna put a link to it here in the transcript. So just look for it. It’s a drive.google.com folder. And is it the entire presentation, Christine?
Link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1jgy9OPWb0FaGg2xDOe5l-dN-sSzVe1eL

Christine:
Yes, it is. It’s only 12 minutes.

Ivan:
Okay. So it’s a short presentation. Check it out. Christine, I am really, truly impressed. This is one of the one of the greatest quantity of members in three and a half years that I’ve ever seen. So, well done.

Christine:
Thank you.

Ivan:
Thank you for being on BNI Podcast. I appreciate you. Thank you. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, that is some great information, I have to say.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
