title.rendered: Episode 836: The Strength of Your Anchor (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This is a rebroadcast of Episode 122, first published on September 16, 2009. Links and offers may no longer work.

It’s not the anchor that holds a ship in place during a storm, but the length of chain connecting it. The captain watches the first mate to see where he needs to lay the chain to counter the winds.

This is a metaphor for BNI. How many links are there in your chain of relationships? Can you extend your chain in times when the economic winds are blowing against you? Have a one-to-one meeting with everyone in your chapter at least once a year.

Your BNI director is like the first mate on the ship, watching to see which way the wind blows and signaling which adjustments need to be made for the welfare of the group.

What are you doing to lengthen and strengthen your chain? Leave your comments here.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 836 (122)

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I am joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. Where are you and how are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great, Priscilla. This week I am in New Hampshire and Maine visiting BNI groups and doing presentations. There are some great groups up here, and I am really pleased to be here.

Priscilla:
Great. Well, what do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
Today I have a topic that I have written a little bit about; it is called “The Strength of the Anchor.” This summer my family took a several-day small ship tour of the Great Barrier Reef. The first night we noticed the anchor being used to secure the ship in the middle of the Coral Sea was really small compared to the size of the ship. It was a pretty decent sized ship, and the anchor was pretty small.

The second night we were anchored off of Hope Island in the Great Barrier Reef, and some really strong winds came up and our captain started the engines, and he backed the ship up, letting out more length of chain to the anchor. I am really curious, maybe a little bit concerned, so I asked him, “How is it possible that such a small anchor will hold the ship in place when the wind is blowing against it so strongly?”

And he said, “It is the chain that is holding the ship, not the anchor.”

After the anchor is lowered, the captain looks to the first mate, who signals from the prow which direction the chain is laying on the bottom of the sea. The captain then maneuvers into the right position and lets out the necessary amount of chain to hold the ship in a particular place and at particular time. This night, with the winds growing stronger, he recognized that he needed to let out more chain, and I began to see how this dynamic was really relevant to BNI.

Our anchor is the system, the process of doing business the BNI way. But it is not the system or the process that has the strength at all, or not completely. Clearly, it is a powerful process. It is the length of the chain holding the chapter, or the ship in the metaphor, in place that makes it all come together.

If you take a look at your chapter, members that are listening to this podcast, think about the links or relationships you have formed with the individual members in your group. How many links does your chain have? Do you have strong relationships with all the other members in the chapter, or are you closely linked with some but disconnected and detached from others in your group, for whatever reason? How do you go about letting out more chains during times when the economic winds have strengthened against our business?

I submit that it is time to get serious about developing strong relationships with each and every member in your chapter, even the ones you don’t think you have contact with that might run a business that is not exactly symbiotic with yours.

We talk about forming power teams with those businesses which are closely related to yours, but what you do about those members whose businesses are totally out of sync with yours or members who seem to be unable to provide quality referrals to you? Have you done one-to-ones with those members? Spending time to do the one-to-ones with each and every member of your chapter helps you to develop a longer and stronger chain. Each person in your chapter is one of the links that lengthens and strengthens that chain. The wisdom of laying down a longer chain to strengthen the ability of the anchor to hold strong is critical for the success of your chapter.

Another aspect of this anchoring process is watching the first mate. I thought that was really interesting. Your BNI director is trained and qualified to signal your chapter of the direction the chain is lying as the dynamic in your chapter changes. This is really important. The first mate signals where the change is going for the captain, and without him, the captain would really, really struggle. That is your BNI director; they can signal the direction of your chapter and help you if you take their advice.

The chapter president is basically the captain of the chapter, the ship in this metaphor. He or she watches the director for guidance and what adjustments to make to ensure the chapter is pointed in the right direction. At one point in the anchoring process, you see the first mate literally, literally dove into the water…

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
…to loosen the chain where it had become hooked on some coral formation. Your director cares about your chapter. As we travel around the world meeting with BNI directors and conducting trainings and conferences, we’re both gratified to see the heart of our BNI directors and our presidents and leadership team people and chapter members. And we encourage chapter members to keep their eyes, particularly the chapter president to keep their eyes on the director as he or she signals which adjustments need to be made so that the system works well for the BNI group.

I love this metaphor, and I wanted to share it with BNI members today.

Priscilla:
It is interesting, especially the idea that the chain, rather than the anchor, being the most important part, which is just counter intuitive.

Ivan:
I had no idea, no idea at all. I guess when winds really pick up is when they need to lay more chain down, because when I was on the ship the first night, there was not much wind, and it was no big deal. As the winds really kicked in, they laid more chain down, and they said that is what really helped to grip the bottom. I am listening to that and thinking about BNI, and I put these two things together. I talked about it with my wife, Beth, who helped me to create this visualization. I think it is very relevant to BNI, and I would love education coordinators to share it at BNI meetings.

Priscilla:
I just want to make one more comment in terms of the actual group. If you have been in a group for a while, as I have, and you develop a certain number of relationships with people and then you don’t renew them by doing one-on-ones in a fresh way, or more often than you would have thought to do them, I think you put yourself at a disadvantage.

Ivan:
Definitely. I think you should probably do a one-to-one with those people, certainly all new people, and all of the people that you want to maintain a strong relationship with, no less than once a year. Most people say, “Well, it’s not that much.” But you have a chapter of 30 members, that can be a lot of one-to-ones. And I am saying that is a minimum not a maximum; that is a minimum. You have to meet with those people if you want to create those relationships, if you want to maintain those relationships with them.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
No less than once a year for one-to-ones.

Priscilla:
Do you think a party counts?!

Ivan:
No, I don’t think that is a good one-to-one! But that is a good way of staying in touch with people, absolutely.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, do you have anything else you would like to share?

Ivan:
No, that is everything today, and I would love for those of you who listened to this podcast to leave a message, leave a note what you think. Particularly if you take this back to your chapter, I would love to hear what your chapter thought.

Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Great. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

Well I just want remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you’ll join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: It’s not the anchor that holds a ship in place, but the length of the chain. Extend your chain of relationships through one-to-ones.

title.rendered: Episode 835: A Radical Shift to Greater Success

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The subtitle of Dr. Misner’s new book, The 3rd Paradigm, is “A Radical Shift to Greater Success.” Moving from the first paradigm, competition, through the second paradigm, cooperation, and into the third paradigm, co-creation, can transform a project from failure to radical success.

This was the case for “Richard,” whose story appears in the book. He attempted to build an online platform based on the first paradigm, and failed. He tried again using the second paradigm, and failed again. But when he invoked the third paradigm and co-created the platform with a team, he succeeded beyond all expectaion.

What the book doesn’t tell you is that “Richard” is Ivan Misner and the online platform is BNI Connect™.

If you haven’t read The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success yet, get your copy today.

Complete Transcript of Episode 835

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I… don’t tell anybody, okay? I’m on vacation in Spain this week.

Priscilla:
Oh, wow.

Ivan:
So this is a secret. We can’t share it.

Priscilla:
Okay, my lips are sealed.

Ivan:
Alright, good. Alright, don’t tell anybody. Yeah, we just had the Madrid [BNI] Convention, which is… the international conventions are always amazing. But this week, I’m taking some time off with my fiancé, in Spain.

Priscilla:
Sounds wonderful.

Ivan:
Yeah. So today, we’re going to talk about some content from a new book that came out last month. The book is called “The Third Paradigm.” And the subtitle is: A Radical Shift to Greater Success. And so I’m going to talk about some material from the first chapter, it kind of outlines what the third paradigm is. And then in some future podcasts, I’m going to talk more about this content, because I think this is really powerful content for businesspeople. In some ways, it’s about networking. But in many ways, it’s really about building a successful business if you have a team of people that you’re working with.

So let’s just jump in and talk about the three paradigms. And it begins with this idea that negativity is sort of the norm in business. And but we, the co-authors of this book, believe that there’s hope, that there’s an answer; and it starts with focusing on solutions. You’ve heard me say this a lot. When people focus on problems, they become world class experts on the problem, but when they focus on solutions, they can become world class experts on the solution. And we believe the solution is focusing on a concept called co-creation. So the first paradigm of business is the era of competition. And this paradigm was formulated within the framework of the laws of production by early pioneers of business thinking of people like Frederick Taylor, the Gilbreths, Max Weber, and others who focused on workflow, economic efficiency, labor productivity, basically, scientific management.

And the first paradigm was so focused on productivity and competition that it failed to account for the needs of people in the workforce, and competition was about gaining something by defeating your rivals or establishing superiority over them. And by the way, the first paradigm is still alive in many ways in business, but we’ve gone beyond that. The first paradigm is about having a winner and a loser, and the impact that this had on individuals was generally minimized.

The second paradigm is the era of cooperation. And this paradigm evolved over time as people learned that a strictly production-oriented approach didn’t take into account the interaction of the people involved in the process. I know that sounds crazy today in the 2020s. But the truth is that prior to the second paradigm, people just weren’t that important in the business role. The focus on acting together for sort of a common purpose started to evolve in the 1960s. And around that time, MIT management Professor Douglas McGregor published his work called The Human Side of Enterprise, which introduced the concepts of Theory X and Theory Y. I know I’m getting a little technical, but this is really important. And this is some of the work that I did in my doctoral degree.

And his framework really highlights the motivating role of job satisfaction and argues that people can do their work without constant direct supervision and that people are important in the process. Which again, today we look at it and go, “Oh, yeah, isn’t it obvious people are important and job satisfaction is critical?” But back then, it was considered anything but mainstream. I studied under Warren Bennis at the University of Southern California, who I’ve talked about a few times in the podcast, and he was a colleague of McGregor at the MIT School of Management when this came out – this theory X Theory Y, and they would get bomb threats. Seriously, he told me personally, they would get bomb threats because this was such a crazy idea that people didn’t like this concept that people were important. It’s really about the business or the productivity. And I know it’s crazy, but that was the past.

Obviously, today’s organizations are much more collaborative, the old command and control methods have largely been replaced by more openness and transparency than we saw during the first paradigm. The second paradigm is about cooperation. And we see that with things like the concept of MBO, your management by objective, where you brought people into the decision-making process.

That leads us to the third paradigm, which is what my book is about, and that’s where the radical shift to greater success is. And the third paradigm is about the era of co-creation. And this concept begins where cooperation leaves off. The difference between cooperation and co-creation is the difference between working together and creating together. You may collaborate on a project, but you co-create products and services and co-creation is a significant step beyond cooperation.

And I think most people would recognize the concept of co-creation with the term ‘crowdsourcing’. Crowdsourcing has become a critical tool for engagement. Waze, the navigation app used by millions of people, is a good example of this concept of crowdsourcing. And the widespread application of this digital technologies made customer empowerment a must. And crowdsourcing products and services such as Waze are used by millions of people worldwide. People tend to genuinely care about what they create.

And the reason that I did this book, The Third Paradigm, is that throughout the book, we talk about the many different companies that actively employ co-creation, BNI being one of them. And we include interviews and surveys that we’ve done with people who’ve experienced the co-creative process. And we have some really hard data about the main benefits, and drawbacks – they’re both important, the benefits of co-creation and the drawbacks of co-creation. We surveyed over 4200 people around the world. And many of the strategies incorporated in The Third Paradigm are based on those survey responses. And then in addition, we’ve integrated our experience and our theories, our philosophies, along with various interviews that I mentioned, and we’ve integrated this all into the book. And one of the most interesting pieces of it, running throughout the book, is a story based on a true experience. Now, here’s another secret, Priscilla, and you can’t share it with anyone, alright?

Priscilla:
Absolutely, I won’t.

Ivan:
The story in the book that we talk about, is an online platform being created by a gentleman by the name of Richard.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh

Ivan:
It’s a true story. And we tell the story that Richard had in the creation of this online platform and how it started with competition, and why it was competitive. And how he realized that that was a mistake, and then evolved into cooperation and how that was a failure. And how he finally came to the point of recognizing that this had to be done through co-creation, that that people had to work together to actually create it together. And it’s a true story. And in the last chapter of the book, well, first of all, that story is integrated into every chapter. It’s interwoven into every chapter. So we’ve got, you know, these ideas on what co-creation is, we’ve got the data about what works, what doesn’t work in co-creation. And for those of you listening to this, if you have teams, you want to create co-creative teams, and it has the data as to what to do that will make it work and what won’t work. And I’ll talk about some of the data in future podcasts. And then of course, the story is interwoven throughout and at the end, a very unique concept with a book, we do… Priscilla, instead of a summary chapter, we have the publisher interview us.

Priscilla:
Really?

Ivan:
Yeah. And it’s an interview, that’s the summary chapter. And the publisher asks a series of questions.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh.

Ivan:
And one of the questions that was asked was, was the story of Richard a true story? And one of my co-authors said, “Yes, it is a true story.” And the other co-author said, It is a true story, and it happens to be a true story about one of the co-authors.”

Priscilla:
Hmmm.

Ivan:
But we don’t say who that co-author is. And we don’t talk about that until the very, very end of the book. So here’s the secret part, okay? And this is the part that just got to stay between us.

Priscilla: (laughing)
Okay.

Ivan:
The online platform was BNI Connect.

Priscilla:
Ohhh.

Ivan:
Richard is my middle name.

Priscilla:
Oh.

Ivan:
So although we never really, we do talk about BNI in the book, of course. I wouldn’t do a book that doesn’t talk about BNI to some extent. BNI has been a co-creative organization from the beginning, starting with the Board of Advisors of BNI, in 1986. So co-creation has been an important part of our process. But we don’t say in the book that the story is about us, about me and BNI, because we want people to read the story for what it is, to not just look at and say, “Guys, it’s just a story about one of the co-authors.” And so I’m giving you, I’m giving you sort of a behind-the-scenes look of something that we don’t completely say in the book. And so for those of you that are at all interested in this concept of co-creation, pick up a copy of the book, and you can read it knowing the truth of what you’re reading – the full story of what you’re reading.

Priscilla:
So you don’t divulge that it’s actually you in the book at all?

Ivan:
No, not at all. We just say it’s one of the co-authors.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh, okay.

Ivan:
Well, that’s it for today. There’ll be a link to the book here in this podcast. And I would love to have people take a look at the book. It’s a powerful book that will help you create better teams in your organization. Take a look at it and recommend it to others if you like it. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you so much for all the great, and secret, information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Moving from the 1st paradigm, competition, into the 3rd paradigm, co-creation, can transform a project from failure to radical success.

title.rendered: Episode 834: Identify Your Most Profitable Client

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Mike Adray from BNI Founding Region joins the podcast this week to explain how to use the One-to-One Referral Source Worksheet to identify your most profitable clients so you can revise your weekly presentation to get better referrals and increase your TYCFB.

The key to making this process work is having another member interview you and ask the question. If you try to fill it out yourself, you won’t find the referrals you aren’t asking for or the conversation starters to begin the referral process.

Note: This is not a worksheet to use during your first One-to-One with someone. It’s for members who have been through the GAINS Exchange and developed a relationship.

Download the Worksheets

  • Referral Source Worksheet (Excel)
  • Referral Source Worksheet (Fillable PDF with instructions)

Complete Transcript of Episode 834

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Madrid, Spain this week for the BNI International Convention. Priscilla, I love going to international conventions for BNI because it’s like going to a United Nations event where everybody likes each other.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
It’s incredible. There are several thousand people here. And I just love coming to them. We’ve been doing conventions since 1990. So it’s exciting.

Priscilla:
I got to attend one once and I loved it, too. I felt just like you do right now.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s really good. So, you know, we’re talking about how many years we’ve been doing conventions. And my guest today is from the Founding region. He is the [BNI] Executive Director from the Founders Region. I love the fact that they named the original region the Founders Region. His name is Mike Adray and Mike joined BNI as a member in 2010 after being invited to a start-up meeting by a complete stranger, which I think is funny. He planned to use BNI to market his handyman business, and after taking all the advanced training courses offered in his region, Mike was able to stop all other forms of advertising, because BNI accounted for almost all of his new business. In 2012, he joined the regional team as a Director Consultant, supporting four chapters. And that’s where he really discovered his passion for BNI. And today, Mike is living that passion as the Executive Director for BNI’s Founding Region, the region that I started BNI in, and he supports over 375 members in this region. Mike Adray, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Mike:
Thank you, Ivan. I’m really looking forward to speak with you today.

Ivan:
Well, it’s great to have you on and I love the topic, Identify Your Most Profitable Client. And it came as a result of a conversation that we had with a fellow member of BNI. She’s been on this podcast in the past, and it was that conversation that led to this podcast. So it really is, you know, in a way it’s about VCP –visibility, credibility profitability, but once you’ve achieved that credibility, what do you do next to get to that profitability in terms of working with your fellow BNI Members?

Mike:
You know, that was the exact question that came up between this member and I. She had been in BNI for I think 15 years at that point in time.

Ivan:
Yeah, the member, by the way, is Christine Bond. And Christine has been on BNI Podcast before.

Mike:
Absolutely, yes. So Christine and I had a one-to-one. And she had been [a member] for 15 years, been a Director for many years, [had] a very successful massage therapy business in BNI. And we were just talking about our business and how she could maybe take it to another level. And she says, “Gosh, I don’t know if I could do that, because I’m really doing well.”

And so I said, “Well, let me use this worksheet. It’s called the one-to-one referral source worksheet that was introduced to me by a Director from my past, Gary Reeves out of the SoCal BNI Region, and this opened up my eyes to what we might be leaving on the table as far as asking for our most profitable clients or customers. And so I took Christine through this worksheet and asked her all the questions on it, and really honed in on who was her most profitable client.

And it took a little bit of work to get her there, but once we identified her most profitable client, and then answered the rest of the questions on the worksheet, she actually changed her weekly presentations and stopped asking for people who want massages because she was already getting those kinds of referrals. So instead, we crafted a new ask for the very specific type of client that she wanted, which was a couples massage. Professional couples, you know, looking for some greater self-care and she had some of those clients and they absolutely were the most profitable. So I asked her, “Why don’t you just ask for more of those kinds of clients?”

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mike:
And so she did. And when she did, her thank you for closed business went well over her goal of $100,000 a year and actually got up to about $150,000 a year from BNI referrals by themselves.

Ivan:
Mmm, that’s impressive. It’s very impressive. And Christine is an amazing massage therapist. I’ve known her for years, and she was the massage therapist for my late wife, Elisabeth, who said she was amazing. So it’s good advice that you gave her to help build her own referral business.

Mike:
Yeah, and the worksheet was the key to it, because it really hones us in on the questions. We ask the member… and by the way the worksheet has to be, it can’t be filled out by yourself; somebody has to interview you. Because if you fill it out for yourself, you’re just going to put down what you know. And what will be missing is what you don’t know.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s good to have a conversation with the person. Now, these worksheets, and the instructions are going to be included in this podcast. So look for the PDF that is included with this podcast, and I’ll have the instructions and the worksheet with it.

Mike:
Yeah, and the real important thing to remember too, with this worksheet, is this is not where you start your relationship with somebody in doing one-to-ones. You absolutely need to complete the GAINS profile and work through your one-to-ones using the GAINS profile to really build a basis for a strong relationship. And then you’re able to have these more in-depth conversations and ask these types of probing questions that will come from the worksheet.

Ivan:
Yeah. Now, most people haven’t sat down to figure out what clients are their most profitable, and this is an area that you can get really specific about in your asks. Can you give any other examples, other than the one with Christine, that you have seen that have worked effectively?

Mike:
Um, yeah. So I’ve worked with members that, for example, that do website design. And, you know, typically, when you hear a member who does website design, they’ll say, “Oh, I can make websites for anyone.” And you know, I would ask that member, “Well, okay, yes, you can design a website for anyone, but I’m guessing that there’s those websites that you like to design, that you love to design, that probably are more profitable than others.” And they, of course, they said, “Well, yeah.” I said, “Great. Tell me about that client, who is that client? What do they do? What’s their industry? What makes that website design more profitable for you?” And so we kind of then reverse-engineer it, so we can create more of those same kind of clients by using the questions on the worksheet. And then the real important things on the worksheet are to tell us what to look and listen for. And then the number one thing I see members leaving off their conversation, once I tell you what to look and listen for, I need to give you a conversation starter so that you can lead to the referral. And that’s where most members fail to train their referral partners is that question that leads to the referral.

Ivan:
Talk about that some more. What’s that look like?

Mike:
So it’s a ‘how’ question, typically. It has to be an open-ended question. So if I just say, “Hey, I noticed your website isn’t looking that great. Would you want to meet somebody that could make it better?” That will turn most people off.

Ivan:
Mm-hmm, yeah.

Mike:
Right, because I’m already in the sales mode, and people shut down when you start selling to them. But if I come up with a question, that’s more like, maybe they’ve complained about business isn’t that great or they’d like it to be better, then I could ask a question like, “So tell me how that is affecting your overall business? Like, how is your website experience affecting your business?” So I want them to tell me a story. I want them to paint the picture. I often say think about it like you’re lying on a therapist’s couch, tell me how that makes you feel. And when they do that, then you can say, after they’ve shared all of the pain, the suffering, the grief, whatever it is that they’re experiencing, all you have to say is, “Oh, my gosh, if I knew somebody that can help you with that, would you want to meet them?” And now I can tee up the referral.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s a great segue. So I’m looking at your instructions for the one-to-one referral source worksheet, and the actual worksheet, and you know, from my perspective, this is not BNI 101. This is like BNI 401 or 501; this is more advanced. And I think that’s important. Because, you know, especially people who have been in BNI for a while, they’re looking for things that are more advanced that will take them to the next level. First, a two-part question real quick, because we’re out of almost out of time. Would you agree with that assessment?

Mike:
Absolutely. 100%.

Ivan:
And second, last question for you, is there anything else about the instructions and the worksheet that you would like to share before we wrap up?

Mike:
Absolutely. So once you get through the worksheet, and you’ve identified a ton of referral source partners, you have to complete the homework, which is the spreadsheet and there’s an example on the last page. And if you complete the spreadsheet, and you do it well, you can likely have at least a year’s worth of weekly presentations that will be different, unique, and train your members over time for your ideal referral partners.

Ivan:
Is that spreadsheet… was it a Word file or an Excel file?

Mike:
It’s a PDF example. But I set it up in an Excel file.

Ivan:
Send me the Excel file. If you don’t mind, and we’ll post the Excel file, so people can add to it.

Mike:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Sounds good. Mike, any last thing before I turn it over to Priscilla?


Mike:
Yeah, I just want to say thank you to you, Ivan. It’s been an amazing journey so far with me with BNI. I’m really excited about what we’re doing in the Founding Region. And I just can’t wait to see what great things we are all going to accomplish together in BNI.

Ivan:
Well, Mike, I really appreciate you being on BNI podcast and I thank you for everything you’re doing in the Founding Region of BNI.

Mike:
Of course, thank you.

Ivan:
Yeah. Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you both so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Mike Adray explains how to use the One-to-One Referral Source Worksheet to identify your most profitable clients and get better referrals.

title.rendered: Episode 833: Networking in the Digital Age

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Sean Fernandes from Scion Social joins Dr. Misner to explain how digital marketing, the hungry content algorithm, and AI have–and haven’t–changed networking.

  • It’s more important than ever to stand out and have a tangible brand.
  • When technology replaces proximity, we need to do more to create closeness and intimacy.
  • You need to articulate your vision and attract people who align to it.
  • Thought leadership and education replace advertising and selling.
  • Technology changes, but the fundamentals of networking don’t.
  • The rise of AI highlights the need for personal and physical connection.

Learn more about Sean and the work Scion Social has done for BNI at https://scion-social.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 833

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I just got back from a regional event in Charlotte, North Carolina at BNI headquarters. And I love… this is a great podcast because you know, I’m talking to you; I just came back from Charlotte, I’m talking to you from Austin. You’re, of course, in California, and we’re talking to my guest, Sean Fernandes, who’s in India. Isn’t it one of the amazing things about technology today, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Yeah, incredible. So what is Sean going to share with us?

Ivan:
Well, Sean is going to talk about networking in the digital age. I’ve known Sean for about eight years. He possesses extensive expertise in digital marketing. He’s got more than a decade of hands-on experience and technical knowledge. He’s collaborated with diverse businesses worldwide from startups to corporate giants, including Microsoft to Harmon, 3M, and of course, BNI. And as the managing director of Scion Social, which is a premier global digital marketing agency, Sean has guided the team to numerous accolades, including the distinguished MENAA Customer Service Award. His well-rounded skills and achievements underscore his commitment to driving excellence and innovation in the digital realm. And Scion handles my marketing. They are, of course, involved in BNI. And they handle my social media marketing. Sean, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Sean:
Thank you, Ivan. Thank you so much. It’s an honor to be here. And that’s quite an introduction. Thank you.

Ivan:
Well, listen, you deserve it. Let me ask you a few questions, because these are some important questions for BNI members today. With all the shifts in digital marketing, and particularly AI, what changes in networking, and what remains the same, in your opinion?

Sean:
Right, thanks for that question. So, you know, really where we need to start on the first thing that’s important that underscores all of this, is that there’s just more content, and really in many ways, more noise than there ever has been before in the digital world, in social media. You could scroll all day on any platform and never get to the end of it, and then never see where you left off the last time. You know, the web and algorithms are hungry for content.

And of course, content creators reply with producing tremendous amounts of content. And of course, AI has accelerated this cycle. With Chat GPT, with copy.ai, with generative imagery, we can produce content faster than ever, to sort of feed the algorithm.

But with that, I think the need to stand out and to have a tangible brand is more important than ever, you know, to not get lost in the static. And I think, you know, with that comes in the importance of authentic connection. And, you know, really connecting with people that relate to you where there’s a kindred spirit. Especially where technology is replacing proximity, we kind of have to go the extra mile to create that closeness and create that intimacy.

Ivan:
Well, let me ask you a follow up question. How, briefly, how do you do that?

Sean:
So I think, you know, if we go back through the last few years, in the last few years, if they’ve taught us anything, it’s that, you know, the closeness of real relationships, and the people that matter in our lives are more important than anything else. It really puts a lot in perspective.

And, you know, when it comes to connecting with new people, and sort of reaching out and sort of networking in the digital space, I think the center of it is having a vision and being able to articulate that vision, you know, which then attracts other people who align with it. You know, it’s a shift from advertising and just sort of broadcasting a message to more of thought leadership and being your authentic self, and then, you know, attracting people who relate to that and who align with your vision.

Ivan:
You know, I really agree with the thought leadership. I think that’s the key in social media is to be a thought leader and to provide content where it draws people to you, which of course helps build the brand, which is what you’re talking about.

Sean:
Right. Absolutely.

Ivan:
You certainly help me do that in my social media. I think when you and I started working together, I think I had somewhere around 30,000 or 40,000 followers on Facebook and Twitter.

Sean:
Yes.

Ivan:
And what is it, 237,000 or 238,000 followers now?

Sean:
Yeah.

Ivan:
So it’s been good. It’s been a good experience. And it’s about really being authentic and providing thought leadership and educational content, not just selling to people.

Sean:
Right. Absolutely. And, you know, you’ll know from everything that we’ve done together, how as times have changed, and technology has changed, and trends have changed, we’ve had to adapt our strategies, adapt our tactics to align with that. The frequency we post at, the kinds of things we post, but what always remains the same is that there is a core message behind it. And that, you know, we go back to BNI is visibility, credibility, profitability – you always have to be visible, you know, if you’re not visible, nothing else is gonna matter. But behind that, if there’s no credibility, and there’s nothing to back it up, you know, it’s going to be an empty vessel. And then, of course, you know, having the combination of those two together, is what creates profitability in the long run.

Ivan:
Yeah. So talk to me a little bit about the idea that understanding that technology, no matter how advanced, is still a tool, and that the fundamentals don’t change. I think you were kind of touching upon that just a moment ago.

Sean:
Right. Absolutely. So it’s kind of a funny analogy, but it’s one that I tell a lot when I’m asked about how sort of to think about AI and how it relates to our work. If your goal is to dig a hole, and you have a hand shovel, and someone pulls up with an excavator, they’re going to be able to dig that hole a lot faster than you and there’s no wonder about who’s going to be more effective at that. But nothing really changes, except that you’re just using a better and greater tool; you’re still accomplishing the same goal, you’re still the same person, but you’re using the best of what’s available to you to be effective. And I think a lot of people are afraid of technology, especially AI, because they feel that it might replace them or that there’s the ability for it to replace them in the long run. And I really don’t see it that way. I see it as a partner or an assistant or something that can help us, you know, do what we do much faster and much better. And then free up our time to do what really is more important for humans to do — things like creativity, things like thought leadership, right? You could give AI 30 years, and it’s not going to be a thought leader, because it can’t produce anything original and new.

Ivan:
Yeah, I have found technology to be a way of flattening the communication hierarchy. You know, it’s enabled me to talk to people in ways that, you know, 30 years ago, I could have never communicated with members worldwide. But I’m able to do so through technology today.

Sean:
Right. And you know, that starts with, you know, right now all the conversation is about AI, about machine learning, this sort of advanced technology. But if we just go back a couple of years and even think about video conferencing and Zoom, you know, that, as a technology, has enabled us to do so much more. We can [go] beyond, apart from physically we can be in more than one place at a time. I’ve seen people on two zoom calls at the same time attending meetings, you know, but even if it’s just one, spanning 1000s of miles simultaneously to be making business decisions that impact the world, it was an unheard of just a few years or a few decades ago.

Ivan:
Well, I think we got through COVID because of Zoom. I mean, imagine that COVID happened 30 years ago, there would have been a lot more people that would have either passed away or a lot more businesses that would have failed if we hadn’t had the technology that we have today.

Sean:
Right, absolutely. I was just talking today with a gentleman I met in Birmingham in the UK a couple of weeks ago. And he was saying that they have a recruitment business. And they only realized in the pandemic, how paper heavy their recruitment process and the way they ran their business was, and they were almost debilitated. And they had to adapt so quickly, to change, to be able to just have the business continuity during the pandemic. And then you kind of follow that up with the conversation about now, you know, rehiring and growing again, they’re hiring a lot of millennials. And at the same time… this was kind of happening at the same time they had this new generation coming in, and they looked at their process, like, what’s happening here? Why is there so much paper? Why is it not digitized? So it’s kind of an insight into how quickly things have shifted.

Ivan:
Well, we’re running out of time, but I have one more question for you. Do you think AI is considered a competitor or a threat when it comes to personal branding or marketing at its core?

Sean:
I don’t think so, not at all. In many ways, AI is great at a lot of things. Efficiency is certainly one of them. Authentic connection is probably pretty low down on that list of its capabilities and what it’s good at, and I think in many ways, AI kind of being on the rise is going to highlight the need for personal connection and for physical connection in many ways more than ever. And, you know, if you think about it like an assistant, right, if you know someone well, you kind of get past the assistant, you go straight to them, right? And I think that the relationship that we want to have with other people, the AI can be there and can do its thing, but we want it out of the way when we’re connecting person-to-person. So I think it’s important to use it for what it’s good for. I use it for scheduling all the time. I don’t reach out to people and say, “Hey, when are you free for a meeting?” You know, technology does that. But when it comes to being present and in the room, that’s all you.

Ivan:
Well, Sean, listen, I want to thank you for being on BNI Podcast with me today. For those of you listening, Sean’s website is www.scion-social.com. That’s Scion, S-C-I-O-N, social .com. And great content here today, Sean. I really appreciate you and I appreciate all the work you’ve done in building my social media for BNI.

Sean:
Absolutely. It’s been a pleasure being on here. Thank you, again, so much for having me. And likewise, it is an absolute pleasure to work with you. I appreciate our collaboration and everything we learn from you in the business of helping the world become better networkers.

Ivan:
Thank you, my friend. Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Okay, thank you both for such great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Sean Fernandes from Scion Social explains how digital marketing, the hungry content algorithm, and AI have–and haven’t–changed networking.

title.rendered: Episode 832: Membership Committees Are Critical (Classic)

content.rendered:
Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 663, first published on July 8, 2020. Some links and offers may have expired.

Synopsis

Do you know what your BNI chapter’s Membership Committee does and why it’s important? Dr. Misner shares some of the responsibilities of membership committees before he and Priscilla discuss what it’s like to serve on the membership committee.

From the early days of BNI, there were challenges at the chapter level that really needed to be handled by the chapter–not by Dr. Misner personally. Members of that chapter would always understand the situation better than anyone outside the committee would.

  • Report to the chapter about open categories and BNI policies
  • Provide Quality Assurance for the chapter
  • Review new membership applications and transfer applications
  • Manage classification categories
  • Coach members on their participation
  • Address complaints and disputes between members
  • Enforce attendance policy
  • Enforce non-discrimination policy
  • Enforce no-harassment policy

If you have a problem with another member, talk to that member before you talk to the Membership Committee. Always talk to people before you talk about them.

If you are on your chapter’s Membership Committee, remember to apply the rules more like Mandela than Attila.

Avoid petty disputes with fellow members. Remember that the purpose of BNI is to generate referrals and give each other grace.

Complete Transcript of Episode 663/832

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I love that you were able to ask where am I? Well, I mean, I’ve been holed up for months, many months. Just like everybody. Didn’t get away too far. So nobody needs to worry I’m not out and about traveling much. I headed to my condo in Galveston. So I’m on the ocean at my own condo in Galveston, but it was good to get on the road for a little bit. So thanks, and I hope in the not too distant future, you’ll able to ask me that question a whole lot more.

Priscilla:
Yeah, me too. Okay, so what do you have for us today?

Ivan:
So today, I want to talk about membership committees. And membership committees are really, really important. And in the early days of the organization, we found that there were challenges at the chapter level that I really felt needed to be handled by the members, and not by me, because you know, I mean, when BNI just had 20, 30, 40 chapters, there was a problem.

People kept coming to me and saying, what do we do with this guy? What do we do with this person? What do we do with this woman, trying to get in and that classification is, you know, being blocked by somebody? and they would come to me and that didn’t work. I mean, that didn’t work. First of all, it’s not scalable. But more importantly, we really felt from the very beginning that there had to be local responsibility. And because this is affecting a chapter, that chapter needs to be engaged in it. So we knew that the members had to be involved, and that they understood the situation probably better than I would.

So very, very early in the process, we saw the need to create a membership committee for each and every group. And the roles evolved over the years. And Priscilla, what I’d like to do is cover some of the things that the membership committee is responsible for. And then maybe we can chat about it a little bit if you’d like.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
So the regular membership committee report that’s done every week is only one of their responsibilities. And it doesn’t have to be done every week, but should be done regularly to report to the chapter, addressing things like the categories that are now open, various policies in the organization and why they’re important. By the way, that’s really important – whenever a membership committee talks about a policy, don’t just talk about the how, talk about the why. Why is this policy important? And if you’re on the membership committee and you don’t know, talk to your local director or talk to your executive director or ask questions of senior people, because they know why the policies were created. People don’t care about how until they understand why. So the membership committee should be talking about some of the whys, they should be talking about tips on giving referrals, and those kinds of things in their regular membership committee reports. But that’s just the beginning of what the membership committee does.

They are really responsible for the quality assurance for the chapter. They want to do their best to make sure that referrals that are given out are legitimate, and that the people are following up on referrals. Both of those are important and sometimes don’t happen. You want to make sure referrals are legitimate and people who get referrals, in fact, follow up on them.

If you’re having a problem, you know, I can almost hear members going, “Oh, yeah, I’ve had that problem membership committee didn’t do anything.” Well, alright. We’re dealing with a lot of people. And whenever you’re dealing with people, there are always challenges. So first and foremost, talk to the person that you’re having a challenge with. If you’ve gotten a referral that’s not legitimate or you know somebody that’s not following up, talk to the person directly. If that doesn’t solve the problem, then calmly, don’t go there in a rage, calmly go to the membership committee and let them know what’s going on and ask them what you can do to help them resolve this. So after you’ve talked to the individual, talk to the membership committee and give the membership committee time to address the issue.

We want to make sure that referrals that are given are legitimate. Every one doesn’t have to turn into a sale, but it has to be a legitimate referral. And we definitely want to make sure people follow up on the referrals. Remember one thing, don’t talk about people first – talk to people, and then go to the membership committee. Never go around the chapter talking about the person and what they did wrong. But the membership committee is okay to talk to, after you talk to them.

Other things that the membership committee does is review new member applications and approve membership requests. That’s an important one. They have to confirm the contact information, review details in the application, review the classification request, sometimes interviewing the candidate further. Sometimes they may talk to the sponsoring member. And of course, they may, you know, as I said, interview that candidate and like, these are some of the things that they do in reviewing applications. They also address general classification categories.

Take a look at the episode here on the BNI Podcast about classification cowboys, do a search on “cowboys”. And you can find out more about what they should be doing with classifications. They have to deal with transferring members into the group, they have to coach members as necessary regarding their participation, and a big one is addressing complaints and disputes between members, whatever they may be. That’s an important one.

See, there’s a lot that the membership committee has to do; I’m just giving you the highlights. They enforce the attendance policy, enforce the non discrimination policy of BNI. That’s very important, enforce and address the no harassment policy in BNI. These are important policies and you should ask your vice president to show you those statements, because we have very clear statements in each country, about the non harassment and non discrimination requirements in BNI. We want BNI to be diverse, and we say so in writing and have for many years.

So just sort of wrapping up here, members often see the tip of the iceberg in BNI, and there’s a lot that goes on behind the scenes to make BNI work in each group. And the membership committee is one of those really important elements. I know I’ve said this before: hockey without rules would be boxing on ice. BNI without rules would be a social club. The rules are important. But remember, membership committees, remember, apply the rules more like Mandela than Attila. Don’t be a fascist in the way that the rules are applied. Tough love, show people that you care, and make sure that members understand the why. And I wanted to do this podcast just to get everyone a sense of how incredibly important a membership committee is. It will absolutely make or break a chapter.

So I got a couple of closing comments, but Priscilla, I thought we might chat for a minute before I close. Have you ever experienced that the membership committee is very important in your chapter?

Priscilla:
Yes. I mean, I totally agree with you that it sets the tone for the entire chapter. I served on the membership committee, you know, a while ago, and I don’t know if it’s okay for me to say this, but I didn’t really enjoy it because I didn’t like dealing with these petty complaints that people had about each other. It was a difficult time, we had a difficult member.

Ivan:
I’m glad you brought that up because it is difficult dealing with petty complaints now. Now multiply that by 270,000. That’s sort of my world sometimes. But at the chapter level, yeah, you know, we think we completed high school, but there’s a piece of high school that’s still out there. And that’s, you know, sometimes the pettiness that takes place when you get a whole group of people together.

Priscilla :
And then I liked it even less when there was, like, a reason to really do something about somebody because I felt sorry for them or I had an aversion to it. I knew I was the wrong person on the committee.

Ivan :
Thank you for your service, I appreciate it. We should be thanking membership committee people like we thank people for military service, thank you for your service, because it is hard work and it takes somebody with some patience. And I recommend that you don’t stay on the membership committee for a long, long time. It’s good to cycle off and get a break. But yeah, when you’re dealing with people, there are a lot of challenges.

Here’s my advice. I think people just get too mad at each other too quickly. Two things. One, keep your eye on the ball. The purpose of BNI is to get referrals, and all the little crazy things that happen, you can put those aside and focus on our mission of referrals. The second one is critical. Give each other grace. People make mistakes; give them some grace. If it gets serious, you certainly want to take it to the membership committee. But the pettiness that can take place on membership sometimes is overwhelming for a membership committee. And I get that. So if you’ve heard this, then recognize that your membership committee has had to deal with a lot of difficult things. Anything else you want to add?

Priscilla:
Anything positive?

Ivan:
Think positive. Yeah.

Priscilla:
Well, no. I do think it sets the tone, though, for the whole meeting. And it makes important rules. I mean, I know we’re not supposed to create rules, but –

Ivan:
Yeah, the membership committee it doesn’t make rules, but they implement and apply the rules, right. Yeah.

Priscilla:
You know, it creates a certain atmosphere for the chapter and the people that are invited in by the membership committee can, like you said, it can really change the whole tone of a chapter.

Ivan:
Yeah, they can, for good and for bad. If the membership committee is too strict, and doesn’t act like they care, it can create negativity in a chapter and you’ve got to pull it back a little bit. Remember, Mandela not Attila. And yet if it’s too lax, it’s just, you know, it’s just social. And neither of those work very well.

We’re about out of time, the VP and the membership committee have a difficult and really important role in the organization. I had planned on saying that even before you said what you said, Priscilla. They really do have a difficult role and at your next meeting, I would love for you to thank the membership committee for their contribution to the chapter. It’s not easy to do those roles, but they can make a big difference in your group. Give them some accolades for their work. They deserve it. I’d love to hear your comments about what a good membership committee can do for a chapter. Let’s focus on what a good membership committee can do for a chapter in your comments here at the BNI Podcast. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well thank you Dr. Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Ivan & Priscilla explain the responsibilities of membership committees and what it’s like to serve on the membership committee.

title.rendered: Episode 831: Processionary Caterpillars—a True Story

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Just like processionary caterpillars marching endlessly in a circle, people often continue to do things that are no longer productive. They find themselves caught in a repetitive pattern that leads to unfavorable outcomes–and don’t always look for a way out.

As BNI members, we need to strive to learn from the mistakes that we’ve seen in the behaviors of people who have led us down the wrong path. Don’t just follow the leader – follow the leader who is taking you towards success. Never accept mediocrity when excellence is an option.

New BNI Book!

Today’s episode comes from Dr. Misner’s new book, The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success. The first paradigm is competition. The second paradigm is cooperation. The third paradigm is co-creation. Dr. Misner will be sharing more stories from this book in future episodes.

Next Steps

  • Buy The 3rd Paradigm at https://tinyurl.com/The3rdParadigm.
  • Read about processionary caterpillars.
  • Listen to Episode 560: I Hit HIDWAL.

Complete Transcript of Episode 831

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am home this week because I have a brand-new book that is coming out. And I’m doing a lot of interviews by Zoom and by phone. And we’ll have a link for the book here, and I love this book. It is called “The 3rd Paradigm“. “The 3rd Paradigm”, subtitle is A Radical Shift to Greater Success. We’ll include a link here.

So here’s a couple of things about the book. It is about co-creation. The first paradigm is competition. The second paradigm is cooperation. The third paradigm is co-creation. BNI has really been active in that concept throughout its history. A great example of co-creation is crowdsourcing, where people get together and create something together.

But again, what I love about this book, though, is we’ve got a survey of 4000 people, so we’ve got a lot of great examples. There’s a story interwoven into every chapter and one of my next podcasts, I’ll talk a little bit more about that story.

And then another unique thing is instead of a summary chapter, the publisher interviewed us as co-authors. So BNI is heavily represented in this book, even though it’s not about networking, it’s heavily represented in this book. Go take a look at The 3rd Paradigm on amazon.com. We’ll include a link. https://tinyurl.com/The3rdParadigm

Priscilla:
Great. Okay, it sounds really interesting.

Ivan:
So the topic for today, you were like, “Okay, this is a bizarre topic.”

Priscilla: (laughing)
Yep.

Ivan:
Processionary caterpillars. And it is a true story. And, you know, I started thinking about this, because I used to tell this story all the time, 35 -36 years ago, and I don’t know why, but it just kind of dropped out of my repertoire of storytelling. But it’s an incredibly powerful story about how people will do things that are no longer productive. And nature is a treasure trove of fascinating phenomena, and processionary caterpillars are a great example of that. So, John Henry Fabre, in the early 1900s, experimented with processionary caterpillars by arranging them so they would walk in a circle. Now normally, what they do is one follows another to get to where it is that they want to go either to find food or whatever. But what he thought he would do is put them in a circle to see how long it would take them to realize their mistake and change course.

Priscilla: (chuckling)
Uh-huh.

Ivan:
And he assumed it would just take minutes, or maybe hours. However to his horror, they walked in a circle for more than a week before they started dying.

Priscilla:
Ohhh.

Ivan:
And this intriguing behavior of processionary caterpillars holds, I think, an uncanny resemblance to certain patterns of human behavior that lead to negative consequences. Now, we might not die, but our businesses will die if we keep going in circles, like these processionary caterpillars. And I used to talk about this a lot in the early days of BNI. Oftentimes, people find themselves caught in a repetitive pattern that leads to unfavorable outcomes. And these patterns can be observed in a lot of aspects of life, from professional relationships to societal trends to business experiences.

And I’ve witnessed it many times in networking groups that refuse to change their unproductive behaviors despite marginal results of the group. And the inability to break away from these patterns, despite evidence of their harmful effects, is reminiscent to me of the processionary caterpillars. So the caterpillar circular march, while seemingly devoid of any kind of rationality, also reveals how sometimes, and I know I’ve used this phrase in podcasts before – sometimes you know, people are happy in their hole and they don’t want a ladder. They don’t want to get out of that pattern or that hole that they’re in, despite the evident signs of problems.

People sometimes continue destructive paths simply because they’re unwilling to consider productive alternatives. So this, to me, echoes the human tendency to make decisions based on habits, biases, preconceived notions, rather than a natural or rational evaluation of options based on results that other people have had. So the inability of processionary caterpillars, these are real — do a Google search, this is a real thing.

The inability of these caterpillars to adapt their behavior when circumstances warranted, I think, holds a mirror to some human resistance to change. People frequently find themselves clinging to outdated beliefs, or following a leader who doesn’t really know what the heck they’re doing. And people are following it. The tendency to blindly follow someone leading them in circles, in resisting change, is perpetrating destructive cycles. And I think it’s really important that we, as members, strive to learn from the mistakes that we’ve seen in the behaviors of people who have led us down the wrong path. And instead track record of success, people who have been successful. Don’t just follow the leader – follow the leader who is taking you towards success.

Priscilla:
Hmm.

Ivan:
We have in BNI, at this moment, over 11,000 chapters worldwide.

Priscilla:
Wow. That’s a lot.

Ivan:
And what I see people do is they change stuff, because they don’t want to do the same thing every week, even though it works. They don’t want to do the same thing every week. So here’s the suggestion, before you start changing anything, count out loud to 11,000.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
And recognize that we’ve had some success with our system. 11,000 groups is a lot of groups, Everybody can’t be wrong. And what I do see happen is that people sometimes – this may not be you, I may not be talking to you, but I’m probably talking to a number of people in chapters around the world. Sometimes people, like water, seek the path of least resistance, they want to do what’s easiest. But what’s easiest is not what’s best all the time. As a matter of fact, rarely is it best.

And so I tell members, why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option? Excellence is an option. Don’t get caught just going in circles, in unproductive behaviors. Instead, get into a groove of productive behaviors that makes a lot of sense for the success of your business. That’s what I want. I want businesspeople worldwide to have incredible success in this organization. With 11,000 chapters, we’ve helped a lot of people do that. So before you start messing with the system, count to 11,000 just to see how big a number that really is. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Well, 11,000 chapters is a huge number of chapters, because each chapter has many people in it. So that’s impressive in itself. Our chapter used to call ourselves, ‘No Ordinary Chapter’. And because we’re from Berkeley, California, we took pride in the idea that we could be unique. And so we were, we were changing things, and not always for the best. But I just thought I’d add that.

Ivan:
Yeah, and the “not always for the best” is the key there. You know, changing things when we have 11,000 groups, most of which are following the system.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
You know, changing things is obviously, I think, going down the wrong path. And then you have people following that. And so when you get a leader who starts changing things and everybody’s following that, and they eventually start leading people into mediocrity, leading people into unsuccessful habits. And you know, it’s one of the reasons why sometimes brand-new countries for BNI have so many more members, than countries that have been around for a while. Because the new countries are like, well, let’s follow the system. The system works, there’s 11,000 groups; we’re gonna follow the system. And then they get these chapters of 50, 75, 100 members, because they’re following the system! They’re not saying, “Let’s change things,” and then everybody kind of follows that change, and then they’re doing circles.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
So stick to the system, everyone. It works. It works well. That’s all I have for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great, Ivan. Thank you so much.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
