title.rendered: Episode 830: Inviting Visitors Through Social Media

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Darren Jamieson joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to explain how to invite visitors to your BNI through social media. The most effective method is to target your social media posts to the business category you’re looking for by mentioning members in their contact sphere:

  1. Identify members of that profession based in the same city as your chapter.
  2. Ask whether they get referrals from people in [contact sphere profession].
  3. Tell them you’re meeting a member of that profession at [meeting day and time] and invite them to join you.
  4. When you confirm the meeting with them by email, copy the members in their contact sphere.

Just be sure to let these members know before you do it, so they’re prepared to talk to the visitors.

Visit Darren’s website: www.engageweb.co.uk.

Complete Transcript of Episode 830

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Ooph, Pricilla, I’m taking a breath. And I’m actually home in Austin this week. So I’m headed out soon, but I’m home this week.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s nice. And who do you have on [the] line for us?

Ivan:
I have a guest. His name is Darren Jamieson. Darren joined BNI in 2012. He’s a former chapter president. He’s currently a 100-point member on traffic lights, which is amazing to me. He’s brought in over 30 visitors to his chapter in the first six months of 2023, and all of it through social media. He’s also delivered a TEDx talk on using social media to track a cyber bully’s identity – that would make an interesting discussion, Darren. So he knows a thing or two about getting results on social platforms. Darren, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Darren:
Thank you very much for having me. It’s an absolute pleasure to be here.

Ivan:
Well, it’s great to have you here. And I’m going to start off… we talked very briefly before we got started… I’m gonna start off by saying, I’ve never been a fan of what I see a lot of chapters do with social media and trying to get members where they just post something, “Hey, here’s our website, here’s our chapter, come visit us.” And it’s not very proactive. It’s just kind of putting stuff out there and hoping that somebody bites. And I don’t find that to be a very effective approach. But you’re talking about something completely different than that. Using social media, but completely different than that approach, right?

Darren:
Absolutely. Yes, no, you’re completely right. If you just post something on social media and expect people to come to you, it’s very passive, It’s very unlikely that you’re going to get any kind of results with that. I’m talking about something far more proactive and targeted.

Ivan:
Yes. And I love your first phrase. So I’m really interested to hear this one. So that your first concept is using an existing BNI member as bait. Tell me what that means.

Darren:
Yeah. I mean, you’re a big, big into fishing, I believe. So you know to use the right bait when you’re trying to catch the right kind of fish. Yeah. So when you’re looking for, say, for example, you’re looking for a tradesman, like a plumber, or an electrician, what you would do is you would send them a message, something along the lines of, “Do you get a lot of work from real estate agents, from lettings agents?” Or, “Do you get a lot of work from architects?” if you’ve got one of those in your chapter.

Ivan:
Yes.

Darren:
And you’ll either get the response from them of, “Well, yes, I do get lots of work from them. ” Or “No, I don’t. But I’d really like to.” And it’s very simple then to turn the conversation via social media, via messaging around to, “Well, I’m meeting a real estate agent or architect friend of mine on Thursday morning. Why don’t you come along with me, and I’ll introduce you to him, and you can tell him about your business. And maybe he’ll be able to put some work your way.”

Ivan:
And you find that people will respond to that? They don’t feel like you’re going to try and pitch them on something.

Darren:
No, no, they find that when you do something like that, it’s almost like you’re offering them the benefit, you’re doing them a favor. So if you were to invite them to a BNI meeting, then yes, that would be a cold invite. And they may have had something like that before. Or they’ll be suspicious that they’re going to come along and be charged and get asked to join a meeting. But you’re not doing that. You’re doing them a favor. And that’s the important thing with this strategy. Because a lot of people I speak to are concerned about inviting visitors or [their] worry about inviting visitors is they don’t like the rejection. They don’t like somebody saying no, I don’t want to come along. But if you get the mindset into your way of thinking that I’m doing them a favor. I want them to come along and meet someone who could potentially give them work, then it’s much easier to do.

Ivan:
And you invite them to a BNI meeting where you’re going to introduce them.

Darren:
Exactly yeah. It is important that if you have the architect or the real estate agent in your chapter, that you do ask their permission before you do this. You don’t just turn up with five or six plumbers and say, “Here you go have at them.”

Ivan: (chuckling)
Yeah, good point. It’s not just plumbers or real estate as well, it’s whenever there’s a member in your chapter who tends to be at the start of the process for work. So for example, if you have a wedding planner in your chapter, you could invite photographers, or videographers, or caterers to come along and meet them, because they always want to meet somebody like that.

Darren:
Exactly, yes.

Ivan:
Right. And they can create a Power Team out of that. All right. So tell me about going fishing for specific categories.

Darren:
I love this one, I love this one. And I’ve done this myself with success. We needed a cleaner for our office, because we bought a new two-story office, and we needed a commercial cleaner to come in and work with us maybe every two weeks or every month. And there was nobody in my BNI chapter. And obviously, I wanted a cleaner so that I could refer them and that I could get the thank you for the business, because you know, I want to be on 100 points for as long as possible.

Rather than put a post on social media and say I’m looking for a cleaner, get some recommendations, and then reply to those and say come along to my office and quote for me, what I did was I found a local Facebook group that had a lot of people in it, I put in some photographs of our new office with our new branding, which was also done by another BNI member. So a bit of advertising for ourselves in that way, and said, “Who would you recommend as a commercial cleaner who could come and work for us?” And we had maybe 50, 60, 70 comments from people recommending different cleaners around the area. Each one of those then I responded to and said, “You’ve been recommended in this Facebook group. We have a commercial office, we’d like you to quote for cleaning it. But rather than come to our office, could you meet me on Thursday morning, at my business referral meeting, and we can talk about the work?” And we actually got, I think it’s something like five or six cleaners turn up over a three or four week period. One of them applied and joined. And she now still cleans our office some 18 months later.

Ivan:
Excellent. What a great way to do it. That’s very creative. I’m impressed.

Darren:
I even told this story in an education slot about three months after that, about how to invite visitors this way and get them to come along to the meeting. And the cleaner was there. She was in the meeting. And she didn’t know when she came along that it was a BNI meeting.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Darren:
And I asked her the question, “Know what was it like when you came along? Because you know, you didn’t know what you were getting yourself in for.”

And she said to me, “Had you told me it was a BNI meeting and that I would have to stand up and give a 60-second [presentation’, there is no way I would have come along. But I am so glad that I did because I love it.”

Ivan:
Alright, but you did tell her it was a referral group. So she was expecting a group of people.

Darren:
I told her it was a referral group. Yes, I did. I just didn’t mention anything about standing up and talking in front of people. And I didn’t mention the fact that it was BNI.

Ivan:
Fair enough. You know, I find the less information you give, the better. You know, just come to the group, bring plenty of business cards, that kind of thing. That’s the way I opened the first 20 chapters. I mean, we had no material and there was no website, no internet, no social media. And you know, I just said, “Come along, bring plenty of business cards, we’re a referral organization.” So I used a very similar technique over the first 20 groups. So talk about the invite itself. We got a couple of minutes still; talk about the invite itself.

Darren:
Yeah, this is the thing that really clinches it for me. When I confirm the invite, what I will do is I will send out an email, and it’s always a templated email. So I’m literally copying and pasting the content. And I will invite the member, I’m sorry, the visitor that I’m inviting in the To field. In the CC field, I will put in all of the members of the chapter. And then I will put in the location of where the meeting is. So our meeting is at Rowton Hall Hotel and Spa, which has a bit of gravitas. Anyway, it sounds like a nice venue. And I will put in the date of the meeting. And then in the email field, then I’ll give them the address. Nice and big and bolded. Right at the bottom, I’ll say that, you know, it’s a £16 meeting to come along to. That’s not right at the front, that’s at the back.

But the important thing is you’re copying in the members; that does a couple of different things. Firstly, it shows them all the different members that are going to be there, so they can see there’s a lot of people that are going to be attending. But it also puts a little bit of peer pressure. Because when they know that all of these local businesses are expecting them to turn up on that BNI meeting, then they’re more inclined to make it because they don’t want to let so many people down who know that they’re going to come along.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Darren:
And the final thing that I think is good for this, that works really well, and this can be difficult depending on how engaged your BNI chapter actually is, is that if you have one or two members, particularly ones with synergy with the visitor that you’re inviting, if they then respond and reply to all on that email and say how much they’re looking forward to meeting that person and talking about their business. Not your own business, of course, their business. So for example, if you’re inviting a wedding photographer, and you’ve got a videographer and a caterer in the chapter, if they reply and say how much they want to talk about the photographer’s business, it really makes them feel that they’re going to get the benefit of coming along. And it really shores up the fact that your visitor is going to turn up.

Ivan:
I love this. This is a great approach. And what I particularly like about it is it’s proactive, not passive. And that’s always been my issue with social media, is that people use it passively. But this is a very proactive utilization of social media. What a great concept. And I really like it. Do you have any closing thoughts before I give your website out to everybody?

Darren:
Yeah, I think when you’re looking at somebody who you can use as bait, a good idea is to listen to your other members’ 60 seconds [weekly presentations], and who is it that they’re always asking for? So flooring companies, carpet fitters, and electricians will typically ask for introductions to office removal companies or office fit-out companies, because they can give them work. These are the people you want to get in your chapter because they are the ideal ones to use as bait.

It sounds horrible, saying that, doesn’t it?

Ivan:
It does a little, but it’s funny.

Darren:
They are the ones that you want to use as the bait because they’re the ones everybody wants to be introduced to. And when you’ve got somebody like that, it’s so easy to get visitors. So we’ve just had an architect and a lettings agent, join our chapter. I am very excited for the next couple of months, because I’m gonna get so many visitors using the architect and the letting agent as bait for our chapter.

Ivan:
Well Darren, thank you so much for this BNI Podcast. Anyone who’s interested in checking out Darren’s website www.engageweb.co.uk. That’s engageweb.co UK. Darren, thanks for being a guest on the BNI Podcast.

Darren:
Thank you very much. I’ve loved every moment of it.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you so much for that. It’s very interesting.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Darren Jamieson explains the most effective method for inviting visitors to your BNI chapter through social media.

title.rendered: Episode 829: Facts Tell, Stories Sell

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Charlie Lawson explains how BNI members can use stories to make a difference in their weekly chapter meeting. Stories get remembered. They help you get recalled by different members in your chapter during the course of their working week, and that helps you get referred.

Six Ingredients for a Powerful Story

  • Problem
  • Solution
  • Structure
  • Emotion
  • Characterization
  • Details

What you do to solve the problem is the least interesting part of the story. Clients and prospects want to know how the client you did it for felt before and after, and what the outcome was for them.

More with Charlie Lawson

  • Episode 588: Want Referrals? Don’t Bore Me to Tears
  • Episode 746: The Unnatural Promoter

Complete Transcript of Episode 829

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I just got back from Venice, Italy, where we did a [BNI] regional event and okay, I took a few days off in Venice at the same time.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
And I am on my way this week to Florida for a BNI Cruise – the [BNI] USA Member Extravaganza winners.

Priscilla:
Oh fun.

Ivan:
Still traveling for BNI quite a bit.

Priscilla:
How fun. And who’s on the podcast today?

Ivan:
Well, we have somebody who has been on a couple of podcasts, Charlie Lawson. Charlie has been on twice, in episode 746 and episode 588. It’s great to have Charlie back a third time. Charlie likes to call himself an Unnatural Networker, and most experts talk about networking as though it’s easy to talk to strangers. Charlie helps other unnatural networkers, introverts, I would say, gain confidence by putting networking across from the point of view of someone who’d rather avoid it completely. Charlie is a highly regarded keynote speaker and author, and he gained his expertise from 13 years as National Director for BNI in the UK and Ireland. And he now trains other unnatural networkers, and probably a few natural networkers as well, particularly those in professional services sector, to achieve success from networking. Charlie, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Charlie:
Thanks for having me, Ivan. It’s great to be on.

Ivan:
Well, it’s good to have you here. Today we’re going to talk about Facts Tell and Stories Sell. I could not agree more with that. So give us your perspective, Charlie, why stories are so incredibly powerful.

Charlie:
Yeah, absolutely. I mean, facts tell, stories sell is definitely something I’ve heard you say before. But I think I’m talking about stories in the context of how BNI members, and indeed anyone who wants to grow their business through networking, perhaps can use stories to make a difference in their weekly chapter meeting. Tell you what, I’ll give a good example about when we’re talking about stories is to tell your story. So let me let me dive straight in and then we’ll look at what the story said. So this is a story about a BNI, member, travel agent, lady called Marie. And she was sat in her office at about quarter to six on a Friday afternoon. Now, I don’t know about you listening to this and about your life, and about quarter to six on a Friday afternoon are we proactively thinking, “Let’s do business. Let’s move the business forward. Let’s see what we can do?” No, of course we’re not. We’re thinking about the weekend as she was; she had some friends coming over and they were going to have a takeaway meal. And she was kind of deciding what she was going to have off the menu. That was where her mind was.

Anyway, a call goes off in her office and there’s a lady on the other end of the line who’s very obviously upset. She’s crying. And Marie says, “Well, what’s going on? How can I help?” And it turned out that this lady was due to get married the next day, on the Saturday, and then fly off on her honeymoon – from the UK, this was to the Caribbean, and she was due to fly on Sunday. But the problem was, and the reason she was upset, is because there were no flights to the Caribbean. There was a strike, I don’t know what exactly was the problem, but there were no flights, there was nothing going on. There was no way they could do the flight; effectively, the honeymoon ruined, but potentially, you know, that ruins the whole experience of the getting married. And she was calling up Marie to just say, “Look, I know it’s last minute. But is there anything you could do?”

Marie didn’t want to promise anything because this was last minute, but she said, “Let me see what I can do.” And she just asked one question. She said, “Where are you going to be tomorrow morning? Where are you on the morning of the wedding?” And the lady said, “I’ll be getting ready at my mom’s house.” She gave her the address, put the phone down.

And Marie picked it up again called a couple of contacts. She went online and she managed to source exactly the same type of holiday. So, same spec hotel, same date, same budget, even down to a couple of the excursions that they that they planned. The only difference was it wasn’t in the Caribbean. It was in Hawaii. Next morning, she went around to this lady’s mom’s house and said, “Tomorrow, there you go. There’s the tickets that have normally about 150 pound late booking fee for this, but hey, that’s my wedding present to you. I hope you have a wonderful time in Hawaii. You obviously have a lovely day today.”

So, the story didn’t finish there, though, because about three weeks later, Marie was sat in her office again, and she received a postcard through the door. On one side on the first card was this idyllic Hawaiian beachside scene. And the other just a few short words, “We don’t know what we’d have done without you.”

Ivan:
Mmm

Charlie:
End of story. My question, I mean, you know, you can be answering this if you’re listening to this. But Ivan, tell me what you think would you refer business to Marie, based on what you just heard?

Ivan:
Absolutely, no question about it.

Charlie:
Exactly. And this is the thing where stories cut so powerful for members is, they help you get remembered, they help you get recalled by different members in your chapter during the course of their working week, and they helped me get referrals. So yeah, remembered, recalled, and referred. That’s what happens when you tell good stories like that. The challenge is, and I’ll let you come in just for a second, challenge is that sometimes the stories you’ll hear don’t always hit the mark. And I’ve got a couple of points to help with that in a second, but I’ll let you come in for a moment.

Ivan:
Well, listen, I love that phrase, remembered, recalled, referred. I think that’s a great way of remembering what you want in a story in a BNI context. And you know, if you tell a really good story like that, other people are able to repeat it, even if it hadn’t happened to them. They could say, you know, there’s a great travel agent, I have a friend, this is an experience that she had. And you’re almost able to repeat it word for word because it was such a detailed story that the person gave. So yeah, stories are really, really powerful. And that’s a great example of one.

Charlie:
Exactly. And yeah, it’s that ability to recall, I think it’s really powerful. But sometimes, and I’ve definitely heard BNI members say this many times in my years as National Director of BNI in the UK and Ireland, I’d have people say, “Well, I get it. I know I’m supposed to tell stories, I can understand why they’re powerful. But I never hit them off. They never…, you know, I don’t get people going out sharing the stories.”

So I started to look at it and it’s a bit like when making a recipe, baking a cake, for example. You’ve got your ingredients list; when you’re building a story, you’ve got to put the right ingredients in. Because let’s face it, not everyone is a master storyteller. It takes some work and practice to make it happen. So I’ve got six things that I think I’ll quickly run through them just to encourage anyone to put in their stories.

First thing is there’s got to be a problem. If there isn’t a problem, some sort of issue, some sort of challenge, well, you haven’t got a story. Simple as that. So there’s got to be a problem that needs to be overcome.

There’s got to be a solution as well. Now, sometimes we think that the solution is always going to be a happy ending; sometimes it doesn’t resolve in that way. But at least we’ve got to see the problem sorted out in some way.

You need to have some structure, a sort of start, middle, and end. I’ll come back to that in a second, I have a bit more to say on that.

The key one for me is emotion. You know, if we’re talking about getting people to really not only remember it, but recall and retell that story, as we were just saying, it’s when they feel the emotion, you know, what the person in the story is going through and what they’re feeling and their challenges. Take Marie, she went to great measure with this lady; with just one note, that lady got to go on a holiday on honeymoon. You know, it’s just that emotion because we kind of find ourselves putting us in their shoes.

You’ve got to have some characterization, you know, the goodie, the baddie, the person who’s got a problem to solve – you’ve got to have all those.

And I think the last one as well, that really makes a difference in the story is just putting in a couple of details. You can’t over fill it with details, it would just get unwieldy. But as an example, when did the call come into Marie’s office? It came in at quarter to six. And we remember that because we’ve talked about she was deciding what she was gonna do that evening, what take away she was having. So just a couple of details like that can really help.

So, all of that, those ingredients go in. The key though, as I said, was getting the emotion right. And I said, I’ll come back to the structure and I kind of divide the structure into three areas. You’ve got the setup where you lay out the problem, lay out the characters, who’s in it, what’s the issue? Well, what are they going through? Where are they? You’ve got, in terms of this type of story – this is a member telling a story about their business – you’ve got what they as the member did to solve it. And then you’ve got the afters. You know: what happened after the story finished? You know, did everyone sail off happily into the sunset? Or what will happen afterwards. So in those three sections: set up, what you did, and afters, you then can weight your story appropriately. The challenge is, as I said, many people don’t… BNI members don’t get… their stories don’t quite hit the mark. It’s because they put too much weighting on the “what you did” part. Now I’ll give you some percentages here. I like to weight the importance of these three sections in percentage terms.

Ivan:
We’ve got a couple more minutes.

Charlie:
I will be quick with this. I’ll be really quick. The setup, I give it 47½% of importance. And I give the afters 47½%. I’m very precise on these numbers. And that’s right, for math fans – that means only 5% of the importance comes from what you did. Because here’s the thing, what you do is actually really boring. I’ll say that politely, obviously, because what you do is your life’s work and your business and you love it. But what you do from a storytelling point of view, is actually not interesting at all. It’s boring from the point of view of the story. What we want to hear about is how the client felt. How did they feel beforehand? You know, they were obviously worried about something or upset about something, got an issue, or need something sorted. How did they feel afterwards? Yes, you’ve saved them time, money, what you did let them go on their honeymoon, whatever it was. And it’s those bits that create the emotion and get people remembering and recalling your story. So yeah, it’s not what you do. It’s how the client felt about you and what you did, both before and after. That’s the key to a good story.

Ivan:
Well, this is great content, Charlie. And I think members all over the world could use this. I have one quick question. The fifth item problem: solution structure, emotion characterization details. Can you give me just 10 seconds on characterization?

Charlie:
Yeah… characters. I mean, in this one, we got to think of any book movie, you know, those characters.

Ivan:
You’re talking about people?

Charlie:
Yeah, the people. The goodie, the baddie, the damsel in distress. Now sometimes the problem is that you know, the baddie isn’t necessarily a sort of James Bond style villian, trying to plot about blowing up the world. But it’s maybe a metaphorical baddie. There’s a situation in Marie’s case, this lady can’t get to go on a honeymoon. So that’s the baddie, in this case.

Ivan:
Well Charlie, this is really valuable information for our members, and I appreciate you taking the time to do this today.

Charlie:
Absolute pleasure.

Ivan:
Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 828: Become a Motivational Speaker for Your Business (Classic)

content.rendered:
Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 89, first released on January 28, 2009. Links and offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

Everyone has heard motivational speakers like Jack Canfield and Brian Tracy. The hidden element behind a motivational speaker is passion, and that’s the most important element you can bring to your 60-second commercial and your 10-minute presentation. You can’t afford to be ordinary, and if you’re not excited about what you do, no one else will be, either.

To become a motivational speaker, answer the following questions from your heart.

  1. What can you say about yourself that your competition can’t?
  2. How does your work fulfill you?
  3. What do you most enjoy about your work and why?

Never forget that one of the top ten traits of the master networker is enthusiasm.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcription of BNI Podcast Episode 89

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I am joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
I’m doing great, Priscilla. Thank you.

Priscilla:
Where are you today?

Ivan:
Well, I’m at BNI headquarters this week. I’ve got my top management team for the organization. A lot of members may not know this, but there’s actually over a thousand BNI directors who work for the organization worldwide, and we have good representation of directors around the world in our top management team. Europe is represented and Australia, Asia, and of course, North America is in the top management team. We get together a couple times a year to just guide the organization and keep things going on track. And so this week is our executive management meeting.

Priscilla:
Cool. So what are you going to share with us?

Ivan:
I want to talk about becoming a motivational speaker for your business. Now, I’m sure you’ve listened to motivational speakers like Jack Canfield and Brian Tracy, both good friends of mine, great speakers, absolutely very effective at motivating an audience. And when you listen to any of them, you feel the passion that’s flowing through their words, their actions and the images that they show.

The hidden element behind a motivational speaker is passion. And what I want to suggest to BNI members is that the most important element they can bring to their 60 Second Presentations and to their Ten Minute Presentations is an element of passion. Think about that, passion. Passion really helps to add enthusiasm to the message that you’re delivering. Your personal challenge in networking is having an extraordinary message that not only captures the educational content that you want to get across but highlights your unique selling proposition. It’s called the USP.

The USP is what sets you apart from your competition, and you can’t afford to be ordinary like your competition. You need to do it with passion, so your message needs to be filled with that. If you’re not excited about what you do, no one else in the room will be either. In word of mouth marketing, that’s not a good thing. If you’re passionate about what you do, you draw people in.

So how do you tap into your passion and tie it into your daily message or, in BNI, your weekly message about your business. Well, you can capitalize on your passion and spotlight your uniqueness by specifically answering the following questions, and you have to do it really from your heart, not your head.

Here’s the first one. What can you say about yourself or your business that your competition cannot say? What sets you aside from your competition?

Now, I’m not suggesting your stand up and beat up your competition. That’s a bad idea, as a rule. I’ve seen it done where people stood up and said, “My competition does this and that, and I don’t do those things.” I think that’s a bad idea.

Instead, what you want to do is you want to focus on what you do by simply saying, “Here’s how I stand out from what my competitors do. This is what I focus on. This is what my vision is. This is how we practice customer service.” Focus on what you are doing that your competition is not doing without saying anything negative about your competition.

Here’s one that I see a lot of people wouldn’t think about, and that is: How does your work fulfill you; what does it do for you; how are you excited about what you do?

And oftentimes, I think that members don’t really think about that. I had an attorney in one group who was really stuck in this area, and he was saying, “Well, I don’t know. I can’t say anything…” And he was a divorce lawyer, so all he’s dealing with is just one problem after another problem and their personal issues with the families that he’s working with. And he said, “I don’t know what I can say that’s positive.”

And one of the members, who was a client of his, said “You know, Mark, you know you do for me? You have enabled me to sleep easy at night by helping me solve some of the legal issues that I’ve had.” That’s powerful. “You have really helped me feel at ease with the problems, the serious problems I have.”

And he said, “Wow, you know, I never really thought about that.”

And so his presentation started talking about, “Look, you don’t have to worry as much about these things. That’s what my job is.” And he said that’s what fulfills him, being able to do that, but he never thought anybody would be interested in hearing that.

So I thought that was a powerful ticket.

The third is: What element of your work do you most enjoy and why? What do you really enjoy doing? What’s fun for you to do?

And if you do those three things, you’re going to be able to get across the passion of your business. Never forget that one of the top ten traits of a master networker is enthusiasm and motivation. And that’s based on a study that we did a few years back that was published in my book, Masters of Networking, were the top ten traits of a master networker, and we interviewed 2,000 business professionals in a dozen countries around the world. And we found that one of the top ten traits was enthusiasm and motivation. People want to do business with people who are motivated and enthusiastic.

So you, your sales force, and your word-of-mouth marketing team are the motivational speakers for your business, but that message begins with you, and you’ve got to have passion for what you do.

Priscilla:
Great. Do you have more to share with us?

Ivan:
Well, that’s it. I think the last thing that I would say is, talk about, focus on the parts of your business that you’re passionate about, because the more you can convey that to your fellow members of BNI, the more excited that they’re going to be. When you think about it, if you’re listening to this podcast now, think back to times when you’ve heard speakers who’ve just stood up and said, “My name is so-and-so, and this is what I do, and here’s how you can give me a referral.” Then think about some of the members who’ve stood up and oftentimes, they may sometimes not even be the most slick presenter, but they stand up and they’re really passionate about what it is that they’re doing, and they’re excited, and they get you excited.

Those are the people that other people remember and think about when it comes time to give the referrals, and it’s important for you, the listener, to become one of those people, to be passionate about what you’re doing. So focus on those things that you’re excited about, share that with BNI members, and that passion will spread to the members in the group in the interest in passing you referrals.

That’s it for today.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. I think you’re right. I just want to say it’s infectious and that it draws people to you and so I think you’re really right about all that.

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, thank you, Dr. Misner. And that’s it for this week.

I just want to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Passion is the most important element you can bring to your 60-second commercial and your 10-minute presentation.

title.rendered: Episode 827: Business in the Twilight Zone

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Toxic work environments are like living in the Twilight Zone episode “It’s a Good Life,” where the people live in such fear of a six-year-old with godlike powers that they respond to his destructive behavior by telling him “It’s a good thing you done that.”

Working for a manager who behaved like that six-year-old taught Dr. Misner valuable lessons for business and networking.

  • Learn to recognize toxicity.
  • When you leave toxic work environments behind, new opportunities will come.
  • Choose your business relationships wisely.
  • Don’t accept toxic clients.
  • Maintain a healthy and productive network by removing toxic connections from your network.
  • Take any issues with other chapter members to your membership committee so they can help keep the chapter healthy.

Listen to Episode 792: Has Anyone Ever Tried to Bribe You?

Complete Transcript of Episode 827

827-BNI-Podcast

Tue, Aug 29, 2023 8:30AM • 12:44

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at a BNI regional event in Milan, Italy.

Priscilla:
Oh, man.

Ivan:
I’m really excited to be here. I love my visits to Italy. I’ll be here for a couple of weeks.

Priscilla:
I’m so jealous. (laughing) Okay.

Ivan:
Well. So today’s topic Business in the Twilight Zone is really a follow up to another podcast that I did some time ago. Episode number 792. 792 is “Has anyone ever tried to bribe you?” and I tell a story about a place that I worked as a young man, literally 40 years ago or more. And it was a horrible, horrible place to work for. And I make some reference to the Twilight Zone and my experience in working there. So what I thought I might do is actually tell the Twilight Zone story that I refer to in Episode 792.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan: So I think one of my more unusual moments in business was this, 40 years ago working for this company. It was well before BNI. And it was a short stint that I did working as the general manager of a light manufacturing plant in Los Angeles, California. And actually, the story begins many years earlier than that, when I was a young boy and I would stay up late watching an old Twilight Zone episodes on television with my mother. And I’m assuming that most people that are listening to this have seen at least some of the old Twilight Zone episodes that were done in the 1960s. Do you remember the Twilight Zone?

Priscilla:
Definitely. Oh, absolutely.

Ivan:
Yeah, Rod Serling. They’re amazing, the episodes. And I’d stay up and watch them with my mom. And there was one episode that I particularly loved about a little boy named Anthony, who had incredible powers. And he completely controlled a small town. And he would do horrible things to people and to animals with his mind. And then his family and his neighbors would beg him to send the misshapen or deformed beasts to the cornfields. And as soon as he had done it, you know, inevitably one of the town’s people or family members would say, they would utter the infamous words, “It’s a good thing you done that, Anthony. It’s a real good thing you done that.” And I remember just watching that and being really creeped out as a little kid.

Priscilla:
I bet!

Ivan:
And, of course, then everyone, after someone said that, everyone would nod their heads in agreement and in total fear that Anthony might do it to them next time. So this made for great science fiction, because I love science fiction it stuck in my mind for many, many years. Now, fast forward quite a few years, over 40 years ago, and I’m working for that same company that I talked about in episode number 792. Different situation, but same company. And I was the general manager, light manufacturing plant. While I was there, I had the misfortune of working for the absolute worst, most dysfunctional team of people I have ever seen in my life. And it was headed up by one of the co-owners, and her name was Sally. And her idea of good management was yelling at everyone who didn’t do what she said to do and do it immediately. I kind of thought of her as the Genghis Khan in female form.

Priscilla:
Uh huh.

Ivan:
And as soon as I started the job, almost immediately, I realized it was a horrible mistake. And almost every day, I dreaded going to work. And finally after three months, it happened — the most surreal experience of my entire management career. I was in the middle of a meeting with some of my employees from the production department along with “Ms. Khan,” Sally, when she went into an absolute tirade, an absolute tirade. She screamed at an employee, and then threw the young lady out of her office. At that moment, one of the horror-stricken production employees, who was still in the room, looked solemnly at Sally and said, “It’s a good thing you done that, Sally. It’s a real good thing you done that.”

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
I couldn’t believe it! It was like, “Oh, my goodness, I’m in the Twilight Zone! This is the Twilight Zone, you know, the cornfields are next.” And I’m thinking I am so out of here, I am absolutely out of here. And that night, I went to a computer company, I knew the owner of this computer company. And I said, “Hey, I know you want to put together a marketing department. I’m about to leave the company that I’m with. And I’m going to…” I had been doing consulting on a part time basis, but I told him, “I’m gonna leave and do consulting on a full-time basis. And if you want to create a marketing department, I’m your guy. I can help you create that department.” And he said, “Yes”. And so the very next day, the very next day, I went into the Twilight Zone, and gave my notice.

And I have never regretted leaving that company. It was the experience, it was the experience that prompted me to start my consulting business full time. And anyone who’s listened to my material, or read any of my material, leaving there and starting my own company was absolutely the best decision I ever made. I’ve never looked back, except to chuckle from time to time, because starting my consulting business is what led me to then start BNI. So in a very strange way, I’m grateful for this crazy person, who was a horrible manager, but taught me a lesson about a couple of things. One is that when one door closes, another one opens. And we often forget that. We just look at the door that’s closed. And we don’t look around for the opportunities that may lie on the other side or are in different areas. And it really was an early lesson, Priscilla, about who I want in my room.

Priscilla:
Uh huh.

Ivan:
Most everyone knows about my book, “Who’s In Your Room?” and it was an early management lesson on don’t tolerate toxic environments. Don’t accept toxic environments, because you know, they will just wear you down and you’ll hate the work you’re doing. And you can apply this to clients. Don’t accept toxic clients.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And it’s hard sometimes. Because you know, in some ways that’s your bread and butter; they’re how you’re making money. But I can’t tell you how many people I’ve met that have said, “I had this toxic client, I kept them because I thought I needed the money. But then when I had enough, and I let them go, you know, what? I found other clients were way better to work with. I should have let them go earlier.

Priscilla:
Yeah, right.

Ivan:  
And this, of course, this applies to toxic members of a network. Not that we would ever have a toxic BNI member, but you know, any toxic people in your network, it’s important to move them out. I’ll never forget a really good member, Priscilla, and I told a variation of this years ago on a podcast. A really good man. I remember, I think it was a printer, and he called me up and said he was stepping down from BNI. And I was like, “Man, why? You get a ton business out of this. And you have loved BNI; you’ve been in for several years.” And he said, “Yeah, yeah, I’m so busy. I just got a lot… I’m really busy.” And so I put together this exit interview. I didn’t tell him I wrote the exit interview for him.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
But, I wrote it for him. And I did this exit interview. And in the exit interview, one of the things that you can do when you’re talking to someone or interviewing someone, it’s called semantic differential questioning, where you ask the same question in two or three different ways. And so I asked him, “Can you tell me why you’re leaving?” He said, “You know, I’m really busy. I got a lot of business going on.” And I asked him a few more questions. And I said, “Well, might there be any other reason?” And he said, “No, I’m just really busy.” And then I asked a few more questions. And I got to the third or fourth time where I finally said to him, “If there were another reason for leaving, what would it be?” And believe it or not, the concept of semantic differential questioning, when it’s interwoven with other questions, really works. And that’s when he said to me, “Alright, gosh. There’s a member in the chapter who owes me a boatload of money and I don’t want to see them every week. I’m really mad at them.”

Priscilla:
Oh, wow. Yeah.

Ivan:
And I’m like, “Okay. So you’re gonna leave because of this member.” He said, “Yeah.” I said, “You’ve got to stay. You’ve got to take it to the [chapter’s] membership committee. He said, “No, no. I don’t want to do that. I don’t want to cause a scene.”

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And that’s when I guilt-tripped him. I was like, “You know he’s gonna do this to someone else, right? And if you don’t speak up, then you’re allowing it to happen to someone else.”

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan: 
He’s like, “I hate when you tell me things like that.” He said, “All right. I’ll take you to the membership committee.” And I went with him to the membership committee. And Priscilla, I’m not kidding you, when he told the story to the membership committee how this member owed him money, and how he was a difficult member, he was an angry member, and he owed people money. One of the membership committee people said, “Oh, my goodness, I’m having the same problem with him.” And so there was more than one.

And so that membership committee did the right thing, they got rid of the bad member and kept the good member. So toxic members, difficult members, people who are not doing a good job, or people who owe people money – these are the people that you don’t want in your chapter. And it was a lesson that I learned many, many, many years ago, in working for this company. And that’s my message for today. If anybody’s got their own Twilight Zone experience from working someplace, I’d love to see it in the podcast [comments]. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I just have one question about that. Do you think that the person who spoke that sentence that came right out of the Twilight Zone, do you think he did that on purpose? Did he know it was part of the Twilight Zone?

Ivan:
I don’t think so. I don’t think so. English was a second language. It was a woman who said it and English was a second language. And so I think it was just…

Priscilla:
Just the way it came out.

Ivan:
Yeah, it was just the way it came out. But let me tell you, it landed home for me as, literally, part of The Twilight Zone.

Priscilla:
That is so funny. Okay, great. Thank you, Ivan.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: The Twilight Zone teaches the importance of recognizing and distancing oneself from toxic environments, individuals, and clients.

title.rendered: Episode 826: Is Your Message Clear?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

When you introduce yourself with your 30-second presentation, it’s important to keep your message clear and replicable. You want to pass the message to others who will both use your services and keep spreading your message. That means that you want to do and say things that set the pattern for duplication.

If your message is too complex, it’s not going to be memorable. And if it’s not clearly memorable, it can’t be repeated, it can’t be duplicated.

You need to know how much information your fellow networkers or referral partners are really hearing and understanding. The Cards in a Basket meeting stimulant is a great way to test this within your chapter. If another member can’t describe your business and target market accurately, you know it needs to be clearer.

Share your experiences with making your message clear in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 826

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi, Priscilla. I am at my home office in Austin, Texas, and spent a little time today with somebody who’s been on my podcast a number of times, Sam Horn. Sam and I have Writing Wednesdays whenever I’m in town, and she’s in town. And you know, I think we’ve talked about this in a previous podcast, we should call it “Talking” Wednesdays, we tend to talk about writing. So. So I’m in Austin today.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. What are you sharing with us today?

Ivan:
Yeah, so interesting idea. I think when you’re introducing yourself, either to an individual or to a group, like at a BNI chapter, you have a choice of how you deliver your message. The primary vehicle for your introduction is your verbal presentation. And so you have to ask yourself, does that introduction work? Is it effective? People not only judge the message, of course, they judge the messenger as well. How do you look? How do you carry yourself? How do you listen if you’re doing a one-to-one? And how do you leave the conversation, if you’re doing a one-to-one? All of these things will affect what others do with the message that you’ve delivered.

And the important thing to remember is to speak as if you’re addressing that single person in an authentic and very clear way. As you network with friends and associates and tell them what you do, your underlying hope is that they’ll use your services and pass the message to others. And here’s where my message today is really important. You want to pass the message to others who will also use your services, and in turn, keep spreading your message. And when someone speaks about you or your business on your behalf, those same rules apply. They, too, will be judged how they describe what you do. So that means that you want to do and say things that set the pattern for duplication. Meaning, you want to say things that get duplicated accurately. Priscilla, did you ever play the childhood game called the telephone, the telephone game?

Priscilla:
Yes, that’s so fun. And it also just demonstrates what you’re talking about.

Ivan:
Exactly. The telephone game. And I think this is something that has been done in virtually all countries, I’ve not been to any country that wasn’t aware of the concept. And the way it works is one player whispers a message to the person sitting next to them. And that person then whispers the exact same message to the next person and so on down the line. And the relayed message invariably gets contorted, really contorted, before reaching the last person in the chain. And the end result is often really funny, with the final whispered message bearing little resemblance to the original.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
So you know, words get lost or twisted and more dramatically, the original meaning is often entirely lost; and the longer and more complex the starting message, the less chance it has of surviving the trip around the room. As a game, this can be funny. However, in real life when it involves your business, it’s not so funny. In referral marketing, communication is critical. And success rests on the delivery and the impact of your message.

So similar to the telephone game that we may have played as children, it’s important that you keep checking down the line to ensure that your original message is being accurately passed along by your referral partners. And as you continue to build your network, you need to know how much information your fellow networkers or referral partners are really hearing and understanding. And at times, you may need to make adjustments in the way you disseminate your information so that it’s duplicated and repeated effectively. So, each messenger, or referral partner in our case, may use a different technique and they may have different motives for participating in this process. But the essence of each message is what needs to get across the finish line, what is being communicated effectively to others.

Remember that your message is the description you give of your business and your target market when you introduce yourself to someone in a networking environment, or when you give your weekly presentation. It’s typically a few sentences, it’ll take 30 seconds, maybe 60 seconds in a smaller chapter, to deliver that message. But if your message is too complex, if it lacks passion, or if it flies over the listener’s head, because you’re using acronyms or phrases that they don’t understand, it’s not going to be memorable, in a clear way. And if it’s not clearly memorable, it can’t be repeated, it can’t be duplicated. And so the message for today’s podcast is, you have to ask yourself – Is your message clear? And is it repeatable?

Ivan:
And it would be interesting to test that, and there are a few ways to test this. One of them is a meeting stimulant that we have, it’s called Cards in a Basket. And Priscilla, I’m sure you’ve seen this, you’ve been in BNI long enough. And we have everybody put their business cards in a basket or a cup or anything. You mix up the cards and you have one member reach in and pull out a business card and they have to do that member’s weekly presentation.

Priscilla:
Great. Yeah.

Ivan:
And it’s really interesting to see – is your message being delivered effectively? Because if that person is not delivering your message effectively, it might not be their fault. It might not be their fault. It might be that you haven’t communicated it clearly enough for them to be able to replicate it in a conversation or in a presentation. And if they can’t replicate it within the BNI context, how are they doing outside the BNI context?

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
So that’s one way that you can experiment. And I would urge chapters to do that meeting stimulant, and I don’t know if they’ve been renumbered. But as I recall, that’s either meeting stimulant number one or two. And I’ve seen it done many, many, many times. And it’s a lot of fun to see it done. And it’s also a real learning experience. What are your thoughts on the telephone game and is your message clear?

Priscilla:
What you just described doing some other member’s presentation of their business, is something that we’ve done quite a bit in my chapter. And it can be really challenging if you’re not a good listener, if you’ve not been paying attention. And so it’s nice at least to have the card because sometimes you don’t even quite remember what your what your partner’s name of the business is. You might know their name, but you’ve forgotten what they use for their business name. Some, you know, small details like that. As far as the telephone game, I was imagining that as a fun demonstration, in a Chapter and to see how it works, you know, and send a message all the way around the room and see how it turns out at the end. Kind of a fun experiment.

Ivan:
You know, if you were to do it on just one time around on one phrase or something, just to show how the phrase gets distorted, yeah. So, Education Coordinators or members who are listening to this, that’s a great idea, Priscilla, you know, give it a try. Show how a message gets distorted, give a couple of sentence message and do the telephone game around the chapter. See how the message ends up and that’s kind of the way your message is ending up out in the in the world with your referral partners. You know, it’s going through one or two or three people and, and it may not come out exactly the way you want it. I know we’re almost running out of time.

The very first time I saw this Cards in a Basket was hysterical. I don’t know if I’ve done a podcast on this or not, but it was so funny. This woman, they’re going around the room and this woman pulled a card out. And Priscilla, she could not have been five foot [tall]; she was probably 4’11”. I mean she was tiny. And she pulls out the business card of an ex-college basketball player, who’s like 6’6″, 6’7″, okay. And it was hysterical, because we didn’t know whose card she pulled. Then she stood on her chair, which, by the way, I don’t always recommend; it could be dangerous. But she stood on her chair and did his presentation and nailed his presentation, absolutely nailed his presentation. And everyone was laughing because, you know, here’s this four-foot-something woman doing this presentation of the six-foot-seven guy. Which, the visual in and of itself was funny, but what was really great was that she nailed the message. And so there are a lot of great things that you can do with something like Cards in a Basket.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
That’s my message for today. Keep your message clear, everyone, and make sure that it’s replicable.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 825: Cultivating a Million-Dollar Membership

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jeremy Walsh returns to the podcast to explain why the power of BNI is in the exponential nature of your relationships with your referral partners.

BNI members sometimes fall into the trap of thinking the value of their membership is only in Tier One referrals–the referrals they get directly from other members. It’s also important to count the revenue generated by referrals from the referrals that Tier One referral sends you, and the referrals those people send you. Over time, that first referral can generate dozens more referrals–as long as you continue cultivate your relationships with them.

Watch Jeremy’s presentation, “Cultivating a Million-Dollar Membership,” on BNI Business Builder under “Deep Dives” for more details.

To learn more about cultivating relationships with gratitude, read Dr. Misner’s book Infinite Giving.

Complete Transcript of Episode 825

825-BNI-Podcast

Tue, Aug 29, 2023 8:31AM • 13:01

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, at the moment I’m back in Austin, but I’ve been in Southern California. I did a [BNI] regional event in Temecula; I met with some BNI Directors in Southern California. And I saw my grandson while I was back there.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
It was a great trip. I also did an event here in Austin with RJon Robins, who is a big supporter of BNI with attorneys, so it’s been a busy couple of weeks.

Priscilla:
Yeah, it sounds like it.

Ivan:
I’ve got a guest on BNI Podcast who has been on, we’re talking about this, it’s got to be double digits. I don’t know how many times. But it’s a lot. And almost every time it’s been because of BNI Connect because he is the main guy with BNI Connect.

Jeremy Walsh is the Director of Support for BNI Connect and BNI Business Builders. He’s been a member since 2002! We’re recording this in 2023 so 21 years. And since then, he’s held just about every position from chapter Leadership team through performing the role of an Area Director for the [U.S.] state of Rhode Island, overseeing 22 chapters and more than 500 members. In 2011 he was invited to create a support system and team for BNI Connect and has been working with BNI Global and the national offices ever since then. He and his team have answered over 450,000 support tickets for BNI Connect, and BNI Business Builders and he maintains an average response time under an hour, which I find unbelievable, and they have really good ratings. And so it’s great to have him here.

Oh, in his spare time, Jeremy is also an executive on the Board for the BNI Foundation. He loves to cook and coach and he volunteers for the Boy Scouts. Jeremy, welcome to BNI Podcast again.

Jeremy:
Thank you so much. It’s always a pleasure to be here. I’ll be honest, I’m not talking about BNI Connect today, which feels really, really weird to me. But see what we can do.

Ivan:
Yeah, we have a different topic. Because almost every one, if not every conversation we’ve had, has been about BNI Connect. But this is about a million-dollar membership and somebody who’s been involved in BNI for 21 years, should know a little bit about this. And you definitely do. You’ve been you know, observing it through the BNI Connect system, and you’ve been a member and active and so you understand BNI as well as anybody does. So as a long-term member and director, one common thing that I think you’ve noticed is that people really undervalue their membership in BNI. Would you mind talking about that a little bit?

Jeremy:
Yeah, absolutely. So, you know, I became a member way back in 2002. And one of the reasons I became a member was because I’m a bit frugal. I don’t like to spend a lot of money on advertising. When I started my business, I actually didn’t have a lot of money. So you know, BNI was probably the most cost effective thing. But, you know, I fell into the trap, especially in the first couple of years of constantly questioning was BNI really worth my time. And, you know, about four or five years into it, I really wanted to go back and kind of prove to myself that it was worth my time. And, you know, the common trap that I think we fall into is that a lot of us, when we submit Thank You for Closed Business for revenue that we’ve received, a lot of times we only do that for the first transaction in a new relationship. So, my referral partner, they give me a new referral, I land it, that deal ends, I report my Thank You for Closed Business, and then I kind of forget about it and immediately move on to the next deal, right? But I think where the power of BNI is really in the exponential nature of those relationships.

And just to give you an example, so I went back, and I looked at my very, very first referral in BNI. It was an internal referral. So a Tier One referral of somebody that was in the chapter that just wanted to use my services. And I went back and looked at my books. And in that first year, they did about $5,000 worth of business with me. And right there, I mean, that was enough to pay for my membership a couple of times over. So, yeah, that’s all well and good. However, if I took that relationship and I looked at it over five years, and I looked at the next year they referred me to another person; the next year, they referred me to two more people. The next year, they referred me to two more people. And two of the other people referred me to somebody. And I watched that branch out over the course of five years. And if I really want to look at the value of that relationship, in that five-year period, it was not $5,000, it was actually $75,000.

Ivan:
Hmm, that’s amazing. And now you’re talking about referrals not only from that person over a period of time, but the value of the referrals that they gave in any referrals that this second generation gave of referrals, correct?

Jeremy:
Yeah, absolutely. So when I looked at this one, the original referral referred me to five people. But those five people also referred me to eight or nine more people on top of that.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. This is an important thing that you’re pointing out. I remember one of the founding members of BNI, was in for just a few years, maybe three years. And she had gotten I think six or eight referrals, she was in a high-end business. But she… this is, after three or four years, she dug deep on those referrals. And which one of those referrals gave her referrals, and it ended up going eight deep, the original person referred somebody who referred somebody who referred somebody who referred somebody who referred somebody who referred somebody who referred somebody, and it ended up turning into over four or five years, about 60 referrals from that handful of referrals that she got directly out of BNI. And that’s one of the things that we absolutely don’t measure, and would be almost impossible to measure. But when we do, it kind of blows our mind.

Jeremy:
100%. And it really did, you know, when I went back, it truly made me appreciate my membership, and also really working on not always constantly going back to my chapter, and you obviously want to get those referrals from the chapter but making sure that you’re also concentrating on those referrals and those relationships and building every single one of them. So that you really get all these branches out on all of the possible trees from these referrals.

Ivan:
Right. So you mentioned relationships, and BNI is all about relationships. It’s one of our core values. Talk about that a little bit.

Jeremy:
Sure. So, what I did was I, in part of this, I kind of went back and if you’d like to see it, I did a presentation on this, that’s up on BNI Business Builder, you can actually do a search on Business Builder for this title, “Cultivating Million Dollar Membership” and I kind of went through a lot of my relationships over the course of the last 21 years and looked at different types, and really going back and saying, BNI has put me in all of these different places where I was able to do it. Yeah, I broke them into categories, like, you know, the lucky break, and kind of being in the right place at the right time, and being able to seek out a relationship of actually working with your competition, and giving back without the expectation of return. And, you know, some of these relationships, also playing the long game. I mean, I explain one relationship that took about 13 years to cultivate. Which, by the way, is about how long it takes a pecan tree to produce fruit.

Ivan:
That’s funny.

Jeremy:
Did I ever tell you that I almost didn’t get into my chapter?

Ivan:
No, I don’t think you did. That’s a serious problem. Tell me about that.

Jeremy: (laughing)
Well, when I first went to join, it was in March of 2002, and the chapter rejected me. They rejected me, actually, three or four times. And I tried to apply there over the course of the first couple of months, when I first moved in. I gotta say, they had a right to do so because I didn’t know anybody, I just moved to the state, really it was a fledgling business. But what happened was, I was out and I was cold calling people and stuff like that, the thing that I’ve never done since, but I ran into a relationship doing a job where that person was a member of another BNI chapter. And when I told that person of the struggles I was having getting into my chapter, she immediately went into my chapter and said, “You guys need to accept this person into your chapter.” And if it wasn’t for that particular lucky break, I probably wouldn’t be here talking to you today.

Ivan:
What a great story. You know, and I say to people, in some chapters, I would not be accepted as a member from my business 38 years ago. Because my business was, I was just doing it full time for one year, I’d only been in it full time for a year. And I was 28 years old. And, you know, there are a lot of chapters that wouldn’t have accepted me as a member. And you really want to look at the quality of the person, not just, you know, not just on paper, but what’s behind that and the people that are referring them. Is this “Cultivating a Million Dollar Membership”, is it a video or a PowerPoint? What is it?

Jeremy:
Yes, it’s a webinar that I did. I’ve recorded it a couple of times, you can find it up on BNI Business Builder.

Ivan:
Yeah, and it’s under the deep dive series, correct?

Jeremy:
Yes, absolutely. It’s part of the deep dive series. It’s the one non-technical webinar in that series.

Ivan:
Well, look, if you are not using BNI Business Builder, you need to go check it out right away, because there’s just so much content. I just got an email today, just today, from someone thanking me for the video that I did on the Hidden Elements, which I’m sure you’ve seen me talk about the Hidden Elements many times. And there’s so much content up there. If you’re a BNI. Member, it’s all available to you for free. We’re out of time now, Jeremy. Is there any last thought before we wrap up?

Jeremy:
The only thing I’ll say is that the number one lesson I learned in all of this over the years is to make sure that you are cultivating that garden with gratitude and to make sure that you are thankful and make sure everybody knows that you’re thankful for all those relationships that you’re building over the years.

Ivan:
Yeah, good advice. You know, I talked about that in my book, Infinite Giving. If you get a chance to check it out those of you who are listening, check out the book Infinite Giving, where there’s a chapter in there on “Gratitude is not New Age Psychobabble, It’s Science.” And then we talk about the science of gratitude. So, Jeremy, you’re such a great addition to BNI. It’s a real honor to have you on BNI podcast again, and thank you for everything you do. And thank you for this podcast today.

Jeremy:
Thank you for having me here. It’s always a pleasure talking with you.

Ivan:
Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much, both of you, for some great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard
