title.rendered: Episode 824: Want More Referrals? Show Up! (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 518, which first appeared on August 2, 2017. Links and special offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

Would you like to substantially increase the number of referrals that your BNI chapter generates? If so, listen.

Years ago, a chapter president wanted to prove to Dr. Misner that attendance had no effect on the number of referrals passed. After 6 months, absenteeism dropped by 71%. Referrals went up 62% and membership went up 50%. A second chapter tried the same experiment. After 9 months, absences decreased by 53%, referrals went up 164%, and membership grew by 90%.

If your chapter is lax on attendance, it’s going to affect your pocketbook.

Listen to Episode 490 for more data that supports the role attendance plays in membership growth and referrals passed.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 824/518

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you this week?

Ivan:
I just got back from a visit to the Tucson BNI region. I love that region, I’ve been there several times. It was great to be back!

Priscilla:
Good! What is this podcast about?

Ivan:
So the title of the podcast is, “Want more referrals? Show up!” So here is a question to everyone in BNI listening to this podcast or reading the transcript; Would you like to have a substantially increase in the number of referrals that your BNI chapter generate? If you answered yes, listen up, here’s how. Years ago, many years ago, I had a chapter president call me with a proposition. He said his chapter felt that the attendance policy was a hassle and they didn’t believe it made any difference in referrals. As a matter of fact, he said he felt it was actually a drawback to membership. So, I told him I didn’t create the policy, it was created by a board or advisers which is made up exclusively of BNI members. He said his group- and I’m not making this up, wanted to test something out. They wanted to show me and the board, that the attendance had no impact on referrals and it had no impact of membership. None.

Priscilla, I know you’re chuckling, but at that point I was not because we did not have hard data back then. I’m like “okay, fine.” This attendance thing is driving me nuts. Anyway, so he asked me if I would publish the findings in our newsletter, and ask the board to reconsider this policy if they could document that it made no difference. I happily agreed to both.

So, they used the prior quarter as their baseline and they began focusing on improving attendance starting with the next month. It is important to note that this during this time they did nothing else other than improve attendance. They really didn’t focus on anything else other than improving attendance. Now, I’m going to include a couple of graphics in this podcast and it will show some of the data that I’m talking about here, so that you can visually see the graphics.

So, they used the prior quarter. Then for six months, they looked at it on a quarter-by-quarter basis, they did it for six months. So, what were their results? Well, they decreased their absences, they had a 0.7 absences per member month. So roughly every member missed a meeting every other month, roughly. And in their first quarter, they dropped their absenteeism by 52% – so a big drop in absenteeism. After six months, they significantly dropped absenteeism by 71%. But what about referrals, you ask? Referrals went up to 62%. So, what started at 0.7 absences per member-per-month, they dropped it after six months, the absenteeism, by 71% and the referrals went up to 62%. As an added bonus, membership went up to 50%. Our data, and this was the chapter tracking. So, at the end of our little experiment, The president called me up and said, “Never mind. It’s clear that absenteeism hurts the performance of the group. We do not want to change anything.” So, I thanked him and I thanked the group for the study. I asked if I could publish the results and they graciously agreed.

Buthere is where the study gets really awesome: after I published the original article- I think this was in the nineties, I had another chapter president call me up after he read the article, and said “You made this stuff all up.” I said “I didn’t make this up! Call the president. He’ll confirm that himself.” I’ll never forget what he said next, he said “No. This guy is probably in your pocket. He’ll do whatever you tell him to do.” I’m like wow, you don’t know BNI members, I thought! So, I said “Okay. Well if you don’t want to call him, how can I help? What can I do for you?” So, he went on to say that even if the data were true, he said six months wasn’t long enough to confirm the results until at least a year. He wanted to focus on absenteeism for a year to show me that the results on absenteeism would be different if the study went longer. I told him that I thought it was a great idea and I thought it might be interesting to see if there was a difference!

Interestingly enough, there was. In fact, a big difference in the results by tracking it a year longer. The second group started with very similar absenteeism. They had 0.6 absences per member-per-month. They had really similar results at the end of six months. They dropped absenteeism by more than 50%, their membership went up to 55%, and their referrals went up 71%. Which is pretty close to the first group. But, here is where it gets amazing, at the nine month mark their absenteeism was stable with a drop of over 50% percent. So, they dropped half of their absenteeism, but their membership had increased a whopping 90%.

Priscilla:
Wow!

Ivan:
Yeah, wow! And their referrals went through the roof with an increase of 164%! And you can see all those numbers in this table that we’ll include here. So, after nine months, the president called me up and he also said “Yeah, never mind. We get it. Absenteeism clearly brings down results a lot.” And although they stopped tracking that data after nine months, I stayed in touch with the chapter for a long time. They continued to focus on attendance, and they grew to a well over 40 member chapter. By that time, they were passing more referrals than they ever thought possible.

Priscilla:
Yeah, well that makes sense.

Ivan:
It really does!

Priscilla:
Because, if a person is sitting across from you at a table and it jogs your memory. You start thinking of things you need to do with them, or who you need to talk to about them, you go and you do it.

Ivan:
Yeah, that is right. You know, it’s part and parcel with that time repetition. Every week you are getting in front of people, you’ are constantly getting reminded of them, and they are doing it right. They are teaching you something every week. They are teaching you a little aspect of their business which makes it easier for you to refer them. So, you got to be there. You cannot get a hair cut or the phone. You cannot have a Successful BNI group if you don’t show up. Absenteeism effects membership and referrals.
The results could not be clearer. There is a direct linear correlation between absenteeism and referrals. As a group decreases their absences, they increase their membership, which substantially increases the referrals. High absenteeism results in low referrals. Low absenteeism results in high referrals.

We talked about this in a previous podcast- I don’t remember the number of the podcast- but it was about that study that was done in Italy, Priscilla. I’ll try to find that podcast and include it here in the link, because, yet again more hard data that absenteeism would bring down the success of the chapter.

So, the lesson here – and I would love to hear any thoughts you have, if your chapter lacks on attendance that is going to effect your pocket book. Plain and simple.

Priscilla:
I think you are right. The last thing I would like to say is, if you’re absent, you are not going to bring a guest. And your guests are a great source of referrals, so it will effect it in two ways.

Ivan:
Yeah, again, visitors are a great source of referrals. They are also obviously potential members, which brings in more referrals. Look, I get it. It is not easy to do this, but let me tell you, the results can be amazing if you do it right. I have said this many times in my podcast, “Why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option?” Excellence is an option! Start today, bring excellence to your group, focus on reducing absenteeism. I promise you, you are going to get better results.

Well that’s everything for today. Priscilla, back to you- wait, hang on. I just found the episode that I was talking about where there was another study done in Italy. It’s episode number 490. It was a study done by a BNI member, Beatrice. So, go listen to podcast 490, and you will see even more data that supports what we talked about today.

Now I’m done, Priscilla, back to you!

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you Dr. Misner. Well, I would like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by The Networking For Success channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening, this is Priscilla Rice. We look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Decreasing absences increases BNI chapter membership and referrals. Absenteeism affects your pocketbook. If you want more referrals, show up!

title.rendered: Episode 823: One Reason Someone Should Refer You

content.rendered:
Synopsis

To succeed in business, you have to know your audience and tailor your comments to suit the people you’re talking to. If you’re a contestant on Shark Tank, you need speak a language focused on opportunity, growth, return on investment, and cold, hard cash for the investors. When you’re networking, answer questions in a way that the person you’re talking to will best be able to hear it, addressing the talking points that they’re most interested in.

When you first meet people, begin by asking them questions about themselves. The more you know about the people you’re talking to, the better you’re able to craft your own message in a way that effectively resonates with them.

Listen to Episode 575: How to Become a Key Person of Influence for a guest appearance by one of the “sharks” from Shark Tank.

Complete Transcript of Episode 823

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Ivan, before you start, I just want to say that this is Episode 823. And it’s airing on 8-23-23.

Ivan: (laughing)
I love it. Episode 823 airing on 8-23. Thanks for pointing that out. I appreciate it.

Priscilla:
Okay, great.

Ivan:
So today’s subject, first you’re gonna wonder why I’m talking about this because it’s a story about Shark Tank [the television show]. And really, the message here is, you know, to give one good reason someone should do business with you. That’s the key. So several years ago, I started watching episodes of Shark Tank, it’s called different things in different countries. I know in the UK, it’s called Dragon’s Den, and so it’s called different things in different countries, where investors get a pitch, and they either agreed to invest in the business or not.

And I absolutely got hooked. When I started watching it, and it was probably 10 years ago, I started watching it. And so like I said, it’s a business reality series that shows entrepreneurs making business presentations to a panel of five investors called Sharks or Dragons, depending on what country you’re in. And they decide whether to invest in the person’s company. And I found that there were some serious business lessons, serious business lessons, that can be learned by viewing the show. And I saw one episode that really struck me, and it struck me as it relates to BNI.

There was an entrepreneur in that episode, Raven Thomas, who had started a food business called The Painted Pretzel, which specialized in pretzels covered with chocolate and other confectionaries; things I don’t eat any more, but they sure look good. Raven had a pretty good business and according to the panelists, her product was delicious. And after a fair amount of discussion, one of the Sharks, Lori Grenier, got to the end of the conversation and asked, “Why should we invest in you?” And here’s where I think there’s an important lesson in BNI.

I realized at that moment that this was the big question, and I knew Raven’s answer could make or break the deal. And Raven replied to Lori with, “Well, the main reason is that I have two little kids and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.” She went on talking about being a mom. And I immediately paused the show, it was on my DVR, it was recorded, I immediately paused the show, looked to my now late wife, Elisabeth, and I practically screamed, “She just blew it! She totally gave a relational answer to a bunch of transactional Sharks. They don’t care about the blah, blah, blah. They want something closer to show me the money than I love what I do.” 

And so the key here is that you’ve got to know your audience. Now, in BNI, we want you to be relational. That’s okay. But you have to know your audience. That’s the message I want to give to everyone here– Know Your Audience. And Beth replied to me, she said, “You have to know your audience when you’re talking about your business.” And her statement was spot on. Before I started to play the episode again, we talked about how crucial it was for Raven to speak the language that the Sharks speak if she wanted them to invest in her.

She needed to speak a language focused on opportunity, growth, return on investment, and, for them, cold, hard cash. Instead, Raven talked about how she felt about her business and how it related to her children. And her answer failed to include anything at all that the Sharks would relate to as serious analytical business investors looking for reasons to convince them that Raven’s business would be a wise financial investment.

Now, look, BNI is different than being on Shark Tank, I get that. But the message is not different. Know your audience. If you’re talking to one audience, maybe you want to talk about your kids and family. But this was not that audience. And, you know, I realize that some people may be thinking that’s not right. That’s not fair. That’s not the way it should be. Well, I’m not talking about what’s fair. I’m talking about what’s totally predictable. The moment she started to give that answer I knew she most likely lost all chances of getting funded because she was given a relational answer to transactional business professionals.

So I resumed watching the episode, sure that I would witness the train wreck which was about to ensue. But to my astonishment, one of the Sharks, Robert Herjavec, gave Raven a do-over. He allowed her to do a do-over, which really blew me away. And I really like this particular Shark. You know, if I were ever a panelist on a business show, I’d like to think my style would be a little bit similar to his. So Robert looked at Raven, and he gave her a chance to give a better answer when he said to her, “Okay, let’s do that again. Give us another answer. Give us an answer again.” And she took a moment, she thought, and then she said, “Well, a good reason to invest in me is that I had to walk away from a $2 million deal because I did not have the capital to fill the order. And that door is still open.”

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
That answer was a showstopper. It completely captured the Sharks’ attention. And within a few moments, Mark Cuban, one of the Sharks, who also owns the Dallas Mavericks professional basketball team, offered Raven the $100,000 investment she was asking for. And he also offered distribution of her product at his sports stadium, and at each location of the movie theater chain that he also owned. And of course, she said yes to his offer. And as a result, she expected her company’s sales to exceed $1.2 million the very next year.

The lesson to be learned here is that it’s absolutely imperative to know your audience and tailor your comments to suit the people you’re talking to. This is an extremely important lesson in both the business arena and the networking arena. I’m not saying to tell them what they want to hear. I’m saying tell them in a way that they will best be able to hear it; in a way that’s most relevant to them, addressing the talking points that they’re most interested in.

Now, you’ve got a lot of people in a BNI chapter. So maybe you need to change your talking points around and aim, you know, to have some transactional conversations as presentations and then have some relational. Because BNI is not Shark Tank, and I’m not trying to compare it to Shark Tank. But the lesson is the same. Understand the people that you’re talking to.

This is one of the main reasons I recommend that when you first meet people, you begin by asking them questions about themselves, prior to speaking at length about yourself. The more you know about the people you’re talking to, the better you’re able to craft your own message in a way that effectively resonates with them. And luckily, because of Robert, she was able to do that. I have one last thought before we wrap up, Priscilla. Do you have any thoughts before I do my one last comment?

Priscilla:
I’m a big Shark Tank fan. I watch it all the time. And it’s really interesting that you should use that as the example because I think that it’s a wonderful lesson that we can all learn from.

Ivan:
Thanks. And I agree and, you know, go back. Those of you listening, go back, you can find… I know one of the past Sharks from Shark Tank, Kevin Harrington. And if you recall, Priscilla, I think we did a podcast with Kevin on it.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
And so, Kevin is a really nice gentleman, and he did a podcast here on bnipodcast.com [Episode 575]. So my last comment to everyone listening is I’d love to hear your story about people knowing or not knowing their audience. It could be a story about you, it could be a story about someone else. If it’s about someone else, and if it’s about not knowing their audience, please don’t give their first and last name. If it is about knowing their audience feel free to give their first and last name. But give me some stories about people that either knew their audience or didn’t know their audience when they were talking networking or presenting at an event like a BNI meeting. That’s everything I have for today, Priscilla, thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: To succeed in business, you have to know your audience and tailor your comments to suit the people you’re talking to.

title.rendered: Episode 822: The Days I Dreamed of Being Where I Am Now

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Every businessperson goes through challenging times. When constraints threaten to limit your aspirations, dream big. Persevere. Let your dreams guide you to remarkable heights. Define yourself by your successes, not your failures.

The days you dreamed of being where you are now are a testament to your resilience and unwavering spirit. They are the building blocks of a life well lived, inspiring others regardless of your circumstances, and believing in your dreams, whatever they may be.

Share your stories of the dreams that guided you past obstacles and through challenging times in the comments below.

To learn more about the “Brody Moment,” listen to Episode 753.

Complete Transcript of Episode 822

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi, Priscilla. I’m at home in Austin. And sitting in my office. To my left, I’m looking out on the 10 acres I’ve got, and the deer and I’ve got some baby fawns running around out there. And on the right, I’ve got my swimming pool. And I’m just really enjoying my view, which is a good opening for this particular topic. The topic this week is that I Remember the Days I Dreamed of Being Where I Am Now. And as I sit here in my office, reflecting on my journey, I’m filled with gratitude and a profound sense of fulfillment. Looking back on my life, I realize the significance of remembering the days when I dreamed of being where I am.

And you know, my story begins in a blue-collar neighborhood where financial limitations were a constant presence. I think sometimes people look at someone who’s successful and just assume that they grew up with wealth, or, you know, had financial breaks as a young person. And that wasn’t the case for me. You know, growing up in a community that faced its fair share of challenges, I often found solace in my dreams, envisioning a future that extended beyond my current circumstances at that time. And these dreams provided me with the motivation to strive for something more, to break free from the financial constraints that threatened to hold me back.

When the time came for me to pursue higher education, to go to college, I was actually accepted to a private college, Occidental College in California, and I got a 50% scholarship. The problem was that, and I think I may have mentioned this in a previous podcast, the problem was that I couldn’t afford the other 50%. It was beyond my family’s means to pay for this, even half of a private college, plus all the living expenses and whatnot.

So faced with that financial hurdle, I made the difficult decision to turn down the acceptance to this private college and enroll in a community college instead. Which, you know, those of you in the United States know that community college is very inexpensive college, as college tuition goes. And so, it was a choice driven by, really, practicality and the realization that I needed to find a better way to build a better future without incurring overwhelming debt. I did plan on going to graduate school. So I knew I’d go into debt, but I didn’t want to go into debt, you know, in the first four years of my college.

So at the community college, which was Citrus College, by the way, in Southern California, and I had a great experience there; I really dedicated myself to my studies and took advantage of every opportunity that was available. And despite that initial setback, of not being able to go to the college I wanted, I refused to let go of my dreams and I nurtured my passion for learning, seeking knowledge, personal growth.

And it was really during this time that I discovered my deep interest in connecting with people, and relationships and networking, and its vast potential to transform lives. My time at community college taught me valuable lessons in resilience, adaptability and the importance of embracing opportunities regardless of their origin. You know, I’m telling you this story to members, because many of us go through challenging times, and it’s important to think back to the vision that you had, and it’s important that we hold the vision and not the obstacles throughout life.

So, through the dedication and the perseverance, I really built a solid foundation in business and began to establish connections and foster relationships within my community. And that led me to starting BNI, and I’ve talked about the BNI origin story and my “Brody Moment” in previous podcasts. And as BNI grew, I remained steadfast in remembering the days when financial constraints threatened to derail my dreams. And listen, I hit those in BNI. I had to mortgage my house a couple of times for BNI.

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
A lot of people don’t know this, but almost 20 years ago, I was really close to filing bankruptcy. You know, I was talking to bankruptcy attorneys.

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
So, you know, every businessperson goes through challenging times, and I was no different. And the reason I share this story is that I’m guessing, you if you’re listening to this, you’re no different. You’ve gone through challenging times. And the key is… the key always is to hold the vision, not the obstacles. And so, you know, this awareness fueled my desire to make a business, to make business opportunities accessible to individuals from all walks of life, including those facing similar challenges to what I had.

It became my mission to create a platform where individuals could connect, support one another, and rise together regardless of their socio-economic background. In the tapestry of life, I think it’s crucial to remember the days when our dreams seemed distant, due to the financial limitations or other hurdles we may be experiencing. And growing up in a blue-collar, low-income area, and making the difficult decision to forego a prestigious institution in pursuit of my dreams, really shaped my journey. And these experiences taught me resilience and resourcefulness and the value of seizing the opportunities wherever they arise.

Now, later, I did go to a prestigious university, I went to University of Southern California. I did have some scholarships, but you know, I went heavily in debt with student loans. But I was able to do it just in graduate school; I finished my bachelor’s degree with no debt. So today, after decades of hard work, and having gone to almost a decade of college and many decades of hard work, difficult choices, long hours in business, I truly remember the days that I dreamed of being where I am now.

And it’s also true that I thought I would be here today with the woman that helped me achieve my success. But life has led me in a different path than what I envisioned. And I’m determined to move forward with gratitude for the path that I’m on. Those of you who are listening to this – as you reflect on your journey, never forget the days when constraints threatened to limit your aspirations. Remember and embrace the dreams that emerged from humble beginnings, for they hold the power to drive you toward a brighter future. Cherish the lessons learned along the way as they cultivate the strength and determination needed to overcome obstacles. Define yourself by your successes, not your failures. I think that’s an important aspect of recognizing what you’ve achieved and where you’re at. Dream big. Persevere. Let your dreams guide you to remarkable heights.

And remember, the days you dreamed of being where you are now are a testament to your resilience and unwavering spirit. They are the building blocks of a life well lived, inspiring others regardless of your circumstances, and believing in your dreams, whatever they may be.

I’d love to hear people’s thoughts on this. This message was really a very personal message more than, you know, a lesson on networking or business. Well, it might be lesson on business… it’s more of a, I think, a personal lesson. And it’s important for us all to recognize that we all have obstacles and throughout life, if we can hold the vision and not the obstacles, we’re likely to get to where we want to get to in life. That’s my message for today, Priscilla, unless you have any thoughts.

Priscilla:
That’s a beautiful story, Ivan. And I think it’s really inspiring for lots of people, because there’s so many of us that don’t have the finances to go to a fancy university, but then can afford to go to community college and not to see that as something that’s really, really negative because there’s some fantastic teachers at community college. And it’s really a testament to you and where you’ve come from, and what you’ve done with your life. And you know, so many people admire you and love you and it’s great to hear about your beginnings, your humble beginnings.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. It’s very kind of you. You know, one of the best professors I ever had in all my years of college – undergraduate, graduate school, doctoral degree – one of the best professors I ever had was at community college; it was at Citrus College.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
And I learned a lot. I learned a lot from him, but I also learned a lot on how to teach. And he is an inspiration to me now. I teach Leadership in the MBA program at Concordia University, and I use a little bit of his style, and how he taught – I use now in the classes that I teach. So I think he’s been a big influence on me. So yeah, listen, I managed to get through undergraduate with no debt, but graduate school, I went into debt big time.

As a matter of fact, I’ll end on this one kind of funny story. Graduate school, you know, I paid off all my loans. It took about 20 years, and by the time I paid off my loans, literally, Priscilla, I’m not kidding, I got a letter that said, “Congratulations, Ivan Misner, you’ve paid off all your student loans.” One month later, Priscilla, I got a letter that said, “Congratulations, Dr. Misner, your daughter has been accepted to college. Here’s the bill.”

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
So I literally went 30 years paying college tuition because I paid off all of mine, it took me 20 years, and then it took me 10 years or so to pay off my three kids and their college tuition. Although one’s gone back and is back in school. Anyway. I thought that would be a funny story. That’s it, we’re over time. I’ll turn it back over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay great, Ivan. Well, thank you so much for sharing that. We all appreciate learning all about you. So thank you.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The days you dreamed of being where you are now are a testament to your resilience. They are the building blocks of a life well lived.

title.rendered: Episode 821: Impacting Lives Positively

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Rheet Shroff, Managing Director of BNI One Mumbai Region, joins Dr. Misner this week to explain why BNI is really about impacting lives positively.

People join BNI because they want more business. People stay in BNI not just for referrals but because of the relationships they’ve built.

BNI also changes the way young people look at business, because kids see their parents’ involvement with BNI and even pick up networking skills from them. And the more the youth are exposed to the philosophy of Givers Gain, to the idea that they can find happiness in the success of others, the better the world becomes.

Learn more about BNI India at https://www.bni-india.in.

Complete Transcript of Episode 821

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am actually in Austin this week. I’ve got two good friends who are going to be co-authors on a book, the working title is “Networking Is a Marathon, Not a Sprint,” and it’s Bharat Daga from India, and Phil Berg from the United Kingdom. So we’re going to be working on the book all week, and there might be some wine involved in the evenings.

Priscilla: (laughing)
Okay, well, it sounds like fun.

Ivan:
It does sound like fun. And I have a guest today and she’s also from India. And it’s 12:30 in the morning for her and I really appreciate her being on the podcast. Rheet Shroff has been associated with BNI for the last 13 years, initially as a member. She has been on the [chapter] Leadership Team three times and each time she has helped steer her chapter into the top three chapters of Mumbai, India. She became a Director Consultant nine years ago and is now the Managing Director of BNI One Mumbai Region, supporting over 100 chapters and more than 4700 members. In 2022, she was host at the BNI Global Convention in Singapore. She’s recently completed her master’s in business studies from the Harvard Business School and is married and has two children, two sons. Her passion is to help others achieve their passion and goals. And it’s a real pleasure to have her on. She’s going to talk today about BNI really being all about impacting lives positively. So Rheet, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Rheet:
Hey, good morning, Dr. Ivan Misner. It’s absolute pleasure to be here.

Ivan:
Well, it’s a pleasure to have you on and of course, you’re a woman entrepreneur. So let’s start with that. How does BNI impact lives of women entrepreneurs in your opinion?

Rheet:
Well, to be very honest, in this part of the world, it’s not very easy for women entrepreneurs to grow themselves, not just in business, but as a whole. I strongly believe that, you know, at least for me, BNI has been an eye-opener. It allowed me to grow myself as an entrepreneur. I started my business on a very, very small scale on my terrace with just two spotlights and four chairs. But thanks to BNI, I was able to grow it into a fully blown company. And on the way, along the way, I was actually able to deliver my second baby. Surprisingly, I think anywhere else, people would have expected me to, you know, take a step back. But again, big thanks to BNI, I was able to continue my entrepreneurship journey, and I was also able to continue my leadership journey, despite my pregnancy and my delivery and all of that.

Ivan:
Nice. You know, it really makes me feel good to see business people be successful all around the world. And of course, women business owners in particular, because it’s… you know, BNI is pretty much a level playing field. And it gives everyone an opportunity to build relationships and to generate referrals. It really varies from country to country. In many countries, about 60% of the membership is male and 40% female; in other countries it’s less. In India, it’s less than 60/40 has been my experience, having just got back from India a few months ago. Is that your experience as well? Probably less than 60/40?

Rheet:
Yes, it is. So also I have been fortunate enough to now lead One Mumbai [BNI Region] with 4700 members and when I took on as the Managing Director for BNI Mumbai, I was a little skeptical. I was a little nervous because being a woman leader, I did not know how, you know, my members would accept me, my leadership, but it’s actually been another eye-opener. Very, very happily, they’ve all accepted me. They have… they just follow, and they believe in me. And I think, that, for me, is a big, big win, more than anything.

Ivan:
Yeah. Yeah. And that’s what I hear from many people all around the world. So you had an interesting comment in what you sent me in advance. And I’ll just ask you the question:  Why and how does BNI take a member by surprise? Explain that to me.

Rheet:
So when we have members join BNI, they primarily do it for their business. And that’s the whole point of joining BNI. What they don’t expect is the depth of relationships that BNI gives them; the extended family that every chapter member gets within their chapter. I mean, it is very surprising for a member when, after a year, if they renew their membership, it’s not just because of BNI, but it’s also because of the relationships that they have created within the chapter. They feel that it’s a family and they cannot leave it and go. So that’s the surprise element that BNI gives to its members.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, that’s really true. I mean, we’ve done a lot of survey work with people on, you know, prior to joining a BNI chapter. And then after they’ve been in BNI, for a year or more. And a very small percentage of people say, “Well, I want to join because I want to build relationships with more people.” It’s almost all… it’s substantially, “Well, I want more business.” But what’s interesting is that when people are in for a year or more, the relationships become much more important in the surveys. People say, “Well, I love the organization because of the relationships I built, and of course, I wanted referrals and I’m getting referrals.” So they become much more equal after a year or so, based on the surveys that we’ve done in BNI. So you bring up a really good point.

Rheet:
Yeah.

Ivan:
I’ve got another question for you. How is BNI helping the youth and making communities a better place for young entrepreneurs to grow? I know you have some opinions on that.

Rheet:
So again, sharing a personal story. When I joined BNI, and I grew within BNI, my younger one who is now eight year old, a few months ago, someone asked him, “What do you be when you want to grow up?” And he said, “I want to be a BNI member like Mom, because it seems very enjoyable.”

Ivan: (chuckling)

Rheet:
So I think it’s not… BNI is just not changing the way we are doing business, but it’s also changing the way our youth is looking at business. In earlier days, I think a family-owned business, it was all about the entrepreneur and the business. But today, thanks to BNI, I think the family gets involved, the children are getting involved, and there’s lots more awareness on how you know they can actually have a family who helps them grow their business. So I think it’s the Givers Gain philosophy. I think never before that entrepreneurs realized that they could find happiness in others being successful. I think that was something that… that realization strikes only when you become a BNI member. And the more we can spread [that philosophy] in the youth, it’s only going to be a better world tomorrow.

Ivan:
Yeah. And you know, you bring up a really good point with children and their involvement or understanding what BNI is. Now, I’m going to ask you if you have any last thoughts before we wrap up, but I want to tell a quick story. I think I may have told this in a previous podcast. Quite a few years ago, we had a woman who attended a BNI meeting. And her daughter was out of school that day, and there was nobody to be with her, and her daughter was, I think 13 or 14. And so she brought her daughter with her and she had her sit in a booth aside from the meeting. And the President said, “No, no, no, have your daughter sit with everybody.”

And so this 14-year-old is sitting with everybody and they’re going around doing introductions. And the mother did her introduction, and then they were going to skip over the daughter. The daughter stood up and she said, “Hi my name is so-and-so and I’m a babysitter. But I’m a great babysitter. And let me tell you why I’m a great babysitter, because I don’t just leave your kids alone and you know, let them run wild. I do things with them. I play games with them. I’ll bake cookies for them. If they have homework to do, I’ll make sure they do their homework. I’m the best babysitter you’ll ever have. And if you know somebody that could use a babysitter, you know, call me at this number.”

Rheet: (chuckling)

Ivan:
She sat down and the mother was like, she turned to her, and she’s like, “Wow! That was impressive. I had no idea my daughter could stand up and speak like that.” And you know, you see things like that with children in BNI, you know, and it just makes me feel great. Any closing thoughts, Rheet, before we wrap up?

Rheet:
Oh, absolutely. All I’d like to say to everyone listening here, do not undermine the platform and the support system that BNI can give to us as humans, because for me, it’s not just about business, it’s about self-realization. It’s about growing as a leader, and as a human. It’s all in good karma, I believe that, and that’s what BNI stands for. So here for BNI for a long, long time to come.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. And I think you said it really well there at the end. To me, the whole idea of Givers Gain and BNI is that it’s not just a great way to get business, it’s an even better way to do business. And that’s what I’m seeing worldwide. So, thank you for being here. For anyone interested in BNI India, you can go to www.bni-india.in. That’s www.bni-india.in. Rheet, thank you for being on the BNI Podcast. I appreciate it.

Rheet:
Thank you so much, Dr. Ivan Misner. I will remember this for a long time.

Ivan:
Well, I appreciate you being here. And if you have another great topic in the future, let me know. Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, that was great. Thank you both very much for that great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: BNI is about impacting lives positively. People join BNI to get referrals. They stay in BNI not just for referrals but for the relationships.

title.rendered: Episode 820: The 7 Principles of Givers Gain (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This is a rebroadcast of Episode 676, which originally appeared on October 7, 2020. Offers mentioned in the episode may no longer be valid.

This week Dr. Misner introduces his new book, Infinite Giving: The 7 Principles of Givers Gain, co-authored with Greg Davies and Julian Lewis. This book lays out a way to apply the principles of Givers Gain to your life as well as your business.

“Givers Gain” is more than a phrase. It’s a way of living one’s life. When it’s applied properly, it will change your life—and it has the potential to change the world.

The formula to describe how Givers Gain and the 7 Principles fit together is

GG + 7P = IG

Givers Gain plus the 7 Principles equals Infinite Giving.

The 7 Principles

  1. Are They in Your Light?
  2. Give Without Expectation
  3. Give More Than Expected
  4. Give What You Can Afford
  5. It’s OK to Gain
  6. Stay Humble
  7. Show Gratitude

Pick up a copy of the book and leave a review or add your comments below.

Complete Transcript of Episode 676/820

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you? And where are you?

Ivan:
“How are you? And where are you?” I love hearing those words again, Priscilla. I’ve had so many people say to me, they miss Priscilla saying how are you? And where are you? So I decided that I should start talking about where I am virtually around the world. Because I’m still doing a lot of virtual events all over the world. So we’re going to continue with how are you and where are you but it’ll be where I am virtually until I can get back out and start to see people in-person. In addition to the many recorded visits that I’m doing all around the world this week, I am live in Utah for a big regional event this week. And I will be visiting many, many different regions for large events all over the world, and have been for the last six months. So thanks for asking, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Yeah, it’s great to have you back, Ivan, in your “where are you” mode.

Ivan:
Yeah, I’m glad we added it back. Because I’ve been doing virtual events for the last six months. And I don’t know why we didn’t think of talking about that. But I just want members to know I’m out and about and visiting many, many regions. It’s hard to do chapter visits because there’s over 9700 of them, but regional events I do.

So let’s jump into today’s topic on The 7 Principles of Givers Gain®. I love this topic. It is based on a book that has just been released called Infinite Giving. And the subtitle is The 7 Principles of Givers Gain. And the book was done with Julian Lewis and Greg Davies, who are my co-authors in the book. And to be honest, they came to me with the concept and suggested the book, and I was really excited to do this.

So our goal for this book, and I want to talk about some of the content that’s in the book—our goal for it was to lay out what we believe are The 7 Principles of Givers Gain and how everyone can apply them to their business and to life. Because Givers Gain is more than a phrase. It’s a way of living one’s life. It’s a perspective to view and interact with the world. It’s an attitude, not an expectation, and when it’s applied properly, it’ll change your life. And when it changes enough lives, it’ll change the world.

So in Givers Gain, we talk about The 7 Principles. And an easy way to remember, although the book is called Infinite Giving, an easy way to remember how Givers Gain and The 7 Principles fit together is this – we created a formula. The formula is GG + 7P = IG. And this is what it means: Givers Gain – GG, plus The 7 Principles equals Infinite Giving. And so the title for the book is Infinite Giving, but it is about The 7 Principles. And the idea is that if we respect the principles of Givers Gain, it has the potential to change the world. Full stop, end of discussion, it can change the world. And I’ve seen that with BNI. Here are The 7 Principles. I’m going to give you all seven at once and then I will talk briefly about each of them.

Number 1 is: Are They in Your Light? Are They in Your Light?
Number 2 is: Give Without Expectation.
Number 3 is: Give More Than Expected.
Number 4 is: Give What You Can Afford.
Number 5: It’s Okay to Gain.
Number 6: Stay Humble.
And Number 7: Show Gratitude.

So let’s jump in on some of these and talk about them and as I do, I just really want to thank Greg and Julian because they were fantastic to work with. And I’m really excited to have done this book with them. Let’s start with Number 1: Are They in Your Light? That concept comes from my book Who’s In Your Room? And in a nutshell, it’s really about life being about discernment, that you have to have discernment in the people that you trust and work with, in order to make Givers Gain work. And that Infinite Giving doesn’t mean you need to be an infinite victim. I meet people in BNI where they say, “Well, you know, Givers Gain, I’m giving and giving and I’m not getting anything.” That’s not what Givers Gain is all about, you don’t have to be a victim to make Givers Gain work. And we talk about this in the book and how to make Givers Gain effectively work for you. And it’s one of the principles I’ll be talking about here in a minute. The bottom line here is that the people in our lives determine the quality of our lives. And so it all starts with the quality of your network.

Number 2, the second Principle is to Give Without Expectation, that’s the second Principle. The first is who’s in your light. Second, Give Without Expectation. When we give, we should do so with no expectation of any direct return on our giving. A gift isn’t a gift if we expect something in return. So once there’s an expectation of something in return, then it’s really a transaction, not a gift. Givers Gain works best when there is no transactional expectation. And The 7 Principles, when followed correctly, allow the world to create Givers Gain communities like BNI that sustain themselves in a world of Infinite Giving, and where no one person gets exhausted, and gives up. It only takes a little time and effort to really set you apart from the rest of business people in a community when you have and practice a Givers Gain philosophy.

Number 3 is Give More Than Expected, Give More Than Expected. So the previous one, you might think that it’s contradictory, the previous Principle of Giving without expectation and then this is Give More Than Expected, but it’s not. Giving what you can afford is not contradictory or mutually exclusive. This is part of applying discernment to your actions. So, give more than you may be expected to, but never exceed what you can afford. Do one while respecting the other. That’s the important part of Principle

Number 3, Give More Than Expected. A good friend of mine, Lisa Nichols, wrote, and I know she’s put it on at least one of her books, your job is to fill your own cup so it overflows, then you can serve others joyfully from your saucer. I love that expression; it’s a great expression. And in a nutshell, it’s give from your saucer, not your cup. And that’s what we want you to do with one of these principles of Givers Gain. Okay, so the next Principle of Givers Gain, this is all part of giving what you can afford. And the next Principle, that’s Principle Number 4 is Give What You Can Afford. So you give from the saucer.

Number 5 is It’s Okay to Gain, Principle Number 5, It’s Okay to Gain. Givers Gain is two words and receiving is part of the philosophy of Givers Gain. So it’s not only, giving is not only part of it, but gaining is part of it. With Givers Gain, we all have the right to both give and to receive, to give and to gain. The confidence here is to be vulnerable and it comes from building trust within a team or group. And so you build confidence through vulnerability and building trust within your group. And when you do that, it’s okay to gain, people are comfortable with you benefiting, which means, and we talked about this in the book at length, you have to ask for what you need. And when you’ve built a meaningful relationship, you’ve earned the right to ask people for what you need. And that’s an important part of the principles of Givers Gain.

So Number 6, the sixth Principle, is Stay Humble. Humble people don’t think less of themselves, they just think of themselves less. If that sounds familiar, Priscilla, I talked about that in Episode Number 645 where I was talking about humility and I was actually working out some material for the book at the time. A humble person’s ego does not enter the room before they do, and go take a look at Episode 645, as well as the book Infinite Giving, for how humility comes into play for the concept of Givers Gain.

The 7th Principle is to Show Gratitude. And I talked about that as well in an episode, Episode Number 650. The importance of showing gratitude. And gratitude effect is not new age psychobabble, it’s science, there’s science behind it. And I talk about that science in Episode 650. And we talk about it at length in the book Infinite Giving. Gratitude, like so many other principles of success is simple, but it’s not easy. It’s hard to apply. And the gratitude effect doesn’t take much effort and costs little or nothing.

So these are The 7 Principles that make up Givers Gain and that lead to Infinite Giving. Givers Gain is, I believe, not only a great way to get business, it’s an even better way to do business.

And we do that when we give within a community. We do that by helping others. We give because we know that in the same situation others will do it the same for us. We give because we want to give back from where we’ve profited. We give because when we work together, we get bigger results. And we give because it feels good. These are some of the reasons why I think that our philosophy of Givers Gain is probably the most powerful Core Value that we have. And it’s certainly the first Core Value that we created in BNI. Day to day when we live in a Givers Gain community, I think life is just better for all of us. And the key here is to apply all of these principles in unison.

We have a great story towards the end of the book about a rock tumbler, and how in a rock tumbler you put in a lot of rocks to get these amazing results of these beautiful stones that come out of it. And the Principles of Givers Gain are a little like that rock tumbler. If you just throw one rock into a rock tumbler it’s not gonna come out shiny and beautiful. But by throwing the seven different stones, these seven different concepts, into the mix, you really come out with this concept of Infinite Giving, which we talk about at length in the book. And I’ll make sure that there’s a link here in this podcast.

So, in 10 minutes, you’ve gotten a summary of Infinite Giving. I would love for you to go to Amazon or to your local bookstore, and pick up a copy of Infinite Giving because it really will help you as a BNI member. And if you do, please, please do a review online because the reviews really help the books a great deal. So, any thoughts Priscilla, before we wrap?

Priscilla:
Well, first of all Givers Gain makes me feel very proud of BNI. It’s like what sets it apart, and it’s the first thing I always talk about with a visitor, somebody who’s new to the organization. So I think it’s great that you’ve written a book, and you can talk about it in more depth. And I think when you said, there’s a couple things, one is when you said that it just makes you feel good, I think that is so true. It’s a wonderful feeling, especially for networkers, people that take pride in putting people together and making things happen. I think that’s a really valid point. So I think I had something else to say, but now I’ve forgotten it (chuckling). So I’m gonna pass it back to you.

Ivan:
Well, thank you, Priscilla, I really appreciate that. You know, I really believe that you may not be able to make a world of difference as an individual, but you can make a difference in the world. Giving is contagious, and conscious giving can be infinite. And with that, I’ll turn it back over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan, thank you so much.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
