excerpt.rendered: Givers Gain plus the 7 Principles equals Infinite Giving. Learn how to apply Givers Gain to your life and change the world.

title.rendered: Episode 819: The Importance of One-to-Ones

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The purpose of a One-to-One is to build relationships with other networking partners to move through the VCP process: Visibility, Credibility, Profitability. As we learned in Episode 804, it’s never okay to say “No” to a One-to-One. Here are some ways to make it easier to say “Yes.”

  1. Schedule it well in advance.
  2. Schedule outside your busiest season.
  3. Have a purpose and format for the meeting.
  4. Stack multiple One-to-Ones on the same day at the same location.
  5. Ask for an agenda before scheduling.

Listen to Episode 570: More 1-2-1s = More Referrals to learn more about why One-to-Ones are important.

Complete Transcript of Episode 819

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Belfast, Northern Ireland, this week. I’m doing a BNI event and I’m attending a TLC meeting, a Transformational Leadership Council meeting, which was started by Jack Canfield. Jack’s become a good friend of mine over the years, and I go to most all of the meetings. The [BNI Podcast] guest that we had for the last two weeks, Sam Horn, I met in TLC. So it’s been a great place to meet incredible, successful people.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I’d love to hear more about that sometime. Because I know how much you like to go to those meetings.

Ivan:
Yeah, I always walk away with some great ideas.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm. Great.

Ivan:
So today, I’m going to be talking about the importance of One-to-Ones and I’m gonna start off by referring people to episode number 570. One of the things that I tell people is, people need to understand the WHY before they understand the HOW, and most of my podcast is going to be about the how. But if you go listen to episode number 570, you’re gonna get the why.

Why are One-to-Ones so important? And here’s the Reader’s Digest version: you’ll double the number of referrals you get. Period. Full stop. That’s why. And it’s based on a survey that was done in Europe of a BNI region, and they found that people who do four One-to-Ones a month, versus people who do one One-to-One a month, give twice as many referrals. More importantly, they get twice as many referrals as people who do only one One-to-One a month. Episode number 570. That’s, that’s the WHY. Now let’s talk a little bit about the HOW.

So for years, I’ve said that successful networking is really about building relationships with the people in your network. In addition to attending regular networking groups like BNI, it’s imperative that you have One-to-One meetings with your fellow members outside of the groups. This allows you to get a deeper understanding about their business, their products, their services, their customers, and to learn how you can refer them to the people you know, that’s the key. It’s also a way for them, of course, to do the same for you. However, some people decline to have One-to-One meetings with their networking partners. I’ve had several conversations with Tiffanie Kellog, who’s been on the BNI Podcast many times. And she’s in Central Florida [USA] and we discussed the question on one podcast, when is it okay to say no to a One-to-One? And the answer is? Never.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
It’s never okay to say no to a One-to-One, that is if you still want to generate referrals from that member. So what’s the purpose of a One-to-One? The purpose of a One-to-One is to build relationships with other networking partners to move through the VCP process – visibility, credibility, profitability. You know, depending on where you’re at [in the VCP process] doing those One-to-Ones help you move to the next level, particularly to get you to profitability where you’re passing referrals to each other. If we say no to a One-to-One meeting, we’re, you know, if we’re too busy for our fellow members, then it’s stopping that relationship and it’s putting the brakes on the VCP process. Think about it. If you’re too busy for a One-to-One, how are you going to have time to take care of the referrals that people want to give you?

So you never know what can come from a One-to-One, even if you don’t think that that person has much to contribute to you. I once went to a business meeting and sat next to a college student. And at first I thought, “Geez, what am I doing sitting next to this college student? There are all these business people that I want to sit next to.” And I thought to myself, “Okay, you know, buck up, buttercup. You’re an expert on networking–network with this student.” So I asked her a lot of questions, and I got her to open up. I think I may have mentioned this in a podcast years ago. And guess what happened? After the meeting, one of the business associates came up to me and said, “Thank you.” I said, “Why?” He said, “Nobody ever talks to my daughter like that. They all want to sit next to other business people but you got her to open up, and I really appreciate what you did.” I ended up making a good connection with the father and I hardly talked to him at all in the past, but now I made a great connection with him. You never know where a One-to-One can lead.

So, here are some tips to making a One-to-One easier, at least making it easier to say yes to a One-to-One.

First – schedule it well in advance. Find a date that’s convenient for both of you. One-to-one meetings are always more successful when both members have time to prepare.

Two – schedule outside your busiest season. We all understand that certain times of the year are busier for some professions, such as accountants during tax time, and retail stores during holiday season, so people appreciate when we can respect their time restraints.

Three – have a purpose and a format for the meeting. You can ask, “What’s the intention for the meeting?” Well, what are we looking for? To accomplish? And here… utilizing the GAINS exchange. You should know, if you’re a BNI member for any length of time, you should know what the GAINS exchange is. It stands for Goals, Accomplishments, Interests, Networks and Skills. Go back to that podcast that I mentioned earlier with the study and you’ll see a copy of the GAINS exchange there. It’s a very powerful format for a first-time One-to-One, and for follow up One-to-Ones occasionally.

Next – stack multiple One-to-One meetings on the same day at the same location. This saves you time traveling to multiple locations on different days. And you can also schedule virtual meetings back-to-back, which by the way, I do every year. I call it Founder’s Day. Founder’s Day is the last Thursday of June, where I do about 12 One-to-Ones in one day. And I urge you, on Founder’s Day, to do the same. It’s right around my birthday. And a few years back, I decided that the best birthday gift for me was to be able to talk to members. And so I started doing Founder’s Day. The last Thursday of June every year, do a couple of One-to-Ones. You don’t have to do a whole day like I like I do, but do at least a couple of One-to-Ones on Founder’s Day for BNI.

So you pick one or two days a week, block off time for meetings with your referral partners, or future referral partners.

And last – when someone asks you for a meeting, it’s okay to ask what they want to talk about and how long they think it’ll take. Having an agenda can help you decide how soon you want to schedule the meeting. The GAINS exchange makes a good agenda. And it also helps you, you know, figure out whether you need a One-to-One or just a quick conversation.

So just to wrap up, business networking is all about taking the time to build genuine, trusted relationships. Investing the time and the effort into getting to know your networking members through productive, referral-generating One-to-One meetings is the key to networking success. And if you don’t believe that, look at the data, check out episode 570 – More One-to-Ones Equal More Referrals. There’s hard data. If you do four One-to-Ones a month, you’re going to get twice as many referrals as if you only do one One-to-One a month. That’s my message for today. Priscilla, is there anything you’d like to add?

Priscilla:
Yeah, what I’d like to add, and I’ve learned this from experience, is one of the coolest things about doing the One-to-Ones is that you can then give referrals so much easier.

Ivan:
Yes.

Priscilla:
… to the people, because you get to actually, you get a real understanding of what they have to offer. And if it’s a successful One-to-One, and you feel really confident now that you like this person, you want to refer them, it just makes it so much easier.

Ivan:
It really does. And I mean, it feels great to give referrals, doesn’t it? I know when I go to a meeting, I feel great to be able to give a referral to somebody.

Priscilla:
Right. It’s a magical part of BNI, the One-to-One.

Ivan:
And the beauty of the One-to-Ones is that there’s also a direct correlation in getting the referrals, that you not only give twice as many referrals, you actually get twice as many referrals. Look at the data. It shows it very clearly. So it truly is, I mean, it’s an absolute perfect example of Givers Gain. You go to give and you also gain by doing One-to-Ones.

Priscilla:

Yeah.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah, it really is. It really is. Because from doing that One-to-One, you get to go over the next few weeks and actually pass referrals to your friends. We’re kind of wrapping this podcast up a little early. So I know the next thing you do is mention Misner Audio Programs. So I thought maybe I would just take a minute or two and talk about some of the audio programs that are available at www.misneraudioprograms.com.

There’s a great recording series called The Secret to Great Leadership Teams. And that’s a fantastic audio package aimed specifically at BNI. There’s also Networking & Word of Mouth, which is a generic audio package, an extended package. It’s a long package. Talking about networking in general, one of my favorites is The BNI Visitor Experience. You know, I listen to a lot of my stuff and I go, “Mmm, that doesn’t sound so good.” But when I listened to The Visitor Experience, I was like, “Yeah, that’s good. I did a good job.” So The Visitor Experience is a great audio package.

There’s an audio package on a couple of my books, Truth or Delusion; you know, it’s a summary of the book. And also Masters of Success, a summary of the book. And two other packages, Entrepreneurs: Everyday Leaders, is really about, you know, being an entrepreneur is being a leader. And I have BNI Networking Secrets, some great content from me about some of the secrets to being successful. If you’re interested, there’s a link to all the packages, and you get a pretty deep discount if you get them all and at the same time. I mean, a really big discount, like it’s a fraction of getting them individually.

And of course, here on BNI Podcast, as you’re going to mention, Priscilla, if you use Ivan50, you get 50% off on everything and all the money goes to the BNI Foundation. So I think I’ve used up all my time this week. Priscilla, thank you so much. You always do a great job on these. I appreciate you.

Priscilla:  
Oh, thank you, Ivan.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Learn how to make it easier to say “Yes” when another BNI member asks to schedule a One-to-One. You’ll get twice the referrals if you do.

title.rendered: Episode 818: Talking on Eggshells with Angry People

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Sam Horn returns this week with tips for dealing with people who take their anger and frustration out on us. The key is Interpersonal Situational Awareness (ISA). Before you say anything, ask yourself, “Is it good timing? Is it welcome? Is it additive?”

Words to Lose

  • “Don’t blame me!” (Impatience)
  • Reactive
  • Ignore the room
  • Find fault

Words to Use

  • “How would I feel?” (Empathy)
  • Proactive
  • Read and lead the room
  • Call time out: “Let’s not do this. It won’t help.”

Read Sam’s book, Talking on Eggshells, to learn more soft skills for dealing with hard conversations. And be sure to listen to Episode 817 with Sam Horn if you haven’t done that yet.

Complete Transcript of Episode 818

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at home in Austin, Texas. It’s the middle of July and it is hot as blue blazes here. So the only thing hotter is August, which is really hot. So I have brought back on, and it’s rare that I have a guest two weeks in a row, but I’ve asked Sam Horn, a really good friend of mine who I see almost every week, and she’s a past member of the Transformational Leadership Council. I’ve asked her to come back again and talk about some material from her book, “Talking on Eggshells,” and this week we’re going to talk about Talking on Eggshells with Angry People.

Sam is the CEO of the Intrigue Agency. Her three TEDx talks and 10 books, including “Tongue Fu!, and “POP!”; she also did “Got Your Attention?” and one of my favorite books, “Someday Is Not a Day in the Week.” And her latest book is “Talking on Eggshells: Soft Skills for Hard Conversations”. She has been featured in The New York Times and has taught to Oracle, Intel and Accenture. And we are definitely going to have a link to her book here on this podcast just like we did on the last podcast. So take a look at it. I highly, highly recommend her book. Sam, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Sam:
Oh, boy, thank you, Ivan. And I’m rockin’ and rollin’. I hope people are ready to go; they’ve got their paper, they’ve got a vertical line down the center, because we’re going to talk about some more words to lose and words to use. Sound good?

Ivan:
Yeah, it does. And if you haven’t listened to last week’s podcast, I suggest you listen to it and then come back to this so that you have a quick start on what she’s talking about. So let’s start with my first question for you, Sam. What can we do if people are taking their anger or frustration out on us? Which you know, when you run an organization of over 300,000 people, guess what? That happens. And that happens locally in each chapter.

Sam:
That’s right. Well, 60-second story to show how this works, and then we’ll fill in those two columns. So my Aunt Kay is 84 years old and she volunteers at a hospital five days a week. She even did this during COVID. And I said, “What was it like?” She said one word, “Stressful.” I said, “Well, put me in a situation where someone was stressing you out, taking their anger out on you.” And she said a woman ran into the ER, held her phone up [and] said, “I just got a text from my daughter. She’s in the ER, she was in an accident. I’ve got to see her.” Well, Aunt Kay called the ER; someone was already with the daughter. And you may remember the policy was – there was only one visitor per patient per day. She had to tell the mother she couldn’t get in to see [her] daughter. The woman lost it. She’s screaming, sobbing.

Now, put in the left hand column “impatient” and put in the two words “how rude.” How rude! Why are you blaming me? This isn’t my fault. I can’t control it. However, instead of being impatient, Aunt Kay use these four words. Ready? Over on the right, write “How would I feel?” How would I feel if it was my daughter in the ER and I couldn’t get in to see her. It moved her from impatience to empathy, from contempt to compassion. It gave her the incentive to turn ‘there’s nothing’ into ‘something’. She called the ER back; she said, “Who was with the daughter?” It was the Uber driver who had walked the daughter in.

Ivan: (chuckling)

Sam:
She was able to thank him, explain it. And now Ivan, once again, when people are taking their anger, we can react, “Hey, it’s not my fault. I didn’t make the rules.”

Ivan:
Yeah.

Sam:
What would I feel? And now we’ve got the incentive to turn there’s ‘nothing’ into there’s ‘something’.

Ivan:
What a great suggestion. I have to say, I probably didn’t follow that when Elisabeth was sick and had to go into the hospital. It was you know, we didn’t know it at the time, but she only had a few days to live. They wouldn’t let me come in because of COVID and I, very calmly, I didn’t get upset, I very calmly said, “I think you’ll need two of your biggest orderlies to block me from walking in with her right now.” And he looked at me like, “Okay, fine.”

Sam: (chuckling)

Ivan:
But I did say it calmly at least. What a great suggestion. I love that idea; it’s very good. So, Sam, you think that the ISA, and you talk about in the book – Interpersonal Situational Awareness, ISA is a rare and much needed job skill that can give us a competitive advantage. And I think that’s very true for entrepreneurs. What it is ISA? And give us an example.

Sam:
You know, Desmond Tutu actually has a quote that summarizes it the best I’ve ever heard. He said, “We’ve got to stop pulling people out of the river. We’ve got to go upstream and find out where they’re falling in.”
So, over on the left [side of your paper], put “reactive”; over on the right, put “proactive”. And over on the left, put “ignore the room” and over on the right, put “read and lead the room”.

Now I get to tell a story about myself. I got a chance to work with Rod Laver, [tennis] Grand Slam Champion Rod Laver, right out of college. And so they appointed me to represent Palmetto Dunes, Laver Emerson Tennis, at our Hilton Head Island board meeting. Oh, Ivan, forgive me – I was young, I was eager, and I was contributing my ideas. And after about three meetings, the general manager of the Hyatt pulled me aside and said, “Sam, I think you might want to sit on your hands.” Ivan, I never heard that expression before. I said, “What do you mean?” He said, “Every person in that meeting is senior to you. And you’re doing more talking than any of them.” He said, “From now on, before you say anything, ask yourself, is it good timing? Is it welcome? Is it additive?” And part of ISA – Interpersonal Situation is reading the room? Have I done more talking than my share?

Ivan:
Can you say those three things again? I want everyone to hear that.

Sam:
Yes. Is it good timing?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Sam:
Is it welcome?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Sam:
Is it additive?

Ivan:
Yeah, very good. All right. I interrupted you. Sorry.

Sam:
No, it’s just, you know, when Jeff Weiner, who was the CEO of LinkedIn said, this is the number one skill gap in the world. We can hire coders, we can hire techies, we cannot hire people with this ability to pay attention to nuance. Wait a minute, I’m talking to this person and I can tell, they’re tapping their foot, that they want to get the heck out of there.

Ivan:
Right.

Sam:
Stop talking. I’m requesting something, and I can see that they’ve got their arms crossed, and the answer is going to be no. It’s like — start responding and getting ahead of the room. You know what I call this, Ivan?

Ivan:
What?

Sam:
Think upstream.

Sam:
I love that. I love that. We have a phrase that I’ve been using in BNI for many years that’s somewhat related. And it particularly applies to networking, is that we have two ears in one mouth and use them both proportionately… we should use them both proportionally. So listen more than you’re talking, especially if you’re talking to several people. Good. I love that — ISA, and you talk about that at length in your book. Yeah?

Sam:
I sure do. And with lots of ways, once again, to be preventative. To anticipate when something’s going to go wrong, and to keep it from going wrong.

Ivan:
Well, talk about going wrong. If something has gone wrong, and people are blaming each other, you have a way we can be a pattern interrupt and motivate people to find solutions rather than fault. What is that?

Sam: Yep. So let’s put on the left [side of our paper] ‘find fault’. We’ve all had that situation – something’s gone wrong, people are finger pointing, you know, fault finding, et cetera. Now is the time to do what’s called a pattern interrupt. And guess what, Ivan? We don’t start talking over them. Because if people are yelling, and we talk over them, what will they do?

Ivan:
They’ll yell louder.

Sam:
Right. The voice of reason gets drowned out in the commotion. So instead… you play sports; in fact, you’re very big into martial arts, right, Ivan?

Ivan:
Yes.

Sam:
Okay. Well, anyone who plays sports knows what a referee does when there’s a fracas, you know, he makes a “T”, right? Make a T with your hand and say, “Time out,” and then say these magic words, “Let’s not do this.” We could spend the rest of the afternoon arguing about who dropped the ball on this, it won’t get this client back, you know, or this won’t help. “This won’t help”. You know, do you see how stop it with a hand motion? And then say, “Let’s not do this, this won’t help.” And then you can actually use something that John F. Kennedy said. He said, “Our task is not to fix the blame for the past. It’s to fix the course for the future.”

Ivan: 
Mm, hmm. Very good. We have a mutual friend who wrote a book called “The Solutions Focus”. And that book talks a lot about focusing on the solutions rather than the problems, which is kind of what you’re talking about.

Sam:
Yes, agreed.

Ivan:
Yeah. I love that. You know, many of us use lots of time in meetings, in-person or online. What are your talking on a shelf tips for making them more purposeful, and productive?

Sam:
You know, Ivan, I know that your podcast goes out to more than 75 countries, more than 300,000 people, and this tip is universal. Richard Branson said, “Time is the new money.” And I think time is the new trust, and the clock starts ticking the second we start talking. And if we want people to trust us, we will keep our time promises.

So in particular, here are four things we can do to make our meetings more purposeful and productive so that we keep our time promises. Number one is to have rules. It’s like Ivan, you know, imagine if we just drove out on a road and there’s no crosswalk, there’s no traffic lights, it’d be chaos, we wouldn’t be safe. Guess what? Most meetings have no rules. People can talk as long as they want to, they can interrupt each other. So post rules in advance so that there is etiquette on the call so people are treating each other with respect. Next one, we always start on time.

Ivan:
I need to say, I think that’s one of the reasons that BNI has been so successful, is that everything is timed.

Sam:
That’s right.

Ivan:
The amount of time that you have to introduce yourself the amount of time that you have to give a referral the amount of time to be the featured speaker, everything is timed, which makes it very, very effective in getting through the whole process.

Sam:
See, Ivan? It’s huge. It is… go back to the power of that. Do you know what the definition of anxiety is?

Ivan:
What’s that?

Sam:
Not knowing. Two words. If we don’t know how long this is going to take, when we don’t know when they’re going to put a sock in it; when we don’t know when we’re gonna have a chance to talk, we’re in a constant state of anxiety. We can’t focus. So by BNI keeping its time promises, then people can trust, I know it’s going to start on time; I know it’s going to end on time. I know that person is going to stop in two minutes and then it’s my turn. And it really does result in a purposeful, productive meeting and people show up, because they’re not in a state of anxiety about whether this is going to be a waste of their time.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, we’re out of time. And I know you had one or two more points. You want to throw those out real quick?

Sam:
Okay, real quick is that I know people from around the world go to Burning Man. I would recommend that everyone look up on search the rules of Burning Man. Because, come on, you’re out in the middle of the desert, it could be chaos. And yet they have rules about gifting. They have rules about picking up your trash; they actually have people on their hands and knees crawling around in the sand, picking up the smallest pieces of trash. So I suggest as an exercise, look up the rules of Burning Man and think about how we can adapt those rules for our club, for our company, for our culture, and ask people to vote on them. Because it will create this trusted culture where people have each other’s front and back.

Ivan:
Oh, that’s a great suggestion. And our policies for [BNI] members have been voted on by the members. That’s why we have a Board of Advisors. So that’s exactly what we did to build the organization. Listen, we’re out of time, Sam, and I didn’t give your website last time verbally, but it will be on the transcript. Everyone:  Sam’s website is www.samhorn.com. That’s S-A-M-H-O-R-N as in ‘Nancy’,  SamHorn.com. And I’m sure your book is highlighted. Right there on that front page, I’m guessing, Sam?

Sam:
You would be right.

Ivan:
Okay. I really recommend you take a look at this book. It’s a great book. Sam is an amazing human being. She has great content. And it’s been a pleasure having you on for the second time, Sam. Thank you so much.

Sam:
It’s a joy. I hope people find it interesting and useful, Ivan.

Ivan:
They will. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Great. I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Sam Horn returns this week with tips for dealing with people who take their anger and frustration out on us.

title.rendered: Episode 817: Talking on Eggshells with Complainers

content.rendered:
Synopsis

How do you handle chronic complainers? Sam Horn joins Dr. Misner to share tips from her new book, Talking on Eggshells: Soft Skills for Hard Conversations. Sam divides her approach into “words to use” and “words to lose.”

Lose

  • Explanations: they sound like excuses
  • Curse words
  • Stop
  • Other people own our mood and attitude

Use

  • The A Train: Agree, Apologize, Act
  • Code words
  • Start
  • We own our mood and our attitude

Learn more about Sam Horn and download your free copy of Talking on Eggshells at www.samhorn.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 817

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Charlotte at Global Headquarters this week, having an opportunity to talk to staff and BNI members and directors which is always fun. I really enjoy these visits to Charlotte.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
I have a guest on the podcast. I think this is the first time she’s actually been on the podcast but I did a quick search on her name and I have four pages of podcasts where I have mentioned this very close friend of mine, Sam Horn. Love Sam; I’ve known her for a number of years. She’s the CEO of the Intrigue Agency. Her three TEDx talks and 10 books, including Tongue Fu! (I love that title) and POP!–which, she mentioned BNI in “POP!” before we ever knew each other, I appreciate that–Got Your Attention, and one of my favorite books of hers is SOMEDAY Is Not a Day in the Week, and her latest book, Talking on Eggshells, (I have a copy of it right here) — have been featured in the New York Times and taught to Oracle, Intel and Accenture. Sam Horn, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Sam:
Thank you, Ivan. I’m looking forward to swapping some stories and sharing some insights with your members around the world.

Ivan:
Well, I am too, and I think I’ve said this on previous podcasts, Sam and I have a what we call Writing Wednesday every, almost every Wednesday, we get together for the morning. And the truth is it should be called Talking Wednesday because we mostly talk.

Sam: (chuckling)

Ivan:
But we do talk a lot about writing. And many of the blogs that I’ve done and podcasts are as a result of conversations that I’ve had with this very person. Sam Horn has given me… she’s nudged me and said, “Have you written that down?” I have to admit, “No,” and then I write it down. So I’m really, really glad to have her on the podcast. We’re gonna be talking about some concepts out of her book, Talking on Eggshells. What a great title that is, Sam. You know that already.

Sam:
You know, thank you. I was doing a Tongue Fu! workshop and a gentleman worked for an attorney. And he said, “He’s so demanding.” He said, “Everyone, we just never know what’s going to set him off. We’re just all walking on eggshells all day long.” I said, “Well, you’re not walking on eggshells. You’re talking on eggshells.” Voila, there was the title.

Ivan:
Yeah. So Sam, what can we say when people are complaining? And by the way, sometimes people complain as though it’s an Olympic event. And I’ve checked, it’s nowhere in the Olympics. But yet, I know gold medalists at this. So what can we say when people are complaining?

Sam:
Well, first thing — unless people are driving, I hope they quickly grab a piece of paper and a pen and put a vertical line down the center. Because Ivan, you know that the way my brain works, I juxtapose everything. It’s the quickest way to make complex ideas crystal clear, and to show the shift. So at the top of the left hand column, put Words to Lose. And on the top of the right hand column put Words to Use. So let’s talk about when people complain — over in the left hand column, don’t explain because explanations come across as excuses. They actually make people angrier because they feel we’re not being accountable. Over in the right hand column. Put the A-train: A for agree, A for apologize, A for act.

Let’s say a meeting starts late. Maybe there’s not a quorum; maybe there’s a traffic delay; maybe there’s a storm or something like that. And someone says, “This meeting was supposed to start 15 minutes ago.” “Oh, I know. But we’ve got…” Uh-uh, no; explanations come across as excuses. A for Agree: “You’re right. The meeting was supposed to start 15 minutes ago.” A for Apologize: “And I’m sorry you’ve ended up waiting.” A for Act: “And we’re going to start, jump right into the agenda. Thank you for your patience.” Do you see how the A train expedites complaints? Explanations aggravate them.

Ivan:
Yeah. Yeah, those are great suggestions. I had a boss once who had one less chair in the room than the number of people in the meeting. So if you were the last person in, you had to stand for the whole meeting. People got there early all the time.

Sam:
Okay, Ivan, you know what I’m going to say?

Ivan:
Have I written that down? I think I have actually, I’ll double check. I’m pretty sure I have.

Sam:
Ivan, I’ve seen your PowerPoint slides, and when you speak, you don’t just share insights, you share stories with images. So I already see a PowerPoint slide with this room, you know, with like, six chairs at the table and seven people in the room or something like that, right?

Ivan: (laughing)
I may incorporate that into a speech someday, but I’m pretty sure I’ve done a blog on it. He was a good boss, but that was pretty brutal when he did that, but it got people there on time.

Alright, second question for you. How can we keep our cool in the heat of the moment? That’s a big one. For me anyway.

Sam:
Well, guess what? Over on the left, put “swear”. (chuckling) Right? It’s like, often in the heat of the moment, it’s easy to react and to say what’s on the tip of our tongue, which makes things worse. So over on the right, we’re going to have a “code” word. So on the left is a curse word,  on the right is a code word. And Ivan, I’m going to turn the tables because I’m going to ask you what you say, in the heat of the moment, because you have a wonderful story about this. Remember, instead of using a curse word, what do you say?

Ivan:
Yeah, I say… I literally say the words, “Bad word. Bad word, bad word.”

Sam: (laughing)

Ivan:
And what I love about that phrase, first of all, I was in the middle of a medical procedure, and I woke up in the middle of it, (I) hurt like heck. And I said out loud, “Bad word, bad word, bad word!” just like that. And the doctor laughed, and he said, “Let’s put him back under now.”

Sam: (laughing)

Ivan:
What I love about using this, and you can use it from the stage, is that everybody fills in the blank, right? With whatever bad word they’re thinking of. But you don’t offend anybody when you use that phrase.

Sam:
So Ivan lets everyone unpack that. Is that Elvis Presley [who] said, “When things go wrong, don’t go with them?” And over on the left, all of these words and reactions we’re talking about, they make things worse. When things go wrong, we go with them. Over on the right, look at what you’re doing. You’re setting an example of integrity for people. You know, because if you swear on stage, maybe your members go back and they think, well, our Founder did it, I guess it’s okay.

Ivan:
Exactly.

Sam:
And it’s setting up an unintended ripple effect. So yes to responding on the right with a code word that lets us express our frustration without doing or saying something that sets a bad precedent.

Ivan:
Yeah, very true. You know, I don’t know if you remember watching the old NCIS [television] series years ago. I mean, it’s still on but the old one, Gibbs, Agent Gibbs would kind of smack people up the backside of the head. Do you remember him doing that?

Sam:
Oh, yeah, yeah.

Ivan: He’d smack them upside the back of the head. So I don’t suffer fools easily. Sometimes I really, really feel like just popping somebody at the back of the head. But I’ve learned over the years that’s called assault, not leadership.

Sam: (laughing)

Ivan:
Leadership is much more effective. So, great points. Third question for you. You believe that words have ripple effects, for better or for worse. What are some of the words we should lose? And what are some of the words we should use instead? And I think you’ve already given some of that.

Sam:
You bet. Well, first, a 60-second story, because this is one of the most powerful examples of the ripple effect of words. I was visiting my son, Andrew, in New York, and we were getting caught up in the living room and one-year old Hero crawls across the floor, pulls himself up on a guitar and starts pounding on the guitar strings. Now over on the left, Andrew could have reacted, he could have yanked the guitar away. He could have said, “No”; he could have said, “Stop pounding on the guitar.” All of that would have reinforced the dreaded behavior and made Hero feel bad. Ivan, guess what Andrew said? One word.

Ivan:
What?

Sam:
Gentle.

Ivan:
Mmmm, gentle,

Sam:
I saw Hero’s face transform in front of me. He reached back to the guitar and went strum, strum, strum. And in that moment, he made music because Andrew shaped his behavior, instead of shaming his behavior.

Ivan:
So really, ask for what you want, not what you don’t want.

Sam:
That’s exactly right. And over on the left, put the word “stop”. Because when we tell people stop being late, or don’t interrupt, or don’t get mad, once again, we’re reinforcing the dreaded behavior. So over on the right, “start”. And now once again, we’re being a coach instead of a critic, and we’re shaping their behavior instead of shaming it, and we’re showing them how to do it better, instead of making them feel bad.

Ivan:
Yeah, I love that. Really, really good advice. And as you know, there’s so many things when you’re raising kids, that you do wrong. What a great example of fantastic parenting for him to simply say, “Gentle.” It’s a brilliant way of redirecting Hero without being assertive about it. A gentle way of doing it.

Sam:
Exactly.

Ivan:
Yeah. Alright, I have one last question for you. What’s the single best thing we can do? And I sometimes I hate these questions, “What’s the single best thing?” because I always believe it’s a recipe. But what’s one of the best things, and a recipe is okay, that we can do to set an example of integrity and proactive grace for our employees and customers?

Sam:
Well, over on the left put “other people own our mood and attitude.” And how often do we say, “That person makes me so mad, or that person makes me miserable, or that person drives me crazy.”? And Joyce Meyer said, “Life may give you a cactus; you don’t have to sit on it.”

Both: (chuckling)

Sam:
And when we give other people the power to push our hot buttons or make us crazy, our life will be a seesaw. It will always depend on who we’re with. So over on the right: “we own our mood and our attitude.” And I suggest we all come up with a philosophy. And we say that in these moments, I will be the quality person I want to be no matter what. I will keep my cool no matter what. What is something you almost say as a mantra in the moment to help you be the kind of person you want to be, even when other people aren’t?

Ivan:
Mmmm. What a great idea. That sounds like a recipe to me.

Sam:
Uh-huh, there you go.

Ivan:
There’s a few things that I think are powerful. Any last thoughts before we wrap up?

Sam:
Yeah, it’s, you know, Supreme Court Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg… You know, I think we all agree the world is really getting increasingly divisive. And she was asked by a reporter, “How is it you go out with Judge Scalia? I mean, you’re on opposite sides of the political spectrum.” Ivan, she said six words, you know what they were?

Ivan:
What?

Sam:
“We are different. We are one.” And may we all remember in the moment, that even if we don’t like how this person is treating us, what they’re doing or saying, in the long run, we are all one. Let’s treat them with the compassion and the respect they deserve.

Ivan:
Ahh, true wisdom. Listen, Sam, I’m going to have you back on next week. It’s rare that I have somebody two weeks in a row, but you have given more information in 10 or 12 minutes than many of my guests have over the years. And so I got to have you back again next week. I want to thank you so much for being here. And we’ll talk again next week.

[Learn more about Sam Horn at www.samhorn.com]

Sam:
I look forward to it.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you both for so much great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: When people complain, don’t explain: get on the A-Train with these communication tips from Sam Horn’s book Talking on Eggshells.

title.rendered: Episode 816: OMG–I’m an Introvert? (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 97, which was first published on March 25, 2009. Some links may no longer work and offers may have expired.

Synopsis

This week Dr. Misner is visiting BNI chapters in Paris and Scandinavia.

To his considerable shock, Dr. Misner’s wife recently pointed out to him that he’s actually an introvert, despite his extremely public life. A little research revealed that he’s what’s known as a “situational introvert”: reserved around strangers, but very outgoing in the right context.

So it’s no surprise that BNI is perfect for situational introverts, because it creates a small group with a structured environment. Because you meet with your BNI chapter week after week, the other members are not strangers, and you can relax around them. That means that whether you’re an introvert or an extrovert, you can be great at networking.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcription of BNI Podcast Episode 816/97

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I am joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan, and how are?

Ivan:
Hello. I’m doing great. I’m calling you from Paris. I’m in Paris visiting BNI chapters this week, and later this week, I’ll be going to Scandinavia to do a presentation at the Ice Hotel in Scandinavia.

Priscilla:
Oh, I’m so jealous.

Ivan:
It is fun. It’s going to be fun in Scandinavia. We’re having a great time here in Paris.

Priscilla:
So what does OMG mean?

Ivan:
All right. If you have no idea what OMG means, go ask a teenager. That’s how I learned what it meant when I was getting texts from my kids, OMG.

Okay. Well, just so we don’t offend anybody, I’m going to go with Oh, my goodness.

Now, let’s talk about the introvert thing. OMG, Oh, my goodness; I’m an Introvert. That’s the tile of today’s podcast, and let me explain.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
My wife and I were having a really relaxing dinner one night recently, and we were sitting around the kitchen table, and we were talking when I made some offhanded comment about being an extrovert, and it fit into the context of the conversation.

She looked over at me; she said, “Honey, I hate to break it to you, but you’re an introvert.”

I smiled and said, “Yeah, sure. I’m an introvert.” It’s like insert laugh track here. “I’m an introvert, right.”

And she looked at me, and she quite earnestly said, “No, really, you’re an introvert.”

And I protested. I said, “Come on. I’m a public speaker. I do these BNI podcasts. I’m the founder of the world’s largest networking organization. I am not an introvert. I can’t be. You’re joking, right?”

And she absolutely insisted that I was an introvert, and then she proceeded to share with me all the ways that I have introverted tendencies. Well, I have to admit that I was really taken back by this, and all of the examples she gave me were really on the mark, but I still couldn’t believe I was an introvert.

On the other hand, I’d been married to her for 20 years. There’s a chance she might actually know me pretty well. So I went off the next day to do some research, and I did an Internet search, and I found a test that tells you whether you’re an introvert or an extrovert. Was I in for a shock. The test said that I was a situational extrovert. It explained it, I was something of a loner, that I was a loner, that I was reserved around strangers, but that I was very outgoing in the right context.

It was at that moment that I said, “Oh, my goodness; I’m an introvert.” In the haze of my surprise, some really important things came into clarity for me. I’m 52 years old; BNI is 24 years old; and for the first time, it really struck me one of the really important reasons why I started BNI more than two decades ago. Because the bottom line was – I think most people know this story. I was a management consultant that I was looking for referrals, and I put together people that I trusted and liked, and we met and we passed referrals. Someone came who couldn’t join and asked if I’d open up a second chapter, and I said yes, and we did the second one, and that led to a third one and on and on.

But the bottom line that I never really thought about this until I took this test, I was naturally uncomfortable meeting new people, particularly at Chamber functions or other business functions. So this approach created a system that enabled me to meet people in an organized structured networking environment that did not require that I actually talk to strangers.

Priscilla:
Interesting.

Ivan:
Oh, my goodness; I’m an introvert.

Now, this is funny. If any of the listeners have ever seen me at a BNI event, I generally, when I go to regions of BNI, I ask the BNI director to have someone walk me around and introduce me to visitors and to member so that I can connect with as many people as possible. That’s what I would always tell them. But in reality, it’s because I’m uncomfortable just like anyone else, I thought. I’m uncomfortable just walking around introducing myself alone. And when I came to that realization, it’s like, oh, my goodness; I’m an introvert.

And then I realized the whole notion of the material – and I’ve talked about it in podcasts, I’ve written about it for years – the whole idea of acting like a host not a guest and volunteering to be the ambassador at a Chamber event, or be the visitor host at a BNI group. These are great ways that other people who are introverted can make sure to introduce themselves to other people in a comfortable environment. And then it struck me, “These are the ways I use to move around more comfortable at networking events, not just ways that I recommended for those poor introverts out there to network.

Oh, my goodness; I am an introvert. Who would have thought? Well, okay, besides my beautiful wife, who would have thought? Now more than ever, I truly believe that whether you are an introvert or an extrovert or a situational extrovert, you can be great at networking, both introverts and extroverts have strengths and weaknesses. If you can find ways to enhance your strengths and minimize your weaknesses, anyone can be a great networker, even an introvert like me.

Priscilla:
That’s great. Well, personally, I’ve always felt rather shy, and so I feel sort of similar to what you’re talking about. And I think you’re right, that BNI creates an atmosphere where you don’t have to worry about being shy.

Ivan:
You don’t. And I think this is really amazing, and it sort of an epiphany for me because I’ve always thought of myself as an extrovert, because I certainly come across extroverted in many situations, but in other ways – and there’s a whole laundry list of things that indicate that I’m not. I love being home. And if you look at some of my readings, particularly my blog, I talk about where’s my favorite place on the planet, home; I want to go home. When the phone rings, I don’t want to answer it; I don’t want to talk. I like to go home and spend time with my family or go up to my lodge up at Big Bear and spend time up there.

So there are a number of natural tendencies that are, in fact, introverted, but in certain situations where I feel comfortable, I come out more. But I’ve surrounded myself with an environment that’s all based on relationships. When you think about it, and this really makes sense, because I didn’t create a network that was all about big events; I created a network that was all about people getting together, getting to know each other and trust each other, and then doing business with each other.

But that’s not necessarily a high extroverted kind of approach. So it kind of all put the pieces together for me, and I think what’s important is for the average member – and those extroverts don’t care; they’re going to be out there marketing and selling, but I really want to talk to the introverts here, the people who feel shy about introducing themselves to others, the people who don’t feel comfortable, necessarily, speaking. Now, I do because I took six years of courses on speaking. Maybe that’s why I’ve gotten to be reasonably decent at it. So I did things to push myself out of that shell. But in BNI, introverts can excel because it’s all about building the relationship, and it was really an epiphany for me.

What’s really so funny is that my wife like totally knew it. She was like, “Oh, no, honey, you’re an introvert. You just act like an extrovert out there, but you’re an introvert.” She got it; I didn’t. I learned something about myself, and I hope the BNI members listening to this podcast can get something out of it as well.

Priscilla:
Well, that’s great. I just want to say that I think you have a real natural ability to talk to strangers, though.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. I appreciate that. It doesn’t come natural, I guarantee you.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, great. Do you want to share anything else, or are we pretty much done?

Ivan:
No. I’m sure I will share some of my visits here in France and Scandinavia, being sweetened mostly. I’ll share that on my blog, so after you listen to this podcast, take some time to look at my blog at NetworkingEntrepreneur.com and I’m sure you’ll see some pictures of me at the Ice Hotel.

Priscilla:
Great, Dr. Misner. Thank you so much.

I just want to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you’ll join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: BNI is perfect for situational introverts, because it creates a small group with a structured networking environment.

title.rendered: Episode 815: Edify Your Referral Sources

content.rendered:
Synopsis

“Edify” means “to uplift and support.” The number one rule for referrals is to make your referral source look great. Tell your new prospect how great it is to work with the person who referred you. This helps warm up your relationship with the prospect and encourages your referral source to send you more business.

If you’ve used this technique or had a BNI member edify you to someone you referred to them, let us know in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 815

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am back home in Austin. I just got back from India. And I gotta tell you, Priscilla, it is so funny. Everywhere I go, I can’t tell you how many people say to me. “Hi, Ivan. How are you? And where are you?”

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
It’s really cute. And what I love about it is, you know, I know they’re listening to the podcast because they are always asking that question, “How are you? And where are you?” And I know I’ve said this a number of times, but we do that because I want people to know I’m out visiting regions of the organization. I’m not up in my ivory tower, just hanging out here. Although I like my tower, I do get out and visit a lot of regions.

Priscilla:
I know. And you go to all these exotic places. And I think that’s fun to know about.

Ivan:

Yeah, it is fun. And I love visiting BNI. But the one thing that’s consistent no matter where I’m at around the world is that BNI members are so incredibly giving, and caring, and supportive. It’s like the television series, “Cheers”. You know… “where everybody knows your name”; you go to the bar, everybody knows who you are. I go to a country and there’s just so many BNI people that are there that are incredibly supportive. So I love what I do.

Priscilla:
That’s great.

Ivan:
Today we’re going to talk about an advanced networking skill, which is to edify your referral source. Now, to edify is to uplift or support. And I mean, there’s a couple of meanings, but that’s one of the meanings of edifying. And this is kind of an advanced skill, in my opinion. I think that this is a crucial concept in networking that’s rarely discussed.

When you get a referral, and you meet with the prospect, it’s really important that you edify your referral source. In other words, that you support your referral source; that you talk about them in a positive way. This is a great technique to use because you begin the discussion with the potential customer by talking about an area of overlapping interest and knowledge – your mutual acquaintance, the BNI member.

So before you start talking about your business, before you start pitching what you do, or even asking them questions about what they need, it’s important, in my opinion, that you edify that referral source, that you tell them how much you respect that person that referred you. Talk a little bit about your relationship with that networking partner, your BNI member, and why it’s important to you that you provide great service to everyone that they refer to you because they are such good associates. You spend time talking about how you both know this individual. It’s a fantastic way to warm up the referral, and more importantly, it’s a great opportunity to make your referral source look good for having made the referral. The person that you’re working with is going to be impressed that you have such good things to say about somebody that they also know. And so this is kind of an advanced technique, but it’s a powerful one.

Ivan:
Remember that the number one rule for referrals is, I believe, to make your referral source look great. So you need to demonstrate that you know how to sell to the prospect in a way that doesn’t embarrass the source of your referral – your fellow BNI member. Your networking partner must have confidence that you’re going to consult with the prospective client, discover their needs, offer solutions based on those needs, give them some options and not just push for a sale if you know you can’t provide a good solution.

Alternatively, if your technique is to hold the prospect hostage at their kitchen table or office desk until they break down and buy something from you, your referral source will not be pleased that you’ve abused your relationship with them and damaged their own relationship with the client. So you may get the deal but you’ve shut yourself off for further deals with that client, and from any future referrals from your fellow BNI member. Always edify your referral source and act in a way that would make that person want to refer more people to you.

So how does it start? You might start by saying…. you might ask, how do I do that? So if your networking partner is Tom and Tom refers you to someone, the first thing that you would do is ask some questions of the potential customer.

  • How do you know them?
  • How long have you known them?
  • What’s the situation that they need help with?
  • How did it come about?
  • Are they expecting my call?

These are the questions that you ask the BNI member; I’m not sure I was clear about that. So you ask the BNI member these questions so that when you talk to the potential customer, you’re asking questions that Tom will have the answer to, your BNI member will have the answer to at least some of them. These questions help get the background information about the person and why they were referred to you. And you can do that in just a couple of minutes.

Now that you’re prepared with that information, then you call the referral. And unless your BNI member, Tom, tells you to wait until a certain day, I would call them the same day that you get the referral. Don’t sit on it. Don’t wait a few hours, or a few days, which I’ve seen; that’s really bad. Call them later that day.

When I call a prospect, the first thing I’d say when I was a management consultant was, “I understand that you’ve been a friend of someone I know, in this case, Tom, you’ve been a friend of Tom’s for a long time.” Then I would edify Tom by saying that I’ve known him for years, he’s been my electrician for a long time, or whatever profession he was in. I admire how he always thinks about ways to help others, or whatever you feel about the person who gave you the referral; whatever positive aspects you feel about that person.

Why? Because when you give a referral, it’s important that the first thing that I do, if you gave me referral, it’s important that the first thing I do with that referral is tell them how great it is working with you. And the reason that I would do that is because a third-party testimonial is incredibly powerful. Tom is my third-party testimonial. Your BNI member is your third-party testimonial. And instead of me calling the referral and talking and bragging about myself, what I want to do is talk about Tom first, because the one thing that that potential client has in common with me, is him.

And if I talk about him, by talking about Tom, that helps warm up the relationship a little bit before I start launching into how I can provide whatever service it is that I do. So the person who gave you the referral already has a relationship of trust with the prospect and is lending you their credibility by referring you to them. This is why it is important to make them look good by edifying them and effectively fulfilling that referral, so they get great feedback about working with you and want to refer to you again. That’s my message for today, Priscilla. Any thoughts before we wrap up?

Priscilla: I think that’s great information. And something maybe that people aren’t thinking of doing. But they should be, and it’s really good for you to spell it out.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think you’re right. And that’s why I think this is an advanced technique. We’re so focused on getting referrals that we sometimes don’t think about supporting the person that gave us the referral.

And that’s what I mean by edifying them, is that you talk about that person first, before you start talking about yourself. And you find those overlapping areas of interest. Now you talk to your referral source about the referral, “Tell me about the person,” like I described. You know, “How do you know them? How long have you known them?”

But then when you meet the referral, you also want to talk about how they know them, and how long they’ve known them. And support the person that gave you the referral and say good things about that person, because the referral will feel better about who you are, if they know you feel good about the person that referred them. That make sense?

Priscilla: 
Yeah. And also it draws you closer to that person. It’s like you have a bond now; you have somebody in common and that you both really appreciate.

Ivan:
Yep, it cements that relationship earlier, quicker. I really recommend it. If you’ve listened to this podcast, I’d love for you to comment. If you’ve experienced this in a positive way with one of your networking partners, share it here. You can even name them. Tell us who it is that you’ve had that experience with and how well it worked out. I’d love to see it here on the comments. I read every comment that gets posted on BNI Podcast. So that’s it for today. That’s everything I have, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s it for this week. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material that’s available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
