title.rendered: Episode 814: Start with Gain, End with Give

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The first time you stand, during the weekly presentation portion of a BNI meeting, is all about you. It’s the GAIN portion of the meeting. The second time you stand, during the referral part of the meeting, is all about someone else. It is the GIVING portion of the meeting: you give a referral or you give a testimonial. Nothing else: only a referral or a testimonial.

Elements of a Good BNI Testimonial

  1. Authenticity
  2. Specificity
  3. Before and After Scenarios
  4. Results Oriented
  5. Contextual Relevance

If your chapter wants to share CEUs, One-to-Ones, or other statistics, consider passing around your PALMS report. (Download the “Antarctica” PALMS for an example.)

Download Antarctica PALMS Report

Further Listening

  • Episode 323: What Makes a Good BNI Testimonial?
  • Episode 551: Announcing One to Ones During Referrals
  • Episode 563: The Referral Part of the Meeting
  • Episode 559: Don’t Thank the Speaker (Instead of Making a Referral)

Complete Transcript of Episode 814

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in India this week. I’m visiting Chennai for the BNI National Conference, and then I’m headed over to Goa. So I think it’s my fifth visit or sixth visit to India. But it’s a wonderful, wonderful country. And I love the people in India. You know, I have more followers on my social media from India than I do North America.

Priscilla:
Wow. That’s interesting.

Ivan:
It’s incredible.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Well, what do you what do you have to share with us today?

Ivan:
Yeah, interesting topic or title anyway—Start with Gain, End with Give. So there are two portions of the [BNI] meeting where we go around, and everyone has a chance to speak. I want to spend time with this podcast relating to the second go round: the referral part of the meeting. So some chapters have gotten off track relating to this portion of the meeting. And I want to talk about that. And I’m gonna have a number of podcasts later that I’m going to recommend that you go back and listen to. But I have a little bit different spin today that I think might help.

I truly believe that people need to know the “why” before they can buy into the “how” relating to almost anything that we talk about. So here’s a great way to understand the “why” behind the way I set up the meeting. Particularly in terms of the referral portion of the meeting.

The first time you stand at a BNI meeting, the weekly presentation portion of the meeting, is all about you. It’s the GAIN in Givers Gain. You’re talking about yourself, who you are, what you do.

The second time you stand, the referral part of the meeting, is all about someone else. It is the GIVE, or the giving portion of the meeting. You give a referral, or you give a testimonial.

Ivan:
Now, when I started BNI, it wasn’t that way. I learned along the way how to do it this way. And there’s a great podcast, I explain this in [BNI] Podcast, episode number 323, and I go into detail.

Let me give you the short version of it here. When I first started BNI, literally just the first few months, if you had a referral you gave it, if you didn’t you passed. We only had one chapter, we didn’t even have a second chapter yet. Give a referral or pass, give a referral or pass. Well, one week somebody didn’t want to pass; they wanted to say something. I said okay, what? And they went on and on about how great this chiropractor was in the group and how they really helped him so much with their lower back. And I thought, Wow, I’m missing an unbelievable opportunity by having people pass. What I need to do instead is if you have a referral, give it. If you don’t, give a testimonial.

And I talk about this at length in [BNI Podcast] episode number 323. After you listen to this podcast, go listen to the whole story [in 323]. It talks about why I added testimonials to the meetings. Some chapters, over the last few years, have gotten sidetracked from this key element of the BNI system.

So first, let me talk about what a testimonial should be. This could almost be its own podcast, but I’m gonna try and put it in here in this podcast because this is what a testimonial should be. This is what you should be talking about. If you don’t have a referral, you should do this.

A good testimonial in BNI is a positive and genuine statement expressing your experience, satisfaction, and recommendation of a product or service of another BNI member. Podcast 323, which I mentioned, has some good examples, it has a great example of this with the chiropractor. But I’m going to elaborate further in this podcast. Here are five characteristics of a good testimonial in BNI.

  1. Authenticity. A good testimonial is honest and sincere, it reflects the genuine feelings and experiences that you or your referral had with the member.
  2. Specificity. It includes specific details about the product or service, highlighting its key benefits, and how the other member of your chapter positively impacted you, or your referral’s life or business.
  3. Before and After Scenarios. If applicable, a good testimonial may include a comparison of the situation before using the product or service and how it’s improved or solved the problem after the product or service. It should use compelling language, descriptive words, and persuasive phrases to convey the enthusiasm and the satisfaction of the referral.
  4. It should be Results Oriented. It should focus on tangible results or outcomes that were achieved using the product or service that the member had to offer, such as increased productivity, cost savings, improved health, enhanced quality of life or business.
  5. Contextual Relevance. A good testimonial is relevant to the potential audience, the BNI members. It addresses the pain points and concerns that potential customers may have, and it demonstrates how the product or service can address those issues effectively.

This is what you need to do in the second part of the meeting.

The first part of the meeting is gain, it’s about you. The second part of the meeting is about giving, the give. It’s about talking about another BNI member using one of these, or ALL of these, five different things that I just shared that will help you do a better testimonial.

Remember, a good testimonial should reflect a real customer’s experience and provide valuable insights to potential referral sources.

The first time you stand during the weekly presentation portion of the meeting is all about you. It’s the GAIN portion of the meeting. The second time you stand during the referral part of the meeting is all about someone else. It is the GIVING portion of the meeting – you give a referral or you give a testimonial.

I actually had a [BNI] member recently say to me that their group won’t do that; that they’re going to continue to do [report] one-to-ones and CEUs [Chapter Education Units] during the referral part of the meeting. And that’s the reason I’m doing this podcast is because of this member’s comment to me. They wanted to continue to do one-to-ones and CEUs during the referral part of the meeting.

Well, I can’t make someone do the right thing. But I can do my best to inspire them to do the right thing. And so I ask everyone to make the second part of the meeting what it was designed to be, namely, to give a referral or a testimonial. If you’ve run out of testimonials for members, give a testimonial for something BNI has done for you or others you’ve seen in the group that BNI has helped in some way. It’s not as good as a testimonial for a member, but it’s better than praising yourself for the CEUs and the one-to-ones you’ve done. Oh my goodness, I see that… sometimes it’s like patting myself on the back, “I’ve done such a great job. I’ve done CEUs and I’ve done one-to-ones.” That’s never what this part of the meeting was supposed to be about.

When you talk about CEUs or one-to-ones that you’ve done during the referral part of the meeting, that’s just patting yourself on the back for what you’ve done. If you really want to talk about that, let me give you two alternatives that will allow you to continue to do that in a different way. And it won’t take away from the heart and the soul of this part of the meeting.

  1. If it’s really important to you to tell everyone how many CEUs and one-to-ones you’ve done, then you could do it during your weekly presentation. However, people don’t do that because it takes time away from their GAIN portion of the meeting. So they try to slip it into the GIVERS portion of the meeting, although it was never meant to be there. Doing it during your weekly presentation is an option but here’s a better option, I think for everyone.
  2. Have your [chapter] Leadership Team print out the PALMS report and pass it around each week. Now, I have a current example of one attached in this podcast [thank you to Jeremy Walsh]. It’s the Antarctic region or Antarctic chapter. There is no Antarctic chapter; this is our test chapter that we use to test out different concepts. So it’s a make believe chapter in Antarctica but it’s the PALMS report. And it shows how many CEUs someone may have, or how many one-to-ones they’ve done. Just print that out, pass it around. Everyone can see how good you are at what you’ve done, and you keep the heart and the soul of the meeting alive – of this part of the meeting – alive. It won’t take away from what I think is the most important part of the meeting. The very reason I created BNI: Referrals. And testimonials that lead to referrals. So I recommend option TWO – print the PALMS report.

Let me wrap by recommending a few other podcasts.

  • Episode number 551. It was the very first time I discussed the fact that I saw people doing CEUs and one-to-ones during the referral part of the meeting instead of testimonials. Take a look at that; it’s a little assertive. I think I was a little more tactful this time. But take a listen to that.
  • Episode number 563. I talk about how “feature creep” has altered the why we do the referral part of the meeting. Feature creep is when people just start adding things to something until it becomes too cumbersome. And that’s kind of what’s going on with this.
  • Episode number 559. And Priscilla and I talked about this before we went live on this recording. Don’t use this part of the meeting to thank the speaker. Talk to them after the meeting, “What a great job you did!” But that’s just a waste of effort, I think. This is the testimonial portion of the meeting. Praise the speaker face-to-face, one-to-one, “I think you did a great job.” But doing it publicly is taking away from the opportunity to do the part of the meeting that this was designed to be – to GIVE a referral or a testimonial to someone. Episode 559 talks about the speaker.

Remember, the first time you stand during the weekly presentation portion of the meeting is all about you. It’s the GAIN of Givers Gain, it was designed that way.

The second time you stand during the referral part of the meaning is all about someone else. It’s the testimonial or the referral, it’s the referral or testimonial portion of the meaning. It is the GIVING portion of Givers Gain.

I ask that you embrace this approach. It is the heart and the soul of the program that I first created. And I would hate to see us drift away from that.

I want to make, I want to make a difference for people. That’s what I wanted, I want to make a difference for people.

If you want to make a difference for the people in your chapter, give truly GIVE when you have the opportunity to do so. Don’t make it about you, make it about someone else during the referral part of the meeting with a referral or testimonial.

Priscilla:
What do you think is wrong with everyone in the chapter praising the speaker? Because I think people are doing that because they feel like they’re actually giving to that person who’s presenting.

Ivan:
Yes, so I talk about that in episode number 559. And listen, it is a form of giving, but it’s not giving a referral or a testimonial. And this is the referral part of the meeting. And if you don’t have referral, give a testimonial.

Look, you could give a praise, you could give a gift, you can give a candy bar. That’s not what we’re talking about with this part of the meeting. We’re talking about giving a referral or giving a testimonial.

I urge you to praise the speaker—[but] not during this part of the meeting.

This part of the meeting is about giving a referral or giving a testimonial. This is a perfect example of feature creep you add things and add things and add things. It’s like the TV remote control. You need two or three to operate your TV. Why? Because it’s feature creep. Things are added and added and added. What we want to do is focus and focus and focus. The more we focus, the more successful we are.

I urge you to praise the speaker privately, and publicly give referrals and give testimonials. For those of you listening to this podcast, I urge you to share it with your chapters. And especially those chapters that are maybe doing what I’m suggesting they not do.

And just consider what the benefit would be of GIVING versus patting yourself on the back with CEUs. Just consider it. I think you’ll find it will be better. Alright, I’m done. Let me step off my soapbox now.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I think that was great.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The second time you stand during a BNI meeting, you give a referral or you give a testimonial–and nothing else.

title.rendered: Episode 813: Making Communication More Powerful

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Stephanie Turconi from Genius in 21 Days joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to explain how to use learning styles to make communication more powerful.

There are many learning styles. Stephanie concentrates on four of them in two pairs: global versus analytical and verbal versus visual.

Learn how to adapt your weekly presentation and one-to-ones to make better connections with everyone in your BNI chapter. When people understand us and learn from us, we’re 97% more likely to close business with them.

Complete Transcript of Episode 813

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Sri Lanka this week. It’s my very, very first visit to BNI Sri Lanka. We’ve got a number of chapters here, and they’ve done a great job. I’m really proud of the organization here in Sri Lanka. So it’ll be a fun week.

Priscilla:
Sounds great.

Ivan:

So today, I have a guest on the podcast. Her name is Stephanie Turconi. And Stephanie is an expert in advanced learning techniques, memory improvement, brain optimization, accelerated learning. She’s a mom, a wife, a successful businesswoman who started everything and made Genius In 21 Days, which is the name of her company, possible in the US. She started working with the concept of Genius In 21 Days in Italy in 2013. And we just we talked about this before we went live that I’m going to be in Italy in September of this year. So I’m really looking forward to going there. So she made it available in Italy in 2013 in the customer service department, and her leadership skills and abilities brought her to become partner of the company a few years later. She was born in Italy and grew up in Cantu’, which is a small town in northern Italy. Stephanie, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Stephanie:
Thank you very much. Thank you for having me.

Ivan:
It is great to have you here. So the title for the podcast today is “Making Communication More Powerful.” What do you mean by that?

Stephanie:
Exactly. Because if you think about it, when we were in school, no one ever taught us how to learn. They just taught us what to learn. No one ever taught us how our brain likes to learn, what our learning styles are. And making our communication more powerful simply means knowing what our learning styles are, what other people’s learning styles are. so how we can be more impactful when we talk to them.

Ivan:
Excellent. So what are the learning styles? And why do you feel they’re important?

Stephanie:
Yes, absolutely. So, we have many, many learning styles, I want to talk about the four main ones. And they are being global versus being analytical; being verbal versus being visual. Now I want to take one step back first, and give you an example. Again, think back when you were in school. I’m sure you all had, we all had, that professor that we hated, or maybe we didn’t hate but it was just the boring or his or her classes were making us fall asleep or we were just not understanding them. And the reason is because he was not teaching us based on, or following, our learning styles.

Ivan:
Right. Now the first one… can I just interrupt you real quick? The first one you said was something versus analytical. I didn’t catch the first word.

Stephanie:
Sure. global.

Ivan:
Global, global. That’s what I thought you said, Okay. Global versus analytical. All right, I interrupted you. Sorry. Go ahead.

Stephanie:
No worries. So yes. And what happens with this professor, let’s say if he’s analytical and we are global, and I will explain to you what it means, what they mean, we just have a hard time understanding. And we lose trust in that professor. And this is even worse in our business. I mean, think about it, if they lose trust in you, how can people help us grow our business? How can people pass referrals to us if they don’t understand us, or they don’t even trust us or trust what we’re doing? So for this reason, it is very important to understand how people learn and communicate to them based on how they learn.

So the solution again, is learning styles. And I can tell you what those four learning styles are and what it means. So let’s start from analytical versus global. Now, global – what does that mean? Global means when people understand… so my global people understand things to the big picture Sure, they want the big picture, the full picture first step. I don’t know if this has ever happened to you, it happens to me all the time with my husband, that he’s very analytical and I am global. And he’s explaining something to me. And I’m like, get to the point, please get to the point. What are you going to talk about? I want to see where you’re getting to – the big picture.

And when I say the big picture, I compare it to building a puzzle. So global people understand looking at the picture on the box first, that’s what helps them understand the concept. Versus my analytical people; they understand the concept starting from the details, they think details are important. And that helps them build the puzzle. So let’s say they start putting all the pieces of the puzzle together, and that’s what will help them understand the big picture, the picture on the box.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Stephanie: 
So those are two different learning styles. Another two of the main learning styles are being verbal versus being visual. What does it mean? So let’s start from visual. Visual people understand things through a real life example. They want to see a picture, they want to see an example. Versus verbal people, what helps them understand the concept is the logic behind it. The reason behind the concept, the logic, and then maybe, once they understand, they are able to give us a real life example. Or they also like giving people a real life example, a drawing or something. But what helps them understand is the logic behind the concept. So those are the four main learning styles.

Ivan:
So those are those are the most common ones.

Stephanie:
Correct, yes.

Ivan:
All right. So just to review slightly – global versus analytical, one way that I would maybe describe it is macro versus micro; big picture versus, you know, targeted. Is that accurate?

Stephanie:
Yes, yeah.

Ivan:
Okay. And the second is pretty self explanatory. Verbal versus visual. So these are important things to know. But how do you use them to communicate in a BNI group, to to the people in your chapter, so that they can give you referrals more effectively? That’s the key.

Stephanie:
Yes, that is key. Also, because statistics say that when people understand us, and they learn from us, we are 97% more… it’s 97% more possible that we’re going to close business with them. So communication is crucial. Now, how can we use it, when in a BNI context? I’m going to start from a one-to-one because it’s easier. Because the key is understanding what the other person’s learning styles are, how they learn, and to tweaking, adapting our communication based on how they learn. And I will give you an example. Let’s say I want to present my business. And I’m gonna give you an example with my personal business, Genius In 21 Days, but just to show you the difference, and you’re gonna understand how they learn and kind of mix and match. Right? So let’s say they are global and verbal. What does it mean? It means that they want to see the big picture, they don’t need all the details. And they want to understand the why, to understand why – the logic behind it. So let’s say I would say, “I’m Stephanie with Genius In 21 Days. We teach people how to learn fast and retain information forever. Because in school, they never taught us how to learn. They always taught us what to learn.”

Ivan:
What to learn, yeah.

Stephanie:
That’s where we come in, we give you a personalized method.”

So that’s an example of global and verbal. Now, I’ll give you another example, let’s say the opposite, analytical and visual. So in this case, they want to have examples. Yeah, real life examples. And they want to see the details. So I’d say again, “I’m Stephanie with Genius In 21 Days, and we teach people how to learn faster. So for example, we teach mind maps, memory techniques, strategic speed reading, effective communication. And people who come to us are people who need to pass a certification, learn a foreign language; they want to memorize a speech and be more confident.” So in this case, I gave them examples, and I was more detailed in explaining what we teach.

Ivan:
So correct me if I’m wrong, but the idea with these learning styles is that if you change up the way you present to the membership, you’re going to hit every cylinder in the chapter. You’re gonna connect with everyone, because you use different approaches in describing what you do. Is that accurate?

Stephanie:
That is very accurate. That’s super important. And in fact, BNI, it kind of gives us the perfect opportunity to connect with everyone in the chapter.

Ivan:
Yeah, because you can do it every week. So one week you do global and verbal, and the next week analytical and visual. And you’re connecting with everyone even though it’s the same topic.

Stephanie:
Exactly, yes. And when you’re in front of a group, that’s the way to do it. Because you won’t know what every single person’s learning styles are. Or it’s just impossible to use them all at once. So you pick one topic, and each week of the month, you’re going to mix and match, because at the end of the month you will have connected with everyone.

Ivan:
Perfect. So you gave big picture, a lot of information that you’ve shared in a short amount of time, how can someone master it and be effective reasonably fast?

Stephanie:
Yes, that’s a very important question. So I always give our students three tips. Number one, the first step is, understand what your styles are. So speak, study, understand what your learning styles are, how you like to learn. Number two – understand, and practice understanding what other people’s learning styles are – communication styles. Maybe doing one-to-ones, that’s a great reason to schedule and do many one-to-ones.

Ivan:
Right.

Stephanie:
Focus on how they communicate and how they learn. And the third step is talk to someone, an expert. It could be someone else, it could be us, who’s going to teach you exactly how to use them for what your needs are, and what your goals are in business.

Ivan:
Well, Stephanie, I want to thank you for being on today’s podcast. You were recommended by the local director, and I appreciate you taking the time to be here. For anyone that is listening to this podcast, feel free to check out her website, www.geniusin21daysusa.com. 21 is the number. So it’s genius in 2 1 days usa.com. Stephanie, any last thing you want to say real quick before we wrap it?

Stephanie:
Yes, actually, I do want to say one thing. Remember this. Every time you feel like you’re procrastinating people are not understanding you, or you have a hard time communicating, growing. Remember one thing it’s not you, it’s not your attitude, it’s not your laziness or whatever excuse, but it’s because no one ever taught you a method. No one ever taught us a method and how to learn.

Ivan:
Fair enough. Stephanie, thanks for being on BNI podcast. I really appreciate it. You had great information today.

Stephanie:
Thank you.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you so much. Both of you. That was great.

All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

title.rendered: Episode 812: Being Intentional About Diversity (Classic)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week’s episode is based on a blog post that Dr. Misner wrote with Brian Hilliard, co-author of Networking Like a Pro. With everything that’s been going on in the world, they thought it was time to talk about the importance of diversifying your business network. For us, that means diversifying your BNI chapter.

According to McKinsey’s 2015 “Why Diversity Matters” report, companies with high racial and ethnic diversity are actually 35% more likely to perform above their industry’s national median return. Maintaining a diverse network is just good business.

How do you diversify your network?

Brian and Ivan came up with seven ways of diversifying your business network and your BNI chapter. These are not the only possible ways.

  1. Recognize that diversity is a process, not a program. You have to commit to it daily.
  2. Look at your contacts on your phone and social media to see how diverse they are.
  3. Consider volunteering for organizations which put you in contact with people who are different than you.
  4. Make a point of talking to people who don’t look like you.
  5. Invite different people of different ethnicities to your networking group.
  6. Make this a top down initiative, wherever you are in the organization.
  7. Hard code diversity into the fabric of your business

Read Being Intentional About Diversity by Brian Hilliard and Ivan Misner on IvanMisner.com.

If you’re a BNI member and you have a positive suggestion to help increase diversity in BNI chapters, please share it in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 672

Priscilla   
Hello, everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan    
I’m doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I have a really important topic to talk about. And that’s about being very intentional about diversity. Recently, Brian Hilliard, my co-author on the book we did, Networking Like a Pro, he and I wrote an important article on diversity in your network. And I’d like to share that content on this podcast. And I’ll include a link to the article and to my blog as well. So you can have the article piece in addition to this podcast.

Priscilla
Okay, great.

Ivan   
What we said in the piece was that with everything going on in the world today, we thought it would be a good time to take a step back and talk about diversity and more specifically, about diversifying your business network or for us, diversifying your BNI chapter. Developing a truly diverse network is not only the right thing to do, it’s also the smart thing to do. Because let’s be honest, different people bring different things to the table in terms of who they are, and how they might be able to refer other people to your business.

Now, like we said in our book Networking Like a Pro, networks are by nature clumpy. (That’s the technical term.) Cluster-like. People, you know, have a tendency to congregate and surround themselves with people that are similar to them are similar to us, whether by race, gender, religion, professional status.

Unfortunately, this approach to networking has unintended consequences, namely that we tend to form these clusters. And this is why it’s so incredibly important to be intentional about the way we develop our personal network. A diverse personal network enables you to increase the possibility including connectors to your network. These are people who cross over in some way between two or more groups of people. The best way to increase the number of possible connections in your network is to intentionally develop a diverse network that has connections to other clusters of individuals.

Now if you go with the premise that relationships are the currency of today’s modern business, then it stands to reason that having an ethnically diverse network comprised of people who look different than you, actually is the next logical step when it comes to building a thriving referral based business. But for a lot of people, especially those in the majority, whatever that is, in whatever country you’re in, the question becomes, how?

In other words, how can I diversify my network and get to know more business people in other ethnic communities or other age ranges, you know, people that are different than me? And that’s a great question, one that at first glance seems to be pretty daunting, at the least.

But as with most seemingly complicated questions, the answer is, I think, and Brian believes, quite simple. Be more intentional about what you do. In other words, as a member of any ethnic group, the tendency is to spend time around more people like yourself, so whatever ethnicity I am, I’m more likely to have friends and business contacts of that ethnicity. And while that’s understandable, we feel, Brian and I, that entrepreneurs who diversify their networks based on ethnicity, gender, and a host of other factors are actually better positioned to be more successful.

As a matter of fact, McKinsey and Company did a report just a few years ago. It’s called Diversity Matters, which determined that companies having a high racial and ethnic diversity are actually 35% more likely to perform above their industry’s national median return. That’s an impressive number. And it’s one of the reasons why it’s good business as well as being the right thing.

So the question then becomes what can we do to branch out and overcome the gravitational pull that we all feel towards spending time around people who are kind of like us? How can we instead become more intentional in our actions when it comes to actually meeting and engaging others in different communities? And that’s another great question. And we have some thoughts. And these thoughts aren’t meant to be the only ideas there are. And if you are a member of BNI and you’re listening or reading the podcast transcript, and you have a positive suggestion to help increase diversity in the groups, please share it here. We’re looking for positive ideas, always. And so please share it here.

But I’m going to share with everyone here today, seven different thoughts that we talked about, Brian and I in our article, on how to actually create a more diverse BNI Chapter, a more diverse network.

Number 1. Recognize that diversity is a process, not a program. In other words, diversifying your network has to be something you want to do and commit to do, to be doing, daily. You have to do this on a daily basis. It needs to become part of your core beliefs that you’re going to be intentional about meeting and engaging people who don’t look like you. Anything less than that is almost guaranteed to eventually fail.

Number 2. Looking at your phone and business contacts on social media, do they all look the same in terms of ethnicity, age, education, gender? If so, then you’ve got to keep listening because we have some ideas that might help you with this. As I said earlier, diversity is a process, not just a program, and it has to be an ongoing process. So look at your contacts to see how diverse they are.

Number 3. Consider volunteering for certain organizations, which put you in contact with people who are different than you. This could be as simple as volunteering as a coach for a local sports team, scheduling some time to visit an inner-city school during, maybe, a career day. What a great project that would be for Business Voices, a BNI career day in an inner-city school. It could be sitting on a local community service board. I’m very active on the Austin Boys and Girls Club as a board member. Just take it upon yourself to broaden the scope of contacts that you have with various ethnicities.

Number 4. And this one came from Brian. Make it a point to talk to people who don’t look like you. This is one that Brian personally started doing two years ago and I love it. So as an African American man in his 40s, who grew up in the north, but lives in the south, he actively takes it upon himself to talk to any person who doesn’t look like him.

He says, this is him talking, he says, “I just make it a point while passing them at the grocery store, walking to my car in the parking lot, picking up some Chinese food, and I say Hey, how’s it going?” Depending on the situation, sometimes that leads to more conversation. Sometimes it doesn’t. But it gets everyone out of their comfort zone for just a short bit engaging new folks. I really like this about Brian and I can definitely tell you that he walks the talk in doing this, I’ve seen it.

Number 5. Invite different people of different ethnicities to your networking group. If you’re in a BNI chapter, this is a perfect opportunity for you to engage others and invite them to your group. For example, maybe you’re out networking and you see a person of color and you decide to implement the previous point about talking to people that you are nearby. Then great, during that conversation you let them know about your group and ask them if they’d like to come, tell them you would love to have them come to the group. And that’s it. It’s super easy to do, but you have to be intentional about it. You have to look for people, you have to observe people, and you have to be intentional about making that ask.

Number 6. Make this a top down initiative, wherever you are in the organization. For those of you who have employees in your business, this is a crucial point. If you’re not the boss, but you’re a manager of some kind, that you have people beneath you, you know, take it from the top down at wherever you are in the organization. If you want to have diversity in your organization, and be more successful as a business because of it, then you absolutely have to take the lead in making diversity a thing in your group, and this applies to the BNI chapter.

The chapter Leadership Team has to make it a thing, it has to be something that’s discussed, and is a goal and objective. And it means it’s something that people value when you’re talking about it, something that people do, and it’s something that you as a leader set an example on a regular basis for other people to emulate.

And Number 7. Hard code diversity into the fabric of your business. Similar to the previous leadership point, if you’re going to be serious about diversity in your business, put it in your public material, address it when you’re talking to your team or employees, make it a part of the DNA of the organizational culture. So that people are crystal clear on how you feel about it, and how it plays out in your company. Put it in other forms of communication, just like this podcast, for example. Put it out there publicly, so the world knows, your clients know, your associates know, your employees know, where you stand and how you address this issue.

It’s important to note that there is a subtle but crucial difference between inclusivity and diversity. And most people don’t talk about this. Brian and I had a long conversation as we wrote about this and we thought it had to be included. Your group, your BNI chapter, may feel like it is very inclusive. But when you look at it from the outside, does it truly look diverse? If not, you need to be more intentional about being inclusive to create diversity.

Diversity is a fact. Inclusiveness is a choice. I want to repeat that. Diversity is a fact. Inclusiveness is a choice. Intentionally acting in an inclusive manner is what creates diversity. So, look, don’t beat yourself up if you’re not there yet. Maybe you haven’t done these things as well as you could have. But today is the day to start. It’s never too late to do the right thing. And that’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla   
That’s very good, Ivan. I really like that.

Ivan 
Thank you. I appreciate it. I think it’s really important. And I want to reiterate for those of you listening, if you have a positive contribution to the question, how do you in fact, be more intentional? I would love to have those positive contributions here. I read every post that gets put up, so thank you very much, Priscilla. That’s it.

Priscilla
That’s great. Okay, well, thank you so much, Dr. Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Brian Hilliard and Ivan Misner share seven ways you can diversify your business network and your BNI chapter.

title.rendered: Episode 811: Complaining Is Not an Olympic Event

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Some people seem to treat complaining like it’s an Olympic event. Complaining is not a productive use of our time or our energy. It’s much more beneficial to focus on solutions to our problems, rather than just complaining about them.

If you have an Olympic-level complainer in your BNI chapter, it’s important for the leadership team to address the constant complaining before it spreads to other members. Here are some tips to improve everyone’s experience at the meeting.

If there’s something that several members complain about, consider what might be done differently to address that issue, or to focus on a solution.

Have a private conversation with the complainer and offer them an easy out by saying something like this:

It’s okay if you step down: you don’t seem very happy. Maybe a different group of people would work better for you. Or if you’re in a different place, feel free to come back in, but you’re not enjoying this, and we’re not enjoying the process, either, because you just don’t seem very happy, and it shows.

For additional tips on dealing with negativity in your chapter, see Episode 790: “Why So High?“

How has your chapter handled Olympic complainers? Let us know in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 811

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am home in Austin, Texas, today and I have a friend, a guest here at my house, Dana Coyle. Dana is my personal assistant, anyone who has communicated with me probably is also communicated with Dana. Everybody needs a Dana. She’s amazing. And I’m gonna have her say hi.

Dana:
Hello. It’s really an honor to be here. I’ve listened to BNI podcast for years. And I’m excited to say hi, thank you.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s great to have you here. And we, you know, Dana’s kind of the producer for the podcast, she lines up whatever guests and does, you know, gets all the information in advance. So if it sounds like I’m organized, it’s because Dana is organized and I follow what she says. Great to have you here and she’s going to be in the background as we do this one, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Nice to hear you, Dana.

Dana:
Thank you.

Ivan:
Alright. So today’s podcast is kind of tongue-in-cheek. I hope you find it humorous. Complaining is NOT an Olympic Event. And when I’m done, Priscilla, if you’ve got some thoughts, I’d love you to share. For those of you listening to the podcast–Have you ever noticed that some people seem to treat complaining like it’s an Olympic event? They train for it. They strategize; they even have special equipment like a well-worn soapbox. But let me tell you, complaining is NOT an Olympic event. I think it’s time that we all started training for something more productive. Imagine if complaining were an actual Olympic event. There would be teams from all over the world, each with their own style of complaining.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
One country would be masters of the dramatic sigh, while another would excel at dry sarcasm. There’d be one nation that would be experts at complaining about the food. And definitely one that would be efficient complainers, getting their grievances out in record time. By the way, I had specific countries in mind as I wrote each one of those. So, what countries would you assign as gold medalists in each of those fields? The dramatic sigh, the dry sarcasm, complaining about food, efficient complainers – record time. Think about what country you would assign to that. I didn’t want to make the countries mad, so I didn’t say who I was thinking of for each of those.

But imagine the opening ceremony would be a sight to behold. Instead of athletes parading around with their country’s flags, teams of complainers would strut their stuff, each carrying a well-worn soapbox. The judges would be looking for the most creative complaints, the most eloquent grumbling, the most persistent whining.

But what would the actual events be? There’d be a race to see who could complain the loudest and the longest without taking a breath, or maybe a competition to see who could come up with the most trivial complaint. You know, “I asked for no onions on my burger, and there’s a tiny piece of onion stuck to the bun.”  Who knows? The most interesting event, though, would be the team competition. Teams of complainers would be given a situation like waiting in line at the motor vehicle department.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:  
They’d come up with the most creative complaints to get ahead, the judges would be looking for teamwork, persistence, and the ability to improvise complaints on the spot. But let’s be real… complaining is not a productive use of our time or our energy. It’s a negative habit that can bring down our mood and the mood of those around us. It’s much more beneficial to focus on solutions to our problems, rather than just complaining about them.

And I’ve seen this in BNI groups where you know, somebody just starts on this rant of complaining and they complain, and they complain, and they complain, and it rubs off on other people. And it’s really important in a BNI group to nip that in the bud. You know when you have somebody that is complaining constantly, to have a conversation with them, you know, having a positive and supportive attitude is an important element of a successful BNI group. So I would say let’s start training for something more productive. Instead of complaining about the long wait at the Department of Motor Vehicles, let’s bring a book to read or a game to play. Instead of griping about the weather, let’s plan a fun indoor activity or embrace the rain with a colorful umbrella.

And what about BNI? Think about something that people complain about in BNI and then consider what might be done differently to address that issue, or to focus on a solution. When all is said and done, complaining may not be an Olympic event, but it is a habit that can be hard to break. And I would ask members to strive to be more positive and to focus on solutions rather than just grumbling about problems. And who knows, maybe one day, we’ll have a new Olympic event called ‘Positive Problem Solving.’ I’d like that. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan. I really appreciate it.

Ivan:
I had once a member say to me, true story, not making this up. She said, “We have a guy in our chapter, and he is just a fun sucker. He sucks the fun right out of the room. When he comes in, he just takes all the fun out of the room.” And she actually said, “You know, the best thing he could do for our chapter is call in sick. Because if he’s not there, we have a great meeting.”

And I told her, I said, you know, you got to sit down, have a conversation with him about that. And what’s really interesting is that if you say to somebody, “Hey, you know, it’s okay, if you stepped down, you don’t seem very happy. It’s okay, if you stepped down and maybe you know, different group of people or you’re in a different place, feel free to, you know, come back in, but you’re not enjoying this, and we’re not enjoying the process, either, because you just don’t seem very happy, and it shows.”

And when you open the door like that, it’s surprising how many people will walk out on their own, which is funny, because they will die on the hill fighting if they’re kicked out. But they’ll walk out if you open the door for them. And you want to do that with the complainers. It’s not an Olympic event.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I mean, I’ve been in BNI now for a very long time. And I’ve had a lot of different experiences with different groups in the same chapter, right? Because it’s morphed over the years. And the chapter we have right now is nobody complains, they’re just really nicely homogeneous and pretty happy people. And they appreciate each other and all that, but I’ve had other chapter configurations up, where it was really, like hard to be on the membership committee, let’s just say that, you know, because you have to solve these problems. And they’re, you know, they come up with a lot of them.

Ivan:
Isn’t it interesting, the makeup of a chapter can just make all the difference in the world?

Priscilla:
Yeah, no kidding.

Ivan:
And that’s where the leadership becomes really important. The membership committee or the leadership team, where you get the right people in the group, and you have conversations with the challenging people about working on their attitude, and I’ve seen people turn it around. But more often than not, I’ve seen, you know, chapters successfully open the door and allow people to leave now. And by the way, when you say to them, “Hey, you know, different group of people, or maybe it’s a different time for you, feel free to, you know, consider coming back in.” You don’t have to take them back.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh, right.

Ivan:
And they rarely want to come back in anyway when they’ve been complaining so much, but you don’t have to take them back. But if you open the door, it’s amazing how many people will say, “Yeah, fair enough.”

Priscilla:
Yeah, I think by phrasing it that “You don’t seem very happy,” that’s better. That’s, you know, not like, taking fault with them.

Ivan:
Yeah, it works. It really, really works. I was gonna tell you his story, but I can’t tell the story because that chapter is still very active. They’ll recognize themselves. But yeah, if you make it gentle, about “It’s okay to step down,” it’s amazing how often they’ll do it.

Priscilla:
Good to know.

Ivan:
That’s it for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. I think that was a good one. Well, thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Learn how to head off Olympic complaining in your BNI chapter with these tips to improve everyone’s experience at the meeting.

title.rendered: Episode 810: Strengths and Weaknesses of a Network

content.rendered:
Synopsis

One of the strengths of a good networking group is that most of the members are friends. One of the weaknesses of a networking group is that most of the members are friends. The key is to make sure friendship doesn’t detract from the group’s goal of building each other’s business through networking and through referrals. It’s important for all networking groups to ensure that there’s a strong sense of purpose and a solid structure. And for each member to be committed to carrying out the systems for networking which are in place.

What does your chapter do to maintain its focus and commitment to the process and to the system? What do your chapter leaders do to engage and inspire people to participate in the process in a positive way to be friends, but to also hold accountability with those friendships? Leave a comment below.

Complete Transcript of Episode 810

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Charlotte at the Global Headquarters for BNI this week and I’ve got some meetings and I’m meeting a number of staff and doing a small event in Charlotte.

Priscilla:
So oh, that sounds great.

Ivan:
It’s always fun to go back to headquarters.

Priscilla:
Uh huh. Great. Okay.

Ivan:
So the strengths and weaknesses of a network. And I’ve talked about this a lot in presentations, particularly during Q&A, but I’ve never done a podcast on this I don’t think. I started BNI because many of the networking groups that I attended early in my career, were more like social groups, than true business groups or real networking groups. One of the strengths of a good networking group is that most of the members are friends. One of the weaknesses of a networking group is that most of the members are friends. It is potentially both a strength and a weakness. And it’s a weakness because you have to have accountability. Accountability is crucial in running a good network, because friends don’t like to hold friends accountable.

Priscilla:
Mmm hmm.

Ivan:  
And I’ve found that people who truly understand effective networking recognize the need to have a system and structure in order to get results. And one of the things I didn’t like about many of the networks that I went to when I was a young man was that they were really coffee clashes, there were social groups. And they didn’t have much structure, and they didn’t really have any rules. And that’s why I created BNI to be a structured, reliable, repeatable process. It can be dangerously easy for a networking group, including some BNI chapters that kind of throw the process out the window, it can be easy for networking groups that meet regularly to become a talk session over breakfast with little or no networking actually taking place. And that’s what happens when networking groups lose sight of their purpose, their focus, the system, the structure. Or in some cases, some groups have never had it. And hopefully not a BNI group, but, you know, some networking groups.

Often people begin, in some networking groups, to kind of make their own agenda, they lose focus on actual networking. And when you lose focus, the meetings become very social. Networking should be, in a business context, they should be about business. Now, of course, there’s a social aspect, but it’s mainly, you know, it’s a business purpose. And you want to develop friendships. Friendships help build the business. But if the focus is really all about friendships, then the business kind of gets waylaid. People, like water, tend to take the path of least resistance. And without the proper framework in which to operate the agenda, might become, you know, the current topic of the day, or, you know, the news or whatever the person who’s running the meeting is thinking about, you know, the networking meeting becomes about that – it’s a personal perspective. And so inconsistency like that over time is a problem for any networking group.

Again, the reason I created BNI was to have a system and a process that was repeatable and focused on doing the right things to get referrals. So even if the group has a strong leader, that person’s life will change at some point, or maybe they’ll simply get burned out, and the problem starts if there’s nobody else to teach and help reinforce the existing program. The rules, particularly in a group like BNI, are important but they need to be applied more like Mandela than Attila. And I’ve talked about that in the past. It’s got to be tough love. In other words, apply them in a way that shows that you care. People don’t care how much you know until they know how much you care. And when they know that you care, then you can apply the rules in an effective way.

Another reason for the structure and the process and the training that we do, is the leaky bucket process, which I’ve talked about in podcasts. You begin with a full bucket of information and when that information is taught to someone else, some of it leaks out of the bucket. And when those people teach other people more information leaks out. And at some point, it gets watered down so much, you know, the bucket gets emptied so much that you lose focus on what the purpose of the meeting is. And so that’s one of the reasons why the training that we do is so important. By the time that you’re at the third or fourth generation of people passing along information, you have half a bucket of information remaining. And people start putting in their own content. I learned this in the first year of BNI, by the way, where we didn’t have manuals, and we didn’t do training. I was having the outgoing Leadership Teams train the incoming Leadership Teams. And you know, I went to a BNI meeting once and I’m telling you, I would not have known I was at a BNI meeting, except they kept calling me the Founder and they were wearing badges that said BNI.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
Other than that, it didn’t look like a BNI meeting. And it was at that point that I realized, you know, I gotta have manuals and that we have to have consistency in training. And so that became very, very important. So I learned early on that the best solution was to write everything down, develop and train, train the trainer material, so that there was consistency in the system. Now we have online training, which really gives a lot of consistency to the program. I think it’s a beautiful thing when people in a networking group become close, close friends. And key is to make sure it doesn’t detract from the group’s goal of building each other’s business through networking and through referrals. It’s important for all networking groups to ensure that there’s a strong sense of purpose and a solid structure. And for each member to be committed to carrying out the systems for networking which are in place. We certainly have that in BNI. And this helps to ensure that members agree to be accountable with their business networking friends.

So I would love, if you’ve got some comments or questions, I’d love to hear them, Priscilla. And if you’re listening to this, I’d love to hear or have you give feedback here on the podcast about how your BNI group maintains its focus and commitment to the process and to the system. What does your chapter do? What do your chapter leaders do to engage and inspire people to participate in the process in a positive way to be friends, but to also hold accountability with those friendships? Leave a comment below. Priscilla, any thoughts?

Priscilla:
Just that I think BNI does a really good job of training. And it’s nice to have all of the materials that you’ve developed to be able to use them. When you’re trying to hold somebody accountable, you go back to the manual, and you say, “Well, it says it right here. You know, this is how we’re supposed to be doing it.” And it’s very helpful.

Ivan:
It is, it is. And may I suggest that, if you’re listening to those podcasts, what Priscilla just said is right on the mark. And it’s important to explain why. Because people don’t care about how until they understand why. So when you’re showing somebody that it needs to be done a certain way, it’s very important that you talk about the why.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Why do you want to do this? I had a BNI director in my house just a couple of weeks ago, who was, you know, talking about the fact that they added something to the agenda, (which a number of chapters have done and I have gotten on a soapbox before), where the second time around… You know, the first time you do your weekly presentation and second time around you give referrals or testimonials. Well, they also say what CEUs they did, and what one-to-ones they did, and they didn’t want to drop that. And this is a director telling me this.

I said to them, “Look, the second time around is about talking about someone else.” The reason that I set it up the way I did was the first time to talk about you. The second time is to edify someone else. And what you’re doing by talking about yourself, is the meeting becomes all about you. Where a piece of the meeting needs to be about someone else. You need to give a referral to someone else, or you need to thank someone for the business they’ve done. It’s not all about you. And that’s the why, in the second part of the meeting where you give a referral or you give a testimonial. Talking about yourself is just patting yourself on the back. And so it’s really important to talk about the why whenever possible. So if you don’t know the why, talk to your BNI Director, and get the why. Because we can tell you why the agenda was set up the way it was. Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah, definitely.

Ivan:
Well, that’s all I have for today, unless you’ve got something else, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Just that it is hard to hold friends accountable. That is so true.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
You know, and make excuses for them and bend the rules to make it work for them if they need that. And it’s hard not to do that.

Ivan:
It is and again, so here’s an example of how you do more like Mandela than Attila. We had a person in a [BNI] chapter who was always late. And he was incredibly well respected. And I may have told this story in an earlier podcast, I don’t know. But he was always late. And I don’t mean, you know, late for the Open Networking; he would show up like five or 10 minutes after the meeting started. And he was a well-respected business person. And I knew that if I sat down and spanked him, and said, “You gotta stop coming in late,” he was just going to get defensive.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And he was a friend. And so what I did with him was I asked to meet with him, and he knew what the topic was going to be. And I started off by saying to him, “I don’t know if you know how well respected you are in the chapter.” which kind of took him by surprise, because he didn’t think that’s what I was going to open with. And he was like, “What?” I said, “Well, I mean, you’re very successful.” He was an attorney. “You’re a very successful attorney in the community. And people in this chapter really, really look up to you. And they recognize your success, and they’re really glad you’re part of the group.” He said, “Well, thank you.” I said, “My pleasure. And when you come in after the meeting starts, I don’t know if you’ve noticed, but other people are starting to come in late. And you are such a strong role model in the chapter that it would really help the chapter if you could set a different role model than coming in after the meeting starts.” He’s like, “Yeah, yeah, you’re right. I get it. I will.” And he did. And people showing up late ended, because he was such a strong role model.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Apply the rules more like Mandela than Attila.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
That’s it for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. That’s such a great podcast. Thank you, Ivan.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
