excerpt.rendered: Make friends in BNI, but don’t let friendship prevent members from building each other’s business through networking and through referrals.

title.rendered: Episode 809: BNI in Smaller Markets

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Smaller cities and markets have huge potential for growing BNI. James Cooper, Director of Field Operations West for US CORE Regions, joins Dr. Misner this week to explain.

One BNI chapter can have a huge impact in a smaller market. People in small communities already value relationships, but need the structure of BNI to turn those relationships into referrals.

Using the BNI Connect app, members in these markets can connect with other members in similar markets around the world and open up new business avenues by learning what their chapters have done.

And as these chapters grow, they can sponsor new chapters in nearby towns and cities. The more chapters there are in the area, the wider each member’s network grows, and the more opportunities there are for everyone.

Complete Transcript of Episode 809

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great. And I’m in Austin this week, spending some time at home. It’s nice to be home for a while.

Priscilla:
Oh, I bet. And who do we have on our podcast today?

Ivan:
I have a guest today. His name is James Cooper. He has 12 years of experience in BNI as both a member and a director, and he’s currently serving as the Director of Field Operations West for US CORE Regions. James has extended experience in chapter launches with more than 150 chapters launched under his training and management. He has the record for the first ever Spanish language chapter launch as part of the [BNI] Houston team. And the first ever “launch in a day”, which we actually did in 2020. We launched the [BNI] chapter in one day. And when I say we, I mean James, launched a chapter in one day, in 2020, which was truly amazing. His passion is to help members unlock the potential for their BNI membership. James, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

James:
It’s amazing to be here. And just a privilege to talk with you guys today.

Ivan:
Well, it’s great to have you on as a guest. And you’re going to talk today about the value of BNI in smaller markets. And, you know, we try and pick different topics for people. And this is one that we really haven’t talked a lot about – the smaller markets. And so this is a good one. Our smaller cities and markets have huge potential for growing BNI. Talk about that a little.

James:
Yeah, it’s just I have a lot of passion around our smaller markets. I come from a pretty small town. And, you know, as I got into this role, where I had a little more national opportunities to see things, there was a lot of our markets where we might have 100,000 people and no chapters or one chapter. And so especially in the heartland and different areas, as I met these members or potential members, I kind of saw like, wow, there’s some amazing opportunities, including my home state of Oklahoma, where there was just so much opportunity for growth and the members means so much. And the membership means so much to them. So I’ve kind of spent a lot of the last year thinking about how can we benefit them, and it’s opened up my eyes that we have a pretty big opportunity to grow our membership and also help our members in these markets.

Ivan:
Yeah, and in those, you know, local communities, small communities doesn’t really help businesses, think of larger communities for BNI. But those local communities really need to develop their business, wouldn’t you agree?

James:
I would. And sometimes, you know, in a small community, people tend to know each other. And so maybe the membership lands just a little bit differently, because they kind of tend to do business by relationships. And people tend to know each other. So it really lines up with our core values. And sometimes one chapter can have a big impact on a smaller community.

Ivan:
I remember the first chapter I kicked off in a real small community, it was in Montana. It was a population of 10,000 people. And, of course, you know, the people, as you said, they kind of knew each other. And when they got to the end of the meeting, and I was doing the kickoff, and one of the prospective members looks over at the director, whose name was Bill, and he said, “Bill, Bill, why do we have to get here every week and do these darn meetings? I mean, if we have a referral, just, you know, call each other, give each other a referral.”

And I remember Bill said to him, “How many years have we known each other – ten years? Ten years, we’ve known each other.” He said, “How many referrals have I given you?” He said, “Well, you haven’t given me any.” He said, “How many referrals have you given me?” He said, “Well, I haven’t given you any.” He said, “Well, that’s why you have to get here every week. We have to come to the meetings, because we know each other but we’re not referring each other. And that’s what we need to do.”

James:
That’s so true. And I think you know, the nature of the relationship, BNI – the structure that it brings often thrives in those situations. Because the relationships are there, but the structure, the training, and then that sense of community and pride that we have as part of our chapter, is that missing ingredient. And all of a sudden it’s opening up business opportunities that were right under our nose, but we didn’t know it.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. So members in smaller markets… offline, you were talking about it being important to our mission. Explain that.

James:
Yeah, with our mission to help our members grow their business, I think BNI at large, whenever we expand the network, it really expands all of us – the knowledge base. It expands our ability to do business. You know, the “I” in BNI is “International”. I think sometimes it’s easy to think about the chapter level, but ideas that we get from other members, and the connection we get, can sometimes change the game for us. And I’m finding that, in serving these chapters and getting to know them, sometimes they’ll learn something from a chapter in another smaller market that can change the game for them. “Oh, that’s how you did that.” And so I think sometimes we need to give them that special attention. And if we’re a member in that market, we can reach out through our app and connect with other businesses, maybe in a couple of towns over or another similar market, and it can truly open up new business avenues, just with a thought of how would we do it. So I think that does serve our mission because, you know, for me, personally, I feel like BNI should be a household name; we need to help a lot more businesses, and there’s so many opportunities in these kinds of communities to make that impact.

Ivan:
I have a lot of people tell me BNI should be a household name. But the truth is, you know, we can’t be a household name until we literally have many hundreds of thousands or millions of members. And when that happens, then it’s a brand, it’s been developed enough that every business will recognize the brand. And in order to do that, you need to have more market coverage. And I know that was one of the things that you want to talk about a little bit about, you know, possibly chapters in local communities as supporting and helping each other open up new groups.

James:
Yeah, it’s amazing. Sometimes I’ll meet a chapter member or a chapter that maybe is the only one in the market, and they’ve been there a little while. And they may feel that there’s some lack of opportunity, like, well we know everyone. But the truth is, that’s just kind of one branch of the tree in that community. As you build more chapters in that area, actually it’s kind of like a magnet. That magnet, when you when you put another one and another one, it creates this pull, it gets you closer and closer to the people you need to meet, and it builds that sense of brand. And so I’ve seen chapters actually sponsor the next chapter, because that’s actually helping to extend their reach and helping to build their leadership in the community. And frankly, just build the network of trusted people for other clients that may be a little bit further away.

Ivan:
Yeah.

James:
It doesn’t create a spirit of competition; it helps that community, the brand awareness of BNI to be higher, [and] everyone gets to make more money.

Ivan:
Yeah, the brand awareness issue I learned decades ago.  I was in Stockholm [Sweden]. And someone was driving me through downtown. And I noticed that literally every two blocks, no more than three, there was a McDonald’s. And I thought that was crazy. Every two or three blocks there’s a McDonald’s. If I went more than three blocks, I was surprised that there wasn’t a McDonald’s and I talked to a marketing expert and I said, “How could that work?” And he told me, “It’s actually better for the brand to have multiple locations around because it’s – first of all, it’s really easy to get to. And it makes for more business, not less, because it’s a brand builder. And I realized that was also applicable to BNI and when chapters recognize that another chapter in a nearby or local community is actually building recognition for their chapter, then that chapter really gets it. And to see one chapter help another, literally sponsor another is amazing. You’ve seen that?

James:
I have and it’s truly heartwarming to see the impact of that because the way one of them put it to me when I was talking to them was they come feed off of our energy, and then we help them. And just think about the ties in the community and the business opportunity. It really elevates the Givers Gain for those leaders that are helping those other chapters. And you want to help them and, no doubt, in the community, when they go out and talk about their chapter, they talk about the sense of community and how we’re helping each other. And you can imagine how that helps the first chapter to grow. And really, it’s kind of opening up the business market in that community.

Ivan:
Yeah, which is very important. Any closing thoughts, James, before we wrap up?

James:
I think the biggest thing I wanted to share today was if you find yourself in a market like this, first of all, know that how important you are as an individual member to what we do and what it means to your community. The second thing I would say is really we want to build pride of chapter and if you’re the first chapter or only chapter in your market, then you truly have that special opportunity to go get the best of every kind of professional we have. And like, hey, however you’re networking, whatever you’re doing, we’re special, we’re doing something special over here, and invite from a place of pride. And then if you hear that there’s something, some opportunities, some people that are locked out because of categories or whatever, or, you know, maybe a community over that there’s an opportunity for us to start another chapter, then let’s, you know, lend our full support to that, because as we expand that network, that givers gain works as social capital, then we get the benefits of building our network, and our brand, and also just that leadership skills, and it will come back and benefit us.

Ivan:
Yeah, and that’s the way the brand becomes recognizable. And the more recognizable it is, the easier it is to get visitors to come and to join. I remember seeing a chapter that had, it was in a community of only 5000 people. And I was really impressed that they had well over 30 members in their group, in a city, a community of 5000. And then another chapter opened up in an adjacent community or nearby community that also had 5000 people. And they kicked off with 40 members. And so there are, you know, two chapters side by side with only 5000 people in each community. And they both have chapters near 40 members. And let me tell you, BNI was a recognizable brand in those communities because of it.

James:
Yeah, I love those kinds of stories. And I think we have a lot more, I’ve identified just in the markets that I serve, dozens of opportunities like that. And I think as we kind of shine a light on that… the opportunities are big, and I think the impact on our overall membership, but also just on the stories that you’re going to hear in these communities is going to be amazing to see.

Ivan:
Agreed. James, thank you so much for being on BNI podcast. I appreciate your time today. Thank you so much. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Perfect. Thank you guys very much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Smaller markets have huge potential for growing BNI. James Cooper explains why small markets need BNI to turn relationships into referrals.

title.rendered: Episode 808: Show Up. Make Money. (Classic)

content.rendered:
Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 490, released on January 18, 2017. Some links and coupon codes may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

Attendance is one area where the easy path is harder and the hard path is easier. The more you show up, the more money you make. There’s hard data to back this up.

Back in 1985, there were some groups that met twice a month instead of every week. Those groups passed 52% fewer referrals than groups that met weekly. By 1986, all BNI groups met weekly.

The members who have fewest absences close the most business. Fewer absences = more referrals. Three absences in six months (the maximum) lead to 73% less business.

Has your chapter taken steps to improve attendance? What kind of results have you seen? Share your experience in the comments.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 490/808

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, featuring Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I am in Mexico this week at a TLC conference that is going on in Mexico. I always learn a lot of things from the Transformational Leadership Council.

Hey, a couple of weeks ago we talked about and I did a podcast about hard-easy, easy-hard. The hard path in life is often easier. You asked for an example in BNI and I mentioned attendance is one of those examples. It is hard to make sure and maintain the attendance policy in your chapter. Yet, the chapter ends up being much more responsible as a result of it.

That’s the topic for today’s podcast. I mentioned in the previous podcast that I would be covering that. I think it’s a powerful topic and one of the things that we have seen over time and we have some hard data to back this up is that if you show up, you are going to make more money. Period. End of story.

I mean, you could probably stop listening to the podcast right now if you just did what I said. But no, it helps if I give you some of the facts, so that’s what I am going to do.

Here’s a question: Have you ever gotten a hair cut over the phone? Probably not. It is just one of those things that you have to actually be present for. It turns out that is the same with BNI. People joint BNI to exchange referrals and increase business. Intellectually, we understand that it is important to build relationships, but it may not be obvious just how critical it is. It’s a referral process.

We have discovered- I have discovered over the years the impact attendance has on the organization. Years ago, we had BNI groups that met twice a month. Yes, it’s true. Some groups actually met twice a month back in 1985. Why don’t we do that now, you might be wondering.

Well, easy answer. We discovered that chapters that met twice a month passed 52% less referrals than groups that met weekly. Now, I want that to sink in. Groups that met twice a month back in 1985 passed 52% less referrals than the groups that met weekly. That’s a shocking statistic.

When groups were shown this hard data back in 1985, every single group except one switched to meeting weekly. And the one group that refused to make the transition shut down from lack of success.

So by 1986, all BNI groups worldwide – back then, just in California – had met weekly. All groups now meet weekly. We tried meeting twice a month and those were the results.

Recently, I coauthored a piece in SuccessNet with Beatrice Sparacino. Beatrice did her Bachelor’s thesis on networking and she did a study of BNI groups in her region. In her study, she looked at the amount of closed business compared to the number of absences per member in her region. Now, there is a graph that we are going to include in this podcast that I want you to take a look at.

Those members- this is hard data. Those members who had fewer absences are also the members that received a whole lot more closed business. You can see in the graph that as the number of absences double, the amount of closed business a member generated actually falls by 50%.

Now, does that kind of sound familiar? It hasn’t changed over the years. Less absences within a chapter equal more referrals and more closed business. The data is incredibly compelling. Someone who has three absences in six months, which is the maximum they should have, gets 73% less business, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Oh really? That’s a lot.

Ivan:
Yeah. Only three absences and they get 73% less business. Now, here is a shocking number. If someone has four absences in six months, which they shouldn’t have- three absences is the maximum in a six month period. But if they have four, if chapters are allowing someone to miss more, those individuals get 84.1% less business.

Priscilla:
It’s almost like they are not there.

Ivan:
It is almost like they are not there. It is shockingly low.

This is what I am talking about where attendance is absolutely critical to the success of the chapter and each individual member. You are shooting yourself in the foot if you don’t go to meetings. BNI is an accelerator for business referrals, reducing the time required to build relationships and trust among people.

It’s that trust that enables you to receive referrals and more closed business. In this sence, being present at your chapter meeting every week helps you be top of mind for your fellow members.

There is a member in Italy- and I told Beatrice that this is a great story. He said that some time ago, he had become really passionate about a famous reality TV show. He said particulary, there was one couple that was competing in the show. He started following them on Twitter, Facebook, social media. Then he said the unthinkable happened. is favorite couple was eliminated from the competition and they were no longer on the show.

At first, he was really disappointed but after a few weeks, he kind of forgot about them. They were invited back as guests to the show. It was at this point that he realized that in an incredibly short period of time, he had totally forgotten his favorite contestants. These were people he was really engaged with, following them on Facebook and doing all of this.

But he had forgotten about them after a few weeks.

The old adage “out of sight, out of mind” is absolutely true. And it’s true in BNI. And it’s true in referrals. Members who think that missing meetings regularly doesn’t really matter are clearly mistaken. The data is crystal clear. Miss meetings and lose business or make meetings and get more business. The data is really compelling and it has been since the very start.

If you are listening to this podcast, take the graph that is shown here. Print it out and the transcript. Take it to your chapter. Talk to your chapter and then go back and listen to the podcast that I did that the hard path is easier because it really connects to this podcast in the sense that if chapters really focus on improving the attendance in their groups, they are going to generate substantially more referrals.

That’s all I have for today, Priscilla, unless you have any questions.

Priscilla:
No, I just want to add that I think that it also shows respect for the other members because then you are also attending their 10 minute presentations or whatever goes on in the meeting.

Ivan:
Yeah. And just think, maybe that is part of the relationship process. That is really what we are talking about here. It’s maintaining that relationship. Part of that relationship is, as you said, showing up and hearing that week’s speaker’s presentation. In missing those individuals and their presentations, you just don’t tighten up the relationship as much as you can.

So I think that there are some real good subjective reasons like that one. And there are some really good objective reasons like the data that shows that showing up is going to help you generate more business.

Priscilla:
Yeah. I agree.

Ivan:
Well, listen, I would love for the listeners to leave any comments, especially if you have done anything in your chapter to improve attendance and the kind of results that you have gotten by doing that. I think that is the kind of thing that other members need to see. So if you have tightened up attendance in you chapter and had much better results, share it here on BNI podcast.com. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Thank you Dr. Misner. Well, I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much for attending. I would like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by The Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Regular attendance is one of the keys to success in BNI. The more you show up, the more money you make. Fewer absences = more referrals.

title.rendered: Episode 807: The Prima Donna Syndrome

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Prima donna syndrome is a term used to describe individuals who display excessive egotism, arrogance, and self-centeredness in their professional and personal lives. The behavior of individuals with prima donna syndrome can lead to low morale, high turnover, and strained relationships with colleagues or clients. The earlier you recognize it and the sooner you address it, the stronger your organization will be.

Listen to this episode to learn how to identify prima donna syndrome and how to address it when you encounter it.

Complete Transcript of Episode 807

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am, this week, in Miami at the US Conference, you BNI US [National] Conference. And it’s an exciting place to be, to have a conference.

Priscilla:
Yeah, it sounds like it. So what do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
Well, so you know, at one point, I was thinking about actually doing a book on this topic, but I probably don’t have enough material. So I’m doing this podcast and I’ll have a blog and an article out on it.

The prima donna syndrome is a term used to describe individuals who display excessive egotism, arrogance, self-centeredness, in their professional and personal lives. And this syndrome can be found in people from all walks of life. But it’s often observed in those who have achieved, or think they have achieved, a level of success or fame.

I first recognized this with two individuals that I worked closely with, and neither of them were really (chuckling)…they didn’t really achieve a substantial level of success or fame, but they were beginning to and, boy, they fell right into what I call the prima donna syndrome. The first gentleman was a trainer within the company and he was a good trainer and he was very entertaining. The problem was his territory was in horrible shape. He acted like a prima donna but performed like a deadbeat. The showman just couldn’t show up in results, but he behaved like he was the best in the company. The second person was a co-author of a networking book. As soon as her name was on the book, you’d think she was born a queen. She jumped into the prima donna syndrome with both feet and treated everyone accordingly.

When individuals exhibit these kinds of characteristics, they may find it difficult to build and maintain positive relationships both personally and professionally. Their constant need for attention and validation coupled with a lack of empathy for others can create feelings of isolation and loneliness with other people.

The prima donna syndrome is characterized by an exaggerated sense of self-importance and obsession with status, and an expectation of special treatment. Individuals with the syndrome often believe that they’re the most talented, intelligent, and hardworking people in their industry. They demand recognition for their accomplishments, and they expect others to cater to their needs and to their desires.

Unfortunately, the prima donna syndrome can really have devastating effects on an individual’s career and personal life. Prima donnas often alienate colleagues and clients with their demanding and entitled behavior. They may refuse to collaborate with others, believing that they’re the only ones capable of achieving success. They may also become resentful or hostile when they don’t receive the recognition they feel they deserve.

One of the most insidious aspects of prima donna syndrome is that they can be really difficult to detect in oneself. It’s hard for them to see it in themselves. Many individuals with the syndrome believe that they’re simply ambitious or driven, rather than arrogant or entitled. And as a result, they may be resistant to feedback or criticism, and they may struggle to form meaningful relationships with others.

So here’s some traits I’ve observed relating to this behavior.

One is the need for control. Individuals with prima donna syndrome often have a strong desire to control situations and people around them. They may become frustrated or angry when things don’t go their way. And they may try to exert control over others through manipulation, bullying or other tactics.

[Two] There may be a sense of entitlement. Prima donnas often have a belief that they’re entitled to certain privileges recognition or treatment that others are not. They may feel that they’re above the rules that apply to most people or that they deserve special treatment simply because of their position or their status.

[Three] They may have a lack of empathy. Prima donnas may have difficulty empathizing with others, and they may dismiss opinions or feelings of those around them. They may lack the ability to understand or appreciate the perspectives of others and often come across as cold, aloof… certainly, uncaring.

[Four] They have an inability to take feedback. Individuals with the prima donna syndrome may become defensive or angry. When receiving feedback or criticism. They may view any suggestion for improvement as a personal attack and may be unwilling to acknowledge or address those shortcomings.

[Five] There’s often a need for attention. Prima donnas often crave attention and may behave in ways that draw attention to themselves. And they may seek to be the center of attention or may use attention-seeking behavior to feel validated or important. This was the case with the co-author that I mentioned above. Definitely the case with that person.

[Six] They may be a poor team player. Individuals with the prima donna syndrome may be unwilling to collaborate or work with others. They may belittle word just dismiss the contributions of other people and they may view themselves with as the sole authority on a given topic or task.

[Seven] One additional trait of individuals with the prima donna syndrome is their tendency to think that they’re above menial tasks or mundane responsibilities. They may believe that these tasks are beneath them, and they should be focusing on the other “important” work. However, this can lead them to be seen as lazy or entitled, particularly entitled, by their colleagues.

Overall, people with a prima donna syndrome may exhibit a range of challenging behaviors that can make it difficult to work or interact with these people effectively.

So it’s important to recognize these traits and develop strategies for managing or mitigating their impact on others. So dealing with someone who has a prima donna syndrome can be challenging. But here are some strategies, five strategies, that will help you address the people that have or appear to have this prima donna syndrome. The helpful techniques to work with them.

One is to establish clear boundaries. It’s important to set clear boundaries with individuals who exhibit prima donna behavior. Establish expectations for how you’ll work together and communicate these expectations clearly and assertively. And be prepared to enforce the consequences if the individual crosses these boundaries.

The second one is to stay calm and professional. It’s important to remain calm and professional when dealing with prima donnas, even if they become hostile or difficult. Avoid getting drawn into arguments, or emotional reactions, as this can just escalate the situation and make it harder to resolve.

The third one is to focus on the facts and focus on solutions. When communicating with someone who has prima donna tendencies, try to focus on facts and solutions rather than the emotions that might be presented. Provide clear, objective feedback and offer suggestions for improvement or for compromise.

Fourth is seek support. Dealing with someone with prima donna syndrome can be stressful and challenging. So it may be helpful to seek support from colleagues, friends or, you know, on a personal level, a mental health professional. If it’s a personal friend… it doesn’t tend to work with a professional associate, but on a personal friend, you know, seeking help from a mental health professional.

Actually, I have for both personal friends and professional friends. I have talked to experts, mental health experts, about how to handle the individual. One of the people that I’ve talked to a number of times has been on my podcast, Mark Goulston, Dr. Goulston. You know, I’ve reached out to Mark several times and said, “Hey, I’ve got this person that’s got this issue or that issue,” and he’s been very helpful in giving me suggestions on how to deal with challenges like that. So talking through your experiences and feelings with someone can help you maintain perspective and develop effective coping strategies.

Fifth, consider disengaging. In some cases, it may be necessary to disengage from an individual with prima donna syndrome if they’re consistently difficult or toxic to work with, and if it’s possible to do. If you feel the individual’s behavior is impacting your well-being, or the well-being of others, it may be time to consider ending the relationship or seeking other professional or personal opportunities than with this individual.

In summary, the behavior of individuals with prima donna syndrome can lead to low morale, high turnover, and strained relationships with colleagues or clients. The earlier you recognize it and the sooner you address it, the stronger your organization will be.

And I hate to say it but you know, sometimes we get people in a BNI chapter that behave like this. And so these are some suggestions on how to work with them, and how to work with people that you meet in other contexts.

One of the things that I’ve loved, and I’ve talked about these two gentlemen many times on the podcast and in my writings, two people, that if there were anyone that might act like prima donnas, these two certainly have the credentials. And that would be Richard Branson and Jack Canfield. Both of them, Richard is an associate; Jack is a close friend. And both of them are unbelievably successful in their fields. And yet, neither one of them, their ego enters the room before they do. Those are two individuals that have learned how humility, and being humble, is also powerful. One of my favorite sayings is humble people don’t think less of themselves, they just think of themselves less.

Priscilla:
That’s a good one.

Ivan:
And prima donnas tend to think of themselves a lot. That’s the big difference. So those are my thoughts for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, how does prima donna syndrome differ from narcissism? It seems like they’re very close.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, they can be. The two people that have come to mind that I mentioned in this piece, neither of them I would consider narcissists. What happened was they worked their way into a role where they thought they were more important than they were. Both of them, by the way, are no longer in involved, and they’re not in that role, or any role. So, it had the opposite impact. But neither of them I would consider narcissists.

So a narcissist definitely is a prima donna, no question about it. But a prima donna isn’t necessarily a narcissist. And that’s my opinion. That’s the way I would describe it. And I’m basing it on the fact that I’ve met many people that, you know, all of a sudden, they get into a position and now they’re the king of the hill, and they behave like a prima donna, but they’re not naturally a narcissist. Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah. Is the prima donna syndrome… is that a real thing, or something that you created a title for?

Ivan:
It’s a phrase that I came up with, but I found it on Google. So, you know, it’s a phrase that I’ve been using for a long, long time. I didn’t read it or hear it anywhere, but it appears to have been used in other pieces. So I first started talking about it probably 20 years ago.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh. Okay.

Ivan:
Well, if you’ve listened to this, I hope this helps give you, BNI members, it gives you some sense on how to handle prima donnas.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm. Okay, I think that’s very good. Ivan. Thank you so much.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 806: 10 Tips to Be a Better Networker

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Note: This podcast has similar content to Episode 802, but with additional content and a humorous slant. 

Networking is like going to the gym: you know you should do it, but it can be hard to get started and keep going. Here are ten tips to help you navigate the world of business networking.

  1. Be yourself–unless you’re a serial criminal.
  2. Take the time to get to know people and find common ground.
  3. Follow up with people, but don’t be a stalker.
  4. Be of service, but don’t be a doormat.
  5. Attend networking events, but don’t be a wallflower.
  6. Leverage social media, but don’t be a troll.
  7. Join networking groups, but don’t join a social club.
  8. Offer value, but don’t offer your kidney.
  9. Utilize referrals, but don’t be a “name dropper.”
  10. Attend industry events, but don’t be a snob.

Complete Transcript of Episode 806

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great. I’m at my home office in Austin, Texas. I actually have like three or four weeks where I’m not traveling, which is really nice. I’m glad to have this time. Hey, I’ve got a fun podcast today, a fun topic.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
Ten tips to be a better networker. But it’s got a little humor, at least I hope everyone feels it’s got a little humor. So look, we all have to face it — networking can sometimes be daunting. It’s like speed-dating for business. But instead of finding a soulmate, you’re trying to find clients, partners, referrals, and mentors. It’s like going to the gym, you know, you should do it, but it can be hard to get started and keep doing. But fear not, BNI members, here are 10 techniques to help you navigate the world of business networking.

Number one: Be yourself. Unless you’re a serial criminal, then maybe don’t be yourself.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
But for the rest of us, authenticity is key. People can smell a fake a mile away. So don’t try to be someone you’re not. Unless you’re Batman; being Batman is always cool.

Two: genuine relationships. This is not a numbers game. It’s not about how many business cards you can collect. It’s about the connections you make. So take the time to get to know people and find common ground. And if you both like cats, even better. By taking the time to truly get to know the people you meet, you’ll be more likely to build a lasting relationship. And the cat thing is kind of meant to be humorous. But the truth is, when you have overlapping interests, that’s when you can really start to build relationships.

[Three] Follow up with people. But don’t be a stalker. You don’t want to be that person who sends 20 emails and calls 10 times in one day, that’s just creepy. And by the way, I’ve had people that were pretty close to that. You do want to make an effort to keep in touch with your contacts. So it’s crucial to make a point of following up with your contacts after meeting them. I’d recommend a podcast that I did here on BNI podcast, “24-7-30” [Episode 623]. Take a look at that podcast, and that’s a good example of following up. So follow up. Whether it be through a phone call, email, or LinkedIn message, follow up. And this will help you to keep the conversation going and continue to build your relationship. Maybe even send a funny meme every now and then. Who doesn’t love a good meme? Right?

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
Four: be of service. But don’t be a doormat. You want to help people, but you also want to make sure you’re not being taken advantage of. This is essential when you’re serving others. Remember being of service doesn’t mean sacrificing your own well-being, or allowing others to disrespect you, or push you to do something you don’t want to do. So offer your expertise and resources but set boundaries. And if someone tries to sell you a timeshare run, just run.

Five: attend networking events. But don’t be a wallflower. It can be tempting to hide in a corner with a plate of free appetizers, but that’s not going to get you anywhere. So put on your best smile, grab a drink, and start mingling. And if someone spills their drink on you don’t worry, it’s just liquid confidence. Attending networking events is a great way to meet new people and make connections.

Six: leverage social media, but don’t be a troll. Social media can be a great way to connect with people, but it can also be a minefield of trolls. So use it wisely. Post helpful content, engage with others. And don’t get into Twitter fights, trust me, it’s not worth it. Platforms like LinkedIn, Twitter and Facebook provide opportunities to connect with other professionals to share your expertise and to learn from others.

By building your online presence, you can expand your network and increase your visibility which is an important one. And those of you who follow me here on this podcast or on social media, recognize that I’m very very active on social media. I think it’s a great way of creating visibility and educating people about who you are and what you do.

Seven. This one is kind of obvious for all of us in BNI – Join networking groups. But don’t join a social club unless you simply need more friends, in which case you might just consider buying a dog.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
You might want to make sure that the networking groups that you join align with your values and your goals. You also want to focus on real networking, not just hanging out in a coffee klatch. So these groups provide opportunities to meet other professionals and gain access to valuable resources. Joining groups like a Chamber of Commerce, I’ve recommended Chambers of Commerce for decades now. And of course, BNI can provide a structure and a focus that allows entrepreneurs to be more effective in their networking efforts.

Eight: offer value, but don’t offer your kidney. Okay, that might be another extreme example, but you want to make sure you’re offering something that’s valuable without giving away the farm. Maybe it’s a helpful tool or a connection to someone in your network. And if someone asks for your kidney, well, maybe it’s time to leave that networking event.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
Nine: utilize referrals, but don’t be a “name dropper”. Referrals can be a great way to expand your network, but don’t go around dropping names like they are hot potatoes. Be respectful of your contacts’ privacy, and only make introductions when it makes sense. And if someone drops a name, you don’t recognize, just smile and nod. It’s okay. You can Google them later, that works.

Number 10: Attend industry events. But don’t be a snob. It’s great to attend events that are focused on your industry or your interests, as they provide an opportunity to network with like-minded individuals, and to learn from experts in your field. Niche events can also give you insights into new trends and technologies that you might not have been aware of otherwise. I think it’s really important to network with an open mind. Don’t dismiss people who may not have the same level of expertise or experience as you. Instead, approach conversations with a willingness to learn from others, and share your knowledge and insights.

This process is really about building relationships, and finding ways to help others, not just promoting yourself. So be genuine. Listen actively. Try to make meaningful connections with people you meet. You never know where these relationships might lead to in the future. Networking doesn’t have to be a chore. By building genuine relationships, being authentic, following up, being of service, attending networking events, leveraging social media, and joining associations and networking groups, you can network with humor and style. Just remember to keep it real, be helpful, and have a good time. After all, a smile is probably one of the best networking tools that you have.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
Those are my thoughts on the top 10… well, I don’t know if they’re the top 10, but they are 10 tips to be a better networker. And I wrote this with, you know, tongue in cheek, a little bit of fun.

Priscilla:
Right. That’s very cute. I have to say, it got me giggling.

Ivan:
Well, that was the goal. And so if you’re an Education Coordinator, in particular, take these tips. And I would recommend that you take two or three at a time, and share them over the next several weeks. You know, talk a little bit about them, have the members talk about them, you know, bring up one or two or three. And you describe it and then have members participate in the discussion, make it seminal, make it a discussion, and do a couple of three at a time. And really go a little bit deeper and talking about these and how people use them. I think it’s a great use of Education Coordinator time.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm. Yeah, it’s really nice.

Ivan:
Well, that’s everything that I have for today, Priscilla. Thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay, good job, Ivan. That was fun. All right.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
