excerpt.rendered: Networking is like going to the gym: you know you should do it, but it’s hard to get started and keep going. Here are 10 tips to help you.

title.rendered: Episode 805: Why Collaboration Works

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Collaboration is fundamental to BNI. Groups of business people working together achieve far greater results than they would achieve by working separately. In this episode, Dr. Misner explains why.

The sum of the whole is greater than the individual parts.

Networking is a team sport. You need the whole team working together to win. The more great people you get together in a chapter, the higher the per capita number of referrals in the group.

Sometimes 1 + 1 = 11

When people work together, they can bounce ideas off each other, challenge each other’s assumptions, build on each other’s strengths. The result is a level of creativity and innovation that’s really impossible to achieve when working alone.

The Third Paradigm

Dr. Misner will be releasing a new book based on these ideas later this year. It’s called The Third Paradigm. Stay tuned for more podcast episodes on this topic when it’s published.

Complete Transcript of Episode 805

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, and I am at home in Austin, Texas. I thought I’d open up by just mentioning that last year, 2022, you know that www.misneraudioprograms.com sponsors this podcast and all of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. And last year, we were able to donate… or this year for last year, we’re able to donate $2,500 to the BNI Foundation.

Priscilla:
Yay.

Ivan:
So if you have a chance go to www.misneraudioprograms.com, Priscilla will mention it at the end, and she’ll give a code that gives you a big, big discount.

Priscilla:
Yes, perfect. Okay, great. So what are you sharing with us today?

Ivan:
Well, the idea that collaboration is so important and valuable. The idea of working together to achieve better results is a concept that’s been around for centuries. Whether it’s in the context of a family, a community, or workplace, the notion that a group of people can accomplish more than an individual working alone has proven to be true time and time again. And I’ve certainly seen that in BNI, where groups of business people working together achieve far greater results than they would achieve by working separately. So there are, I believe, at least two key ideas that underpin this concept. The first notion is that the sum of the whole is greater than the individual parts. And I’ll talk more about that in a moment.

The second is something that my friend Sam Horn likes to say, which is “Sometimes 1 + 1 = 11.”  I love that phrase.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
So let’s start with the first one, the idea that the sum of the whole is greater than the individual parts. And that suggests that when people work together, their collective efforts are greater than the sum of their individual contributions. In other words, a group of people can accomplish more together than they could individually. This concept is certainly seen in team sports where a group of athletes working together can achieve a greater level of success than any one of them could on their own. The same can be true in the workplace where a team of employees collaborate to create innovative solutions to complex problems. That’s really the basis of a book that I’ve got coming out later this year, called “The Third Paradigm”.

And we have certainly seen that in BNI, where a group of people getting together and working together generate way more referrals than what people would do on their own. As a matter of fact, we’ve seen, you know, that there’s a direct correlation between the size of a quality group — quality group, good members — the size of a quality group makes a big difference in the amount of referrals. So the more great people you get together, the higher the per capita number of referrals in the group. And so the sum of the whole is, in fact, greater than the individual parts.

The second idea that underpins the concept of working together is the notion, Sam Horn’s notion, that one plus one can equal 11. And this idea suggests that when two people work together, their combined efforts are far greater than what they can achieve on their own. This concept is often used to describe the synergy that can be created when people collaborate when they work with one another. When two people work together, they can bounce ideas off each other, challenge each other’s assumptions, they can build on each other’s strengths. The result is a level of creativity and innovation that’s really impossible to achieve when working alone. We really saw that during COVID Where BNI members got online and Zoom, and they supported each other and help each other, and ended up doing (many of them) really well during a horribly difficult time in the business world. So there are many benefits to working together to achieve better results.

One of the most significant benefits is the ability to leverage the strengths of each team member. Because different people have different strengths. And when people work together, they can pool their knowledge, their skills, their experience, to create a more effective solution. This approach allows each team member to focus on their strengths and contribute to the team in the most meaningful way possible.

Another benefit of working together is the ability to learn from each other. We certainly see that in BNI. When people work together, when they collaborate, they can have the opportunity to share ideas and perspectives, and these can lead to new insights and approaches. This approach also helps to build trust and understanding between team members, which is essential for effective collaboration. That trust is critical, particularly in an organization like BNI. So working together has other potential benefits. You can increase efficiency and productivity. When people work together, they divide tasks and responsibilities in a way that allows them to accomplish more in less time. We certainly see that with leadership teams of a chapter. When that chapter picks a great leadership team, they divide the tasks and responsibilities very effectively. So this approach helps reduce the workload for each team member, and not just in BNI, but in general, which can lead to a better work life balance and an increased job satisfaction.

The idea of working together, Priscilla, I think, is to achieve better results. And that’s a powerful concept that has proven to be true time and time again. Again, whether it’s in the context of a family, a community or workplace, the benefits of collaboration, to me are really, really clear. By leveraging the strengths of each team member, and learning from each other, and increasing efficiency and productivity, teams can achieve success that would be impossible to achieve, impossible to achieve, when working alone. The sum of the whole is indeed greater than the individual parts. And when people work together, one plus one really can equal 11. Those are my thoughts for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
Anything you’d add?

Priscilla:
I love the idea of collaboration. And I think it also is really good for your spirit.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
To have somebody to pass questions back and forth with, and to get your ideas, you know, validated or not. But you know, it’s just, I miss it. Because what I’m doing now isn’t so much collaborative. But when I was younger, and I worked in a construction company with other women, and we were a collective, we really were collaborative, and that was such a fun time. I really enjoyed that.

Ivan:
Well, members can really get that sense by doing one-to-ones.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm, yeah.

Ivan:
And having conversations and, you know, collaborating on ideas that can turn into referrals or business or stronger business.

Priscilla:
Right. In BNI, the leadership team is extremely collaborative.

Ivan:
Yeah. So I’ve got a book coming out later this year, from the original date of this podcast. It’ll be later this year. It’s called “The Third Paradigm.” And it is about, it really is about co-creation, which is sort of collaboration on steroids. And so I’ll definitely be talking about that new book when it comes out. A lot of what’s in here in this podcast is really elaborated on in the upcoming book. That’s all I have for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, that sounds great. Ivan, thank you so much.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Groups of business people working together achieve far greater results than they would achieve by working separately.

title.rendered: Episode 804: When to Say No to a One-to-One

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Tiffanie Kellog returns to the podcast today to say explain that it’s never okay to say no to a One-to-One if you want to generate referrals from the member who asks you. If you’re too busy for a One-to-One, how are you going to have time to take care of the referral they want to pass you?

Instead of saying no…

  • Schedule outside your busiest season. Members understand that certain times of year are bad for certain professions.
  • Schedule well in advance. One-to-Ones are always more successful when both members have time to prepare.
  • Have a purpose and format. It could be the G.A.I.N.S. exchange for a first One-to-One, or an exercise like the one in Episode 683.
  • Stack multiple One-to-Ones on the same day at the same location. This saves you time traveling to multiple locations on different days.
  • Tailor the One-to-Ones to the member’s Room Full of Referrals behavioral style. Go Getters, Promoters, Nurturers, and Examiners prefer different types of One-to-Ones.
  • Ask what they want to talk about and how long they think it will take. Having an agenda can help you decide how soon you want to schedule the One-to-One and whether you need a One-to-One or just a quick conversation.

Further Reading & Listening

  • Episode 683: One Tool for Hundreds of Referrals
  • Episode 788: Room Full of Referrals, 10th Anniversary
  • Download Tiffanie’s free ebook, The Referral Mindset.
  • Visit www.tiffaniekellog.com or tampa.asentiv.com to learn more about Tiffanie.

Complete Transcript of Episode 804

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I think I mentioned in the previous weeks, I went to the Inland Valley in Southern California, my old stomping grounds in Orange County. It’s great to be back to where I grew up, and to see my grandson, Ethan. He’s my first grandson. So it was a great trip. By the way, Priscilla, this week marks the 16th anniversary for the BNI Podcast. It’s been a great experience.

Priscilla:
Wow, wow, wow! What can I say? That’s amazing.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s amazing. And you’ve done like 95% of the podcasts with me.

Priscilla: (laughing)
I know.

Ivan:
I have a guest with me today. And anyone who’s been listening to the podcasts over the years will recognize this guest. It is Tiffanie Kellogg, who has been a part of BNI since 2005. She’s the Training Team coordinator in BNI West Central Florida. And she has the honor of traveling all over, both in-person and via Zoom, sharing her expertise in referral marketing and networking. Tiffanie is featured on both BNI Business Builders as well as the BNI Podcast, where she and Jeremy Walsh have had the most guest appearances. Jeremy and Tiffanie have had the most guest appearances here on BNI Podcast. Tiffanie, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Tiffanie:
Thank you for having me. And I’m really excited because it’s the anniversary of the Podcast. And it’s actually my 18th year anniversary this week of the very first BNI meeting that I went to.

Ivan:
Well, that’s fantastic. I’m so pleased to have you in the organization for 18 years. And it’s ironic that it’d be the same week that the Podcast started. So you’ve got a great topic, and I want to jump right in. And that is: when is it okay to say no to a One-to-One? So answer that for me. When is it okay to say no to a One-to-One?

Tiffanie:
So the answer is NEVER is it okay to say no to a One-to-One, if you still want to generate referrals from that member.

Ivan:
Yeah, well, I think that’s really good. And by the way, you’ve tricked people with that question.

Tiffanie:
(laughing)

Ivan:
I love it. Because the answer, I think, is absolutely correct. So what’s the what’s the purpose of One-to-One?

Tiffanie:
So our goal is to build our relationships — to move from visibility, to credibility to profitability. And if we say no to a One-to-One, we’re really stopping that. And we may not actually say no to the One-to-One and the person hears it and says, “Oh, they don’t want to be in the relationship with me.” But if we’re too busy for our fellow BNI members then it is stopping the relationship. If you’re too busy for a One-to-One, how are you going to have time to take care of the referral I want to pass you?

Ivan:
Right? Yeah, very true. And you never know what can come from a One-to-One, even if you don’t think that person has much to contribute to you. I think I may have mentioned this in a podcast, I went to a business meeting and sat next to a college student. I first thought, “Geez, what am I doing, you know, sitting next to this college student. There’s all these business people that I want to sit next to.” And I thought, okay, you know, buck-up, buttercup. You’re an expert on networking, network with this girl. So I just asked her lots of questions and got her to open up.

And you know what that did? One of the business associates at that meeting came up to me afterwards and said, “Thank you.” I said, “Why?” He said, “Nobody ever talks to my daughter like that. Nobody ever, you know, they all want to sit next to the business people. But you just got her to open up and I really appreciate what you did at dinner.” And so I ended up making a good connection with the business person, and I hardly talked to him at all. So you never know where a One-to-One can lead.

So another topic that you want to talk about was letting members know that it’s okay to schedule in advance. Talk about that.

Tiffanie:
Yeah, sometimes I think people are like, “I want to do a One-to-One. How is today? How is tomorrow?” And what I’ve found is there’s two kinds of people in this world. Those that keep their schedule today and tomorrow. And those that keep their schedule “Let’s talk two to four weeks from now.”

Ivan:
Yeah. That’s me.

Tiffanie:
I’m the same way, most of the time. And so I think it’s important to share with people – Yes, let’s get it on the calendar, even if it’s later this month. And I know there are certain professions that have busy times. For example, the accountants trying to get the taxes done by April 15. People that do retail type space and being busy in the holidays, and I think most people would easily understand the CPA saying, “I would love to do a One-to-One with you. Can we schedule for the week after the April tax deadline?”

Ivan:
Totally. Yeah.

Tiffanie:
We just have to be open with people and say, “Yes, I want to.”

Ivan:
Yeah, totally agree. And you have to understand what their time restraints are. Just as you said, yeah. Alright, so how do you make the most out of each One-to-One?

Tiffanie:
So I think it starts with setting an intention for the One-to-One. Many BNI members that I know are like, “Do you want to do a One-to-One?” And that’s kind of it. I love to say, “What is the intention? What is the purpose of the One-to-One? What are we looking to accomplish?” And if it’s a beginning One-to-One, I love utilizing the G.A.I.N.S. profile tool to help me move from visibility, credibility into profitability. If it’s somebody that I’m at that higher level with, one of my favorite podcasts that you’ve done that I reference all the time is Episode 683, by you and Tim Roberts talking about one tool for hundreds of referrals. That’s a brilliant podcast to say, let’s do this in our One-to-One, listen before, and then make it happen.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s a great podcast. And was there one thing in particular that you liked, that you pull out of that, that you think is relevant?

Tiffanie:
What I think is most powerful about it is that we are taking what’s already on our calendar and using that to generate referrals. So it’s not taking a lot of extra time out of our day, it’s making the most of what’s already in your calendar.

Ivan:
Right.  Well, I’m glad you brought up the G.A.I.N.S. exchange, because I think it’s very powerful. And I like to use it, I think every now and then if you’re having a One-to-One with somebody for the third or fourth time, it’s good to update the G.A.I.N.S. exchange. But you know, certainly the second time you have it, you probably don’t need to do it. I’m glad you brought it up. Do you have a couple of time saving tips with One-to-Ones?

Tiffanie:
One of the things that I like to do is a stack day. And I probably did this more in-person than when doing the Zoom. But I would schedule a stack day where I would pick a quiet restaurant corner or a nice little business setting, and I would schedule 3, 4, 5, 6 back-to-back One-to-Ones, and I would meet with the person, then the next person, then the next person. And this saved me a lot of time because I wasn’t traveling to each of them. I wasn’t trying to find parking. I wasn’t dealing with traffic. It’s just, I’m here, let’s do the One-to-Ones. Especially when I’m driving far away.

Ivan:
Yeah, fair enough. I love that. And I don’t know if you know this, but I do that. I started it a few years ago called Founder’s Day. I do it the week before my birthday. I felt the best birthday gift for me would be to talk to as many [BNI] members as I possibly can. And so my birthday is June 30, so I think it’s the Thursday before June 30th is the Founder’s Day. And if you’re listening to this, go to my social media or go you know, talk to your director. I’ll be posting it up in various places. And you can apply, and I do about between 14 and 16 One-to-Ones, 30-minute One-to-Ones all day long.

Tiffanie:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And I stack them up and I think it’s a great way to connect with members. So look for that in June. It’ll probably go out in the end of May, the information. So the end of May look for the Founder’s Day information, which is basically a stack day. Love the idea, Tiffanie. Okay, give us give us a P.S. or a bonus. I know you have a bonus statement that you want to share. So tell us what it is.

Tiffanie:
So not that long ago, you did Episode 788, “Room Full of Referrals” the 10th Anniversary.

Ivan:
Yep.

Tiffanie:
And I think Room Full of Referrals and the behavioral styles plays a massive part in creating successful One-to-Ones and having more successful One-to-Ones. So you talk about Go Getters, Promoters, Nurturers, and Examiners. And would it surprise you… I mean, we won’t, but maybe those of you listening… to know that you might want to do your One-to-Ones a little bit different based on the behavioral styles of the person you’re meeting with.

Ivan:
Yeah, definitely.

Tiffanie:
And so I actually just got done with a free e-book that’s coming out, and we take a look at… even with the G.A.I.N.S. So if I’m meeting with a Go Getter, I might want to start with Goals and Accomplishments. If I’m meeting with a Promoter, they may not have a G.A.I.N.S. If they do, then they may want to start with Interests. Examiners, please go in order, because that would just be weird to go out of order.

Ivan:
I love it. And you’re absolutely right, using the behavioral profiles, the concepts in “A Room Full of Referrals” is a great idea to do One-to-Ones with. I think that’s just about everything. But I know Priscilla had a question that I would love for you to ask Priscilla. We’re running out of time, but I think we have enough time for it.

Priscilla:
Well, I was just talking about not having enough time to do the One-to-Ones that people request. And so I was hoping there was a way to deal with that.

Ivan:
Yeah, Tiffanie, share your thoughts on that.

Tiffanie:
So when somebody says, “Hey, Tiffanie, I’d love to do a One-to-One,” of course I would. And then we might follow up with, “Would you please send me the agenda of what you want to cover? So we can get the One-to-One on the calendar.” Because if somebody’s like, “Oh, I want to do a One-to-One to brainstorm on how to generate this referral for you,” that might want to get on the calendar quicker. If it’s just a, “Hey, let’s catch up.” then there’s not as much of an urgency. And so it gives us the opportunity to, you know, see where to put it on the calendar. Though I’ll share, sometimes Priscilla, people don’t follow up with the agenda. And if they don’t follow up with the agenda, maybe they weren’t serious enough about really wanting that One-to-One.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
Yeah. So that eliminates some and then what I would add to that is that those who do follow up, it’s okay to say, I’d be happy to do it. You know, I’m available in three weeks or four weeks, you know, set it up for a time that you’re actually available. You don’t have to drop everything to do that One-to-One. But if the person is going to spend the time to say, “Hey, here’s what I’d like to talk about.” I think it’s important to do that One-to-One.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I like the idea of the agenda. That seems like a great idea.

Ivan:
Yeah. And that response might be as simple as, “Hey, I’d like to, you know, redo the G.A.I.N.S. exchange or something.” But at least they’re getting back and you know they’re serious.

Tiffanie:
Yes, yes. They’ve got an intention. Because sometimes somebody… I’ve had a member be like, I have a new product. And I’m like, “How long do you think that’s gonna take?” “Five minutes to tell you about it.” “Why don’t we catch up after the meeting? We’ll do that and we’ll save the One-to-One for later.”

Ivan: Great idea. Great idea. All right, Tiffanie, for people who want to get a hold of you, they go to www.tiffaniekellogg.com. That’s www.tiffaniekellogg.com, or tampa.asentiv.com. You also have a link for your complimentary e-book. You want to share that?

Tiffanie:
Yeah. So it takes the Room Full of Referrals, puts it together with One-to-Ones and the G.A.I.N.S. profile. And that link is bit.ly/thereferralmindset.

Ivan:
Fantastic. Tiffanie, thank you for being on the BNI Podcast once again. It’s always great having you on.

Tiffanie:
Thank you so very much, Ivan. Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You’re welcome.

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla.      

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I think that’s it. Thank you so much to both of you.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: When is it okay to say no to a One-to-One? Never, if you still want to generate referrals from the member who asked you.

title.rendered: Episode 803: Do What You Love and You’ll Love What You Do (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 93, first released in 2009. Offers and links may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

What you should do is what you like to do. You can’t achieve sustained success if you don’t love what you do. More and more businesses are based on hobbies and personal interests.

Rather than finding out the hard way that you can’t stick it out at a job you hate just for the money, think about what you really love to do before starting a business or choosing a profession. Loving what you do is no guarantee of being successful, but if you’re not happy at what you’re doing, you can never achieve sustainable success over time.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcription of Episode 93/803

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I am joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
Doing great, Priscilla. I’ve got a topic that’s near and dear to me. I really love this topic, and it is, as you said, “Do what you love, and you’ll love what you do.”

As an adjunct university professor for almost 20 years, I’d often have students say to me, “Well, Dr. Misner, what kind of business or profession should I consider going into right now?”

And I’d always tell them, “Well, what do you like? What do you really enjoy doing?”

And they’d say something like, “No, no. You don’t understand. I’m asking what I should do.”

And I’d say, “No, no. You don’t understand. What do you like to do? What are you really interested in?”

And they’d look at me like I was from outer space. They were perplexed. They’d stutter out something about trying to figure out what a good profession to go in would be rather than what kind of business that they like.

And I’d go on to explain to them that you cannot achieve sustained success over time. You cannot achieve sustained success over time without doing something you love. Therefore, I would tell the students, “You need to think about what you really enjoy doing and then look for opportunities in those areas.”

I don’t know if you know this, but according to the National Federation of Independent Businesses, it’s estimated that 60 percent of all businesses started in the late ‘90s were based on hobbies or personal interests. More and more you see people turning their dreams into their livelihood.

I saw a great example of this really in action when I attended a fundraiser at a local university here in Southern California, it’s Azusa Pacific University, and I was there at an evening fundraiser that the BNI Foundation was supporting. And the program highlighted a graduate from the Physics Department, and his name was Steven. He graduated in 2001 from APU, and after graduation, he went to work at NASA’s JPL in Pasadena. He had a great job. And while he was working at JPL, a close family member passed away in a really tragic accident, and Steven and his wife, she had also had a very successful career, sat back and really evaluated their lives. And they decided that they were going to make a major change, and they chose to pursue a hobby that they had for some time and to walk away from very, very successful jobs that they had. And their hobby was making all natural body care products, and they wanted to turn it into a full-time business, because they just loved it, and they loved what it did throughout the world. And I’ll explain.

One of the reasons they did it was, it enabled them to, of course, work from home. They had a new family; they could spend time with the kids. The name of the company is Anti-Body, A-N-T-I hyphen Body. Here’s the interesting thing is, it not only offers all natural body care products, but it promotes global Fair Trade. That’s what really interested them, was to have some product that was natural but to also help those in need. And so what it does is it sources out all its raw materials directly from workers in developing countries creating sustainability for those who might be exploited in those very same markets.

And to me, this was a classic example of doing what you love and loving what you do and the impact that it has on the community or on other communities throughout the world. This decision really changed Steven’s life; it changed the course of his life. And now he’s really happy doing what he’s doing. He loves working every day, and his business is thriving, and I think it’s because – and by the way, he gets to do what he was trained. You may not think it, but he basically runs the physics department of the company, which is what he was training – and although it’s not aerospace – part of his training was in physics, and so he is able to use some of that training in the labs to help create the products. And I think that’s an incredible story.

I understand that loving what you do is no guarantee that you’re going to be successful. That’s important. Some people say, “I love what I do, but I’m not successful.” However, I’m equally confident that if you’re not happy at what you’re doing, you can never achieve any sustainable success over time. And that’s important.

So if you’re thinking about starting your own business, you’ve got to find what you love to do. I use the crazy story, but it’s almost like what I used to tell my kids when they were really small and we’d go to an ice cream shop and they just couldn’t decide what they wanted to have for ice cream. I’d say, “Well, what do you feel like?”

“Well, I don’t know.”

“Stick your tongue out, wave it around. What’s it feel like?”

They would laugh and stick their tongue out and wave it around and say, “Well, it feels like chocolate chip.”

“All right. Well, pick chocolate chip.”

So what I tell business people and certainly what I tell college students now, “Stick your tongue out, wave it around. What do you feel like doing? Pick something you really want, something you really want to be doing, and you’ll be a lot happier in life.” And I’m not just talking about picking ice cream.

Priscilla:
Yeah, you keep talking to us about that hidden ingredient, the secret ingredient of passion and how it affects the energy that you bring when you talk about your business, and I think that this topic is an example of that.

Ivan:
Yeah, if you’re not passionate about what you’re doing, you not going to be successful in the long haul. You can get by for a certain period of time, but certainly, when times are tough and the economy is a challenge, that’s when it really hits if you’re not passionate about what you’re doing. And that’s really important. I think if you can find something where you’re doing what you love, you’re going to love what you do and the chances of you being successful are much, much greater.

And I don’t know if you’ve noticed this, Priscilla, but I love what I do.

Priscilla:
I have noticed that, Ivan.

Ivan:
This is a fun business. I really feel like BNI is absolutely my calling. It’s the greatest business in the world. I’m able to help hundreds of thousands of business professionals in dozens and dozens of countries all around the world to build their business through a really great way. To me, BNI is not only a great way to get business, it’s an even better way to do business, and so I certainly have found what I’m passionate about. And anybody who’s out there that’s able to find theirs is going to achieve a much higher level of success.

Priscilla:
Well, thank you, Dr. Misner. That was wonderful.

I think that’s it for this week.

Ivan:
That’s it. Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I’d like to remind all of the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: What you should do is what you like to do. You can’t achieve sustained success if you don’t love what you do.

title.rendered: Episode 802: 7 Techniques to Be a Better Networker

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week, Dr. Misner shares 7 techniques that help you to be a better networker. You may recognize some of them from earlier podcasts.

  1. Build genuine relationships.
  2. Be authentic.
  3. Follow up.
  4. Be of service.
  5. Attend networking events.
  6. Leverage your social media
  7. Join associations and industry groups.

The key is to be consistent and to maintain the relationships that you built over time. The process of relationship networking is a marathon. BNI is a marathon, it’s not a sprint. It’s the BEST way to create referrals for life.

Read Dr. Misner’s article in Entrepreneur, 10 Easy Ways to Be a Better Networker.

Complete Transcript of Episode 802

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
You know, everybody that I meet around the world asks me that question. They all owe it to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
And they have no idea how much fun we have, before and after the recordings. We really do have some fun; we just had some fun now. (chuckling) I really enjoy working with you, Priscilla, thanks for doing this.

Priscilla:  
Oh, Ivan. Thank you. That’s great.

Ivan:
I am on my way to Los Angeles and Orange County; I’m going to be doing some presentations in LA and Orange County. For those of you who think I just stay home and do nothing but podcasts, or sit in my hot tub, I actually still travel a lot for BNI. And I LOVE traveling for BNI. I enjoy meeting BNI members all around the world. And I’m going back to the home region, the Founding Region of BNI this week. And I’m gonna also go down to Orange County and do a presentation down there. So I love being there. I lived in Southern California most of my life. So it will be good to be back.

Priscilla:  
California misses you.

Ivan:
Yeah, I’m not gonna go there. (both laughing) I’m happy to be in Texas, let me just say that. I love the people there in California. So I’ve got something that I wrote for entrepreneur.com that I’m going to share today. That I think is… it’s not a lot of fun, but it’s good content. It’s really important concepts to understand in order to be a better networker. And I think most BNI members who are listening to this understand that networking is a crucial aspect of building a successful business. And it allows entrepreneurs to connect with potential clients and partners and mentors. But there’s so many networking events and online platforms that are available, it can be difficult to know what is effective.

So what I want to do is explore what I think are the top seven business networking techniques that have proven to be successful, and that I published with entrepreneur.com recently.

The first is, you’ve got to build genuine relationships, it’s about relationships. That’s one of BNI’s principle core values is relationship building. Networking is not just about collecting business cards, it’s about genuine relationships. Instead of focusing on making a quick sale, entrepreneurs need to focus on building a long-term relationship with their contacts. By taking the time to truly get to know the people you meet and finding ways to meet them, you’ll be much more likely to build a lasting connection with people. Networking is more about farming than it is about hunting. It is about relationships, not transactions. That’s one.

Two – you gotta be authentic. The key to successful networking is being authentic. Entrepreneurs should be true to themselves and their business when they’re networking rather than trying to be someone that they’re not. By being authentic, you’ll be more likely to attract the right people and opportunities to your business. People that resonate with your values and the way you do business, which fits with the book I wrote, “Who’s In Your Room?”. Being authentic is about living your values. And that’s a key element of one of the top seven techniques of networking.

Third –  is one that most people recognize but aren’t all good at. And that’s follow up. The most important aspect of networking, I think, or one of the most, is follow up. It’s crucial to make a point of following up with your contacts after meeting them. Whether it be through a phone call an email, a LinkedIn message, a Facebook message, some form of social media, this will help you keep the conversation going and continue to build your relationship. If you you may remember, Priscilla, I’ve talked about in previous podcasts, the 24/7/30 follow up system: [within] 24 hours, you send them some note or email; within seven days connect with them on social media, and within 30 days, set up an appointment with them. That’s the follow up system. So go back and listen to the podcast on 24/7/30.  {Editor’s note: Podcast #623}

Priscilla:  
Do you make notes about that? Just asking.

Ivan:
I do yeah, I usually… now listen, what I’m about to say works in western cultures; it doesn’t work in Asia. I make notes on the back of business cards. So when I’m talking to somebody, I make a few notes on the back of the business card to remind me of the conversation. Otherwise, you know, if I meet 20-30 people, I have no idea when I look at their card, I’m like, “Who was this person?”

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Unless they have a photograph of themselves on the card, which a lot of real estate agents do, but not too many other professions do.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh.

Ivan: 
So, yeah, I write notes. Now, in Asian cultures, it’s unprofessional to write on a business card. So if you’re in Asia, you know, have a notepad where you can write the person’s name, and then whatever notes that you want to write, but don’t write it on their card. That’s really, really bad form.

Priscilla:
I was meaning do you make notes in your calendar that, “Oh, yeah, I’ve got to connect now on social media with so and so? Or?

Ivan:
Yes.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
Yeah, I live and die by my calendar. And so I will make notes in my calendar to do follow up. Absolutely. And it’s, you know, I color code my calendar, I think I’ve talked about this in previous podcasts. I color code my calendar by the things that I want to be doing, and certainly following up with people is very important. And so, you know, I’ll make a note if I’ve been to an event, ‘okay, it’s time to follow up with people from that event.” You know, connect with them on social media or connect with them, you know, by setting up a one-to-one. So yeah, I definitely put it in my calendar.

Priscilla:
Okay. What’s number four?

Ivan:
Number four is really important. And that’s to be of service. And I’ve talked about this a lot, especially when we’re talking about networking up. If you’re networking with someone, rather than trying to sell to someone, you want to help them in some way. So networking is really about being of service. So entrepreneurs should focus on finding ways to help their contacts, rather than just try to sell them something. So by being of service, you’ll be able to build a reputation as a valuable connection or contact, which will make it more likely that your connections will want to do business with you. They’ll want to build a relationship.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan: 
That’s four.

Number five, you’ve got to go out there at networking events. You know, networking is a contact sport. Attend networking events. It’s a great way to meet new people and make connections, Entrepreneurs, BNI members should attend as many networking events as possible, which, you know, some BNI people are surprised. “What you want me to go to a chamber [of commerce or a] service club?” Yeah, get out there, meet people. Networking is a contact sport. And when you go to these events, it provides you a great opportunity to meet new people and make valuable connections… people that you might want to bring into your BNI chapter. How do you think I built BNI? I built BNI by going to a lot of networking organizations, meeting people and inviting them into BNI. The ones that I thought would make really good people to do business with, I brought into BNI.

Priscilla:
Mm-hmm

Ivan:
Number six – leverage your social media. Social media has become an essential tool for networking. Platforms like LinkedIn, Twitter, Facebook, of course, BNI Connect, provides opportunities to connect with other professionals to share your expertise and learn with them. By building your online presence, you can expand your network and increase your visibility – globally. That’s what I’ve loved about BNI Connect and about what BNI has become since the Great pause of COVID, is that I’ve seen BNI members network with one another all around the world, because of BNI connect and because of social media.

Priscilla:
Mm-hmmm

Ivan:
Number seven – join associations and networking groups. Joining industry groups and associations is a great way to expand your network and stay informed about the latest trends and developments in your field. These groups provide great opportunities to meet professionals and gain access to valuable resources. Joining networking groups like your professional association and Chamber of Commerce, and of course, you guys are in BNI, can provide a structure and focus that allows entrepreneurs to be more effective in their networking efforts. And these groups provide a consistent and reliable environment for building relationships and getting referrals. Networking isn’t about shaking hands, kissing babies, and passing out cards.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
It’s about building genuine relationships. It’s about being authentic. It’s about following up. It’s about being of service. It’s about attending networking events, leveraging social media, and joining industry groups that are all effective techniques for building a successful business network. The key is to be consistent and to maintain the relationships that you built over time. The process of relationship networking is a marathon. BNI is a marathon, it’s not a sprint. It’s the BEST way to create referrals for life. That’s the way you build a successful business. And that’s my message for today, Priscilla, unless you have any questions.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. I made my notes here, Ivan.

Ivan:
Excellent. There will be a test next week.


Priscilla: (laughing)
I’m ready. I’m ready.

Ivan:
Okay.

Priscilla:
Okay. All right, then. I think that’s it.

Ivan:
That’s it. Thank you. Thank you, Priscilla, I appreciate you.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great.

All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
