title.rendered: Episode 801: Captivating Educational Moments

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jennie Edwards returns to the Official BNI Podcast to share the three E’s of captivating educational moments that cross over to business.

Engagement

Start with an enrolling question to get people focused and involved, such as “How many of you want to have more success in your BNI Membership?” Challenge them to take specific actions before the next meeting.

Emotion

Connect with your audience emotionally by telling stories. Help them see how what you’re sharing will relieve their frustrations or anxieties. Excite them about the possibilities of using a new technique.

Entertainment

Add drama or fun to your education moment with role-playing and props. (See Episode 620 for more on this topic.)

Beyond Education Moments

By applying the three E’s to your presentations and your networking, you can increase your Visibility within your chapter and your network.

Complete Transcript of Episode 801

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at home in Austin, Texas this week, and I have a guest on the BNI podcast who I’ve had before, Jennie Edwards. Jennie is a photographer and videographer who teaches people how to show up confidently and vulnerably in front of the camera. And in her best-selling book, Empowered You, Jennie helps readers activate their best self to live their most fulfilled life. Jennie gets her, I love this phrase, her heart pumping while chasing her two little girls, ages three and seven. And she earns her nickname “Joyful Jennie” while juggling the three-ring circus of parenting….  Jennie, that is so true. Nobody tells us this before we have children, that it’s a three-ring circus, but it is….  three-ring circus of parenting, entrepreneurship, and inspiring people to change the world. Welcome back to the BNI Podcast, Jennie.

Jennie:
Thank you so much, Ivan.

Ivan:
It’s good to have you here. The three-ring circus of parenting entrepreneurship, and inspiring people to change the world. That’s a great phrase, you have a good turn of the phrase. That’s, that’s excellent.

Jennie:
Thank you very much.

Ivan:
And you do education moments with your [BNI] chapter. And that was the topic of the last one we did. And the full title for this one is The Three E’s of Captivating Educational Moments that Cross-over to Business. And the three E’s are engagement, emotion, and entertainment / edutainment. So let’s start with the first one, engagement.

Jennie:
Engagement.

Ivan:
Talk to me about that.

Jennie:
Yeah, that’s what we all want, right? We want people paying attention to us. We don’t want someone up there just winging it. Right? With nobody paying attention, everybody checking out on their phones or something. So this is really about how to, of course first, ask them an enrolling question. When you start your education moment, what’s a question that gets them to say, yes? “How many of you want to have more success in your BNI Membership by show of hands?” Get them to raise their hand, really encourage the participation. And this is going to help people focus on you and your message more intricately. That’s a really big part of it.

Ivan:
That’s engagement. Engagement is really important. It’s one of the things that I’ve seen over the years, is that people who are engaged in the BNI program at many different levels, if they’re engaged, they’re way more likely to be successful. So I agree completely. Go ahead.

Jennie:

Yes. And one other thing I want to mention about that is, how do you want them to engage with your message after the meeting? Because sometimes, they might forget, what did you even talk about? They’ve tuned it out, they’ve totally lost it after they leave the meeting, and they might not ever really recall that information. So some way that you could maybe challenge them to do some kind of action stuff, after the meeting, throughout the week. You know, maybe challenge them for, however it’s related to your content, challenge them to do three One-to-Ones, or challenge them to listen to specific podcasts. I love that one.

Ivan:
Oh yes, I like that one.

Jennie:
Yeah, that way, they’re getting all their Power of One components, and they’re doing something that is going to help them get more benefits from their membership.

Ivan:
Totally agree. Engagement is absolutely critical in a successful membership. What about emotion?

Jennie:
Okay, I love the emotion because we’ve all seen education moments where somebody is standing up there and reading off of a piece of paper, like a robot, blah blah blah blah, blah blah. And what happens to the audience? They’re not engaged.

Ivan:
They’re zoned.

Jennie:
The person isn’t even looking at them. They’re looking down at the paper. So when you can really include more emotion, and more storytelling during the message, this is really what’s going to make the education moment more memorable, and way more effective. And this translates to your business as well. This is how you’re communicating with your prospective clients, and your clients as well, your values, and really aligning with the values of the prospective audience. We all as members understand certain values that we have about… obviously, this is about education, about learning and growing our business and being successful in our business, giving referrals, all of these things. So how can you include emotion in that? Maybe, you know, come in as, “I’m so excited because I learned this great tip on the latest podcast.” Or maybe even feeling their pain, noticing what they’re going through, “Oh, if you’re feeling like this, here’s an insight, here’s a step that’s going to boost you to get more joy, more happiness. ” You know, whatever it is just throwing in the emotion, rather than being robotic.

Ivan:
It’s so important. You know, great storytelling is about giving facts, giving information, and wrapping it in an emotion. It could be funny, it could be pulling on the heartstrings, it could be you know, something that just makes people feel emotional. And that fact, wrapped in an emotion, compels people to take action, which transforms them in some way. That’s great storytelling in my opinion, wouldn’t you agree?

Jennie:
Absolutely. Yes. And the storytelling is what is so memorable. And they’ll share that story with a family member or friend.

Ivan:
Right.

Jennie:
And that just keeps getting passed on. I mean, you think about how we learned stories from our grandparents, our parents, and they’ve just been passed on for generations. This is why facts are in a different realm. And wrapping that story is so juicy.

Ivan:
Yeah, it makes the facts interesting, I think. Which brings us to the third point,  entertainment/edutainment, which I love as a phrase. Talk to us about that.


Jennie:
I love edutainment, because I do a lot of videos for my business and for my clients. And to wrap it in with entertaining the audience is more exciting for them. It really makes it more, again, more memorable. And I love putting in some drama and some theater into my education moments.

Ivan: (chuckling)
Yes, you do. And for those of you who don’t know, Jennie’s last podcast was episode number 620, Adding More Fun to Education Moments. Episode 620. And yeah, she definitely includes drama and a lot of fun.

Jennie:
Thank you. And since then, I have done several One-to-Ones with [BNI] members across the globe. And some of them that have stepped into the role of education coordinator and want to pick my brain for ideas on how they can do that more confidently. And I’m excited because this has really inspired so many people to get out of their comfort zone and be more playful. Grab a prop that they might have in their closet…

Ivan:
You have a couple of new characters, don’t you, Jennie?

Jennie:
I sure do. Yeah. My latest one, which just debuted this morning in my BNI group was Vinnie the Giver who used to be Vinnie the Taker but is now Vinnie the Giver.

Ivan:
That’s better.

Jennie:
Exactly. So Vinnie the Giver, he talked about giving and relationships. And, you know, how do you give? Givers Gain, that’s the philosophy of BNI. And then we’ve also got Barbara the Baker. Barbara the Baker, who talked about the recipe for success. And mixed in all of the ingredients of the recipe to bake up the perfect amount of success. This was also a reference to a [BNI] podcast that you did about creating a recipe for success.

Ivan:
Yes.

Jennie:
So it’s perfect that I can weave in all these podcast episodes, and really invite people to step into the content that’s so good. Oh, and I don’t want to forget about my Little Red Riding Hood character, Red; she’s also from New York. So I don’t know why New York accent just wants to come through but I love playing with accents. And she was really talking about the path, finding the path. Of course, there’s all these detours on the path to success. And that was referencing a really great podcast as well. And Amelia Chardonnay.

Ivan:
Chardonnay, yes. We’ve got to talk about that, I love wine. So I’ve got a nice wine cellar. So we’ve got to talk about Amelia.

Jennie:  
Amelia. Amelia Chardonnay. She was an interior designer. And she spoke about who is in your room. A wonderful book that everyone should read, and discussing how you can decorate. How you can have a doorman. And why it is important to be in alignment with the values of the people that are surrounding you in your life.

Ivan:
Oh, Jennie, I wish every education coordinator had as much fun with teaching people these important concepts, because these are really important concepts. But when they’re delivered in an entertaining way, you educate people. And that’s what you do and you do it brilliantly. And I’m really, really pleased to have you on the podcast, we’re almost out of time. But I know you have one other topic about relating VCP to the three E’s. Can you do that pretty briefly?

Jennie:
Absolutely. I love talking about visibility, plus credibility leads to profitability. And why is that related? Because we can use these three E’s: education, edutainment, we can use engagement and motion, and we can roll that into our visibility. So how we’re using that for our business, we’re using the storytelling, we’re talking about our business, we’re getting that visibility. We’re developing the credibility, because we can roll in the things that we’ve done, the people that we’ve helped into that emotion-based storytelling, and then that, in turn can lead us into profitability. So these can all convert into your business. Or you can get the greatest success, of course, starting with education in your chapter, but leading into crossing over to your business to really pack a punch, make a splash, and make a difference.

Ivan:
Jennie, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast again. I really appreciate you. For anyone interested in finding out more about you, they can go to your website, www.yourdynamicstory.com. That’s www.yourdynamicstory.com. Thanks for being on BNI Podcast again. I’d love to have you on a third time.

Jennie:
Thank you so much, Ivan.

Ivan:
Great having you here.

Jennie:
I appreciate it.

Ivan:
You bet. Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay. Thank you both so much. That was really fun and really informative.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Jennie Edwards shares the three E’s of captivating educational moments that cross over to business: Engagement, Emotion, and Entertainment.

title.rendered: Episode 800: Are You Wasting a Fantastic Testimonial?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Christine Bond from the BNI Referral Solutions chapter joins Dr. Misner to explain why it’s important to focus on one person when you’re doing the testimonial at your meeting. If you try to squeeze in multiple testimonials, your referral partners won’t remember all of them–and may not remember any of them.

Specific is terrific in your testimonials as well as your presentation.

A truly memorable testimonial can lead to a lifetime of referrals, because other members of your chapter will share it with their networks. Ask for written testimonials, too: they act as social proof when people visit your website.

Links

  • Episode 176, “What makes a good BNI testimonial?”
  • Christine’s website: www.onbalancemassagetherapy.com

Complete Transcript of Episode 800

800-BNI-Podcast

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi. 800! We should have music playing right now.

Priscilla: (chuckles)

Ivan:
There should be like a celebration music playing.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan: 
And Priscilla, you have been here for almost all of them.

Priscilla: 
I know. Isn’t that crazy?

Ivan:
It’s fantastic.

Priscilla:
Yep.

Ivan:
I am doing great. I’m in Austin, Texas. This is a great day. This is my oldest daughter’s birthday today. And I bring it up because, you know, I now have a grandson. So I’m really excited about having a grandson. This is the daughter, Priscilla, who once said… [by the way] she hates when I tell this story. This is the daughter who once said, “Dad, you’re not only not with it, you’re not even near it.”

Priscilla: (laughs)

Ivan:
I said, “You still have to be home at 10 o’clock. I don’t care.” And it’s so funny, because we are now such good friends, her and I. And it’s her birthday today.

Priscilla:
Happy birthday.

Ivan:
Yeah. Okay, so I’m in Austin. Today I have a really special guest, Christine Bond. Christine is the chapter president of the BNI Referral Solutions Chapter, that in and of itself is amazing. But she’s a 20-year member of BNI.

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
And she’s a member in the Founding Region of BNI. She says that joining and engaging in the BNI platform not only launched her business by helping her get the clients that she really needed in the beginning. But it’s helped her get the clients that she loves working with and she’s never had to advertise. She is a massage therapist. I’ve known Christine for 20 years. Christine, it’s great having you on BNI Podcast.

Christine:
Thank you. It’s great to be here with you on my very first BNI Podcast

Ivan:
Very first, and you know, something tells me this may not be your last.

Christine: (laughs)

Ivan:
You have a great story that you were telling me about and I really loved it. You know, we’re always competing to be remembered. And you know, a short, sincere, specific story is memorable in a testimonial, and you had a visual that just really resonated with me. Would you like to share it?

Christine:
I would. So here’s the visual. I used it at an in-person meeting as an Education Coordinator. I brought three members up to the front of the room. I used Julie Foong and Simon Wong and Kent Lai. And I gave a fantastic testimonial for Julie. And once I was done with that, I immediately placed the next member, Simon, directly in front of her and I hid her from view. And then I gave Simon an equally fantastic testimonial. And then I got the third member, Kent Lai, and I placed them in front of Simon and I gave him a testimonial as well. And then I asked the group, “What did I say about Julie and Simon?” They couldn’t remember.

Ivan:
Mmmm. Now, this is great. You did this when you were Education Coordinator. So you were trying to make a point.

Christine:
Yeah.

Ivan: 
I think it’s a great point. Tell everyone what the point is.

Christine:
The point is, is that during the referral and testimonial portion of a meeting, our members have a short amount of time to give a testimonial, and some of them, in their enthusiasm, will give multiple testimonials in really quick succession. It’s very similar to how Oprah Winfrey gave out multiple cars in one of her shows, and the BNI version of this is “YOU get a testimonial, and YOU get a testimonial, the entire CHAPTER gets a testimonial!”

Ivan:
(laughing) It’s like the Oprah story, is what you’re saying? “You get a car and you get a car.”

Christine:
Yes, but unfortunately when multiple testimonials are given, because of the limitations of our human memory, which only hold a certain amount of information, the first two testimonials can get completely wasted.

Ivan:
So true. I’ve been saying this for years. I love the visual that you gave with this about having one person stand in front of the other and you kind of forget who the people are behind them. Everyone listening to the podcast, remember this as you’re doing testimonials. It’s the person that you last talk about that’s going to be remembered. Therefore, focus on one person when you’re doing the testimonial that you’re going to do, which is really, really important. And your next point, I think, leads into that really well, Christine. A memorable testimonial can open the door to a lifetime of referrals. Talk about that.

Christine:
Well, sometimes one testimonial that’s fantastic can be better than one referral, because you’re sharing a story with a group of potentially 25 to 50 people. And now, the story I tell can become theirs, and they can brag about one of the members. Can I give you an example?

Ivan:
Yes, please.

Christine:
Okay, we have a member in my own personal chapter, his name is Kent Lai. He’s a CPA; he is so knowledgeable about tax strategy and planning. And he’s one of the most professional CPAs that I have ever found. He’s always thinking proactively about his clients’ best interest. And he used six months of planning and saved an LLC client, with multiple partners, about $40,000 to $45,000 each in their taxes. So you’ll love working with Kent. And I’d love for people to remember that one thing.

Ivan:
So that’s, you know, see, that’s a great example of being very specific. And, you know, I talk about the fact that doing your weekly presentation — specific is terrific. But the truth is that testimonials, specific is also terrific. And this goes all the way back to research and work done by Ken Blanchard in the book, The One Minute Manager where, you know, talking about one minute praising — something short, specific, detail oriented, makes all the difference in people remembering. And that’s really what you just did. And that’s the way most testimonials really should be done.

I’ve talked about this in previous podcasts, by the way. You can find previous podcasts where I talk about how we came up with doing testimonials as part of the meeting, because we found just how powerful they were. You get… you know, we think a referral is good. And it is. But a testimonial can be worth many referrals. You give one fantastic testimonial, and that one testimonial can be 2,3,4,5, 10 referrals. Testimonials are every bit as important as a referral. Wouldn’t you agree, Christine?

Christine:
I completely agree. It’s social proof of someone’s competence or their expertise or their professionalism. I love testimonials. I’ve never had to… because members have given me great testimonials, it has spread my brand, and I’ve just never had to advertise outside of BNI. But I love it.

Ivan:
Yeah. And as I as I recall, you have like five stars on Yelp reviews for what you do.

Christine:
Mmm hmm.

Ivan:
You have a boatload of Yelp reviews. And you’ve got this all five star. And I think I said this in the introduction, Christine is a massage therapist. And it’s not too many people or companies that have five star Yelp reviews. Well done. And it’s not like just two or three. You got a boatload.

Christine:
Yeah. And what’s nice is they’re all spoken in a different voice. So it reaches many different potential clients.

Ivan: Yeah, yeah. So okay, so we’ve talked about doing the testimonial at a BNI meeting, being specific; doing one, not two, not three, not four – one; being specific, doing a testimonial, like you just did. Talk a little bit about posting written testimonials, because those are also valuable. You don’t do that during a meeting, but there’s still a lot of value to doing that.

Christine:
I agree, I have to say one of the members of my chapter opened up a Yelp profile for me and posted a testimonial. And probably my first eight testimonials were from BNI members who had been clients. And a written testimonial can go a really long way. One person put one on Yelp, LinkedIn, Google, and Facebook, and BNI Connect. So the reach was very broad. It was incredible.

Ivan:
And so if somebody’s going to do a verbal testimonial, wouldn’t it be a good idea to then ask that person, you know, thank you…. or say to that person, “Thank you so much for that testimonial. Would you mind also putting that on Yelp or up on social media so that it can be documented somewhere?” Yeah?

Christine:
Yes.

Ivan:
Ask for the written testimonial. There’s nothing wrong with that.

Christine:
Most people are happy to do it. Plus a written testimonial can be more detailed. It can be a lot longer than the spoken one in a BNI meeting. So it can go a far distance.

Ivan:
Yeah. And, by the way, the first time I talked about testimonials, and the story about how we changed the agenda, because in the beginning, if you had a referral you gave it, if you didn’t you passed. I don’t know if you know that, you might you probably know.

Christine:
I do. I read Givers Gain.

Ivan:
You do. You read the book, all right. I used to do it – if you if you had a referral you gave it; if you didn’t, you pass. And the first time I realized that that was not the best way to do it was when somebody asked if they could talk about one of the members that they had just used. And I said yes, and it was such a great testimonial, I literally changed the agenda. Because the testimonial was so powerful that that one testimonial turned into three or four clients that week for that member. And if you want to see that episode, it’s episode number 176, “What makes a good BNI testimonial.” Episode number 176 tells the story that really is the underlying power of what Christine has just talked about here today. Christine, is there any closing thoughts that you have?

Christine:
I think we covered it all. And the one, I guess the one thing is when you take the time to share this with someone instead of keeping it in your mind, you can do a tremendous amount of brand building for someone and make them more referable which will go a really long way.

Ivan:
Testimonials are really the heart of Givers Gain, I think.

Christine:
I agree.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well listen, anyone that’s interested in information on Christine go to www.onbalancemassagetherapy.com That’s www.onbalancemassagetherapy.com. Christine has been a 20-year member of BNI and currently the chapter president. Christine, welcome to BNI Podcast and thank you for being number 800 on BNI Podcast.

Christine:
Oh, it’s been my pleasure. Thank you for having me and I look forward to possible future endeavors.

Ivan:
Absolutely. Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, I think that was just great. Thank you so much to both of you.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you try to squeeze in multiple testimonials during a BNI meeting, people won’t remember all of them–and may not remember any of them.

title.rendered: Episode 799: But I Don’t Have a Personal Mechanic

content.rendered:
Synopsis

James Wilson from the BNI Dream Team Chapter in Derby, Kansas [USA] joins Dr. Misner to explain why not filling out your BNI Connect profile is like taking out a half-page ad in the newspaper and leaving it blank. If you don’t have your BNI Connect profile filled in, BNI members from other chapters can’t find you and refer you for the services that you offer.

At the end of the day, if you do not have your profile filled in, you don’t have your photo, you don’t have your basic contact info, it just seems like you are not taking your BNI membership seriously. If you have not completed your profile, at the end of this podcast you need to go to BNI Connect and complete your profile right now.

Find out more about James at www.icscollisioncenter.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 799

799-BNI-Podcast

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi, Priscilla. I’m back in Austin, Texas. I just got back from Necker Island. spent a week with Richard Branson. And I’m sure I’m gonna be talking about it in some future podcasts. And I’m gonna have some blogs on it. Branson’s an amazing man. And I enjoyed my time as I always do.

Priscilla:
Oh, gosh, I’m jealous, as I always am.

Ivan:
So I have a guest today. My guest is James Wilson. James is a founding member of the BNI Dream Team Chapter in Derby, Kansas [USA]. And he’s been a Support Director Consultant for two years in the Kansas area. He’s a member of the worldwide director consultants (WWDC), which is a BNI Global Power team of 43 BNI members representing over 30 countries, and their purpose is to share best practices and connecting chapter members throughout the global BNI Network. James began his career in the collision repair industry in 1986. James, you’ve been doing this almost long as I’ve been doing BNI. And he owns ICS Collision Center with his wife, Tara. Am I pronouncing that right?

James:
That is correct. Absolutely.

Ivan:
And they deliver safe and proper repairs for their clients. James, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

James:
Thank you, Ivan. It’s a pleasure to be here, both with you, and Priscilla, today.

Ivan:
It’s good to have you here. So you have a topic that is really important to me. And it just drives me nuts when people don’t do what you’re about to talk about. Tell me why it is important to have your BNI Connect profile completed.

James:
Well, I believe first and foremost, if you do not have your BNI profile filled in, then fellow BNI members are unable to find you and unable to refer you for the services that you offer.

Ivan:
Yeah, this is so important. I remember when I moved from LA to Austin, and I wanted to search for some BNI members. And I went, of course, to BNI Connect to find BNI members. And there were a number of people that I was looking for. And they didn’t have their profile completed on BNI Connect. And to me, it’s like taking a half page ad out in a newspaper and leaving it blank.

James:
I would agree with you 100% on that.

Ivan:
It makes no sense. I ended up hiring a real estate agent here in in Austin, which I have now done about… I think 13 or 14 real estate transactions. Kristi Kraus, Kristi, I’m calling you out. What a great real estate agent. She was the ONLY residential real estate agent, eight years ago, who had her profile completely filled out. And that’s why I hired her and I’ve done at least 14 transactions with her as a result.

Priscilla:
Wow.

James:
Wonderful. Wonderful.

Ivan:
What makes you bring this point up? Why is it that you’re saying that about the profiles?

James:
Okay, well it was pre-COVID. And I was hoping to share the story at our national convention earlier. But the reason being is I was able to send a referral to a mechanic in Austin, Texas. And it was basically a blind referral because outside of yourself, Ivan, I don’t know anyone else that lives in Austin. And how this started out was here at our facility, one of our niche markets is working on recreational vehicles. So, campers and RVs.

And I had a young military couple here that had recently purchased their RV. And within just about a month of receiving it, they had damaged it. They brought it to us, we repaired it. And then I found out that when basically the husband was going to be stationed overseas for six months and during that timeframe, his wife was going to take her girlfriend and two young children and travel across the United States for about two months. And the interesting thing was, I wanted to put her mind at ease, and I asked her to contact me directly if she had any issues with a camper while traveling, because I had a network of professional business owners that I could contact, would any issues arise while she was traveling.

And so, two weeks after she left, she had contacted me and stated that she had a water leak on the inside of the vehicle, on the inside of the camper. And I asked her where she was at. And she’s like, “Well, we’re in Dallas right now. But we’re leaving Sunday for Austin.” I say, “Great. Let me reach out to my contacts in Austin; I’ll find out who you can take it to that I trust. And we’ll take care of it from there.” And so she thanked me for that. And at first I panicked, because I’m like, I really don’t know anyone down there. I’m like, I can’t pick up the phone and call Ivan. But let’s go to BNI Connect, right? That’s the tool. That’s our resource as BNI members.

And so I did that. I went in there and at first started searching for RV repair facilities. Unfortunately, there were none listed. And I thought, well, second best would probably be an auto mechanic. And I started at the top of list. Again, I don’t know anyone in Austin. And I just started that top list, how it populated, and I went through profile number one, it wasn’t filled in. Went on to profile number two, it wasn’t filled in. Went to 3, 4, 5. Number six was not completely filled in. And lo and behold, lucky number seven, his profile was filled in.

James:
So I was able to pick up the phone, I called his mobile number left a short voicemail for him, requested that he called me back. And he called me back about 10 minutes later and said, “Yeah, absolutely. That’s something we can take care of. Where is she going to be at in Austin, because I have three locations.” I found out the address, gave it to him, he’s like, “Hey, yeah, this one’s like five minutes away from this location. So just have her be at this shop at eight o’clock on Monday.” And I told the mechanic, I said, “All right. If it’s anything related to the side of the vehicle that we worked on, do not invoice our client, just invoice me directly.” He said, “Sure, no problem.”

And so after about 48 hours, I had not heard from either one of them, so I called my client. She said, “Yeah, he was fantastic. Got me in, got me out real quickly.”  I said, “He didn’t charge you, did he?” And she says, “No, he did not. ” I said, “Great.” So then I called up the owner of the shop. And I said, “Well, my client said you didn’t invoice her. So if you would just send me the bill for repairs.” He’s like, “Oh, well, there’s no charge for this. It was just simply a loose hose clamp.” And I thanked him graciously, and numerously. Because here’s the thing – at the end of the day, had the other one through six BNI members had their profile filled in, I would have gladly referred to them. And beyond that I gladly would have written them a check for even tightening hose clamp.

Ivan: Mmm hmm.

James:
And here’s my take is at the end of the day, if you do not have your profile filled in, you don’t have your photo, you don’t have your basic contact info, it just seems like you are not taking your BNI membership seriously.

Ivan:
Yeah, no kidding. I mean, think about it for a moment, you’re going to a meeting, hour and a half every week, 50 weeks or so a year. And you don’t take an hour to complete your profile? That’s crazy, you guys. If you have not completed your profile, at the end of this podcast you need to go to BNI Connect and complete your profile right now. I’ve been talking about this for, I don’t know how long. And James, I agree completely. I’m glad you have allowed me to bring it up again.

James:
Absolutely. And to your point of taking an hour to fill it out. I hear it numerous times throughout the week from members where they say they don’t have the time to fill it out. And I say, “Well, do you have the time for referrals?” And they’re like, “Absolutely.” I say, “How about this? Do you drink coffee or tea in the morning?” And most of them do. I say, “How about when you’re taking your 10 or 15 minutes to have your warm cup of Joe, that you just log in your profile and just fill out one section. Just baby-step it one section at a time.”

Ivan:
Yeah.

James:
Guess what, after a week or two, you’ll have it completely filled in. And that usually gets them motivated to do that.

Ivan:
Well, listen.  I know you have a lot more to talk about. We’ve got to wrap up our time. What would you have done if you were not a BNI member? I know you wanted to talk about that and I want to make sure we have time to do it.

James:
Absolutely. And thank you. That’s a great question. What would I have done? I honestly don’t know what I would have done. I would have probably gone old school and just started researching shops, mechanic shops, and reached out to those within the region that were close to her zip code and then just cold called. But the terrifying thing is, I wouldn’t have known who I would have been sending my client to.

Ivan:
Right. There would be no trust level to begin with.

James:
Exactly. And without that, I don’t know if that experience would reflect poorly or if it would reflect good on myself as a business owner who prides himself on taking care of his clients.

Ivan:  
Yeah. Yeah. Well, James, what a great story. And I really appreciate you sharing it. Is there any closing thought you have before we wrap?

James:
Closing thoughts – I would just kind of put the icing on the cake here and follow up with what you said already. It’s just, I encourage all members to just take the time, fill out your profile, fill it out completely. And then beyond that, I always encourage everyone to do a review at least every 90 days. Because life changes, life happens.

Ivan:
Update it.                                                                   

James:
And you don’t want to be caught in the middle of a one-to-one conversation, and, you know, find out that Petie, their favorite dog, had passed away a year prior to that. So it’s always good to just be able to sidestep those landmines by having your profile updated, as well as completed.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. And BNI, by the way for those of you listening, we’re working on the website search function. You’re going to see improvements over the next year or so. So be looking for that. [It will] make it even easier to do searches. James, thank you so much for being on the BNI Podcast. I really appreciate you being here.


James:
Absolutely. And I truly appreciate your time, Ivan, and yours as well, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Thank you.

Ivan:
By the way everyone. James’s website is www.icscollisioncenter.com. He’s a BNI member. And thank you, James, for being on. Priscilla, back over to you.

Priscilla:

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 798: Givers & Takers (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 661, first released in 2020. Offers and links may no longer be accurate.

Synopsis

“Givers Gain” does not mean that you should expect an immediate financial return from giving. It means that given enough effort and time, your generosity will be returned by and through your network of contacts many times over.

“Givers Gain” also does not mean that you should be a Taker’s victim. Give freely to those who value a giving approach to life. Use discernment to avoid the ones who do not.

If you have given several referrals to a fellow BNI member and not received any, schedule a 1-2-1 and come prepared with as much detail as possible about the referrals you passed to that person. Go through these referrals and asked how they turned out.

If none of those referrals turned into business, ask how you can give better referrals.

If any of those referrals turned out to be good and turned into business, tell the person that you’re really glad that they worked out. Pause before asking how you can make it easier for them to refer people to you. Be specific about what makes a good referral for you and how to refer you to their clients and contacts.

One BNI member reported to Dr. Misner that he’d tried this technique and gotten a great referral from the other member within a very short time.

Share your stories about the conversations you’ve had with fellow members about the referrals you’ve given them and how those worked out.

Complete Transcript of Episode 661/798

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, thank you very much. Today’s topic I call “Givers and Takers.” It obviously revolves around the philosophy of Givers Gain. Givers Gain is about giving to other people first, it’s a standard that you should apply to yourself, not a stick that you hit other people with. Within this context, the giver cannot and should not expect an immediate return on their investment based on Givers Gain. What they should focus on is that, given enough effort and time, their generosity will be returned by and through their network of contacts, friends, colleagues, fellow BNI members many times over and in many different ways. I incorporated the philosophy of Givers Gain® into BNI 35 years ago because I saw that many networking groups were just far too mercenary in their approach. They use face to face networking or in person networking as a face to face cold calling opportunity. I believe then, and I know now, that networking is all about relationship building, and that one of the best ways to build a relationship is to help others first. Through giving, you can gain in so many different ways. I also recognize that there are takers in the world. And I wanted to do this podcast because every now and then I have someone say to me, “I’m dealing with somebody who I think is just in it for them and they’re not giving.” So I recognize that there are takers in the world, there are people who either don’t understand the power of Givers Gain or who don’t really care or believe in the concept.

The people who either don’t care or don’t believe, I put into two categories “Can’t Do’s” and “Won’t Do’s”, which I’ve talked about a few times on the podcasts. The Can’t Do’s in this context, just don’t know how to do something or they don’t understand why it’s important to do something. For these people, I’ve learned that with the right coaching, they may become willing to make that transition. Then there are the people who are the Won’t Do’s. They just want what serves them best and have no intention of giving. It’s important to recognize them as soon as possible, because they will abuse the relationship, not nurture it. So, the point here is that life, and certainly your BNI chapter, requires discernment. Sometimes, that is about evaluating the people in your network and whether they’re willing to contribute to your relationship. This is where discernment comes into play. This is important: Givers Gain does not mean you should be a takers victim. Let me repeat that: Givers Gain does not mean you should be a takers victim. The world is full of givers and takers. So apply contextual insight and use appropriate judgment to give freely to the people who value the giving approach in life. Use discernment for the ones who do not.

I know a member who gave a half a dozen referrals to someone in his group over an 18 month period, but the individual never reciprocated and the man came to me seeking advice. I coached him to do the following; I said, “Invite the person out for a One-to-One meeting and come prepared to the meeting with as much detail as possible about the half a dozen referrals that you gave.” I may have mentioned this briefly in a previous podcast, but I want to go into more detail here. I said, “Go to the meeting prepared with as much details about the six referrals as you can recall and instead of sitting down with them, which is common and say, ‘hey, I’ve given you six referrals, you haven’t given me any.’ Start with the referrals you’ve given him. Start with the oldest and ask the following questions. “How did it work out? Did it turn into business? If so, was it as much as you had hoped? Did the relationship work out as well?” Use open ended questions to determine how well that referral worked out for the individual. I said, “After a few minutes, do the same for the next referral, and then the next referral, and so on until you discuss all the referrals that you’ve given that individual.” Now, here’s where your discernment needs to be fine tuned. What if all those referrals you gave this fellow member didn’t work out like you thought? Then you need to ask the person how could you give better referrals in the future? Because the problem is not them, the problem is the quality of referrals that you may be giving them. So before you say, “Hey, give me some referrals, too.”, you might want to improve the quality of your referrals.

However, if any of those referrals turned out to be good and possibly resulted in business, then take the following tack; tell the person that you are really glad that the referrals that you gave have worked out well. Then pause and take a moment and say, “Since some of them have worked out for you, I’d really appreciate if you could do something similar for me. Maybe we could talk a little bit about how I can help you do that.” And from there, talk to the person about what a good referral is for you, how they can refer people to you, and even dive deeper into specific clients that they may have that would be a good referral for you.

That was the advice I gave this gentleman. He came back to me a few weeks later and he said he was so glad he followed my advice, rather than just end the relationship or just be mad and not give him any referrals. He told me the individual apologized profusely in the conversation and acknowledged that this needed to be a two way relationship. He said that they spoke at length about how he could reciprocate with him, and just within the last few weeks, he had already done so. He said the referral that the gentleman gave me was a really big referral and it ended up being a great client. So this is what I would tell you if you were talking, or thinking about talking, to somebody about reciprocating and you think they may be a taker, find out first. Talk to them about the referrals you gave and ask them how they worked out using those questions that I just shared with you, and if they worked out well, then let them know that it would be nice if they could reciprocate.

Sometimes I find that people are so busy in life, that they’re just not thinking about the importance of having a reciprocal relationship. Sometimes they don’t know how and sometimes they don’t care. All three require discernment and that discernment requires a different response strategy. If they don’t know how, it is a much different response than they don’t care. They don’t care – that’s a taker. If they don’t know how, then it’s important for you to show them how. So your giving energy should be focused on people who are aligned with the need for reciprocity. They may or may not be able to give back to you directly, but observe their behavior before you continue to blindly evolve into a giving victim. The more energy you have for giving, I think, the more you are able to give, and giving more where you have strong relationships makes you able to practice this philosophy in a healthy way. I believe, and I know I’ve said this in the podcast before, Givers Gain is about taking off your bib and putting on an apron. It’s about serving others. It’s about building a relationship by helping people first. And that’s my theme for today, givers and takers. Priscilla, any thoughts?

Priscilla:
Oh, that was great. I think you covered it really well. I don’t have anything particular to add on.

Ivan:
Okay. Well, thank you. And so I would love for you, if you are listening to this podcas,t to put a comment in here if you’ve had any kind of conversation with somebody when you sat down with them and talked to them about the referrals you’ve given and ask if they could reciprocate, I’d like to hear your stories. Share here on BNI Podcast, your stories about having conversations with people and how it has turned around, and if it hasn’t, go try it right now. Try it over the next week or two, and then come back to this podcast and post your results. I’d really like to hear your results.

Before we wrap up, Priscilla, I know you’re going to mention Ivan’s Inner Circle and it is the sponsor of BNI Podcast. And I really love doing the Inner Circle. It is a site where I’m doing coaching with members and it’s open to the public. I just wanted BNI members to know that I donate 100% of my profits. The royalties that I receive from this, it’s another company that runs it, but I do receive royalties and I give 100% of my royalties to charity. At this time, right now, it’s the BNI Foundation that I’m donating all of my profits to. Should anyone decide they want to be a part of Ivan’s Inner Circle- and right now we have about two dozen webinars that are up, and I do private Facebook Lives just for the Inner Circle, and anyone who wants to participate in that, feel free to go to www.IvansInnerCircle.com. Back to you Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that sounds like such a great opportunity. I hope some of our BNI members take you up on it. So I think that Ivan has covered it!

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: “Givers Gain” means your generosity will be returned by and through your network of contacts many times over.

title.rendered: Episode 797: You Have a Book in You

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The more we read success stories, self-help action stories, self-initiative action stories, the more successful we become. Best-selling author Mark Victor Hansen returns to the podcast to explain how make your story compelling and bring out the book inside you.

Once you’ve decided to share your story with the world, you can submit it to a traditional publisher, self publish, or use a service like the Mark Victor Hansen Library that helps you write, publish, and distribute your book as a hardcover, softcover, ebook, and audiobook.

Get your free copy of You Have a Book in You at https://www.hanseninstitute.com and check out https://www.markvictorhansenlibrary.com when you’re ready to tell your story.

Complete Transcript of Episode 797

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I am on my way back again, this will be my fifth trip to Necker Island. And I’m going to spend a week with Richard Branson and a number of other people on Necker. I have really enjoyed my last visits there. And I’m sure that there will be a podcast or a blog or two, based on my visit, which is…. I’m leaving now, but I’ll be there next week.

Priscilla:
Sounds so great. I’m totally jealous.

Ivan:
Well, I’m excited. So, thanks.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, what is this? Tell us about this.

Ivan:

I have another guest. Last week, I had both Crystal and Mark Hansen. Today. I’ve got Mark Victor Hansen on he’s going to be talking about the topic of You Have a Book in You. Mark, of course, is known as one of the world’s bestselling authors of the Chicken Soup for the Soul series. He’s also written The One Minute Millionaire and Ask!: The Bridge from Your Dreams to Your Destiny. They have sold over 500 million copies, and he is my guest on BNI Podcast today. Mark, welcome.

Mark:
Ivan, thank you for having me. And I’ve loved being in Necker, I’ve loved being with Richard Branson in several places. I hope everybody on here gets to see him live, because he’s probably the best self-promoter on the planet.

Ivan:
He is. And in his ego does not enter the room before he does. He’s a really nice individual to spend time with.

Mark:
I agree 100%. And he, you know, he’s let me endorse his book, or invited me to, and we’ve had great friendship, and he does phenomenal work. I mean, here’s a guy, you know, that’s dyslexic, meaning he reads backwards and upside down, and he still runs, I think, 421 companies and the last number I read was $5.2 billion.

Ivan:
Amazing.

Mark:
So he doesn’t have to worry about whether he pays for lunch tomorrow or this week when you’re down there.

Ivan:
No, he doesn’t. So Mark, you say, “You Have a Book In You.” Talk about that. Do you think everyone really has a book in them?

Mark:
Everyone has a story in them. And what we do is, we own a publishing company called www.MarkVictorHansenLibrary.com. And what we want to do is bring your story, especially an impactful story, to life. And we’ll ghost write it with you. But there are four ways to do it. You can obviously self-publish a book and my first book was Stand Up, Speak Out, and Win! When I was bankrupt, upside-down living in New York, 1974. I started speaking, and people said, “Man, you’re the best storyteller. Can you put that in a book?” So I did a book, Stand Up, Speak Out, and Win! And, as you know, because we’ve been around each other a lot, is that I did four talks a day. I did 1000 talks the first year; I think only Tony Robbins and I have ever done that many. And I sold 20,000 copies at $10 each.

Back then you could buy a book for $1, sell it, self-publish. I said, this isn’t a best seller, not a New York Times bestseller, not a national bestseller, but it’s my best seller. You know, I sold 20,000 copies at $10. That’s $200,000. Remember, that’s like $2 million today, 50 years later. I thought I’d died and gone to heaven. I was having so much fun and pure loving it. I was loving it. And I’m a humorist so everybody was having a great joyous time. And it was rough times then, like now, the oil embargo had hit and now we’re sort of in those crazy caustic times again, in some parts of the world anyhow. And it’s exciting that everyone’s got a book in them.

So you can self-publish, you can try to go to a major house — like I’ve gone with eight houses, I’ve written 318 books, like Random House has given me a million dollars for The One Minute Millionaire and the whole series of books I did on money, like Cash in a Flash and The Richest Kids in America and Cracking the Millionaire Code. Or you could not do anything, which would be heartbreaking to me, because the richest place in the world, I say, is a graveyard because it’s got all the people’s dreams, all their hopes, all their books, all their songs, all their inventions. And you’re supposed to live fully like we said last week. And then number four, you can come to a house like mine, where, you know, last year — our first year in business — we had 18 Number One Amazon Bestsellers, and we just help people bring their impact stories to life. And their stories are phenomenal. With your permission, I’ll tell you one.

Ivan:
Yeah, please.

Mark:
So Crystal and I, my beloved beautiful, wonderful wife, my wife and I are down talking in Houston [Texas] and at the collective – 700 people. A little kid, 14-years old comes up to me with pimples on his face, shaking like a leaf. And I could see that he was uber-bright, smart, smart, smart. And I said, “What, are you shaking?” He said, “You’re considered the World’s Bestselling Author by Guinness Book of Records, and you intimidate me.” I looked at him and I said, “Look, I couldn’t intimidate anyone, but especially somebody who’s smart and wise, and handsome as you. What’s your story, kid?” He says, “Well, I’m 14-years old. I’m worth $843,000. I’m an Eagle Scout.” (And that’s a Boy Scout that’s gone through the ranks.) And he said, “I’m getting my black belt in Aikido, right now.” I said, “My boy, we got to do a book on you. No one’s done it. And it’s important.”

And we helped him write a book at 14. He’s been number one in the last nine weeks with a great book called, “The Garage.” And it’s important, I think, because you and I totally believe an entrepreneur finds a problem, fixes it, scales it and makes a vast profit. Well, that’s what this kid has done. And I think kids need to know, I didn’t know that I could buy real estate when I was a kid. But he passed a real estate test at eight. And then he bought what’s called ‘owner carry back houses’ in Ohio [USA]. And it just, there’s a lot of ways… ‘There’s a million ways to make a million’ I wrote in The One Minute Millionaire and there’s one right, perfect, easy, acceptable way. But every one of us, like you just asked the question so articulately, everyone, I think, has a story to tell. Have you ever met, Ivan, in your whole life, somebody that didn’t have a story if you could warm up to him?

Ivan:
No, everyone’s got a story. The key is making it compelling.

Mark:
Yeah. And what we do is, our publishing house, www.MarkVictorHansenLibrary.com makes irresistibly compelling reading, I’m thankful to say. Knock on wood. And I don’t do that out of ego. I just, I’ve been doing the business 50 years, there aren’t very many of us that hang in there that tough, that long. And I always go out of my way, just like we were talking about Richard Branson, to get people to wake up and say, “Look, if you can’t write it, let us help you write it, because you’ve got a great story.” Like I was just on with… the biggest builder here where I live in Scottsdale, Arizona [USA] is Mike Ingram. And he started by selling rat poison. And he sold six cases of rat poison to farmers and gave them a free rifle. Well, he got the rifle for $10, it was worth, you know, $30. And they all won the rifle and said, “If that rat poison doesn’t work, I’m going to use your gun on you.” And it’s called a premium. And what happens is, nobody knows his story. And now he’s a multi billionaire. But the point is, everybody’s got a story. And the more we read success stories, self-help action stories, self-initiative with action stories, the more successful we become. So we need… and we do it in all four forms – we do it hardbound, soft bound, audio and audible. And so we make sure everyone gets a full shot at 100% success, and becoming number one if they’re willing to do the work.

Ivan:
So listen, I’ve been writing books, you’ve been writing books longer than I have. And I’ve seen the publishing industry change a lot over the last several decades. How have you seen it changed in the last few years?

Mark:
Well first of all, I love everybody in the publishing industry, like you do. I mean, they’ve all been nice to me, the bookstores, the publishers, the distributors, the vendors, international, all that. But thanks to the whole pandemic, the world got shut down. We’ve gone from, in America, I can’t talk about the world, 19,000 bookstores down to 400. Those are nice people. They’ve been wonderful to people that have been at the big meeting in America is called ABA. And then there’s one in Frankfurt, Germany, I’ve been to and one in Monterrey, Mexico, and I won’t talk to that. But in America, 60,000 people show up and you know, you sign free books to them, and they go back to the bookstore and sell your signature for 10 cents, or $75 or $100, or whatever – it depends what they can get for it. But the fact is, I love all those people. And it breaks my heart that they got squished because nobody could go to a bookstore; basically there are only a couple of bookstores left and I love all of them. But I want everyone to do their book. And we’re finding brand new ways to market a book, which I call bypass marketing, which we can talk to you if you’re open to it.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. But isn’t a book in a way, especially for entrepreneurs, which is my audience, isn’t it like a really great brochure to what you do?

Mark:
It’s the best door opening business card ever. Back when I was training life insurance, we had a guy named Elmer Wheeler. And in his suit he had stitch-in lining, where I would put my glasses [in mine], he had little books. And he’d walk up to someone and say, “Have you read my bestselling book yet?” And they said, “No, I don’t even know who you are.” He said, “Well, let me just sign it to you.” Because nobody… they throw away business cards, they go into cylindrical file called a garbage can, but nobody throws away a signed book. And if you sign it to the wife AND the husband, now you’re really golden.

Ivan: (chuckling)

Mark:
So signatures [are] what they’re paying for, I mean, if you’re selling it like I do. And the book is free. It’s an add in. And it just, I want everyone to have a book, everyone to have the thrill, everyone to have the excitement of what it feels like to hold their words, their thoughts, their ideas, their impressions, their message in their hot little hand.

Ivan:
So Mark, you’ve sold over 500 million books. What do you think makes a book sell?

Mark:
The story — irresistibly, compelling reading story. And by the way, just what you said is correct. But I’m going to live be 127 with options for renewal because you live as long as you expect to live if you got it in writing, like you were talking about earlier. And I, in previous times. And what happens is, I plan to sell a billion books. And by the way, that’s why Jack Canfield, and I got turned down by 144 publishers. Because the first thing I wrote in my “Wow” of a business plan… I interviewed the 101 bestselling fiction authors and nonfiction. I didn’t ask about how to write. You don’t walk up to somebody like James Michener or Clive Cussler or Nora Roberts and say, “How do you write?” Well, hell, yeah, you’re supposed to know how to do that or get a ghost. But I said, “How did you market and sell so many books?” I created this “Wow” of a business plan, Ivan, I wrote a “Wow” of a business plan: we’re going to sell a billion books. Well, remember going into a publisher, you look like an idiot, because nobody has sold that many, and you’re not going to either. Well, when I was on the biggest podcast for Amazon with Mark Devereaux, he said, “You are the Roger Bannister of books.” Do you remember who Roger Bannister was, Ivan?

Ivan:
Oh, yeah, absolutely. He broke the four minute mile.

Mark:
Right. But do you know what happened? I said to Mark, “Do you know what happened the next week?” He said, “No, what happened?” 119 people did it. So I’m breaking the barrier. You and I are here, and BNI is here to be a history maker, a record breaker, a new pace setter, and see how far we can go and what we can accomplish and make everything infinitely better. How’s that?

Ivan:
That sounds fantastic. And Mark, it’s a real pleasure having you on BNI Podcast. And those of you who are listening to the podcast, Education Coordinators, make sure to share this with your local chapter. And make sure to share Mark’s website www.MarkVictorHansenLibrary.com. Mark, any last thought?

Mark:
Yeah, I’d like people to go to www.HansenInstitute.com, H-A-N-S-E-N, we do it the Danish way, HansenInstitute.com, and get a free copy of my book, “You Have a Book In You.” I want you to claim it. It’s yours free, you can’t beat the price. And it’ll make you infinitely better off. And by the way, hit us up for two, give one to somebody that you love and care about. It could be your spouse, could be your dad, your mom, your sister, your brother. And I believe something I think you believe, Ivan. Everybody ought to write their autobiography once every decade because all of us morph and change every decade.

Ivan:
Yep.

Mark:
It’s like that caterpillar, you know, growing into a chrysalis, and becomes this beautiful butterfly. A lot of you are beautiful butterflies, but you need to write every decade because we all forget so much.

Ivan:
Well, Mark, thanks for being on. You and I have known each other for many years and it’s a real pleasure having you on the podcast. Hope to have you back again sometime.

Mark:
Glad to be your lifelong friend, my friend.

Ivan:
Thank you. Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect.

All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 796: The Art and Science of Asking

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The difference between having a little success and having vast success is learning how to ask for what you want. Learning how to ask makes you a superstar.

Mark Victor Hansen and Crystal Dwyer Hansen join the podcast this week to introduce their new book, Ask! The Bridge from your Dreams to Your Destiny, which is a perfect tool for using during One-to-Ones with your fellow BNI members. Get past the 12 roadblocks to asking as you work through the 178 questions that help you to determine where you are now, where you want to be, and what it looks like when you get there.

Get a free introduction to how to become a master asker at www.thebookask.com and pick up a copy of Ask! The Bridge from your Dreams to Your Destiny.

Complete Transcript of Episode 796

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am still in Austin this week. I’m not traveling at the moment and enjoying a little bit of time at home. Thanks for asking. So I’m really excited. I’ve got two guests on BNI Podcast. The two guests are first, Crystal Dwyer Hanson. She’s a number one bestselling author, entrepreneur, certified life coach, and wellness nutrition expert whose personal coaching speaking, CD video programs, books and articles have helped people all over the world. Crystal’s expertise is in the field of human potential. And she’s co-authored her latest book, “Ask!: The Bridge from your Dreams to Your Destiny“, with her husband, Mark Victor Hansen, who I’ve known for a number of years. And he’s also on this podcast. Of course, you may recognize his name as being one of the world’s best selling authors with the Chicken Soup for the Soul series, which has sold over 500 million books worldwide. Crystal, Mark, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Crystal:
Thank you, Ivan. We’re so happy to be here with you both.


Mark:
And all your team. We just We love entrepreneurs and good business people.

Ivan:
Well, we have lots of entrepreneurs and good business people, almost 300,000 of them now, all around the world. So it’s great having you on the podcast. So let me ask you, why did you write the book, “Ask!”?

Mark:
We wrote “Ask!” because we’ve traveled around the world and what we find out – there are great people, good attitude, wonderfully educated. And we’ve been in 80 countries. And what happens is the difference between somebody who has a LITTLE success, and VAST success is one thing, and one only. And that is they know how to A-S-K to G-E-T what they want. And we discovered, there’s three channels: you’ve got to ask yourself, ask others, and ask God.

Ivan:
Crystal, anything you’d add to that?

Crystal:
Well, I would just say, you know, when we started writing the book, we looked at ourselves, we said, what is that one thing we could help people with because people who are smart, talented and amazing still struggle in their relationships and their finances, in their health and wellness. And so, you know, we said, look, we’ve struggled both independently, and together through a lot of things in life. So what is that thing? What’s that thing that allows us to reinvent ourselves and keep going and transcend our issues and our struggles?

And it came down to that ability to be able to ask the right question, at the right time in the right way that helped deliver a new solution; you know, a new understanding, a breakthrough, a new idea, or a different plan, and ultimately a new beginning. And it was so exciting to us that we had to share it. We just got super excited about it. And the more we delved into the topic of asking, the more interesting, amazing, and complex it became so we said we have to write this book. People need this because it’s the one way that we could help people help themselves in any area of life.

Ivan:
So I’ve got an interesting question, at least I think it’s interesting. And it’s based on one of the books that I wrote about business networking and gender, the difference between men and women and how they network. And I interviewed 12,000 people, or surveyed 12,000 people, for this book. One of the things that we found was that men tended to be probably too direct in the way they dealt with people in networking, and women tended not to ask for the sale as much as men, according to the survey. Would you agree with those results? Or do you think that it’s off?

Crystal:
I think so. I think men are really bold to ask in some ways. And that they’re not bold to ask, when they have this perception that they’re going to look foolish, they don’t like asking for directions; they are less likely to ask for a better table at a restaurant.

Ivan:
Interesting.

Crystal:
Something that kind of, I don’t know, gets to their self esteem or makes them feel less powerful. So I think men and women ask in different ways. But I think, generally speaking, we’re all a little afraid to ask, and you know, sometimes that starts in childhood. Because if you think about depending, you know, on the way you were parented, what your upbringing was, we all start out being these perfect little uncorrupted askers – we’re literally asking for everything. And we want it all, right? And we want to know everything who, what, when, where, why, how, and then depending on, you know, how you were parented, did your parents, you know, say, “Be quiet, you’re asking too many questions.” Or at school, you’re shut down, you know, “Do not ask until you’re called upon.” And so much of the time life sort of shuts us down in that curiosity and that desire to have and to know. And so what we found is often people are standing, there as full grown adults, we’re standing here, afraid to ask anything and sort of ashamed that we don’t have all the answers. It’s tragic, because there’s no way any of us will ever have all the answers in this lifetime. So we just love the idea of embracing this – the art and science of asking, and embracing that childlike curiosity that we were born with. You know, that’s what helps us grow and evolve. That’s why children grow and evolve so quickly, during those years.

Ivan:
Yes, absolutely. Their favorite word is “why.”

Crystal:
Yeah, exactly.

Ivan:
Isn’t that the truth? So what are some of the main principles of asking?

Mark:
We have twelve roadblocks to asking. It starts with, the seven roadblocks go like this. It goes first of all, a sense of unworthiness, then it’s a sense of naiveté, then it’s a sense of doubt and excuse-ology, then it’s fear, then it’s pattern paralysis. And last but not least, it’s disconnection. And a lot of people say, “Well, I have a cell phone, so I’m always connected.” And then, no, no — connection is two people together. In sales, it’s eyeball to eyeball, bellybutton to bellybutton, and toenail to toenail. And one of the stories I like best is, you probably know Jim Stovall. And I did the foreword to his book and it made him $100 million in a movie, and he’s a guy who went blind when he was going to be a [USA] National Football League player. And he said, “I now, thanks to Mark Victor Hansen, write books I can’t read and make movies I can’t watch.”

So learning how to ask makes you a superstar. So everyone needs to get, we believe, two copies of the book, Ivan, and go over it with somebody else in BNI, and decide, ‘Hey, wait a second, I’m going to ask you these 178 questions, you ask me them. I’m going to find out what my destiny code is.’ Because our subtitle is “The Bridge from Your Dreams to Your Destiny” because everybody’s born with a phenomenal destiny. And most people miss it during their life.

Ivan:
So one of the things we tell BNI members to do is one-to-one meetings where they sit down and talk to another member of BNI. This would actually be a good exercise for people to do on a one-to-one, wouldn’t you say?

Mark:
Yes, absolutely. We’ve got stacks of letters from people that say it changed our life. We’ve been on podcast with the biggest podcasters in the world. And one of them said, “I was looking furiously around my house to find your book. And all of a sudden I go to my 13 year-old daughter, and I say, ‘Have you seen Mark Victor Hansen, Crystal Dwyer Hansen’s book “Ask!”,’ and they said, ‘Oh, yeah, I’ve read every page. It’s amazing.'” And he said, “Holy cow, give it to me, because I got him on a giant podcast with hundreds of thousands of people, you got to let me have it back, darling.” And it just, it’s amazing people read the first story of “The Fable of Micaela” which, God bless my wife, it’s prophetic dreams that she’s got in there. It’s now looks like it’s becoming a movie. We’re under contract and all that. It is just, it is amazing. The people that read it say, “Oh, you’ve changed my life.” and it has the same essence that I created with Jack Canfield and Chicken Soup for the Soul book.

Ivan:
Yeah. So any principles that you want to share that an entrepreneur or business person, it might resonate with them in particular?

Crystal:
Yes, I think going back to the three channels that we discovered, ask yourself, ask others, and ask God or the divine or universe, whatever, you know, that relationship is for you. But, you know, it’s very important as we move through life, because asking is what’s going to deliver that next illumination to us. And so the asking yourself part is that reflective journey, and it strikes me how few people do that. You know, you can’t know where you’re going until you know where you are. And so asking yourself, there’s sort of three phases to it, you know, where am I now? And second phase is, where do I want to be? What does that look like? And then in that second phase, really using the power of your imagination, because the problem is when we’re embroiled in struggles or turmoil where we only see what’s around us. So it’s really, you know, using that imagination, so where do I want to be? And getting clear visions of what that looks like? If my career is… I’m performing perfectly in my career or my job, what does that look like? Who am I meeting with every day? Who are my colleagues? What am I producing every month? How are my clients responding to me? And literally imagining what that looks like and feels like; what those relationships look like and feel like when you’re sitting there celebrating a business success. And if you start to imagine those things, ask the questions then imagine the perfect result, the perfect answers, you can literally engineer your perfect life backwards and it works for your career, your financial goals, your relationships, and even your health and wellness. It is such a powerful thing when you learn to implement the art and science of asking.

Ivan:
Yeah, and asking with clarity. I mean, I don’t know if you know this, but in BNI members have a chance every week to stand up and say who they are and what they do. And one of the things that I tell them is, you got to be crystal clear about at least one element of what you do every week. And over the course of a year, you’ve given a lot of information. But clarity is key. And asking for what you want is absolutely key if you want to get referrals from people.

Crystal:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Is there any closing thoughts that you have, that you’d like to share about your book? Again, the book is called “Ask!: The Bridge from Your Dreams to Your Destiny.”

Crystal:
Well, I just wanted to just reiterate — the questions you ask really will determine the outcome of your life. And you know, you honestly don’t get what you deserve in life, you get what you ask for. It’s just such an important thing. Do you have anything?

Mark:
Yeah, we’d love people to go to www.thebookask.com {Editor’s note: this is the correct website per Crystal’s upcoming correction}, and we give you a free introduction to how to become a master asker because as you become a master asker you become a no limit, fully functioning, self-actualizing superstar, and every one of us is supposed to become a masterpiece in our own lives.

Ivan:
Great, can you give us that domain one more time?

Crystal:
Oh, no, no. You know what? Sorry, that’s the old one. Can I correct that?

Ivan:
Sure.

Crystal:
It’s www.thebookask.com

Ivan:
Okay, that’s it: www.thebookask.com. Everyone go check that out. And can most of my members get the book on Amazon and at bookstores?

Crystal:
Yep. Amazon and at bookstores. Both places, yes.

Mark:
And in audio or whatever form they want. It’s there because it’s a best seller, we’re thankful to say.

Crystal:
Yeah, we would love to hear from them. We love to hear people’s stories when they’ve read the book. It’s really fun to see, you know, what they’ve learned, the illuminations they they’ve gotten from reading it. It’s really, we’ve received so many letters, and it’s very inspiring to us to hear about their successes.

Ivan:
Well, fantastic. Crystal, Mark, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast. I appreciate you both. Priscilla, back to you.

Mark:
Thank you. We loved every minute of it.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you both so much.
