title.rendered: Episode 795: Who’s in Your Room? 2nd Edition

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week, Dr. Misner introduces some important new concepts from the second edition of Who’s in Your Room?

  • The Doorkeeper (first edition) is your conscious and subconscious mind screening people before they come into your room or into your life.
  • The Concierge (second edition) is your conscious and subconscious mind bringing the people in your room closer or moving them farther away.
  • Dealbreakers (first edition) are behaviors that tell you immediately that you don’t want someone in your room.
  • Dealmakers (second edition) are behaviors that make you say “Wow, I want this person in my life.”

There are some people you have to have in your room–but you can make sure they keep their baggage outside.

Get your copy of the book on Amazon for more great material, with exercises and examples.

Complete Transcript of Episode 795

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great. I’m home in Austin, Texas this week. This is International Networking Week®. And so I’m doing lots and lots of Zoom presentations all over the world.

Priscilla:
Wow. And it happened again, just a year later.

Ivan:
Yes, indeed.

Priscilla:
So what do you have to share with us in this in this podcast?

Ivan:
Well, just a couple of weeks ago, the Second Edition of one of my favorite books came out, it’s “Who’s in Your Room?” We have a different subtitle. The subtitle for “Who’s in Your Room?” Second Edition is “The Question That Will Change Your Life.” And I think that’s really true. I think the idea of who’s in your room and understanding the concept of who’s in your room will change people’s lives. And it has changed many people’s lives. So the Second Edition – we’ll have a link for it here on the podcast. But you can find it in bookstores, you can find it on Amazon. This edition is the blue cover. The blue cover is the second edition. And again, the link will be here in this podcast. And we have gone, Priscilla, from 15,000 words to 45,000 words in the Second Edition.

Priscilla:
Oh, wow!

Ivan:
Yeah. So it is three times as much content as we had in the first edition. So even if you have the first edition, you’re gonna love this one, because there’s two things that we’ve added, in addition to a lot of other ideas, some of which I’ll talk about here. But in addition to other ideas, we have a lot of stories in this edition. And we have exercises in this edition, things to complete. And we’re even considering putting together a coaching program for coaches that are out there who want to be certified on you know, an exceptional room – creating an exceptional room. So we’re thinking about doing that. So if you’re a coach, and this sounds like something that you’d like to get certified in, drop me a note here on the podcast, and we’ll make sure you get information as we develop the program. It’s, I think, excellent for anyone who does coaching, particularly life coaching, but even business coaching. So you want to hear some of the new content that we’ve added?

Priscilla:
I do, please.

Ivan:
So, one of the things that was added, and we have a new co-author, Doug Hardy. Doug really came up with a couple of fantastic ideas for the Second Edition, one of which is the Concierge. If you remember, Priscilla, we had the idea of the Doorkeeper or doorman. We’ve switched it to be a little more politically correct to Doorkeeper in this version. And the Doorkeeper is the person that, you know, lets people into your room. And of course, at some point in our life, we didn’t have a door keeper and people just kind of came as they did, and we had no control. But the idea of the Doorkeeper is, you know, your conscious and subconscious mind screening people before they come into your room or into your life. And you know, maybe I should start with, for those of you who aren’t familiar with the book, I’m assuming that people are familiar with it, they may not be. So let me give you a minute or two on the concept of the book.

Imagine you live your life in one room and that one room has only one door and that one door is an enter only door so that when people come into your room or into your life, they’re there forever. You can’t get them out. Now, it sounds like a metaphor. But we argue that it’s more than a metaphor because when people come into your life, their fingerprints are all over your brain. Dr. Daniel Amen, who we interviewed for the first edition and he’s also in the second edition, said that when you have a relationship with someone personally or professionally, their fingerprints are all over your brain. And they’re in your room for the rest of your life. And your room begins on, you know, take your index finger and put it on your right temple, and [take] your left hand and put your index finger on your left temple. Your room is everything in between. It’s your mind, it’s your history, it’s your experiences. And so the Doorkeeper is your conscious and subconscious mind keeping people out of your head, out of your room, who shouldn’t be there. And that we talked about in the first edition.

But what we didn’t talk about in the first edition was this concept of the Concierge. And the Concierge is your conscious and subconscious mind figuring out where people belong that have gotten into your room. You’ve either let them in, or they are family and you had no choice, or you let them in before you understood the concept of “Who’s In Your Room?” And, you know, there’s basically already a crowd of people in there: your significant other, family members, friends, work associates, people that you’ve let into the room in the past. And whether you’ve intentionally let them in, or you’ve accidentally allowed them in, they’re in and so some of them have to be kind of put off to the distance, and some of them are brought up close. The Concierge works on your behalf, bringing people closer or walking them further away. It’s a conscious decision. We talk about the concept of the Concierge in this book, which is a great concept and it’s much more complex than we have time to talk about. But it’s a great concept that Doug brought up.

Another thing that we talk about in the book is a story from Geneen, Geneen Roth, who wrote a number of books, including The New York Times bestseller, “When Food Is Love.” But one of the things that she talked about in one of her books is experiencing the breaths that you have left, enjoying the breaths that you have left. I think as we all get older, that becomes really important. Living our best lives and enjoying the breaths that we have left in life are really important. And I urge people to take a good look at Geneen’s story which is in the book, and very, very powerful.

We also have exercises in this version of the book about… I mean, there’s several. I’m going to share a couple of them with you on this podcast. One is – who’s close? and who’s far away? You know, we have many people in our room, our room is huge. It’s all the people that have come into our life. And you want to think about what people are close to you. And so we have you draw concentric circles with yourself at the center, and decide how close or far away people are today and where they should be. Maybe there are people who are in concentric circles far away, that you want to bring back in closer and maybe there are people that are close that you want to put out further in your room. And so we have to do a little exercise with that.

And there are a number of questions with this exercise that I’ll share with you now.

  • One of the things that you should think about when you are thinking of the concentric circles is who comes up in your memory often?
  • Is there some moment that seems to come up a lot? And is it good or bad?
  • And should the people involved in that be close to you in your room, or far away?
  • Who do you pay instant attention to when they call or email you? And why? Why do you pay instant attention to them? And where should they be in those concentric circles?
  • Who do you resist? Who do you put off answering on those phone calls or emails?
  • Is there someone that you’ve resented for years? Or resent right now? And are they close, or are they far away?
  • Do you have someone that you truly dislike with enough energy that just thinking about them can change your mood or have you obsess over your history with them?

These are questions that we have people ask themselves in the Second Edition that… none of this was in the first edition. And so there’s really a lot of additional material that we’ve added into the book.

Another concept that we brought into this, we talked about Dealbreakers, what are the things that when you think of — Boom, you know you don’t want this person in your room. But what are the Dealmakers? That’s a new concept. What are the Dealmakers? What are behaviors that you say, “Wow, this is the kind of person that I want in my life.”

And so some of the kinds of questions that you should ask in terms of Dealmakers are:

  • Who brings in things that align with your values? Are you meeting somebody who brings in things that align with your values?
  • Do they bring you joy? Do they offer you support?
  • Are they happy for you when something good happens?
  • Do they accept you as you are?
  • Who brings out the best in you?

We talk about, in the first edition and this edition, Anchors and Engines. Engines driving you forward, anchors bringing you down. And that, too, is in the Second Edition. And then we have some great stories, new stories that weren’t in the first book. I’ll give you one story and then we’ve got to wrap up on this podcast. One of the things that we talked about in this book is that, you know, somebody may be in your room, but their baggage has to stay out.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
And there was a fantastic story that was given to me after I did the first edition of the book. And it was a woman who said that she had a family member who would always come to family events. And she would, during the family event, throw in this verbal ‘grenade’ that would explode and everybody would just get furious with whatever it was that she said. And she would just rile people up at almost every family event. And they were almost thinking about stopping family events because of her.

But she read the first edition of “Who’s In Your Room?” and the family got together without this person. And they all decided that the next time this woman came to a family event and threw in a verbal ‘grenade’, they would all look at her and say, “Oh, okay.” and then turn back and get right back to their original conversation. So basically – ignore the comment. It worked amazingly well. The next time she came to this event, and she threw in a verbal ‘hand grenade’ and people looked at her, went, “Oh, okay.” and then turned back and continued their conversation. And it was amazing, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
She stopped throwing in the ‘hand grenades’ because she didn’t get a response.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
And so, this is the beauty of this book, “Who’s In Your Room?” is it gives you the tools to learn, not only who to allow in your life, but also the tools to learn how to deal with the people in your life. So I urge you, if you’re listening to this podcast, go take a look at the book and please do me a favor. Put up a review. Well, put up a review if you liked the book; if you don’t, forget about this comment, you don’t have to put up a review. But no, I would love your reviews up on Amazon, especially because reviews really make a difference. That’s my material for today.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great, Ivan. You know, Geneen Roth, I went to a workshop that she gave, like, years ago.

Ivan:
Really?

Priscilla:
Yeah. And it was so powerful. It was all about, like compulsive eating. And she would have everybody take a little tiny piece of chocolate and eat it for like, you know, as long as you could keep it alive in your mouth and enjoy every little, tiny taste of it. And, you know, she is she did all these incredible exercises. And I found one of her really old books in my library, and I wanted to give it to somebody who needed it, and so I’ve been thinking about her. I mean, she’s just an amazing person. And I lost track of her.

Ivan:
I had no idea. I’m glad you shared that story. And I’m glad that she is in the Second Edition of “Who’s In Your Room?”

Priscilla:
Yeah. Great. Well, I’m looking forward to reading this book.

Ivan: 
Alright, everyone, take a look at the link on here and check out the book on Amazon.

Priscilla:
Sounds great. Okay, well, thank you, Ivan

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 794: Givers Gain, Chapter 8–A New Audience (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Chapter 8 of Givers Gain covers the years 1998-2000. It was during this period that Dr. Misner realized he needed to write a book about networking to educate BNI members, and wrote The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret.

The surprise discovery was the media benefits of publishing a book. Thanks to his 10 books, Dr. Misner has been interviewed well over 1000 times. His first live TV appearance was right after the Village People and his book nearly set the host on fire.

Since that time, Dr. Misner’s books have become a vital part of the BNI culture.

Four main factors determined BNI’s phenomenal growth during this period:

  1. Had already built a solid foundation for BNI.
  2. BNI is locally owned an operated.
  3. Jumped the borders and started spreading into new countries.
  4. Gained greater exposure through publishing books.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcription of BNI Podcast Episode 794/51

Priscilla Rice:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by networkingnow.com which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. How are you, Ivan?

Ivan Misner:
Doing great, Priscilla. Today I am going to be talking about Chapter 8, A New Audience, of the book, Givers Gain. I have been covering the book over the last several months, and my plan is to talk a little bit about each one of the chapters so that people who have the book in front of them can also listen to a little bit of it in my own voice. So they are reading some of the material and they hear me talking about it as well. I think it’s a great tool to use as you read the book.

This section, Chapter 8, really talks about mostly from 1995 through 2000 for the most part. Some really interesting things happened right about that time. I was really trying to spend more time working on the business and less time working in the business. It was really at that point that I was coming to the conclusion that I needed to be spending more time on strategic planning, content and material.

Over the years I had read a lot of books and journals on marketing but I hadn’t read anything on networking. If you do a literature search prior to 1995, you will find almost nothing on business networking and referral marketing prior to the mid-90s. I wanted to do a book that was a comprehensive book on networking and word-of-mouth. I did a book in 1989 called Networking for Success but it was a self published book. It wasn’t distributed much.

I really wanted to do a real book, a book that was published. So I worked with Ray Bard of Bard press in 1994 and we published my first primary published book, The World’s Best-Known Marketing Secret. It’s probably important to note that Jeff Morris was the editor of that book and Jeff is my co-author of this book, Givers Gain. Jeff has been involved in so many of my books that he ended up being the co-author with me of Givers Gain.

That’s where I came out with the book, The World’s Best-Known Marketing Secret. That was really the core book for success in networking and particularly in BNI. What I wanted to do was sort of write a book that was a blueprint that BNI members could use to use in BNI and their other networks to become successful in the networking process. There were a lot of really core philosophies of the organization that are spread throughout this book.

Priscilla:
Is that the reason you’ve written so many books? Aren’t there like 10 now or something?

Ivan:
There are. That led me into writing a number of other books. There are now 10 books including Givers Gain that I have written. We have about six or seven more in the works. You know, the bottom line when I started writing the books was to provide educational content for members who were reasonably new to the organization.

When we first came out with The World’s Best-Known Marketing Secret, there were no educational coordinators of the chapters. It was because there was no content. We didn’t have a lot of stuff. As we started doing more and more books, it was like how do we communicate this to members? How do we get this content to members? So we started doing the books as a way of providing additional content for members and then later education coordinators.

You know, we discovered along the way which is an amazing thing is the publicity that you get from doing a book. If you go to the media, particularly radio but even newspaper and television, and say would you do it interview on me for my business? They will say, “Take an ad out. We don’t want to interview you about your business, about BNI.”

But Priscilla, the media will interview any idiot with a book. I have 10. I have lots and lots of interviews. That’s one of the reasons why I’ve been doing the books is because you get this incredible exposure and media opportunities to build the brand. I have now done well over 1000 radio, TV and newspaper interviews and almost all have started because of the book. They want me to talk about the book. I talk about the book and then of course they always ask what is one of the reasons that led you to write this book? Then I bring a BNI and guess what? They end up spending most of the rest of the interview talking about BNI.

I talk about this in this book. I talk about one of our strategic alliance partners, Jim Blasingame in Chapter 8 of Givers Gain. Jim Blasingame runs a radio talk show called the Small Business Advocate. If you would like to go to his website, www.smallbusinessAdvocate.com is his website. He has interviewed me dozens and dozens and dozens of times. If you do a search on me you will probably find 20 or 30 radio interviews that I have done with Jim.

He has been a real supporter of BNI. If the listeners of his podcast want to hear half-hour interviews with Jim and me, you should go do that because there’s some great content there that’s been done over the years. My interviews with Jim have gotten a lot better than my first one. I talk about my very first television interview in this. Have you ever heard about my live TV interview in Connecticut, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
No. I would love to hear about that.

Ivan:
Well, it’s a real embarrassment. You know, this is back before the time of tape delay. Since Janet Jackson, everything is tape delayed now. But when I did this it was my first live television interview. I will give you the short version of the story and if you want the whole version, you’re going to have to go read it. It’s in Chapter 8 on page 68 of Givers Gain, the BNI story. I was in Connecticut. It was a live television show in the morning. I’m in the green room. It was a big show and a lot of people were there.

While I was sitting in there, Priscilla, a guy who walked by me, am Indian. Then some guy dressed as a construction worker. And some guy dressed as a cop. Someone says, “Gee, it looks like the Village People.” I was like, yeah right– the Village people are to be on. Then we are actually watching the TV show and it says, “Next on the Fairfield County exchange, the Village People and Dr. Ivan Misner to talk about networking. I was thinking all why. I’m on after the Village people!”

Well they wanted me to do something visual so I decided I would do this little magic trick. You’ve seen magicians where they take stick thier hand out and the flame comes out of their hands. Have you ever see that, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Maybe.

Ivan:
You’ve seen fire come out of a magician’s hand or out of some box or something. Well, I had this little magic trick. I’m an amateur magician. The key word being amateur. I had this little thing or fire would come out and I was going to open the book and have a flame come out. I thought oh my, I am on after the Village People. This is going to be really scary. I better put more of that magic paper in there that makes it flame up. I put some more in and I watched the Village People. They were great. Of course, they are very visual. They want a visual on the show. So what is the last song you think that they would do to be visual?

Priscilla:
I have no idea. You’d tell me.

Ivan:
YMCA. So they appear out in the audience and there’s the entire audience standing on their feet, doing it with their arms, YMCA. I am thinking, oh my. I am going on next. This is going to be a total embarrassment. This is horrible. I’m going to put a little more of that magic paper is better to get the flame to really kick.

So I get out there. It’s a long story but the bottom line is that we got to the end of the interview and the host says, “I have here a copy of Dr. Misner’s book,” she opens it up and I had a little flame. I was going to say, “Be careful the book is hot.”

I did the flame thing and it shot way up into the sky. She jumped into the lap of her co-host, screaming at the top of her lungs. The camera stops. The director stops. Nobody can say anything. All she could say as she’s clutching her chest is, “Thankfully I didn’t swear on live television.” This is still coming out on the air. The last thing they said before they cut was her co-host looking out to the audience and looking back stage saying, “Wardrobe, new pants for her, please.”

The director says to cut and go to commercial. That was my first time on live television talking about the book. I’ve gotten a little better since then. In most states, I am considered an expert in networking, but in Connecticut, I am considered an arsonist.

Priscilla:
Did she forgive you for that?

Ivan:
She did. I tried to tell her two or three times what I was going to do and I talked about this in the long version of the story. If you read the book, you get the whole story. But I did try to tell her two or three times, but the director was in a rush and I didn’t get a chance to tell her. So that’s what happened.

Since The World’s Best-Known Marketing Secret, I have come out with books like Business by Referral, Masters of Networking, Masters of Success, and Masters of Sales. I should have a book coming out called the 29% Solution- 52 Weekly Networking Strategies. These books have really become a vital part of the BNI culture. There is just so much content available for education coordinators, but you know, you can’t be a member of BNI without having this wealth of content at your fingertips. These podcasts are a great example but also the material that is in the books. It’s just amazing.

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
I know we need to wrap up. I think to wrap up this chapter, Chapter 8, I think there are really made four main factors for BNI’s phenomenal growth from 1995 through 2000. First, we had given BNI a solid foundation. You hear about us talking in the first seven chapters about a foundation of good organizational structure, capable leaders, well-designed manuals. You cannot build a house until the foundation is solid. Everything started with the foundation. The system and the way we run the meetings and the schedule, the meeting agenda in the manuals — we now have almost a thousand pages of manuals for our directors. That started really in the early days.

Second, the fact that BNI is locally owned and operated. 95% of all the revenues stay within the country that operates in. It’s a locally owned and operated program and I think that’s one of the reasons that have made it successful.

Third is we jumped our borders in this period of time, in 1995, and started spreading into new countries and spreading and new come trees has helped to grow the organization.

Fourth, we made ourselves better known by publishing books. It has opened the door for media interviews, and we just got a ton of exposure for the organization. In that five-year period, we ended up opening in half a dozen countries or more. That really laid the groundwork for the next 10 years of the organization. That’s a pretty good summary of Chapter 8 of Givers Gain.

Priscilla:
Can I add a number five?

Ivan:
Sure.

Priscilla:
I think it’s having somebody like you as a leader and CEO of the company. You’re an amazing person and you have a lot to offer. It’s wonderful to have you in the leadership role.

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla. I really appreciate you saying that. I stepped down as CEO, actually. And I’ll tell you why that’s important. Because it gives me an opportunity now to do this full-time. This is my function in the organization. I believe that the founder of an organization is really well positioned to be the spokesperson of an organization, better than almost anyone else because they started the company. So I spend the majority of my time now really working on the business, writing, speaking, doing these podcasts, writing articles, publishing books as a way of building the brand.

It’s helping to educate and train members. I appreciate you saying what you said. It’s a concerted effort really peaking in with this chapter’s topic. 1995, that is when I started thinking that I really wanted to be the spokesperson for the organization and it was a darn near 10 year journey to get to where I at this point. I like to tell people that I’m a 20 year overnight success. It took me 10 years when I decided I really wanted to work more on the business to be able to, in fact, be working on the business. But thank you very much.

Priscilla:
You’re so welcome. Well, I think we’ve come to the edit is podcasts. Thank you, Dr. Misner. I just want to let the listeners know that this podcast has been brought to you by networkingnow.com, which is a leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thanks for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Gaining a new audience comes with pitfalls. In his first live TV appearance, Ivan Misner almost set the host on fire with a “magic trick.”

title.rendered: Episode 793: Influencing People with Stories

content.rendered:
Synopsis

A story is information wrapped in emotion that compels people to take action. If you want to influence people, inspire them, and transform them, tell great stories. Stories that make them want to become a better version of themselves. Stories that compel them to take action.

More About Stories

  • Episode 51: Givers Gain, Chapter 8–A New Audience
  • Episode 588: Want Referrals? Don’t Bore Me to Tears
  • Episode 597: Networking Wind Sprints
  • Episode 601: Why Make All the Mistakes?

Complete Transcript of Episode 793

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan: 
I am doing fantastic. I am in Charlotte for BNI, at the global support headquarters and meeting with staff and directors and some members. I love visiting global support. I also love having my own office in my house, so that’s even better. At one point they asked me, they said, “Hey, do you want an office here at Charlotte?” And I’m like, “No, please.” I’d have to be there all the time. I love my office in Austin. But I also love to visit Charlotte. They’re always so welcoming when I come to the global support office.

Priscilla:
That’s nice. So what do you have here? What is this influencing people with stories?

Ivan:
So what influences people the most? Position? Power? Success? I don’t think so. I think it’s stories. In BNI it’s important to tell stories about how things look after you’ve done what you do in business. How does the client or the customer feel? What is their experience? Listen to the episode of 588, “Don’t Bore Me to Tears.” It’s a great episode to hear, a fantastic example of a very specific BNI story and how it makes people want to do business with that member.

There is nothing in the world more powerful than a great story, one that touches upon an emotion, one that makes people smile, one that tugs on the heartstrings. One that inspires people, one that empowers them to take action and to transform their life in some way. Nothing is more powerful than a great story.

So what makes a great story? I’ve talked about this before. In the book The Elements of Persuasion–Robert Dickman was one of the co-authors of the book, and I met Robert years ago at the home of Peter Guber, who was the CEO of Mandalay Entertainment. He may still be the CEO; Mandalay Entertainment has done a lot of movies including some of the original Batman movies. [A] very interesting man, Peter, and I met Robert there. It was a storytelling day; we all got together and talked about what made stories work. And Robert shared something that I have shared for years with people. The formula of what I think makes a great story and I’ve probably talked about this in a podcast in the past, but I want to emphasize it here today.

A great story is a fact–it’s information that you have, you are conveying information. That’s first. Second – wrapped in an emotion. It could be excitement, it could be tugging on the heartstrings, it could be humor. I like humor, I use humor, probably more than anything else. But it can be tugging on the heartstrings; it could be a passionate, excited presentation, which I’d like to think I also do. So it’s a fact wrapped in an emotion, that, three, that compels people to take action. That when somebody hears it, they go, “I gotta do that. Why am I not doing that? I need to do that.” That’s what I love. You know, what I love about speaking to an audience, is that I’ll take a fact, I’ll wrap it in an emotion, and I can look in the audience, and I can see in the eyes of the people who are listening to me speak, I can see them light up. I can see them thinking, “Yeah.” They’re nodding. They’re like, “Yeah, I need to do that.” So it compels people to take action. And by taking that action, it transforms them in some way. That’s the key. That’s what makes a great story. Is that it transforms them in some way. That if they hear that information, and if it’s packaged in some emotion, and it compels them to take action, they transform. They transform because they take action. They make a better life for themselves; a better business for themselves. They make a better version of themself. That’s what storytelling does.

And one of the keys in telling a great story…. and you know, this is one of our 3+1, one of the key success factors for BNI is storytelling. And one of the key elements of storytelling is that you have to remember that you don’t just retell a story, you relive a story. Don’t just retell a story, relive a story. Imagine you are there. Describe something about what was around you. Be descriptive and make the story compelling. Relive the experience as you tell the story. Relive a story, listen to episode number 597, where I talk about networking and wind sprints. When I tell that story, in Episode 597, I am living that story. I remember that football field I was on; I remember the wind sprints, I remember the conditioning. I remember meeting the Los Angeles Rams, it was an amazing experience. And I relive it when I tell it. Listen to episode number 601 about making mistakes. That’s my Portuguese man-of-war story, where I talked about getting stung by a jellyfish. And when I tell that story, I relive it. I visualize what is taking place in front of me, and as I tell the story, I try to describe that.

If you want to influence people, inspire them, and transform them, tell great stories. Stories that will want them to become a better version of themselves. Stories that compel them to take action. Stories are powerful. And we all have within us the ability to share that power with others. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Oh, well, what I’m thinking about is, first of all, you are a great storyteller. And I had the opportunity to hear you tell a very funny story when I was at the International Conference that I attended once in Southern California for BNI. And you told the story about going on television and doing one of your magic tricks that went awry.

Ivan: (chuckling)
It did.

Priscilla:
You have to say something about that story because that story was hysterical.

Ivan:
Oh, it’s a great story. And I tell it, we go way, way back to 2008. If you go to episode number 51, I was talking about Givers Gain, chapter eight of Givers Gain back in 2008. And I tell that very story and what we should do, Priscilla, is next week on a classic let’s do a classic of Episode 51, where people can listen to that story. It is very funny. You know, sometimes stories. I think, you know if you can show humility in some of the stories that you tell, if you can be self-deprecating.

Priscilla: 
Right.

Ivan: 
Especially if you have any level of success…

Priscilla:
Yes.

Ivan:
…being self-deprecating is a way of really connecting with people.

Priscilla:
Yes.

Ivan:
And what I did was so embarrassing, and so stupid, and I looked so bad, that it’s funny.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
I mean, you know, in the moment, I was horrified as to what was happening. Horrified. But later, it became one of my better stories because it was just so crazy. And oftentimes, that’s what makes a great story is — you’ve got to make it personal, transparent. It’s got to be authentic; it’s got to be compelling. And there was a message to that story. And if you go and listen it, the message is: we all screw up. Okay, you know, you just got to get back out there and practice and do it and over and over again. You know, I screwed up on live television. It could be worse. Most people don’t screw up on live television. Thank goodness YouTube didn’t exist then. So, telling great stories.

And before we wrap up, I’ve been mostly talking about telling stories about your business, and that’s where the first episode that I was talking about, “Don’t Bore Me to Tears,” Episode 588, but telling stories about BNI is good. You know if you want someone to visit a BNI chapter, tell a great story about how it’s impacted you or someone you know. And storytelling works with just about anything in terms of influencing people. It’s not position. It’s not power or success. You want to influence people? It’s storytelling. That’s my message for today, Priscilla. Thanks.

Priscilla:
That’s great. I feel slightly embarrassed that we are having so much fun chuckling about your story, but we want to share it with you. So definitely listen to the classic podcast, it’s next week.

Ivan:
Yes, please.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: A story is information wrapped in emotion that compels people to take action. If you want to influence people, tell great stories.

title.rendered: Episode 792: Has Anyone Ever Tried to Bribe You?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Refusing an attempted bribe from a vendor 40 years ago ultimately led Ivan Misner to start not only his own business, but BNI. By turning down that bribe and leaving that company, he ended up starting a business that’s helped businesspeople around the world generate over 100 billion US dollars’ worth of business for themselves.

Most regrets that people have come from situations where people didn’t live their values or didn’t live the values that they would later come to hold. Forgive yourself for whatever you’ve done or not done, but from this moment forward, draw a line in the sand as to what’s acceptable to you and not acceptable. If you live your values, you live a life of abundance.

Dr. Misner talks more about this in the second edition of Who’s in Your Room?

Complete Transcript of Episode 792

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great. And this week I am near Puerto Vallarta in Mexico doing a BNI event and attending another Transformational Leadership Council event. TLC was the organization started by Jack Canfield. He’s become a really good friend over the years. As you pointed out before we went on-air, Priscilla, TLC goes to some of the coolest places. And that is true.

Priscilla:
That is so true.

Ivan:
So I have an interesting topic today. “Has anyone ever tried to bribe you?” is the title. And the reason that I decided to do this is a good friend of mine, a BNI director, recently asked me if there have been times where it was difficult for me to live my values. She and I were having a conversation about values, and she asked me if there have ever been times where it’s difficult to live my values. And I told her that there were many times where my values were challenged, that I have strived to always be true to the values that I have throughout my life. Probably one of the first times this happened, and I’m going to tell this story and then I’m gonna connect it back for all of you to think about something [after] having heard the story I’m about to tell you.

One of the first times something like this happened to me, it was over 40 years ago, I was working as the general manager for a light manufacturing company in Los Angeles. Which, by the way, almost no one knows, I rarely share that I was the general manager for this light manufacturing company in LA. And this was before I was a management consultant, before I started BNI. The owner wanted me to use her real estate agent to find a new location for the business, that I was the manager of, that she owned. And she was the co-owner. And the real estate agent tried to get me to recommend a specific property that he represented. It was a really large commercial property and it was valued in the millions. And this was years ago. And I wasn’t keen on the property for a number of reasons. I didn’t think it fit what the business needed and I told him so; I told him that. As a result, he offered me almost one-third of my entire annual salary if I recommended it to the owner and she bought the property.

Priscilla:
Mm-hmm.

Ivan:
Well, not only did I not recommend it, I told the owner about the attempted bribe. Well, ironically, she didn’t believe me, because she knew this guy for some time and she considered him her friend. And I mean, she basically was like, “No, no, you must have misunderstood him.” Like, really? How do you misunderstand “Do you want $10,000?” I was making $35,000 then. “I’ll give you $10,000 If you recommend this property and she buys it.” How do you misunderstand something? That’s a bribe.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
This was just 40 years ago. $10,000–wow! That was that was huge!

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Then soon after that, something else happened in working with her and I decided that this was not the kind of person that I wanted to work for. And I went out and started my own consulting company, literally left that company. There’s another great story that I should tell someday that I had a ‘Twilight Zone’ experience with this person. And after that Twilight Zone experience, I left that company, and I started my own consulting company. And that consulting business eventually led me, as most people know, to starting BNI.

So in a way, an attempted bribe worth many thousands of dollars, set in motion a series of circumstances that allowed me to help hundreds of thousands of people over the last four decades. So that by turning down that bribe and leaving that company, I ended up starting a business that’s helped businesspeople around the world generate over 100 billion US dollars’ worth of business for themselves since I began BNI.

Words have power. And I strongly believe that. And at the same time, the older I get, the less I believe in words, and the more I believe in behaviors. Behaviors stem from your values. I think most regrets that people have come from situations where they didn’t live their values or didn’t live the values that they would later come to hold. And this is why I think it’s important for all of us to think about our personal values. What are they? And why are they important to us? I talk about that in “Who’s in Your Room?”, and I’ve got a second edition of “Who’s in Your Room?” coming out real soon. I talk about it, definitely, in the second edition. And when you live those values on a daily basis, you rarely have regrets in your life. I believe that if you live your values, you live a life of abundance. And that’s the way I prefer to live my life. That short-term bribe would not have led to the long-term impact that I’ve been able to have in the world, and I’m glad I didn’t take it.

Priscilla:
Mm-hmmm. That’s great. I love that story. The real estate industry is kind of full of that–massaging your palms, so to speak.

Ivan:
Yeah, it might be. Or you know, I know people give finder’s fees.

Priscilla:
Uh huh.

Ivan:
And you know, finders fee’s… I don’t know the ethics of it in the real estate business. I do know that if one real estate agent refers another real estate agent, it’s completely, I believe, completely legitimate and ethical to do, and that’s fine.

Priscilla:
Exactly.

Ivan:
I think if a party refers somebody and the real estate agent wants to give them a spiff or a royalty, or some kind of gift, I think…  I’m not the expert – real estate agents, feel free to chime in, don’t beat me up; I don’t know the answer. I think it’s okay. But here’s where it wasn’t okay, and clearly not okay in this situation. There was a conflict of interest. My job, I was being paid to find the best location for the company. So to try to bribe me to pick a location that he particularly wanted me to pick and I think the reason he wanted me to pick it was he was the listing agent and the selling agent.

Priscilla:
Uh-hum. Oh, yeah, right.

Ivan:
He [would have] got a greater commission. And so he preferred that I picked that one over some of the others that he showed me. And so where he, in my mind, CLEARLY crossed the ethical line was that there was a conflict of interest in him giving money to me to pick something.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
If it’s a referral, I don’t think that’s an ethical breach. I could be wrong and please feel free, without slamming me, to share here on the podcast, if you’re a real estate agent, as to what the ethics are. And my guess is they may vary from state to state, and may vary from country to country. But for me, there was no doubt about it. It was an ethical breach for him to be offering me money to do it. And it wasn’t the first time.. it was probably nine years earlier was the first time someone tried to bribe me. I was student body president… I didn’t plan on telling this story. I was student body president.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh.

Ivan:
And you know, there are big ring companies. There’s only a couple of big companies that do class rings.

Priscilla:
Uh-hum.

Ivan:
And it was my job to pick the company that would do the class rings for all the students. And one of the big class ring companies said to me, “Look, if you pick my company, I’ll give you a free ring of your choice. You pick the ring you want.” And I was like, “You’re trying to bribe me.” He’s like, “No, no, no, no, it’s just the thank you for…” I said, “Thank you very much. I appreciate it.” And I immediately went to the student counselor who handled Student Government and I say, “Look, this guy just tried to bribe me with a free ring.” The truth is, I probably wouldn’t pick their company anyway. But that was like a deal killer for me.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
He said, “Oh, my goodness. Thank you for telling me.” And I picked the other company. And so I mean, that was the first time someone tried to bribe me. And even, you know, a high school kid, those rings were worth.. they’re gold. They were worth hundreds of dollars way back then.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
So, you know, I’m just glad that throughout my life, I’ve tried to live my values. And I think that if you remember and you’re listening to this–look, forgive yourself for whatever you’ve done or not done. From this moment forward, draw a line in the sand as to what’s acceptable to you and not acceptable. Get good with your values; know what they are. And if you live those values, and those are healthy values, and they’re values that don’t hurt other people, then you’re not going to have regrets in your life. And, I certainly don’t have regrets on the values I’ve lived. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Perfect. Great message. Well, thank you so much. I think that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 791: Your Chapter Needs You to Be Great

content.rendered:
Synopsis

BNI CORE Launch Manager Andrew Johnson has a message for BNI leadership teams: your chapter needs you to be great. In fact, your chapter needs every member to be great. When you get team buy-in on your chapter’s goals and what you need to do to achieve them, you build momentum and revitalize the chapter. Momentum makes showing for the meeting, inviting visitors, and expanding your network fun. It gives you pride in your chapter.

  • Start with the best of what other chapters are doing–listen to the podcast for examples.
  • Park untested ideas that sound good and start with proven methods.
  • Get member buy-in on the actions you’re going to take.

Learn more about the power of momentum in John C. Maxwell’s book, The 21 Irrefutable Laws of Leadership.

Complete Transcript of Episode 791

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I just got back from California. I was doing some business there and I took some vacation time. And you know, I don’t usually answer the “How are you?” But I’m going to do it, because just about well, over the holidays, I got a report from my doctor that I’m fully in remission from the CyberKnife treatment that I got for the prostate cancer that I was diagnosed with 10 years ago. And as you know, I’ve been going a holistic route, and finally decided to do the western medicine route, and it’s been successful. I had an 81% drop in my PSA.

Priscilla:
Wow, that is so great. I’m so happy for you.

Ivan:
Yeah, thanks. I feel really good.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And my guest is going to have a little bit of a story towards his end about the health journey of his wife. What I’d love to do is to introduce my guest now, Andrew Johnson. Andrew is currently serving as BNI’s US CORE Launch Manager, and he has a real passion for helping people grow their networks. He originally joined BNI as a fifth-generation farmer with a small fitness coaching business. And as a member Andrew has been credited with over $500,000 in Thank You For Closed Business. And he has sponsored 66 members – that blows my mind, in multiple chapters, I’m guessing. He’s helped his chapter earn a perfect 100 on the chapter traffic lights, and was hired as a Launch Director for BNI in March of 2020. He has now supported the launch of 88 new BNI chapters. And he is forever grateful, he says, to BNI for how Givers Gain® has worked in his life in a powerful way. Andrew, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Andrew:
Ivan, thank you so much for having me on the call. It’s an absolute honor. I feel like this podcast has been, you know, such a training ground and support system for my own skills. And so [it’s] a complete force full circle moment to be able to give back today.

Ivan:
Well, it’s great having you on. I really appreciate it. You’ve got an amazing story. Taking over as a chapter president before you were a director, I assume, taking over as chapter president, you got inspired, I hear, by seven words of advice given to the Leadership Team trained at the Leadership Team training. Could you tell us what those words were and why they impacted you, and how they impacted you?

Andrew:
Absolutely. Well, I was the kind of guy that was voluntold to be on the Leadership Team. So I showed up to the training, kind of out of obligation.

Ivan: (chuckling)

Andrew:
But when the guy who was leading it, Glen Betts, I want to give him some recognition, looked me dead-in-the-eye, and said, “Your chapter needs you to be great.” And at that moment, I really got what was at stake, that I had to care and shepherd my group as much as I cared about my own business. And part of it was fear. Like, I didn’t want to be the chapter president that helped my chapter go downhill, you know.

Ivan: (laughing)
I believe it. We get that; I totally get that, Andrew.

Andrew:
No doubt. But I also was sitting in a room with some other very successful chapter leadership team members, and I saw how good BNI could be. And it really started to open my mind about…. maybe I was putting a ceiling on this thing. Maybe I could dream a little bit bigger, and maybe my team could start to play a little bit bigger.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know what, if you believe it you can become it. That’s what I think; and if you don’t believe it, you absolutely cannot become it. You can’t achieve it if you don’t believe. So it’s got to start with that dream of thinking that something is possible. So you helped your chapter grow from 28 members to 52 members, and [helped] more than double your Thank You For Closed Business. You also added 18 people to your own Power Team, which blows me away. Talk about that for a little while. That’s amazing.

Andrew:
Yeah, I mean, so I think when you look at what we could do (and) you’re setting the goals for the year, one thing that we were really intentional about is just not letting good ideas be good ideas. We wanted to take massive action on those good ideas. So I was a student of this podcast; we took the best advice and brought it back to the chapter and we went to work. I looked at the top chapters in the region – what were they doing? We got laser-focused on things like the chapter traffic lights and the power of one. And when you look at those reports, you realize that no individual contributor can bring the whole chapter up to the top, that you really needed to create some team buy-in. So we had some tough conversations, we had some great conversations. We definitely had some doubters at first.

Ivan:
Oh yeah.

Andrew:
But once we started to win, once we started to go from a bottom-third chapter to a middle-third chapter, once we started everybody making more money, once the visitors started to come in, once the energy in the room started to pick up, the culture changed.

It became part of who we were that we wanted to be the top chapter. And even though we weren’t the biggest, maybe we didn’t even pass the most Thank You For Closed Business, what we had was momentum. And that momentum made showing up to our meeting such a fun place to be. It made inviting and connecting other people outside of our network and to the network a lot of fun, because we had so much pride of the chapter. And quickly, we became a chapter that was in the green on the chapter traffic lights. And then we realized that ‘Well, 70 is good; well, what if we can get into the 90s or even 100?’ and it became part of our identity to go for it. So we did achieve a perfect 100 on the chapter traffic lights. We all held it for nine days. But it was a tremendous achievement, and the feeling in the room as we started to grow, realizing that you could give the gift of the chapter away to the new people as the visitors. And we also created quite a dynamic network. In Longmont, Colorado, [USA] we had 18 People in our health and wellness Power Team and felt like our community got healthier through our BNI chapter.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Ivan: Oh yeah.

Andrew:
We had something for everybody. We passed a lot of business, and we truly changed our community.

Ivan:
Wow! There’s so much in… so many layers in what you’re talking about. I’m going to probably take it in reverse order, starting with community. This is all about – BNI is all about impacting the local community. And the larger the group of successful, healthy, fun, interested, committed members you have, the more you impact your community. And I love that you brought that up. I’d love to talk for a second about the green – about being at 100. You said only nine days, but my guess is you were in the green for a very long time.

Andrew:
Oh, yeah.

Ivan:
Okay. And so people come to me and they say, “I’m at 100 in the traffic lights.” That’s fantastic. That is awesome. And the harder thing is to stay in the green for a year.

Andrew:
That’s right.

Ivan:
That’s really difficult. And it’s difficult, but it has a huge impact on the success of the chapter. So being at 100 is incredible. And being in the green is absolutely incredible on steroids. You mentioned the word momentum; I hadn’t planned on saying so much to what you had, but you have so many nuggets in there. John Maxwell calls it…it’s one of his 21 Irrefutable Laws of Leadership. He calls it the Big Mo and it stands for momentum. And what you just described is the power of the Big Mo, that when a chapter gets momentum, and it is on a tear, you know, it’s just really rolling. It just grows and grows and grows. The reverse is true, also, by the way. You could get negative momentum. And you see that happen. But when you have a chapter that has positive momentum, it’s magical. Of all of his laws, John’s laws, this is the one that I think is the hardest to describe to people because you can’t really point to ‘just do this.’ It takes a lot of things. It’s a recipe.

And the last thing I want to say – we want you to talk here – is you said you got a lot of good ideas, but I want to point out where you got them. You got them off the [BNI] Podcast, you got them from experts, you got them from leaders. You didn’t just get good ideas. When people throw good ideas at you or at a chapter, and it’s not something that’s been tested, do what I call ‘putting it in the parking lot.’ “Let’s put that in the parking lot. We’ll come back and look at that. Until then let’s do what we know works for others.”

Listen, you just gave all kinds of nuggets there and I really appreciate it. I want to make sure we have time to hit one other topic and that’s about your wife Sarah. I met her; we sat at a conference together, I think it was at a dinner. And she was in a car accident and suffered a traumatic brain injury. One year later, she was diagnosed and overcame ovarian cancer. You said your chapter was really instrumental in recovery. First of all, how is she now? And how did they help?

Andrew:
Yeah, my wife is great. If you would see her today, you would probably not know that she’d been through anything. Miraculous level healing. And today we’ll talk about the power of the chapter. I didn’t know, Ivan, how much I was going to need that great chapter. And it’s fun to, you know, share the accolades and the achievements that were in the Thank You For Closed Business. But when stuff goes sideways, or something unexpected happens, you really have no idea how powerful a network can be.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Andrew:
I remember when I got the phone call saying, “Hey, is this Andrew? Your wife has been in a car accident, but she’s okay.” And having my whole world just be flipped upside down, not knowing what was going to be. There’s no way you could ever be prepared for a moment like that.

Ivan:
Can you give me one or two things that the chapter did to help you?

Andrew:
Absolutely. So the real estate agent in the chapter, her name is Heather McChesney, she would watch my kids for me while I took my wife to her appointments. The chiropractor would drive to my house to treat her; the acupuncturist of the chapter treated my wife the day of the accident to take her body out of shock. Even things like the financial advisor making sure we had an emergency fund in place, because I’m a big believer that money doesn’t necessarily buy happiness, but it can solve problems. We could write a check for everything we needed to write a check for so I could actually focus on my wife. And it was because we could take action, because we had people that cared about me, not just people really good that we could use, we were able to get my wife the care she needs and the healing she needs to really be able to be there. And I will never be able to give back to my chapter members for all that they gave to me in the moment when I needed it the most.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s powerful. Umm, gosh, I wish we could talk more about this; we’re out of time. Is there any closing thought that you’ve got, Andrew?

Andrew:
Well, I would just say if you’re listening in this podcast, and you want to go for it, go for it. You know, the power of Big Mo; you’ll never know what it’s going to do, for not only your own life, but the other members of your chapter. And Dr. Ivan, I just really appreciate the opportunity to share. And thank you so much for having me on the podcast.

Ivan:
Oh, my pleasure. And remember everyone, remember Andrew’s message: your chapter needs you to be great. And the reason for that is if you’re a great chapter [it] gets greater, and the more you have great members in the chapter, the greater the chapter is going to be.

One last thought. Wayne Baker wrote a book; Dr. Wayne Baker wrote a book called Achieving Success Through Social Capital. And I read it probably 15-20 years ago, and he talked about how a strong network actually improves people’s health. And I remember reading that going “Oh, yeah. Come on, give me a break.” I have lived that myself; talked about going into remission, and it is because of my network. And you just shared your story, Andrew, I think Wayne was definitely correct with that. Andrew, thanks for being on BNI Podcast. I appreciate you.

Andrew:
Thank you so much.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, thank you so much, Andrew. That was an incredible story. I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for all your great information.

title.rendered: Episode 790: Why So High?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week, Dr. Misner shares a leadership secret for dealing with negative attitudes. It’s human nature to focus on problems. Part of a leader’s job is to bring about a “mindset reset” through the Why So High process.

  • Ask the person with the complaint what score they would give the problem on a scale of 1 (very severe)-10 (everything is great).
  • Respond to low scores by asking “Why so high? Name reason you gave it a 4 instead of a 3?” Write down the answer.
  • Ask someone else to name one factor that’s working on this issue. Write down the answer.
  • Repeat with the rest of the room.
  • Review the list with everyone, stopping on the items you believe are particularly effective.
  • Explain that they already know how to solve the problem.

Once you can get people to focus on what is working, they can solve problems instead of obsessing about them.

For more on this idea, listen to Episode 600: Tell Me One Thing That’s Working.

Complete Transcript of Episode 790

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Southern California this week. My eldest daughter is having my first grandchild. I’m gonna be a granddad. [Editor’s note: This episode was prerecorded. Baby Ethan was born on December 20th.]

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s so great.

Ivan:
And so I’m hanging out in Southern California so I can be there for her. I already bought the… it’s a, it’s gonna be a boy, and I already bought him shirts that say “Don’t make me call Grandpa.”

Priscilla: (laughing)
Oh, okay.  

Ivan:
And I’ve got even better ones, but I like that one.

Priscilla:  
Uh-huh, that’s so cute. You’re gonna have so much fun.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
Okay, so what is this interesting podcast that has a great title, “Why So High?”

Ivan:
Alright, I’m gonna give away some secrets here. 

Priscilla:  
Okay.

Ivan: 
I mentioned that I am teaching leadership at Concordia [University] and this is one of the leadership secrets that I gave away. When I was teaching the young men and women at Concordia University about leadership, and about managing and working with people, and inspiring and influencing. Leadership is really about influencing people. And years ago I began using a technique in BNI that was a game changer in dealing with negative attitudes about some issue that might arise within a group of people. And it’s a variation of a concept that I learned through discussions with my friend, Dr. Mark McKergow, who is the author of “The Solutions Focus” book. And I use this in BNI. I have used it for many years; it’s a secret. I’ve never shared this with anyone, but it’s an incredibly effective technique.

It’s often human nature to focus on problems, and not the solutions. In fact, many times people seem to fixate on the problems over and over and over again. They focus on them to the point that they become experts on the problem. And I’ve discovered that it’s critical that we facilitate a “mindset-reset” with people like this in any organization to get them to focus on the solutions instead of the problems. For example, when talking to a group of franchisees, or employees or members of BNI, I learned to ask a series of questions about the anxiety that they had on a particular issue. For instance, I might ask a group, particularly a BNI chapter, “What are some of the things that are a concern for you?” And sometimes they’ll say something like, “Referrals, we’re not getting enough quality referrals.” Once I knew the problem, I was able to apply the process. And here’s the Why So High? process. First, I would start by telling them that I think I can help them with the solution to this problem. And I would ask the person who posed the problem the following question, “On a scale from 1 to 10, with 1 meaning that you’re getting no quality referrals at all, and 10 meaning that you’re getting more quality referrals than you ever imagined. What score would you give the problem?” All right, you with me so far?

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
All right. Now, ironically, people to whom I’ve asked this type of question almost always say something close to a four, they’d say a three or four. So in this example, let’s say the person says to me that the score they give the problem is a four. My response to the statement is always, “Why so high?” And when I say that, the person almost always looks totally perplexed, and says something like, “No, I said, I said a four. That’s low, right?” And I respond by saying, “Yeah, I understand. You said a four, four out of 10. But my question is, why did you say four instead of a three, or a two or a one? Name just one thing that made you give it a score of four instead of something lower.” They then offer one positive reason why they ranked the problem a four instead of a lower number.

Now, and this is really important, I go to someone else. I don’t let the person with the complaint control the dialogue. Okay, this is such a powerful technique. I go to the rest of the room and I say, “Someone else, give me just one factor for you, just one, that is working for you on this issue.”

Now, if it’s at all possible, if you could write this on a flip chart, that’s fantastic, so you get a list. If you don’t have a flip chart, write it down on a sheet of paper in front of you. Give me one factor that’s working for you on this issue, then someone else inevitably gives me another factor that is a reason why it’s a four and not a three or two or a one.

And I repeat the procedure over and over again with different people. Each time I write down the answer, again, preferably in a flip chart, if not, on a notepad in front of me. Ironically, I almost always have someone who says something like, “Well actually, I don’t think I’d give it a four, I give it a higher score. I’d give it like a seven.” And I say, “Great, why so high?” And they add their thought to the list, which I write down on the flip chart or the notepad.

Once I have a fair number of ideas, somewhere between eight and twelve, I review the entire list with everyone, stopping on the items that I think are particularly effective, and explaining why I believe they are so effective.

Ivan:
And at this point, I say something that surprises almost everyone. I say, “Actually, you guys, I don’t think you need my help. I don’t think you need my help. You already know what it takes to solve the problem. You’ve done a fantastic job of outlining almost everything that it takes to overcome your challenge about referrals and to be successful with this very list. And if you do most of the things on this list consistently that you gave me, you’re going to create one of the most successful groups we have in the organization.” Then I promise to meet with their leadership team after the meeting and I help them triage the list to prioritize the topics in the order of importance for the group.

I have found, Priscilla, that all too often when people are facing a problem, they just focus on the negatives. They continue to put their attention on what’s not working, instead of looking at what is working. The truth is, if we focus on the problems, we just find more problems. However, if we focus on the solutions, we find more solutions. Solutions are what the world needs. We have enough problems.

Priscilla:
That’s for sure.

Ivan:
It’s the solutions and this technique is, I have found, one of the best techniques I’ve ever experienced to get people to focus on how do you solve the problem instead of obsessing about the problem. And by asking people, “Well, why so high? Why did you give it that score instead of a lower score?” They give you a reason. And that reason is a good one. And if you take another good one, and another good one, and another good one, and another good one, you end up having all the answers necessary to succeed. The Why So High? technique is one of the most effective techniques that I’ve ever used to get people to focus on solutions rather than problems. And this is one of my biggest secrets. I’ve never shared this publicly. What do you think?

Priscilla:
I think it’s great. It’s like reverse psychology, right? That’s why it’s working.

Ivan:  
Yeah, I hadn’t thought of it as reverse psychology. It might be. I’ve thought of it as…. we individually have the answers. But as a collective, we haven’t thought about them. And as a collective, if we think about the answers, the sum of the whole is genuinely greater than the individual parts. That’s the way I’ve thought of it. You know, rather than me stand up and tell you what your… I mean, look, I could stand up in front of a chapter and tell them what they’re doing wrong. I’ve done it before and it didn’t go over well.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Because I’m an outsider telling them what they’re doing wrong. That doesn’t work. Instead, get people to talk about what is working, even if it’s not working great, what is working? And if you focus on what is working over and over and over again, then you get the solutions and that’s where the sum of the whole becomes greater.

Priscilla:
Uh hmm, that’s neat. I really like that.

Ivan:
It’s a great technique. I urge you to, if you’re listening to this podcast, to try it – not only in BNI but try it in your business. Do what I’m talking about it. It’s amazing how incredible it works when you get people focusing on solutions, rather than problems. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You’re welcome. Thank you. That’s so interesting.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
