title.rendered: Episode 789: Hope Is More Powerful Than Fear (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 656, originally published in May 2020. Links and offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

In this week’s podcast, Dr. Misner responds to two different critiques regarding some of his social media posts about “hope” during a crisis.

One respondent was outraged that Dr. Misner was offering people “false hope.” The other was a BNI member who found it difficult to stay positive but was willing to keep an open mind.

What is the positive in being locked down at home with your three children needing to be home-schooled?

Daniel, a BNI Member

Fear vs. Focus

Even in a global pandemic, we are in charge of our attitude. Rather than get caught up in fear, focus on the solution.

Almost all of us are stuck at home. We can choose to make the most of it and come out better and stronger, or we can choose to be overcome by it.

False Hope?

To the first critic, Dr. Misner replied that hope is hearing that little voice inside you whispering about what can be when everyone around you is shouting about what can’t be.

Hope is more powerful than fear. Hope plus action leaves fear in the dust. Have hope, take action, and that’s the way you deal with situations in life.

Read “Hope Is More Powerful Than Fear” and watch the video on IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 656/789

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
Hey, Priscilla, I am doing great. And I have a podcast today that I think is unique. It is hope is is more powerful than fear. And you probably know, I know you’ve seen a lot of my posts. I’ve been posting messages on my social media ever since the COVID virus and all of the resulting things because of that. I’ve been posting a lot of my social media about hope and focus when times are difficult. And as a result, I had someone give me a gift. His gift was a rather harsh critique. He said, basically, in part, “What are you thinking, giving people false hope with posts like this?” Now I call it a gift because it gave me an opportunity to do this podcast. I want to comment about that critique in a minute.

But before I do, I want to talk about another message that I received from Daniel in Canada. He’s a BNI member and Daniel’s comment was much different. Now come back to the critique towards the end. Daniel said he appreciated my positive feedback. But he asked for help understanding what is the positive in not being able to work while being locked down at home with his three children needing to be homeschooled?

Yeah. So I mean, I get that. And what I liked about Daniel’s comment was, he appreciated the positive approach, but he was unsure how he could apply it. But he was open minded, and I feel like I can work with that. You know, people don’t have to agree with my position. But you know, if they ask for how do you apply that? I get it. Well, first of all, I’ve talked about in the special episodes that I did, you know, the importance of making effective use of being online and online or in person.

There are a number of things that you could do, but here’s what I said to Daniel, because we were as he said in lockdown. I like to call it the great pause. We’ve all had to pause in life, but call it what you will. This was my answer to Daniel. I said to him, “It sounds like you have your hands full. And I remember being at home with kids. And in the summer when I would work from home one day a week. And so sometimes it was in your hands to feel full.” So I said to him, “I like to be positive. I’m also a realist. And the situation we’re in with COVID virus, the situation we’re in sucks.” I said that twice. And I also said, “That’s the technical term. It sucks. And I get that.”

And I quoted to him part of that, Charles Swindoll, quote, about attitude and a lot of people have read that and I’m not going to read the whole quote, but I’ll read an excerpt from it. So Swindoll said

“The longer I live, the more I realize the impact of action today on life. Attitude to me is more important than facts. It’s more important than the past, than education, than circumstances and failures, than success, than what other people think or say or do. I am convinced that life is 10% what happens to me and 90% how I react to it.”

Charles Swindoll

And so he said, “And so it is with you. We are all in charge of our attitudes.” And I really, you know, I read that many, many years ago, and I printed it out. I it’s up in my office. That printout is up in my office. I have to remind myself of it periodically, but I really believe in it.

And so I continued my response to Daniel by saying that my spin on this concept is fear versus focus. Rather than get caught up in fear, I try to focus on the solutions. I told him, “I don’t have the specific answers to you, but I do have the questions that you can reflect upon.” I’ve talked to BNI members about the kinds of things they can do online like one to ones, but he was talking a lot about his children and being there with his kid. So I tried to address my question to that. So as you listen to the questions I posed to them, ask the questions of yourself that are relevant to you in your situation, in terms of dealing with whatever life is, whatever, whatever life has handed you, how do you deal with it?

And so I said to him, “These are the questions: How can you deepen the relationship with your children at this time? What can you do to have fun in teaching them when they are studying? Wouldn’t it be a good time for you to study or watch something motivational? Or do one to ones with your BNI members? Can you find something you’ve read or watched as a lesson for your children? Who can you offer moral support to during this challenging time? These are questions that only you can answer and I think the answer to those questions, helps you know how to put a positive spin on it here.”

The bottom line here I told him is that almost all of us are stuck at home, we can choose to make the most of it and come out better and stronger, or we can choose to be overcome by it. I choose better and stronger and to do that I choose to focus on solutions. And when I do, I almost always feel better. So, back to my critic, who I shall leave nameless. I will not name him. He and I live with a completely different worldview. Priscilla, I believe a positive attitude has little to do with what happens to you. It’s more about what you do with what happens to you. Am I giving people false hope?

My answer to him is no. Absolutely not. Hope is hearing that little voice inside you whispering about what can be when everyone around you is shouting what can’t be. I believe that hope is more powerful than fear. Hope plus action leaves fear in the dust have hope to take action. And that’s the way you deal with challenges in life. And that was my message to both parties. Priscilla. Any thoughts?

Priscilla:
Yeah, I love it. I’m definitely an optimist. So even in the worst of times, I try to figure out what could be the lesson or what could I take from this that would help me, and it’s hard sometimes. I mean, you know, especially at first if you get a blow of some kind, you know, it takes awhile to come to terms with it, but I’m always looking for the bright side. Always. Yeah, I do agree with you that it’s more powerful than fear.

Ivan:
You can absolutely can be and, you know, when times are tough, people tend to live in that place of fear, rather than to try and find hope. And look, I’ve been there. There have been times where I’ve been very concerned. And there have been times where I’ve thrown myself a pity party. And you know, I’m here to tell you, at the end of the pity party, nothing has changed and I feel just as bad. So pity parties, I realized a long time ago, they better be short, because you’re not gonna really accomplish anything in doing so.

That’s why I really feel like you got to focus on on what’s potentially positive and in one of my I think it was a special podcast number: “Why?” I talked about how I, when I was diagnosed with cancer, the first thing I did was come up with a list of possible positive outcomes and that everything on that list ended up coming true but I had to focus on what could be possible. And I think that’s that’s the key here. And that’s my goal with this podcast, is you know that there’s a lot of repercussions that are still left yet to be felt from the COVID virus and I really am a believer that hope is more powerful than fear. Any last thoughts or questions, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
No, I think that’s great what you’ve said. I really appreciate it.

Ivan:
Well, thank you Priscilla. And that’s it for today.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Hope plus action leaves fear in the dust. We can choose to make the most of this situation and come out better and stronger.

title.rendered: Episode 788: Room Full of Referrals 10th Anniversary

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dawn Lyons joins Dr. Misner on the podcast to celebrate the tenth anniversary of the publication of Room Full of Referrals, which they co-authored with Tony Alessandra. The Room Full of Referrals® method applies DiSC® profiles to networking by mapping Drive, Influence, Steadiness, and Compliance onto networking personalities.

  • Promoters score high on Influence. They’re fun-loving, energetic, and love meeting new people, and step right into the crowd.
  • Examiners score high on Compliance. They observe the group and make a plan before they take the next step.
  • Nurturers score high on Steadiness. They like to have longer one-to-one conversations at networking events.
  • Go-Getters score high on Drive. They already know who they want to meet, and they move through the room with purpose.

Knowing a person’s networking type is crucial when trying to deepen a relationship. When you know more about people than you do your product, you will be unusually successful in business.

Don’t forget to pick up a copy of Room Full of Referrals if you haven’t already.

Complete Transcript of Episode 788

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Austin, Texas this week and getting prepared for the holidays. It’s great to have the holidays and be with family. And I’m really excited to have our guest on today. And I think you have something to say about our guest.

Priscilla:
I do. Our guest is Dawn Lyons, and she actually was the person who referred, I guess myself to Ivan, and or Ivan to myself. But she brought us together, so that I’m so grateful. Thank you, Dawn.

Ivan:
And that was like, what, 14 years ago? Practically, yeah.

Priscilla:
Right. Yes, 2007.

Ivan:
And I am so glad that Dawn made that referral. Dawn is the co-author with me and Tony Alessandra of the book Room Full of Referrals®. She’s also a BNI Executive Director for 22 years. She started I think, at 18 with BNI, and she has 70 chapters, seven-zero chapters in the San Francisco Bay area [California]. We’re also the co-founders together, Dawn and I and a couple of other people, of Asentiv, an international training and consulting organization in 23 countries. And we’re here today to celebrate the 10th anniversary of our book, Room Full of Referrals. Dawn, my friend, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Dawn:
Thank you. Thank you so much. And thank you, Priscilla, I’m so happy that this connection was made. And it’s still going today. It’s just a testament to the two of you and the dedication. I love it.

Ivan:
A 14-year relationship came about as a result of it. Wow, great referral.

Priscilla:
Thank you.

Dawn:
And Ivan, I was actually, I was a little embarrassed. I actually did the Room Full of Referrals® [method] for our training just last weekend here in Santa Rosa to about 100 BNI members. And I told the audience, Ivan was the one that reminded me that it was the 10th anniversary of our book. So thank you, I can’t believe it’s been 10 years.

Ivan:
My pleasure. And you know, I tell people, Tony Alessandra is probably the world’s leading expert on DiSC® systems – Drive, Influence, Steadiness, Compliance. I’m considered to be a pretty good expert on networking. But you, you my friend, I believe are literally the world’s leading expert on DiSC® profiles and networking. I don’t know of anybody who understands the process of networking as it relates to behavior profiles like this, better than you. And I’m really happy to have done the book with you, and to have you on the podcast today.

Dawn:
Excellent. Yes, so excited.

Ivan:
So tell people, in a moment, about the four behavioral styles. And just, you know, if you’ve got somebody in one of your chapters that does DiSC® behavior profiles, talk to them. Sometimes we get people mad, because we’re talking about DiSC® profiles. Don’t get mad; instead, talk to the member in your chapter that does DiSC® profiles, because they can help you in what Dawn’s going to be talking about here. So talk about the four main major behavioral styles.

Dawn:
Yeah, so there’s four of them. And the key thing is that we’re all a blend of each of the styles. So it just is, it’s about the different intensity levels. And I think that’s the thing that really shows up in people is that high or that really low intensity level. So we’ve got some cool titles for them. We change the titles from the DiSC® titles, just so that they were more appropriate while seeing people networking.

So if you’re at a networking function, and you kind of see this person that’s be-bopping all around, and they’re giving big hugs, and they got big smiles, that’s what we call our Promoters. Right. And our Promoters are just those fun-loving, energetic folks. They love meeting new people.

Ivan:
And in DiSC® they’d be a high I, right?

Dawn:
Correct. Yes, that’s high I, absolutely. And then there’s another group of folks that tend to stand around the outside of the room, kind of observe things, kind of take notice of what their next step might be, and then kind of set a plan for that next step. But they tend to hang out on the outside of the room more often, and that’s what we call our Examiners.

Ivan:
And what would they be in the DiSC®?

Dawn:
That would be the C, the compliance.

Ivan:
High C, yeah. And you know DiSC® and we renamed them for networking styles, and I think it’s really appropriate. So Examiner is the high C.

Dawn:
Yep. And then the high S is what we would consider the Nurturer. And the Nurturer, when you see them networking, they’re networking in more of an individual conversation, right? The Promoters might be talking to 5, 6, 7, 8 people. But the Nurturers are going to be more on that one-to-one, a little bit more intimate. You’ll see them talking closer together even, it’s because they’re really looking to build that relationship and get to know you as a person first.

And then last, but of course not least, normally I start with them because they like to win and like to be number one. And that’s the high D, or what we consider the Go-Getter. And you’re going to notice the Go-Getter networking; they tend to be a little bit more dressed up, maybe than the rest of the folks. They just look good as an overall package. But you’ll see them move through the room with purpose. And they kind of have this goal set, and they want to meet the right people. They want to spend the right amount of time, and then they’re probably going to get the heck out of there because they’ve got other things to do and to work on. But that’s what we call the Go-Getters.

Ivan:
The Go-Getter. Now, I don’t think you mentioned the high S.

Dawn:
That’s the Nurturer.

Ivan:
Ah, the Nurturer, okay. So those are the four characteristics from the “Room Full of Referrals.” The four major behavioral styles, and I can certainly relate to the high D, as probably where I lean. Well, it’s definitely where I lean. And Dawn, you are a Promoter.

Dawn:
Uh, actually, my Go-Getter and Promoter are tied.

Ivan:
Are tied? Whoa. Well okay.

Dawn:
Absolutely, tied in a very, very high segment; I normally come in at either ones or zeros on both of them. I can slip either way. And if I’m with more people, I think my Promoter definitely comes out. So I want everybody to have fun. But, boy, you get me down to business, and I can be down to business in about a half a second.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s true. So why is it important to understand these behavioral styles in building relationships with people?

Dawn:
Well, especially in any referral relationship… I saw this the first time and I told someone, I said, “You know what, that referral relationship right there is not going to work.” And it happened to be Mike Macedonio. And I told him this during a training program where two referral partners were trying to deepen their relationship. And one of the people was a Go-Getter and the other person was an Examiner. And I could feel the stress of the Examiner because the Go-Getter was really pushing forward in a way that the Examiner just was not ready for. And I said, “If he keeps pushing like that, that relationship is just going to explode because the Examiner was not being treated the way that they need to be treated in that relationship.” So anytime we want to build a relationship, we want that strong foundation. But if we understand people, that’s going to change the way we build the foundation. So that’s why I think it’s so critical.

Ivan:
Now, not everyone knows who Mike Macedonio is in relation to you. Do you want to share that?

Dawn:
True. Thank you, Ivan for referring me to what was then called The Referral Institute. Mike is actually today my husband and best friend.

Ivan:
Yeah. Awesome. And so you pointed out why that relationship could potentially, the two people you’re talking about, could be a challenge.

Dawn:
Yeah.

Ivan:
I’m guessing it was. And I’m just curious here, I’m running with this. What did you do because of witnessing that?

Dawn:
Well, that was when I basically shared with him that, “Listen, I’ve been trained in behavioral styles. And if we’re going to teach people how to build referral partnerships, we better be teaching them about behavioral styles so that they can treat people accordingly within the relationship. Otherwise, relationships are just going to keep, you know, fading out because people just aren’t being treated appropriately.”

Ivan:
Yeah, and you’ve got a really good story about the idea of treating people the way they want to be treated, which our co-author Tony Alessandra calls The Platinum Rule – treating people the way they want to be treated. You have one of the best stories and I hope you remember it about two people that you gave referrals to and they gave you a gift. You remember that story? They gave you a gift and one was the way they liked to treat people and one was the way you wanted to be treated. Do you remember that story?

Dawn:
I think so, I hope this is the right one. So I got a referral to speak, and so the woman that thanked me, she thanked me by giving me a huge basket, it was beautiful. It was all full of coffee. And I hate coffee. If you know me at all, I don’t drink coffee, I think it stinks. I just don’t like it. And then I’ve had other people, you know, give me gifts, Mike was one of the first people who gave me a gift, it was the Frommers Guide to Greece, and a Greek calendar. And that was the place that I wanted to go more than anything at that point in time. The interesting part is he had to call a friend of mine to find that out. So he took the time to understand what I really wanted to do versus…

Ivan:
What I love about that story is a big basket probably cost more money than a calendar or the book, but the calendar was something that really interested you, like photographs of Greece. And so this is really what Room Full of Referrals kind of focuses on is treat people the way they want to be treated, not the way you want to be treated. And there, you were motivated by Greece, not by coffee, and Mike took the time to learn that and that was particularly important.

Dawn:
Yep.

Ivan:
So the 10th anniversary of “Room Full of Referrals,” and we’ll put a link here in BNI Podcast. You gotta get a copy of it if you don’t have a copy. It’s a great book. It’s had an amazing reach. Any thoughts about the reach before we wrap up?

Dawn:
Yeah, you know, this book has helped people with not just their business relationships, or their referral relationships, but I’ve had people tell me multiple times, we’ve saved their marriage. So it works all across the span of your entire life.

Ivan:
Well, listen, I’ve been a big believer in behavior profiles for a long time and understanding people. When I was a management consultant back in the early 80s, I learned the DiSC® system. And it’s been very helpful. And I highly recommend it. If you’ve got a consultant in one of your chapters that does it, talk to them because it can really help you in understanding the people that work for you. And if you would like to understand how to network more effectively, pick up a copy of Room Full of Referrals. Any last thought before we wrap up?

Dawn:
One thing my mentor said to me early on: “When you know more about people than you do your product, you will be unusually successful in business.” And I love that and that’s what I want for everyone.

Ivan:
Yeah, great observation. Dawn, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast. You’re a good friend, and it’s a pleasure to have you as part of BNI.

Dawn:
Excellent. Thank you for having me.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you, Dawn. I remember when I attended one of your workshops on the Room Full of Referrals, and it’s a wonderful reminder of that day, all these categories.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Room Full of Referrals shows you how to identify networking types and use them to deepen relationships with your referral partners.

title.rendered: Episode 787: Credibility, the 2nd “C”

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Have you ever referred business to a person you weren’t confident was trustworthy? Of course not. The importance of trustworthiness is closely aligned with our second “C,” Credibility. When you’re networking, speaking from the stage, on a webinar, or during a one-to-one business meeting, make sure you’re showing both your Competence and your Credibility.

BNI members have the opportunity to build Credibility in one another because they spend time getting to know each other.

Exercise

Think of the key people in your network: the top 5 people that you have a really good relationship with. Rate your Competence and Credibility with them. How confident are they in your ability to provide a quality product or service? Make a table with one column for names, one for Competence, and one for Credibility.

If you don’t think that you’re at a 10, it’s really important to sit down with that referral partner and have conversations so that you can get to that point. Ask whether they have any concerns and work to address them.

See Also

  • Episode 771: Work Your Network
  • Episode 772: The First “C”—Competence
  • Episode 774: The Four “C”s

Complete Transcript of Episode 787

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Austin, Texas, where I live. And it’s really been exciting because the last several weeks I’ve been teaching at Concordia University, I’ve been teaching in the MBA program. I’ve been teaching leadership. And I gotta tell you, Priscilla, it’s a blast getting back into the university, and teaching. I’ve missed it and I’m really glad to be doing it. And it’s amazing how the students there have so embraced lifelong learning. And what’s great was, and I don’t think I have said this before, I think I may have mentioned that I’m teaching at Concordia, but it was a referral. We have a BNI member from a print shop, Eric, who is a BNI member representing Concordia, and they have a separate print shop at Concordia University. And he’s a BNI member and he referred me to the School of Business. And I’m now teaching in the MBA program. And he’s been amazing to work with. Eric, thank you.

Priscilla:
That’s great. I love that.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla: 
So tell us a little bit about what you’re going to teach us today, Professor.

Ivan:
Well, today I’m going to talk about the second C in the book that I wrote with Dr. Oudi. The book is called “Work Your Network with the 4Cs, and in Podcast 774, I spoke with Dr. Oudi about the book. And then in Podcast 772. I talked about the first C which is Competence. And in that podcast, I made the case for the importance of competence as the primary driver of referrals in a business practice.

However, competence alone won’t unlock referrals. The second C is Credibility. Have you ever referred business to a person you weren’t confident was trustworthy? I hope not. We both hope not, Dr. Oudi and I hope not. The importance of trustworthiness is closely aligned with our second C, Credibility. Now the 4Cs, for those of you who haven’t listened to Podcast 774, the 4Cs are Competence, Credibility, Clarity and Connectivity. And I’ll be talking about clarity and connectivity in the future. But today is about Credibility. And it’s the quality of being believable or worthy of trust. And trust and credibility are so closely related that some people use them synonymously.

Credibility, when combined with Competence, the first C, will decrease the time it takes for you to reach the confidence point in the Referral Confidence Curve. And the work of social psychologists helps illustrate the interrelated nature of credibility and competence. In their studies, they’ve studied how people evaluate each other. People ask themselves two questions when they meet someone new, “Can I trust this person?” and “Can I respect this person?” And these ways of evaluating others are referred to as the warmth, akin to our use of credibility and competence respectively. Ideally, you want to be perceived as having both qualities.

Now, research about networking behavior and characteristics supports the importance of credibility in this process. Almost 3400 people surveyed in a BNI survey we did all around the world, found that the participants received a list of roughly 20 different characteristics on networking, and they were asked to pick the top behaviors they’d like to see in a great networker. And the survey found that the category of credibility and trustworthiness was high in those characteristics. Trustworthiness ranked ahead of most other characteristics in the survey we did of almost 3400 people. As a matter of fact, one respondent in the survey summarized people’s sentiments best when she said, “It doesn’t matter how successful the person is, if I don’t trust them, I don’t work with them.” And when you refer someone, you are putting your reputation on the line.

Ivan:
For years, I’ve said, when you give a referral away, you give a little bit of your reputation. When you give a good referral, it enhances your reputation; when you give a bad referral, it hurts your reputation. So you have to be able to trust your referral partner and be trusted in return. Neither you nor anyone else will refer a contact to someone who can’t be trusted to handle it.

And this is, I think, incredibly important. In a business context, many people assume they must prove that they’re smart enough to handle new business before potential customers will be willing to part with their hard-earned cash. And while this is true, people are misguided if they don’t also focus on making sure that they demonstrate they are worthy of trust. And when you’re networking, speaking from the stage, on a webinar, or during a one-to-one business meeting, make sure you’re showing both your competence and your credibility.

In the 4Cs book, when we speak about gaining the confidence of members within your network, we believe that means we trust you as a person and that you’re good at your job. The faster you’re able to showcase your competence and establish credibility with a person, the faster they’ll have confidence in you. And of course, the quicker they’ll give you referrals. In some networking groups, the vetting process, particularly in BNI, that takes place before a person is approved in the organization, is a solid step towards earning high credibility and competence score with fellow members. But you still need to take time to really get to know one another at an individual level. Because you can’t expect a referral if others don’t believe you’re credible and competent.

That’s one of the reasons why we meet every week in BNI is to establish that credibility on a repetitive basis. Now, credibility is a key component of a powerful concept in BNI and in a lot of my books that I talk about is the VCP Process. And I’ve talked about it a lot in my podcast. And it relates to the time it takes to gain other people’s confidence in your abilities. Visibility, credibility, profitability, when you’re not at a networking function, you’re invisible. And when you make your first appearance, you’re starting to become visible, and many active people and networking groups in the community complain that despite being seen it most networking functions, this doesn’t lead to business.

And this is because they have a high visibility, but they haven’t established credibility yet. And BNI members meet on that weekly basis as a group, so they can meet individually at meetings, in between meetings, and this presence creates high visibility. And members have the opportunity to build credibility in one another because they spend time getting to know each other. And with the credibility in place, the chances of someone confidently referring business to you leads to profitability.

Having members of your networking group view you as credible is critical for them to pass your referrals. It’s one of the important 4Cs. And we have to emphasize, again, that as you are becoming more visible, you’re being judged on your level of competence. And when you’re visible enough and perceived to be competent enough, combined with established credibility, you’re well on your way to receiving referrals, and the profitability that comes from it. So to gain more referrals and impact your bottom line, you need both credibility and you need competence. Tick these two boxes in the minds of your fellow business professionals and members of BNI, and you’ll be on your way to reaching the confidence point on the Referral Confidence Curve that we talked about in a previous podcast about the 4Cs, and which I’ve talked about in my books, and we talk about in BNI a lot.

So here’s something that I’d like you to do if you’re listening to this podcast. I’d like you to take a moment to rate yourself from 1 to 10 on how you perceive the key people in your network. Let’s say the top five people that you have a really good relationship with. Rate yourself in terms of the first two Cs – Competence and Credibility. Create a simple table and list those names, five names, ten names, and add a column for competence and one for credibility and rate yourself from 1 to 10 on each of those people. Where are you in terms of what they believe to be your competence and most importantly, your credibility? And if you have some room for improvement, I recommend that you reach out to those people and set up a meeting, either in person or virtual, to reconnect and see how they’re doing. And to continue to improve the relationship so that they have a higher level of belief in your ability to provide a quality product or service.

Remember that it’s not your confidence that counts. It’s their confidence in your ability to provide a quality product or service. So how credible are you with them? And if you don’t think that you’re at a 10, it’s really important to sit down with them, and have conversations so that you can get to that point. Because once you are at a strong credibility stage, that’s when you start getting referrals from BNI members. What are your thoughts are, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I think it’s great. I’m wondering, what is the difference? And how can you prove yourself to be credible? What’s the difference between credibility and having confidence in someone?

Ivan:
Well, when someone has confidence in you, that’s when you become credible. So the question is, how much confidence do they have in you? And your credibility is based on their confidence in you. That’s what I mean when I say it’s not your confidence that counts, it’s their confidence. When they have high confidence in your ability to provide a quality product or service, then you have high credibility with them.

Priscilla:
I see. Okay.

Ivan:
And so it’s not your confidence that counts, it’s their confidence. Their confidence determines your credibility.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
And so, if you aren’t getting referrals from somebody that you have a really strong relationship with, you want to sit down with them and talk to them about are there any things that you have concerns about in my ability to do this? To do what I do with your clients or patients or customers? And, you know, whatever concerns they have, that’s what you need to work on in terms of developing credibility.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm. Okay, that sounds good.

Ivan: 
All right. So I’d love for you, if you’re listening to this podcast, to post on here about your thoughts about what’s it take to establish credibility with the people that you’re working with. The second of the 4Cs of the book, “Work Your Network with the 4Cs.” That’s all I have for today, Priscilla. Thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thanks so much.

So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 786: The Butterfly Effect (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 654, first released on May 6, 2020.

Synopsis

The Butterfly Effect is a concept in chaos theory where huge effects can be traced to small causes. Something as simple as the flapping of the wings of a butterfly in a tropical area makes some minor change in the environment, which changes something else, which changes something else, which changes something else, which changes the weather.

In networking, the Butterfly Effect works like this. A BNI member called Ivan to ask a favor. Doing the favor led him to a speaking engagement, which led him to meet Jack Canfield, which led him to join an organization, which led him to meet someone who invited him to Necker Island, which led him to meet Sir Richard Branson.

When you meet someone, you never know who they know–and you never know who those people know. A small interaction can have big effects down the line. Never hesitate to make new connections. They can take you places you never imagined.

Read Dr. Misner’s 2007 blog post, “The Butterfly Effect of Networking.”

Complete Transcript of Episode 654/786

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to the official BNI podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chief visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I’m doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I’m going to share with the audience here that we just spent five or 10 minutes looking on the podcast to see if I’ve talked about a subject that I have done speeches on for years, but we could not find it. And that’s, that’s the Butterfly Effect of Networking. Now, I’ve made reference to it in a couple of podcasts, but I’ve never shared the story, apparently. You know, when you do, we’ve got six–over six hundred podcasts that we’ve done. And we do that many sometimes you don’t realize some of your long-standing stories don’t get out as a podcast. And this is such a great concept that I want to do a podcast on it.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Tell us about it.

Ivan:
Well, it’s a story on how I came up with the concept. And I was thinking about this because I had, just a month or so ago, I just spent a week in the Caribbean on an island called Necker Island. And it made me think of the first time I was there and came up with this concept of the butterfly effect. So I’m going to share it with you here.

This was 2007. I’m on this island in the Caribbean in the great room. A huge room that had a 300-degree view of the Caribbean, there was just a little bit behind us that I couldn’t see. Otherwise, I was able to see the entire Caribbean. It was just so beautiful.

And I had a deadline to write an article for Entrepreneur at the time, my blog, IvanMisner.com. Around September 24th, I think, is when I did the article to this blog, and I was stuck. I had nothing. I don’t know if you write, but sometimes when you write, you’re just completely dry. I couldn’t think of a single thing to write, and I had a deadline to get that out.

So I’m sitting there, completely clueless as to what I’m going to write about and while I’m sitting there, up walks Sir Richard Branson—who owns Necker Island. You may or may not recognize the name, but he’s the founder of Virgin Atlantic, Virgin hotels, Virgin cruise ships, Virgin Galactic—Virgin everything. And he walked by, Priscilla. He looked at me and said, “What are you doing?”

At the time in 2007, it was the only place you could get internet on the island, and I said, “Well, I had to come here to write an article and upload it. I’ve got a deadline today.” And he said, “Oh, well, I understand deadlines. But look, we’re down by the pool in the ocean. We’re having a blast. Come on down and join us. If for some reason I don’t see you, make sure to sit next to me at dinner tonight. I’d like to learn more about this networking thing.” And I said, “Sure!” and he walked off.

I sat there and I thought, “Dang, how did I get here? What brought me to this moment in my life where I’m sitting on an island in the Caribbean and a billionaire walks by and says, ‘Hey, what are you doing? Come on out and play. Come join us. We’re having fun. And if I don’t see you sitting next to me in the lunchroom, I want to talk. I want to get to know what you do more.'”

And I sat down, I thought, “How did this happen? How did I get here at this moment?” And so what I did, well, as I was sitting there, is I reverse engineered how I got there. Who was it that invited me to spend a week on the island in the first place? It was a special seminar that I was invited to, and who was it that invited me? How did I meet them and where did I meet them? How did I get invited to the place that I met them—which was actually the Transformational Leadership Council.

I met the person who invited me to Necker at The Transformational Leadership Council which I’ve talked about many times on my podcast, it’s an organization started by Jack Canfield. It meets twice a year and has transformational leaders and thought leaders. And it was somebody that was in there that invited me, and I thought, “Why did I get invited to TLC? It came from Jack Canfield, he invited me, so how did I meet Jack?”

I reverse engineered it and it went back two and a half years. So, this is 2007. It was somebody I met back in 2004, who asked me for a favor. The person who asked me for a favor was a BNI member, and she was a solopreneur business coach and she asked me for a favor and—we all get asked favors and sometimes they’re really on mission for you and sometimes they’re not. Well, this was something I really wanted to do. She wanted me to do some training material for this coaching company. So I said, “Sure I’ll do it.”

Now, there was no money upfront, but it’s possible down the road and I did it. And that led to being hired to speak for that organization, which led to me meeting Jack, which led to me meeting several people in TLC, one of which led me to be introduced to someone who knew about the seminar on Necker that then got me onto Necker Island. That whole process took two and a half years. And when I reverse engineered it, I realized that this is a great example of a concept called the Butterfly Effect.

The Butterfly Effect is a concept in mathematics called the Chaos Theory. The Chaos Theory is explained through the flapping of the wings of a butterfly. That something as simple as the flapping of the wings of a butterfly in a tropical area makes some minor minute change in the environment, which changes something else, which changes something else, which changes something else, which changes the weather.

And it hit me that what I had just experienced was the Butterfly Effect of Networking—that is that a simple conversation with a solopreneur who is a business coach, led me to meet somebody, which led me to meet somebody, which led me to meet somebody, which led me to meet somebody, which led me to spend a week on Necker Island and get to know Richard Branson.

Having just spent another week as my third visit there, recently the story came back to mind and that became my article that day. You can find the original article on my blog now at IvanMisner.com. If you go back to September 24th, 2007, you can see the original article that I wrote, and I’ll make sure there’s a link here in the podcast.

But here’s the relevance to anyone listening to this podcast; You never know who someone knows, and who they can introduce you to. Who it is that they can introduce you to. Now, that person may not be a Richard Branson, but they’ll know somebody who knows somebody, who knows somebody and if you keep following those connections, it’s like the ripple effect by dropping a pebble in the water. You drop that pebble and there’s a ripple that goes out and that ripple connects you with somebody else, who connects you with somebody else, who connects you with somebody else.

So I know that I mentioned the Butterfly Effect of Networking—at least I’m pretty sure I did, in the podcast called Networking Up that I did here on my podcast. That is part of the Networking Up because when you’re networking, peer to peer or you’re networking with somebody who’s maybe not as successful with you, all too often we kind of think, “Well, that person doesn’t have any contacts that will help me then why would I? Why would I connect with them?”

Well, the truth is, they know people, who know people, who know people and this one simple introduction led to multiple relationships and a lot of very strong friendships, including Jack, who was just on our podcast a few months ago. I met him as a result of this and it’s led to several visits on Necker and had an opportunity to spend a fair amount of time with Branson and I know I’ve talked about him and humility. He’s a very humble person. It’s really quite amazing.

One of our upcoming podcasts I plan on talking about how he is at networking and how he is as a person and how we can all learn from that, because oftentimes, I have found really successful people can be very arrogant. Their ego enters the room before they do, but his does not. I’ve been very impressed with that. So I plan on, in a future podcast, talking about how someone could become a billionaire and not be totally arrogant. What I see as some of the characteristics of that, and I’ll tell you what, I’ll include a photograph but maybe we can put up in the podcast of Richard, my wife, and I on one of our very first visits there.

He invited us to a new island that he just bought Moskuito Island–not like the insect but with a K instead of a Q, Moskuito Island. And he just bought that island, and he invited us for a little tour of the island, which was really quite amazing. I’ll put a photograph of that.

But the Butterfly Effect of Networking is very powerful. Here’s the secret: look for people who are good at what they do. Whether you’re networking down, peer to peer or networking up, look for people who are good at what they do, make a connection, and then follow that connection through the process. It can lead to amazing places. And by the way, when you find yourself in one of those experiences, where you go, “This is what he (Ivan) has been talking about. I’m experiencing the Butterfly Effect.”

It’s really important to pick up the phone and call the person that started the flapping of the wings of that effect. In my case, it was a woman named Kim [who] was a BNI member solopreneur business coach. So two and a half years later after I had worked with her, I called her up and she said, “Ivan, well, what a surprise! It’s great to hear from you.” I said, “I was just calling you to thank you for introducing me to Richard Branson.” And I’ll never forget, she was like, “Uh, hello, Ivan? I don’t know a Richard.” And I said, “Well, let me tell you a story…” And I told her the story about how my meeting her led me to be on Necker Island.

So the last thing I want to leave you with is if that happens to you, pick up the phone and call the person that started the process of your own personal Butterfly Effect. And that’s the podcast for today.

Priscilla:
So I think that’s great. I just have one little thing to say, which is you might want to trace the butterfly effect of meeting Richard and the way that it had on you after you met him, what were the changes?

Ivan:
You know what, that’s a great question. And when I do the next podcast where I’ll talk about some of his characteristics that I think are a really healthy characteristics for any businessperson, I’ll add that. Because he did absolutely have an impact. I thought I was pretty good at something, but observing him, I thought, “Oh, yeah, I could be better.” So I’ll share that on the next podcast I do about Richard in a few weeks.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. I look forward to it. Well, that was some wonderful information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: A small interaction can have big effects down the line. Never hesitate to make new connections. They can take you places you never imagined.

title.rendered: Episode 785: Inviting to BNI is Child’s Play

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Why is a BNI meeting like a child’s birthday party? As Shawn Yesner discovered when his son turned five, the best invitation is a simple one:

  • Hey, I’m meeting with 80 of my referral partners, can you join us?
  • Hey, I’m having a business meeting with a bunch of referral partners. Can you join us?
  • Hey, I’m having a meeting with this specific professional who I know can pass you business. Can you join us?

Remember that you’re inviting them to visit, not to join. Don’t try too hard to explain what a BNI meeting is like–let them experience it for themselves.

Visit Shawn’s website, Yesner Law.

Complete Transcript of Episode 785

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m back in Austin, Texas, Priscilla, I had to get back from the Singapore [BNI] Convention because I’m teaching in the MBA program now at Concordia University. I’m teaching leadership.

Priscilla:
That’s great!

Ivan:
It will help if I actually show up to teach, so… I had to be back here.

Priscilla:
Okay, so who do we have on the podcast today?

Ivan:
We have a guest who I have had on once before. and his name is Shawn Yesner. Shawn is the owner of Yesner Law, a firm that helps its clients have more money at the end of the month rather than month at the end of the money. Love that phrase. And when he started the firm in 2004, Shawn joined BNI Referral Masters [chapter] in Tampa, Florida, and has been a member since then. Shawn has held almost every Leadership Team position multiple times, and was part of the Regional Director Consultant team and professional development team. He’s also been a previous guest on BNI Podcast, episode number 494 – Why everyone should go to leadership team training. Shawn, welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast.

Shawn:
Thank you, Ivan. I appreciate the opportunity and keeping up my streak. I’ve been listening since episode number one.

Ivan:
Wow, that goes way back. And I think one of those early episodes was… nobody knew what a podcast was back in, what was it? 2012, 14, 13??

Priscilla:
2007

Ivan:
2007–oh, my goodness! Okay. Yeah. So I think nobody knew what they were. And so one of the early ones was, “I’m a baby boomer, what’s a podcast?” That had to be funny to listen to it, you know?

Shawn:
Yeah, I enjoy all of them. That’s all I listen to, and I don’t listen to audibles or anything else. Just [BNI] podcasts when I’m driving around in my car.

Ivan:
I’m impressed. Thank you very much. Alright, let’s talk about this. The title is “Inviting to BNI is like child’s play.” But you have a very specific story about your child’s birthday party. Tell us that story.

Shawn:
Yeah. So unfortunately, this lesson comes out of something that is never going to get me nominated for Father of the Year. But I can turn it into a positive. So my son just turned 11. But when he was turning, I don’t know, I think five or six, so five or six years ago, we, my wife and I, had done everything for his birthday party. We had scheduled the location, we got the prizes for the kids, we got the theme, we got the cake, we did everything. We did every single thing that we needed to do for this birthday party, except we forgot one very important thing. We forgot to invite any of his friends to the birthday party.

Ivan and Priscilla: (both chuckling)

Shawn:
And so about a week out from this party, my wife and I start freaking out. And we start calling all of the parents of the kids that were his friends. And for every single one of them and it was you know, literally we had to get in, invite, and get out. So hey, my son is turning five, can you come to the party? Hey, the party is this weekend, can you and your kid come? Hey, here’s where the party is. Can you be there? And the interesting thing is everybody that we invited, said yes. And in looking back on it, that’s when it kind of hit me. It was a very easy, very simple, very direct invitation. And everybody said, Yes. You pointed out, when you were introducing me, I’ve been in BNI now since 2004, so I’m into year I think 19, I’m an attorney, not an accountant, so my math may not be correct.

Ivan:
That’s okay, I got I got the number of years the podcast is going wrong, so you’re good.

Shawn:
So, you know everything in my statistics, I’ve given referrals; I think I’m over $2 million approaching $3 million in Thank You For Closed Business. I think I’m in the top four or five in my chapter in BNI Business Builder (BNI University Online) CEUs, One-to-Ones… I’ve got all those statistics. But the one where I’m weakest is inviting. The one where I’ve always been weakest is inviting. And what hit me is, I was overcomplicating the BNI invitation.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shawn:
And so when I started approaching people and saying, “Hey, I’m meeting with 80 of my referral partners, can you join us?” Or, “Hey, I’m having a business meeting with a bunch of referral partners.” Or, “Hey, I’m having a meeting with this specific professional who I know can pass you business. Can you join us?” When I started doing the invitation like that, people started saying Yes. And what I did was, I turned this into an educational moment, also, for my chapter, and a couple other chapters in my region have asked me to do it.

And what I do, is I say, well, here’s what the birthday party invitation would sound like, if I were doing it as I used to do my BNI invitation, I would say, “Hey, my son is having a birthday party this weekend. Now, his actual birthday was yesterday, but we have the party next week, because that was the timing. And when you walk in, it’s going to be at this bounce house location. And when you walk in there’ll be a table there. And when you get to the table, you’ve got to sign the waiver. After you sign the waiver, you’ll see some bins to the right-hand side, you can put his present in there, although you don’t have to get him a present if you don’t want to get him a present. And then you got to have your kid watch the safety video, make sure he brings socks, and he’s wearing socks, and then… and on and on and on.

Ivan: (laughing)
I just want to repeat what you said. That’s an example of how we overcomplicate invitations to BNI.

Shawn:
Yeah, it would be an analogy to what we would do for BNI.

Ivan:
That’s very funny.

Shawn:
And I realized, when I started making the invitation simpler… and now I am Gold Club badge qualified, I don’t recall exactly how many people have been inducted, but I regularly get a couple of visitors. And I have sponsored at least a dozen or more members into BNI. And so, one of the things you also mentioned is that I was on the professional development team here in the Tampa Bay area. And so I used to facilitate the Member Success Program. And so I’ve taught the ABCs, which I think is now GRIP, the GRIP method of inviting. For me, those are great techniques. But for me, simplifying it made it easier for me to make the invitation in a conversational format.

Ivan:
Wow! What a great example that is. I love that analogy. And I think one of the things that I heard there was that it’s an invitation to visit, rather than to join. Do you want to talk about that?

Shawn:
Yeah, absolutely. It’s an invitation to visit. I never say join; I never say any word that is join or remotely close to joining. It’s just, get them there and then let the meeting sell the meeting. One of the things I’m proud of in my chapters that we are a huge chapter; we’re, I think, we were over 100 at one point, we’re pushing 90 at this point, and let the members in the audience sell the meeting. Let the fact that we have 10s of millions of dollars of Thank You For Closed Business on a rolling one-year basis sell the chapter to the visitor. And you know what? If they don’t join, that’s okay, too, because we’ve had plenty of visitors that have done business with members.

Ivan:
Yeah, listen I agree with everything you’ve said so far. I think members, new members, existing members, way overcomplicate the invitation. And it should always be about visiting, not joining. You can’t talk somebody into joining something they haven’t seen that is maybe a little complicated. It’s not like — put an ad in the newspaper; people understand what that is. You know, if you haven’t been to a BNI meeting, you don’t really know what it is. So visit rather than join. And I think you wanted to talk a little bit about keeping it simple and having it flow naturally in the conversation. You want to talk about that at all?

Shawn:
Yeah. So it’s that whole concept of where I always got flustered, personally, was when I started to use the GRIP method, and you say, “Do you want more business? Do you rely on referrals to grow your business?” It always seemed to be way too practiced for me. So when I simplified it to, “Do you enjoy networking?” Yeah. “Would you like to meet X number of business professionals? (Whatever the size of the chapter is: 20,30, 50, 90, 100, whatever.) “Do you want to meet those business professionals?” Or what I would do is if I was talking, for example, I know that our contractor, our kitchen and bath remodeling contractor, wants to meet architects. And so if I’m speaking to an architect I can say, “I have a Residential Contractor that I meet with every Friday morning that wants to pass you business. Can you be there?” And who’s going to say no to that type of an invitation?

Ivan:
Yeah. I have one little tweak on one. One of the things you said, “Do you like networking?” Some people really don’t like it. But if you tweak it by saying, Do you get business from networking?”

Shawn:
Yeah.

Ivan:
You know, I did a survey of 12,000 people years ago, and it ended up being in a book that I did. And 91% of the respondents said that they had gotten business through networking. 91%! I mean, when have you seen 91% agree to anything ever? So that’s the one little tweak I would make. But otherwise, I love what you’re describing.

Shawn:
Yeah, for me, it’s what works. And that’s what always appealed to me about the BNI system. Not only is this how to invite to the chapter, but it’s also a metaphor for getting a referral for your referral partner. In other words, I’m not going to ask somebody to hire you. But if I can make the introduction, and then let you sell your business, that’s the referral.

Ivan:
Well, we’re about out of time. Shawn, is there any last thought that you have before we wrap up?

Shawn:
Well, the one thing I did want to say… last time I was on the show, I freely admitted that Vice President was my favorite role on the entire Leadership Team. And since then, I had only been on the Leadership Team as either a Visitor Host or on the Membership Committee. This year, our incoming President contacted me and said, “Hey, would you be my Vice President for the chapter?” And number one, I couldn’t turn him down because I have a lot of respect for our current President, Richard Ficca. But the other thing I said to him, “Look, I’m on the record on the BNI Podcast as saying Vice President is my favorite role in the Leadership Team. How can I possibly turn that down now that I’ve said it to Dr. Misner?” So I am currently the vice president of BNI Referral Masters in Clearwater.

Ivan:
Well, condolences. I mean, congratulations.

Shawn:
I still do love the role. It still is one of my favorite roles on the Leadership Team.

Ivan:
Let me tell you, I think it’s one of the most important for sure, no question about it. It can make or break a successful chapter. Well, listen, Shawn, thank you so much for being here. Shawn’s website is www.yesnerlaw.com. yesnerlaw.com. Shawn, thanks for being on BNI Podcast again. I appreciate you.

Shawn:
Thank you for the returning invitation. I appreciate it.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for that great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Why is a BNI meeting like a child’s birthday party? As Shawn Yesner discovered when his son turned five, the best invitation is a simple one.

title.rendered: Episode 784: Nonprofits in BNI?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Joshua Levy, executive director of the nonprofit Joshua’s Stage and president of BNI 360, joins Dr. Misner this week to share his experience in BNI. In less than two years as a member, Joshua’s Stage has received over $15,000, in thank-you-for-closed-business. Here’s Joshua’s take on how membership works for nonprofits.

Giving referrals works just the same as for any other business. Asking for referrals goes beyond asking for donations or introductions to potential donors:

  • Ask for introductions to like-minded professionals who serve the same people your nonprofit does.
  • Ask for volunteers who can help with your programs and services).
  • Ask for people who can serve on the Board of Directors.
  • Ask for people who can provide services like bookkeeping to the nonprofit.
  • When you ask for donations, provide specifics about who needs help and how much.
  • Ask for contributions via Amazon Smile.

Links

  • BNI 360: https://bni360austin.com/
  • Joshua’s Stage: https://joshuasstage.org
  • Support Joshua’s Stage on Amazon Smile: https://smile.amazon.com/ch/81-1971449

Complete Transcript of Episode 784

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am still in Singapore at the BNI Convention. And there’s still a little bit of time. If somebody wants to join online live this week, you can do so. And anyone listening to this – if you have a chance to go to a BNI Convention, you gotta go. It’s amazing. It’s like going to a United Nations event where all the countries love each other.

Priscilla: (laughing)
That sounds great. So tell us who’s coming today.

Ivan:
I have a guest His name is Joshua Levy. Joshua is the Founder and Executive Director for Joshua’s Stage, a nonprofit organization, based right here where I am in Austin, Texas. Joshua’s Stage is all about enriching the lives of special needs individuals with activities including theatre arts, improv, music, dance photography, create a story showcase. He features after school programs, camps, workshops, online programs, individual enrichment programs, and a whole lot more. He’s been a proud member of BNI for going on two years, and he is the President of the BNI 360 Chapter. Joshua, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Joshua:
Thank you, Ivan. Great to be here.

Ivan:
It is great having you here. And I love nonprofit organizations. My late wife and I have supported nonprofits throughout our entire 31 years of marriage. And she and I created the BNI Foundation, which is now operating all on its own and supporting children in education. So we love to see nonprofits in BNI. So talk a little bit, Joshua, about fundraising, because I think nonprofits are great in BNI; they just need to know how it works and why it works.

Joshua:
Absolutely. I am delighted to be a BNI member as a nonprofit organization; super excited with our chapter supporting Joshua’s Stage. Just being in it [BNI] for almost two years through, whether it’s donations from chapter members, business sponsorships, also referrals, specifically for special needs families, the children who participate in our programs, we’ve received over $15,000, in thank-you-for-closed-business in just less than two years. And for us, that’s absolutely amazing, and we’re very humbled by that.

Ivan:
That’s fantastic. I’m really, really pleased. And I think nonprofits don’t recognize the many different benefits that they can get, besides donation. So are there a couple of other ways that you’ve gotten benefits out of BNI?

Joshua:
Absolutely. We have opportunities through the referrals where I have the opportunity to meet other like-minded professionals in the Austin area who are in the field that Joshua’s Stage is in, which is serving special needs families. We’ve had opportunities for members to volunteer with our programs; we’ll continue that as well. So there is a myriad of opportunities in addition to not just asking for funds week to week. So we have many asks.

Ivan:
So tell me how referrals work for a nonprofit versus for profit, which you know, most members are a for profit business; how does it differ and how is it the same?

Joshua:
Yeah, I’ll start with the same. The essence of the referral, for me personally, I love giving. I know that is BNI, the Givers Gain. I love giving, I speak truth when I say when I give a fellow chapter member or even a member who’s in a different chapter a referral, I literally jump up and down. I won’t do that now. But believe the theater that we do Joshua’s Stage I take that to heart that means a lot that I’ve been able to help out a fellow member whether it’s myself or Joshua’s Stage, making the referral for or on behalf of somebody else. That’s just amazing. Then it’s the same as a for profit.

I would say the small nuance with the difference is when we do ask for a donation –  in our case, when I receive a phone call from a family who might say to us, we would love to participate in your program, we are struggling on the finances, is there something you can do to help? We say yes. I’ll come to the chapter and say, “Hey, we just received this phone call, and they asked for help. I am looking for some superheroes to help this family out to me in our program.” And they’ll ask how much and even last week, even today, we had several superheroes from our chapter help those families out.

Ivan:
So what are some of the ways that your fellow chapter members have been involved with Joshua’s Stage?

Joshua:
We have a lot of involvement from our chapter members. In fact, one of our chapter members is the chair of our Board of Directors. So in technical terms, that individual is my boss. So the nonprofit reports to– the Executive Director role reports to the Board of Directors. So that’s pretty neat. More than two of our members are business sponsors; we have sustaining donors in the chapter. Joshua’s Stage is on Amazon Smile; several members contribute to Joshua’s Stage via Amazon Smile. Our Social Media Marketing Agency person in our chapter is our social media for Joshua’s Stage. It’s phenomenal. The bookkeeper in the group keeps the books for Joshua’s Stage. Several members have had their children participate in our programs, especially in the summertime.

Ivan:
So I mean, this is amazing, what a great list. And it always it surprises me when nonprofits say, “Well, I don’t see a fit for our nonprofit being in a referral group like this.” And then I hear you talk about member after member after member who supports your organization through service to the organization, as well as the thousands of dollars that you’ve been given. What a great story! More nonprofits should be involved in the organization.

Joshua:
Absolutely. I wholeheartedly agree. I will advocate for BNI, for nonprofits; for nonprofits, for BNI. It works absolutely both ways. I am passionate about what I do. And I bring that passion the best that I can every week to our weekly meetings, and present that in either through stories of our organization making a direct positive impact on particular participants. And also how, as I mentioned earlier, chapter members, funds, or others from the community can also directly positively impact immediately. And as a nonprofit, whatever the cause and the mission is, bringing that passion weekly, that is what it’s all about. That is that essence right there. And those chapter members start to hear that. Bringing it home in the One-to-Ones is especially wonderful. In fact, earlier this spring during a One-to-One, the person I was with said, “Oh, I’m going to do this right now.” I didn’t know what she was talking about. She leaned back, took out a check, and wrote a donation. And that was the first time that’s ever happened. I was like pinching myself if I was dreaming. So it’s truly, truly a blessing. I’m humbled to absolutely encourage nonprofits to be a part of a BNI chapter.

Ivan:
Yeah, you never know when that person is going to write a check out.

Joshua:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Building those relationships. So Joshua, as a nonprofit organization in BNI, what would you say is the biggest impact on Joshua’s Stage so far?

Joshua:
Just several months ago, our Managing Director of our [BNI] region Becky Isbell, she referred Joshua’s Stage to the director of a special needs private school, in a town called Killeen, Texas, which is about an hour north of Austin. I went out to meet with the school, talked about what we do, talked about their school, got to know each other. And we were both very excited for a potential opportunity. And just a couple of months later, so now that was mid-August, we started a weekly program where our team travels to Killeen, and we run their theater program once a week on Wednesdays. It will be culminating pretty soon in a showcase for the school and the community. And it is like Zen, it is like having this as an amazing opportunity. So we’re making an impact for our mission. We’re executing the mission weekly in Killeen, Texas, with these great, wonderful participants, awesome team members coming. And that’s what it’s all about. And without BNI, I can tell you that likely, that opportunity was probably either not going to happen or maybe long off in the future. So this is a truly humbling experience.

Ivan:
Well, again, I’m really glad that you’re in the program. I’m gonna ask you a question here. You may not have been prepared for this one, but I’d really like to hear it. And it doesn’t have to be a BNI story, but give me, we’ve got about a minute, give me a one story about an impact that your program has had on a family or an individual. And it doesn’t have to be related to BNI at all. I want you to just tell a story about what you do and how it’s impacted someone.

Joshua:
Yeah, a few months ago, we were running one of our programs that happened to be on a Saturday. We had ages… I think our youngest at that program was six years old, maybe the oldest was 10 or 11. There were about four to five participants with a wide range of special needs. We had just started with arts and crafts for the first 10 minutes of the program; the participants were coming in, some of the families stayed to be in the background. We call them angels, by the way; they’re the angels on the stage in the background. So the families that stay can go grab coffee and run errands.

So I was checking in with families as our team was helping the participants conduct the arts and crafts, and one of the parents was appearing to be somewhat quiet. She had perhaps some, I don’t know, water, maybe tears in her eyes. And I checked in and said, “Hey, how are you?” Then as we were chatting, she said to me, “My son (who was in our program) has been in various other programs, whether they’re sports or enrichment and whatnot. And he was asked… they had said, “You know, we don’t think it’s a good fit.” And the reason was, he’s nonverbal in his communication, as far as verbalizing his wants or needs, may not have been the fit, perhaps, the organization was describing.

I was, as I am now, very touched by that, and somewhat like, Wow, I don’t even know, I can’t even imagine. I am a parent, we have two kids. And I can’t imagine being asked, ‘I’m sorry, this isn’t working.’ I looked at her and I said, “The vision came in 2015 for you.” I don’t know what else to tell her. I said, “Of course he’s welcome here. I mean, he seems to be having a blast. He’s smiling. He’s wearing Superman costumes.” In fact, this was the second program they did with us. I said, “I could see no reason like that to ask in our program, that would happen, that we say or go in that direction.” I was saying, “This is absolutely for you. This is why the calling that I have was put to me.”

Ivan:
That’s what makes it all worthwhile, isn’t it?

Joshua:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Well, listen, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast. www.Joshuasstage.org is the website. That’s Joshuasstage.org.  And Joshua, email me your Amazon Smile link. I believe it’s a special link that connects, I think. If you send that to me, I’ll make sure that goes in here as well. Thanks for being on BNI Podcast.
(Amazon Smile link for Joshua’s Stage: https://smile.amazon.com/ch/81-1971449)

Joshua:
Thank you, Ivan. It’s great to be here. Thank you very much.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much to both of you for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
