title.rendered: Episode 783: Are You Passionate about Your Business?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

LuAnn Buechler joins Dr. Misner to talk about why passion is the key to your success in both business and networking. If you want your customers to love your products and services, you’d better love them, too.

When you’re networking, loving your business, your employees, and your customers helps you to become an attractor drawing people toward you instead of a tractor dumping your sales pitch on them.

In the absence of passion, there can be no success.

Michael Gerber

Links

  • LuAnn’s Website: https://luannb.com
  • Episode 635: Love Is Good Business
  • The Passion Test (website) (book)

Complete Transcript of Episode 783

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m out and about. Last week, I was in Charlotte at the [BNI] Global Headquarters. And this week, I am in Singapore. Just getting ready for our big international convention. It should be exciting.

Priscilla:
Oh wow. In person, huh?

Ivan:
It will be in person AND streamed online. Both.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
If you haven’t signed up, you can still sign up online; go to BNI.com. Priscilla, I have a really good friend, I’ve known her for years, who is my guest today – LuAnn Buechler. And LuAnn is known as the Little Spark, and I know why. She’s a Certified Facilitator of the Passion Test, and the Passion Test for Business, and she uses that in her coaching practice to ignite the spark in you and your business. LuAnn is the co-author of Exceptional Care for Your Valued Clients, and ihug: My Journey as a Hugger. She’s an avid networker, BNI Chapter Success Coach, BNI member for 18 years, and a member of the Transformational Leadership Council [TLC]. I invited LuAnn into TLC and she’s been there for a long time. Her favorite quote is “Do What You Love, In Service to People Who Love What You Do!” [by] Steve Farber, and I know you’re gonna mention Steve. Welcome, LuAnn. Welcome to BNI Podcast.

LuAnn:
Thank you, Ivan. I’m so excited to be here.

Ivan:
And I’ll share, because you do talk about Steve Farber a fair amount, and for those of you interested, Steve was in episode number 635 of BNI Podcast, and it’s very congruent with what LuAnn is going to be talking about today. So tell me, LuAnn, what the world does passion have to do with business?

LuAnn:
I love that question. And first of all, we have to talk about what does passion mean? Often people think of it differently than the true meaning of the word. And it’s really simply boiled down to those things you love the most. Those things you love the most?

Ivan:
Yeah, very true. Go ahead.

LuAnn:
And you better love what you do, right?

Ivan:
Yeah. If you don’t love what you do, you’re gonna be miserable. I mean, one of the things I always told my children is, you know, they would ask, “What major should I do?” And I’m like, “I don’t really care. Just do something you love. That’s what’s most important.”


LuAnn:
I remember a story that you told me, Ivan, when you were a professor, I can’t remember what university it was. But you said that a student asked you, “Well, should I do X? Or should I do Z? Which one’s gonna make me more money?” And you said, “Which one are you passionate about? Which one do you love the most? That’s where you’ll make the most money.” Right?

Ivan:
Do you remember what they said to me?

LuAnn:
No.

Ivan:
I said, Do what you’re passionate about. And they said, “No, no, that wasn’t my question. My question was what business should I go into? Where do I have the biggest chance for success?” And I said, “Well, but I’m asking you what, what are you passionate about?” And they actually looked at me and said, “You are not listening to me.” And I said, “No, you’re not listening to me.” And then I went ahead to say exactly what you’re saying here.

LuAnn:
Yeah, yeah. And, you know, society leads us down that path of chasing after the money, instead of chasing after what we love, and I encourage people to chase after what they love, and what they’re passionate about. And when you’re doing that, and you step fully into that, you naturally attract the right people to you that are also passionate about those same things and are aligned with you. And after all, as Steve says, “Love is just good, damn good business.” And if you want your customers to love your products and services, you better love them, too.

Ivan:
Yeah.

LuAnn:
And you better love your employees because your employees are the ones that are going to love all over your clients to keep them around.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. You’ve got a great quote from a good friend of mine, Michael Gerber. You want to share that quote?

LuAnn:
Ahh, yes. In the absence of passion, there can be no success.

Ivan:
Explain your thoughts on that.

LuAnn:
And so if you don’t feel passionate about it, you can make millions of widgets, but if you don’t feel good about that, then it doesn’t feel like success.

Ivan:
Yeah, you can even have money and still not feel like you’re successful. Michael [Gerber] is 100% right with that.

LuAnn:
I talk to many of people like that. I talked to a gentleman who was a six-figure income executive in the Twin Cities [in the US state of Minnesota] and found himself totally unfulfilled by his work, and realized that he had been working so hard, he lost connection with all of his family members. And he really had no self-identity anymore; it was so wrapped up in whatever that J-O-B was that was paying the bills. And that’s just no way to live.

Ivan:
No, it really isn’t. And I have a good friend here in Austin, Stacy. Stacy had a six-figure job making good money and she was not happy, and started a nonprofit–clearly not making the same kind of money–[she] started a nonprofit, but is just happy as can be as to the difference that she’s making in people’s lives.

LuAnn:
We want to make a contribution. We want to make a contribution in the world and know that we’re making a difference. And that’s what we all want. And this is the way to find it.

Ivan:
How does this relate to becoming a great networker?

LuAnn:
Well, you’ve got to be passionate about what you do, as I said, first. You attract the right people around you, the right clients, the right team members, the right strategic partners. And when you’re networking, and you’re talking about your business, you need to be excited about it. Get other people excited about it. If you’re monotone, you know, come across like everything is drudgery, well, who wants to hang out with that? No. We want to hang out with people that are excited and enthusiastic about what they do. And that comes out of passion.

Ivan:
Yeah, so talk about being an “attractor” rather than a “tractor.” I love that term.

LuAnn:
That’s a really good one, right? So when we put that energy out, which really is our authentic energy, we attract the right people that often carry the same core values, are excited and passionate about the same things. So we want to be an attractor, not a tractor. And you’ve talked about this for years, right? You don’t want to go into a networking event and dump all over people, what you do and how you can help them. You want to listen to them when you’re in a networking environment. And it’s only the beginning of the relationship, not the end. So you want to attract those people that want to come over and talk to you. And then, add in that “be a good listener.”

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. Good networker has two ears and one mouth and uses them both proportionately.

LuAnn:
Yeah, I love that one. I use that all the time to remind people.

Ivan:
I love that phrase be an “attractor” rather than “tractor.” Great, great concept. [BNI Chapter] Education Coordinators, share this with your members.

LuAnn:
Right.

Ivan:
So how do you apply passion in the workplace?

LuAnn:
So we start by helping companies to identify their core values using the work of the Passion Test, and it’s helping them to identify their top five passions for their business. Most people turn that language back into core values. We see core values and passions just a little bit different. Passions are action based; they have an action, a verb, with them that puts them into action. You must implement them into the workplace. Oftentimes, you’ll see workplaces that just have one word like “We’re professional.” Well, I hope we’re all professional. How do you implement that? How do you live that in your organization? [That] is what we help people to do. And then it becomes a decision-making tool. We say in the Passion Test that whenever you’re faced with a choice, a decision or an opportunity, consistently choose in favor of your passions. So now your core values, your passions for your business, are a decision-making tool for everybody in the organization. And as long as you make decisions in alignment with those core values, then it’s always going to be a good decision, if you can justify them in the core values.

And it’s the same way we use core values in BNI, right? Like if we want people to live and walk and breathe those core values every day. And if we have a concern with anything, we bring people back to that discussion. Hey, that situation was not aligned with our core values. Let’s fix it. So it also gives people this foundation for the conversation. So it’s never pointing fingers, or he-said she-said, or he-did, she-did. It’s hey, let’s face this back in our core values. And is this a good decision of what we’re about to do or did do, and usually everybody can come to the same conclusion. Ah, yeah, it obviously wasn’t, or it obviously was, in alignment with the core values. So it helps open up a communication that then ultimately creates culture in your organization of how we live. And I love our BNI core values and creating that culture in our BNI chapters using the core values every single week.

Ivan:
Yeah, core values are the core. They are literally the core of the foundation of culture within an organization. You mentioned a couple of times the Passion Test. Do you want to mention the book that that comes from briefly?

LuAnn:
Right. So the book is The Passion Test: The Effortless Path to Discovering Your Life Purpose, written by Janet Bray [Attwood] and Chris Attwood, and they are my mentors in this work. So it’s a best seller out there, anybody can put their hands on it. But I’ll tell you one quick thing. Never, ever do the Passion Test alone. It’s not really a test. It’s a process that I facilitate people through to help them uncover their top five passions. And when you try and do it alone, your logical mind gets in the way, and this is a heartfelt conversation, not a logical conversation.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s a good point. I’ve done the Passion Test and the Passion Test for Business, and I agree completely.

LuAnn:
If I might quickly compare it to something other people might know better, which is the Strengths Finder.

Ivan:
Yep.

LuAnn:
And the concept of the Strengths Finder was that we should identify people’s top five strengths, and play to those strengths, and let them live in their strengths. Like you say, Ivan, live in your flame, not in your wax.

Ivan:
Yeah.

LuAnn:
We all have weaknesses. Stop trying to fix those weaknesses and live in your strengths. And the same is true with your passions, right? The passions are that flame. So I like to help people live in their flame and not in their wax.

Ivan:
Definitely. Well listen, we’re about out of time. I know you wanted to say something about your experience in BNI. Can you do that? And then I’ll share your website.

LuAnn:
Okay, so I love to tell people the story of how I got started. And “Givers Gain” is the only two words that had to be said to me. I was invited once, and with that philosophy in mind, and I went, I want to live like that. You mean rather than being that tractor and dumping out there trying to sell myself, all I have to do is help other people? And then I will naturally gain in return? That sounds so much more pleasant to me. And so it is the Givers Gain philosophy that lives in my heart. Thank you.

Ivan:
And you’ve been a member for almost two decades now. And I’m really glad you’re still part of the organization. Thanks for being on BNI Podcast, LuAnn. I appreciate you.

LuAnn:
Thank you, Ivan. I appreciate you as well.

Ivan:
And LuAnn’s website is www.luannb.com. Thanks so much. Luann. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I think that’s it for this week, and I want to thank you both for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: LuAnn Buchler joins Dr. Misner to talk about why passion is the key to your success in both business and networking.

title.rendered: Episode 782: The Gratitude Effect (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 650, first published on April 8, 2020. Some links and references may not be current.

Synopsis

This week, Dr. Misner wants to present some hard facts about gratitude and what the scientific research shows about its positive impact.

  • Harvard Medical School reports that people who express gratitude are more optimistic and feel better about themselves personally.
  • The Yale Center for Emotional Intelligence concluded that expressing gratitude completes a feeling of connection with other people.
  • Paul Zach, a professor at Claremont Graduate University, states that neuroscience shows that recognition has the largest effect on trust, especially when it’s tangible, unexpected, personal, and public.
  • UC Berkeley used fMRI scans to show that people who wrote gratitude letters had a better activation in the medial prefrontal cortex than those who did not write the letters.
  • Studies by the Cicero group, published in Forbes, found that people who are on the receiving end of gratitude have a 33% increase in their innovation and a 22% increase in work results.

Gratitude improves attitude. It improves feelings of connection, and it improves results. It’s not New Age, it’s science.

In BNI, if you don’t have a referral, you give a testimonial. Testimonials are about expressing gratitude in a specific and public way.

Here’s how you can start to practice gratitude today. Many people have helped us during our lifetime. Have you acknowledged them? Have you thanked them? Have you recognized the difference that they’ve made for you?

The gratitude effect doesn’t take much effort and it costs nothing, yet it makes a difference in yourself and the people around you. And when you acknowledge people in this way, people are drawn to you like a magnet because it accelerates the relationship process.

Complete Transcript of Episode 782/650

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla.  I wanted to just say one quick thing about your opening line, which I love. And I do it because I want to show people that I’m not just sitting around in an office all the time, but I’m traveling and visiting a lot of chapters, more and more personal. And I think this is so cool. People just walk up to me wherever I’m at, and they just say, “Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?” And I absolutely love that because I know they’re listening to the podcast – and more and more people are using your line. And I’m grateful for you for using that line.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. Well, it seems to work. So I get to find out where you are every week. That’s fine. Yes.

Ivan:
And today’s topic, I think is appropriate because it’s, it’s about the gratitude effect. And, you know, I recognize that when some people hear a phrase like, you know, we should have an attitude of gratitude. They’re thinking yeah, yeah, more New Age cycle, but we want hard facts. And so what I wanted to do with this podcast is to give you some hard facts that I think are important. And, and they’re from some pretty reputable sources who argue convincingly about the science of gratitude’s positive impact. So I’m gonna give you a few, and then talk a little bit about it.  I’m happy to talk to you if you have any questions, Priscilla.

So here’s the first one. Pretty reputable source, Harvard Medical School. They recently reported that there have been multiple studies showing that people who express gratitude are more optimistic and feel better about themselves personally. There was a paper published by the Yale Center for Emotional Intelligence which concluded that expressing gratitude completes a feeling of connection with other people. Something I’d say is pretty important and building relationships and being that’s a parenthetical comment from me that –  that last comment that it’s important in building a relationship.

And here’s another one. Even neuroscientists argue that the gratitude is effective. Paul Zach, a professor at Claremont Graduate University states that neuroscience shows that recognition has the largest effect on trust. I’m going to repeat that because that is all about big neuroscience shows that recognition has the largest effect on trust, especially when it’s tangible, unexpected, personal and public. Tangible, personal and public. Again, that’s Paul Zach that came from the Claremont Graduate University. UC Berkeley, in your neck of the woods, Priscilla, conducted fMRI scans. They’re a form of MRI scans on Individuals who wrote gratitude letters and compared them to the MRI scans of people who did not. And they found that people who wrote gratitude letters had a better activation in the medial prefrontal cortex than those who did not write the letters. Now the medial prefrontal cortex responds to things like nicotine, drugs and alcohol. In other words, showing gratitude lights those things up as much or more than alcohol or drugs, which is like a real, positive, safe way to get those feelings in my opinion.

So, studies by the Cicero group that were published in Forbes found that people who are on the receiving end of gratitude have a 33% increase in their innovation and a 22% increase in work results and they stay with your organization longer than those who are in companies who do not have a practice of appreciating their people.

So, so much for psychobabble. Gratitude improves attitude. It improves the feelings of connection. And it feels and it improves results. It’s not New Age, it’s science. And the gratitude effect works when people win or works when someone is coming from a place of being grateful and acknowledging people along the way. This means that it’s important to take time to notice the good things that you might be taking for granted, like so many other principles of success.

It’s simple, but it’s not easy meaning that it’s a simple concept. But it’s not always easy to apply regularly in your life. It’s not easy because the easy thing is to notice what’s wrong, what you don’t like what annoys you or the problems that you face. What I’ve learned over the years is that if you focus on problems you become a world class expert on problems. And it’s hard to show gratitude when you’re obsessed with the problems around you.

However, if you focus on solutions, you can become a world class expert at solving those problems. And this process begins by recognizing what’s around us. rom that starting point, we can be grateful for the elements and begin to acknowledge those things around us and the efforts that people are making. The gratitude effect requires a long, lifelong journey of developing our ability to be grateful. And I mean, think about BNI. It is built into the infrastructure of BNI that one portion of the meeting is about giving referrals or giving testimonials.

And remember, it’s not the “I Have” portion of the meeting. It’s not how many CEUs you have, or how many 1-2-1s. It’s the referral part of the meeting. We only have one part of the meeting for our mission, and that’s the referral part of the meaning. If you don’t have a referral, you give a testimony. BNI testimonials are all about expressing gratitude in a specific and public way, which is exactly what I think was what the university, Claremont Graduate University, talked about. So expressing gratitude completes the feeling of connection with others.

Here’s how you can start this practice today. Many people have helped us turn our lifetime. They’re all in our story. Have you acknowledged them? Have you thanked them every recognize the difference that they’ve made for you? Many members of your BNI chapter have helped you in some way. Maybe it’s a referral, maybe you can give them a testimonial. That’s a great way to do it. Maybe it’s that they’ve helped you because you needed advice in some way. If you listen to this podcast, pick up the phone and call them.

Priscilla:
Send them an email.

Ivan:
Send them a note, express your gratitude for something that another big member has done for you. It’s not psychobabble, it’s science. It’ll make you feel better. It’ll make them feel better. And it’ll have an incredible impact. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I really enjoy thanking people for what they do on an everyday kind of regularity. I mean, just small things that people do, if you just say “Thank you so much,” it makes a big difference to how they feel and how you feel. And I also really suggest people smile, because the other part of it is, you know, to give off this positive energy towards another human.

Ivan:
Yeah, and that’s important, and you have to really sometimes think about that. You know, my natural state is not necessarily with a smile. I think I picked that up from my dad. I’m afraid people will look upset, and he was fine. And if I’m, you know, my face at rest is not smiling. And so I really have to try to focus on making sure to smile. But when you’re with other people, I think that’s really so important.

Priscilla:
I think this is a great message that you’re giving us here.

Ivan:
Thank you. I appreciate it. You know, I think the gratitude effect doesn’t take much effort and it costs nothing. Yet it makes a difference in yourself and the people around you. And when you acknowledge people in this way, people are drawn to you like a magnet because it accelerates the relationship process. And, and so I just want to wrap up by saying thank you, Priscilla. You’ve been the host of this podcast now for many years. And I think since 2008, so like 12 years, you’ve been doing it an incredibly long time. You’ve done a great job. And, you know, this podcast wouldn’t be what it is without you, and I wouldn’t have people coming up to me from all over the world saying, “Hi, Ivan. How are you and where are you?”

Priscilla:
Well, that’s great. Well, I get to say thank you to you because you are just like a super coach. And it’s so wonderful to have this connection. And to be a part of this process with you. I just love it.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. I appreciate that. And I would love for you if you’re listening to this podcast to send an email, send a message. Thank somebody. Thank somebody here on the podcast. Wouldn’t be great if you just publicly thanked a BNI member right here on BNI Podcast for something that they did for you? I’d love to see those. I read every message that gets put up there. So let’s put something up there. And, and that’s it for today. Priscilla, thank you so much.

Priscilla:
Oh, you’re so welcome. Well, I just like to let all of you know that the sponsor for this podcast is www.ivansinnercircle.com. And Ivan has assembled a wide variety of topics and has invited you to participate. So go online and check it out at ivansinnercircle.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: There’s solid scientific evidence to support the positive impact gratitude has on our lives and our businesses.

title.rendered: Episode 781: Make a Difference in the World

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The BNI Foundation supports children and education through its Business Voices™ Initiative and the Givers Gain® Grant Program. Becky Isbell, BNI Managing Director at BNI Global, joins Dr. Misner this week to share three ways BNI members can get involved with their communities and support these efforts.

As a Donor

Donors are the people who will write a check to the BNI Foundation and say “Do what you can with this check.”

As a Doer

These are the people who get out and do things in the community by joining a Business Voices team and using their time, talent, knowledge, and experience to meet children’s educational needs.

As a Door Opener

Door openers are the people who make the introductions and referrals to connect educators to the Donors and the Doers. All BNI members act as Door Openers for other BNI members.

More About the BNI Foundation

  • BNI Foundation Website
  • Episode 43: The BNI Foundation: What Every Member Should Know
  • Episode 79: BNI Foundation Monthly Heroes
  • Episode 119: BNI Foundation Awareness
  • Episode 395: Business Voices
  • Episode 571: The BNI Foundation: What’s in It for Me?
  • Episode 625: Cause Networking

Complete Transcript of Episode 781

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
You know, this week I did another Facebook Live, which was a global event. And I love doing the Facebook Live events because I get to talk to members all over the world. And I started doing them again earlier this year. I love doing them. If you ever see on my Facebook page that I’ve got one coming up, please come and join us because I really enjoy doing them. And I enjoy answering questions that BNI members and directors have.

Priscilla: 
That sounds like fun, actually.

Ivan:
It is fun. It’s part of the fun stuff that I get to do it at this point in my career.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, who do you have with us?

Ivan:
I have Becky Isbell on the podcast today. Love Becky. I’ve known Becky now for a number of years. She’s a Managing Director for the BNI US CORE, which is the BNI global regions. She currently manages three regions that consist of 116 chapters, almost 2600 members. Becky’s business model is simple. If you love what you do, you’ll never work a day in your life. And I gotta say, Becky is a close personal friend. I really enjoy spending time with her. And it is fantastic, Becky, to have you on the BNI Podcast. Welcome.

Becky:
Thanks, Ivan. I always enjoy getting to spend time with you, whether it’s on a podcast or in person. We always have fun together, right?

Ivan:
We do, we do. And sometimes there’s a glass of wine involved.

Becky:
Most all the time. (chuckling)

Ivan: (laughing)

Becky:
And I’m trying to work that into our core values, like that should be our unofficial core values. We’ve got to have fun together. Right?

Ivan:
Absolutely. You got to keep the fun in the fundamentals.

Becky:
That’s exactly right. And we get to talk about one of my favorite things that I enjoy doing, which is the BNI Foundation and Business Voices, today.

Ivan:
Yeah, and making a difference in the world. You know, one of the things that I have said for many, many years is that you may not be able to make a world of difference, but you can make a difference in the world.

Becky:
Absolutely. And you know, our theme in BNI this year is We’re Better Together. And so when you and I were chatting, it’s like, we ARE better together, and together we CAN make a difference in the world. And you had a vision in 1998 that started, which now we call the BNI Foundation, that definitely has made the world a better place. So if you could tell us a little history about the BNI Foundation. How did it get started?

Ivan:
Well, you know, Elisabeth, my late wife and I, who you knew very well, Becky, in 1997-1998, we were talking about, the business is finally starting to really grow. You know, I joke around about being a 20-year overnight success. But it took 20 years for BNI to be, really, a global enterprise, successful. And it started right around 1998, where we were doing pretty well, and Elisabeth and I always felt like we had an obligation to put back into the communities that we drew from. We had a personal obligation to support the communities that we drew from.

And so we decided to, in 1998, create the BNI Foundation, which originally, here’s a bit of trivia, was originally called the BNI Misner Foundation. And it was actually part of a community foundation in California, the California Community Foundation, fund under the California Community Foundation. Today, BNI [Foundation] is a full 501(c)(3) charitable foundation based out of Charlotte, for those of you outside the United States, that means it’s a charitable foundation. And it operates out of Charlotte, but it started in 1998. And we decided that our focus would be children and education. And we did that with the help of the Board of Advisors. BNI – the International Board of Advisors helped us come up with that theme because you know, a foundation… there’s so much that needs to be done in the world. We had to pick a theme, and our theme was children and education. So that’s sort of the history of how the Foundation got started.

Becky:
I love it. And when I first met Elisabeth many years ago, she had me at education. Because, as you know, both my parents, I come from a background of educators. And I have the most, utmost respect for educators because on average, an educator spends $1,000 a year funding their classrooms. And so when I met Elisabeth, and we were talking about this, I thought – this speaks to my heart. Because I’m going to do a quote that she always says, that I say, in my weekly presentation with BNI. It’s “Children are 20% of our population, but 100% of our future.” And that speaks to me. And so when people ask me, Why are you so involved with the Foundation? That’s the reason; it’s for our future. And I’m so glad that you and Elisabeth saw the vision, to have children and educators, that’s how we wanted to pay back.

Ivan:
Yeah. Just you know, Becky, not in all countries, because this podcast plays worldwide, believe it or not, not all countries have that situation where the teachers spend that kind of personal money. There are a number of countries where they spend nothing; the government covers it all, which is nice, but they have other issues.

Becky:
Right.

Ivan:
There are other issues. And so in each country, whatever issue you have related to children in education, we want to help focus on.

Becky:
Absolutely, and I love it. So, we have the BNI Foundation, which is great. We do the Givers Gain Grants, which is a $1,000 [USD] grant that we give. It is a grant that you apply for, just like you apply for BNI membership, you apply for it. But Ivan, as you know, as much as I love the Foundation, I get really fired up about Business Voices. And if you’ll allow me, I’d love to tell you some stories about what we’re doing here in Texas with Business Voices.

So one of the biggest questions I get asked is, okay, we understand what the Foundation is, but what is this Business Voices thing? And this is where we really get to get involved with our community, just like you said, Ivan, your vision was how can we give back to our community? We will always accept donations. But it’s even better when we can get out in our community and give back to our community doing a project. And so I think it was, Ivan, correct me if I’m wrong, 2013-14 when Elisabeth had the idea, like let’s take this to the next step, where we actually get chapters working out in the community.

Ivan:
Yeah, it was about at that point. Yeah. And she came up with the term Business Voices.

Becky:
Yeah. And so it’s getting us involved in the community. I say us – as our BNI chapters, our families, getting us involved was the education theme in the community. And so I will tell you, here in Texas, we really embraced it. And we’ve got chapters who go out into communities, and it’s easy. Find something tied to education, children, and do a project there, do a service project. One thing that we do here in Texas is we call it “Seats and Feets.” So we do a huge sock and underwear drive for the local school districts and the homeless shelters here in the central Texas area. And last year, we’re still doing it, we’re collecting to the end of this month. But last year, we collected over 25,000 pairs of socks and underwear. And that is just BNI members asking each week – come bring a package of socks and underwear. And the impact that we have had is so huge, because that’s something that children need, they need those socks and underwear. So this is just one of the many things that we’ve done.

And Ivan, I don’t know if you remember, about 10 years ago we hosted a circus that came to town. And we were able to provide children who may not have that circus experience, we were able to buy tickets and send them to the circus. And we also, it was a two-for, because we got to do that, as well as raising money for the BNI Foundation. And so we do have a website. It’s the BNIfoundation.org that has [information about] how you can get involved with the Foundation or Business Voices.

And I always say that we, as our community and BNI members, fall into one of the D categories. There’s the three Ds that I talk about. There’s the Donors, they are the people who will write a check and say do what you can with this check. There’s the Doers, which falls into my passion, which is the Business Voices projects that we do. These are the people who get out and they want to do things in the community. They want to help the educators, they want to help the children. And then the third D is the Door openers. This is what we do in BNI, wouldn’t you agree, Ivan? We open doors, we ask for referrals.

And so I’m still a member of a chapter and I represent the BNI Foundation in my chapter. And oftentimes my ask for the week is I would like a warm introduction to so-and-so, and this is the person, the door opener, who can get me into that school, or the door opener who I need to meet an educator, how can we help an educator? And every week my BNI chapter or BNI members around me come through and they’re able to open those doors for me. So that’s the three Ds I love talking about – the Doers, the Donors, and the Door openers. And with all that together, Ivan, wouldn’t you say that we could be better together and make a difference in our world?

Ivan:
Yes.

Ivan:
Oh, there’s no question about it. And I love the concept of the three Ds; I’ve never heard that before – Doer, Donor, Door opener. What a brilliant concept and that perfectly fits the BNI Foundation. You mentioned www.BNIfoundation.org. If you want information on Business Voices, when you go to BNIfoundation.org, there’s a tab across the top called Business Voices just click on that. This is where sort of the rubber meets the road, wouldn’t you say, Becky? I mean, this is where chapters can actually do an activity to support the community.

Becky:
Absolutely. And 100% of the time, it’s the other core value we talked about, which is fun. You’re having fun, you’re giving back to your community, and to me, it’s a win-win for everyone.

Ivan:
And, you probably know this, Becky, but most of the members may not. Elisabeth came up with the idea of Business Voices based on a conversation that we had on Necker Island with Richard Branson. Do you know this?

Becky:
I have heard this story, but let’s share it with our audience.

Ivan:
Well, he talked about the fact that, he talked about the “B-Team”, the Business Team, and how business can make a difference in the world and how business is able to act quickly if they just act. And from that, Elisabeth came up with this concept, literally on the back of a napkin in San Bernardino, California. Right before she was about to be introduced, she came up with this idea of Business Voices. [She] showed it to me on the back of a napkin, I said, “Wow, that’s great.” Then she walked up there and introduced the idea, and we fleshed it out afterwards. And that became the program that we see today, where chapters really take action locally to do things, like you have done. And Becky, you’ve been an incredible leader in the Business Voices and BNI Foundation program. And I appreciate you very much.

Becky:
Well, thank you. It truly is a passion of mine. Again, coming up with educators and grandparents and aunts and uncles; I was the first person in my family not to go into education. But it truly is a passion. And when you’re passionate about something, it becomes easy. And again, fun. And I just I love anything that we can do to help our children and our educators.

Ivan:
Well, in many ways you are an educator, just within the business community. And I appreciate that. Any last thought before we wrap up?

Becky:
Just thank you for your time today. Again, if you have any questions – for our listeners – please visit the website www.BNIfoundation.org. There’s everything about the Foundation Business Voices on there. Or if you want to reach out to me personally, you can, and my email is available if you want it. But I’d love to help. I’d love to see, like maybe even a nationwide Business Voices day that we could do, and truly make an impact on our world because we are better together.

Ivan:
I love that. And I agree completely. BNI Foundation is, in a way, BNI’s best kept secret. So please feel free to share with all BNI members this podcast. We have made a difference in the world, all over the world, and have contributed millions of dollars to causes all over the world. And by the way, just for the record, at this point, the administrative fees, the percentage that’s taken out of any donations, are zero. Because the BNI organization pays all administrative fees.

Becky:
I love it. So when you give a dollar, the dollar goes right back out into our community.

Ivan:
That’s exactly right. Becky, thanks for being on BNI Podcast. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Thank you guys, both, for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Ivan:
And can I just jump in and say all the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You’re welcome, Ivan. Thank you.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Whether you’re a Doer, a Donor, or a Door Opener, you can do your part for Business Voices and the BNI Foundation.

title.rendered: Episode 780: Launch Love Worldwide

content.rendered:
Synopsis

BNI Director of Global Marketing Sam Spuhler joins Dr. Misner this week to discuss Launch Love Worldwide, a global campaign designed to inspire and motivate BNI directors and members around the world to launch new BNI chapters. As part of this campaign, Sam and his team have developed training, digital assets, and other tools for directors to get their core groups off the ground, start new core groups, and get members involved.

As of the time this episode was recorded, 650 new BNI chapters have been launched in 54 countries in 2022.

When a new chapter launches, nearby chapters flourish and thrive as well. When you see a chapter launching in your area, help them—because they’ll help you.

October 20th is World Launch Day. Visit the Launch Love website to join the celebration and get access to the tools and trainings.

Complete Transcript of Episode 780

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m actually in Austin this week. But I’m kind of excited, because it’s been 16 or 17 years since I’ve taught at university. And when it rains, it pours. I’ve been asked to teach a course on leadership in the MBA program at two universities that start this week, Concordia University in Austin and the University of LaVerne in Southern California, which I’ll be doing. So I’m really excited to be teaching again.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great.

Ivan:
Somebody told me, “Ivan, you’ve always taught.” But to be teaching at a university again, it’s really good.

Priscilla:
Right. That’s so cool. So who do you have with us today?

Ivan:
I have a great young man, Sam Spuhler. Sam is going to talk about a launch program in BNI called Launch love Worldwide. Sam Spuhler is the Director of Global Marketing at BNI global. He has supported growth-oriented activities around the organization for the past three years, most notably the Product Manager of the BNI Online program through 2020 and 2021, which was obviously critical to the organization. Now Sam is leading the Global Marketing Team into 2023 and beyond.

And he’s here today to discuss Launch Love Worldwide, a global campaign designed to support directors and members around the world to achieve the chapter launch goals in the organization. You know, if you’re a BNI member, you might be wondering, why should we support launching chapters? But the truth is, I have found that when there are a number of chapters throughout a community, it is easier to bring in members because there is a name recognition and a brand recognition that exists. It’s actually way harder, and Sam you may not know this, it’s harder to open up a BNI chapter in a community where nobody’s ever heard of BNI than it is to open up in a community where BNI has a presence or has a nearby presence. And so talking about launching chapters is, I think, an important topic for all chapters of BNI. So welcome to the BNI podcast, Sam.

Sam:
Thank you very much, Ivan. Really excited to be here. I get a lot of energy listening to your podcasts and learning from you. It makes perfect sense that you are restarting your pursuit as a professor. I always am learning from you, always learn from your podcast. And I am just thrilled to be here to offer a little bit of information myself. So thank you for having me.

Ivan:
Thanks. And I’m excited to be teaching the leadership class. And maybe I’ll pull in a few things into the podcast from what I’m teaching with leadership.

Sam:
I would love it.

Ivan:
So although this campaign is scheduled for a set period of time and it’s about launching, the concept, I think is evergreen—everyone listening to this can apply it years from now. So with that said, why this campaign? Sam, why are you implementing this in BNI?

Sam:
Yeah, absolutely. The team has been working and doing a lot of research on, you know, names and themes for a Quarter Four 2022 campaign. Specifically we wanted to inspire and motivate our directors and members around the world to launch chapters because launching chapters at BNI is such a special thing. Our team feels that we, in a way, overlook it sometimes, and what possibilities it can create in communities and for small business owners and their families. So Launch Love Worldwide is a campaign, and really a mindset, that celebrates human connection and the opportunities that these BNI chapter launches, these just astounding events that happen all over the world—it’s a celebration and a recognition for what these launches can create. So we’re really excited to provide some tools and resources in the forms of training and digital assets and other ways for directors to get their core groups off the ground, to start new core groups, and to get members involved. As well as getting excited, really understanding what that possibility is and what it really means to be a part of a BNI chapter, particularly a new BNI chapter.

Ivan:
So talk a little bit about the importance of launching chapters. And you mentioned something earlier about igniting communities to me as we were talking. Talk to me about that.

Sam:
Yeah, yeah. And so, you know, I was just looking at some stats. So just, again, I reference that these chapter launches happen all over the world, we have had in the BNI, system 54 countries so far here in 2022, at the date of this recording, who have launched at least one chapter. And so that is just really astounding to think about the impact and the footprint that BNI has around the world. And in these 54 countries, that totals up to over 650 total chapter launches, in just this year alone. Again, at the time this podcast airs those numbers will be higher than the ones I just cited.

But that alone is just so inspirational to me, and to a lot of others on the Global Support Team. We got a lot of energy thinking about that. And really, you know, and you can speak to this as well, Ivan, but when a chapter launches, chapters nearby within the same region, flourish and thrive as well. And we know that from a mindset standpoint, it’s an abundance mentality. The more chapters that launch, the more [members] not just in that very chapter that benefit, but members of other chapters in the area can benefit as well, because the network deepens and strengthens.

Ivan:
Well, you’re so right. You said it with abundance versus scarcity mentality, I have seen it over and over and over again, when there are a number of chapters in an area that support each other, and that’s the key, they’ve got to be there to help one another and support each other, they do better by working together. Because if there’s an opening in one chapter, they refer them to the other chapter. Let me tell you a story. Sam, I bet you’ve never heard this story—an absolutely true story. Someone called me once and said, “What do you think about two chapters meeting in the same venue on the same day?” And I said, “No, I think that’s a bad idea.” And they said, “No, it’s a great idea.” And I said, “How could that possibly be a great idea that two chapters meeting the same location on the same day?” They said, “It works incredible.”

And I sat there and I listened to this story about how people would come in, they’d walk down this hall, and on the left would be one chapter on the right would be another chapter, two different visitor hosts, one of them would ask, “Who invited you today?” and they would say who invited them, and then they would refer them into that chapter, so they would go into that room. Now as they start to go into that room, they’d find out if that profession is already represented. And if that profession was already represented in the chapter on the left, they would walk them across the hall, and I went there and I saw this, I personally saw this, where they would literally walk them across the hall to the other chapter. And they would go to the other chapter, meet with the other chapter, and join that other chapter because [in] the first chapter that profession was already taken.

And to me, this was the best example of an abundance mentality versus a scarcity mentality. Now, I don’t necessarily recommend that chapters meet in the same location at the same time. But wow, that just blows me away as to the support that two chapters can give one another, and what a difference it makes. And so when you see a chapter launching in your area, help them because if you help them, they’ll help you. What do you think of that?

Sam:
I love that. I hadn’t heard that story before. On one hand, I think it’s similar to you, I’m sure, I think that’s crazy. But you know, hearing the outcome, it makes perfect sense. And so that is just a testament to Givers Gain. And, you know, that’s really – I started this off, it’s a mindset thing. And we want to really celebrate that human connection, but also just inspire others to realize what’s possible. I’ve always been a big believer that all limits are self-imposed.

Ivan:
Yes.

Sam:
And this campaign is just one step that we are taking as a Global Support Team to realize the potential and the opportunity. So I know we’re coming up on time here, Ivan. But I want to just pass it over to you really quickly. What are a couple of your key observations about, maybe some of your favorite launch related stories similar to the one that you just shared, that members and directors can benefit from, as we’re wrapping up here.

Ivan:
Well, in addition to the story that I told, I have seen many chapters who have a chapter nearby, and they volunteer to be sort of their sister chapter and help them launch their group. And then what happens is, those two chapters then work together to refer people back and forth. So if one person shows up to the chapter, they can’t join, because of a conflict in classification, they refer them to the other chapter, and the other chapter does the same for them. And so by working together, you end up growing together, and this is a great example of abundance versus a scarcity mentality. And the truth is, you can’t change the way the world does business, unless you impact the world in a greater way. We have hundreds of thousands of members; we need millions of members to really, really change the way the world does business.

In the past 12 months from this recording, we generated 19 billion US dollars in thank you for closed business. That is amazing to me, I think we can be doing 100 billion. But in order to do that, we need to bring more people into the program who increase the amount of thank you for closed business that is generated in the organization. With that said, Sam, we’re almost out of time. Any last comment before I give a website out for everybody?

Sam:
Yeah, just really one. This has been a lot of fun, too. I know the thematic word of my team has just been fun. We are really trying to have fun with this campaign. Promote fun around the organization. Because growing is fun. And launching is fun, it’s a really special thing. So thank you very much for giving me this platform to discuss with you. And hopefully this is a fun endeavor for all of those listening as well.


Ivan:
Hopefully. And I have said for years, Sam, you may not have heard it, Keep the fun in the fundamentals.

Sam:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
It’s really important. There is a website. This website may not be available for years and years, but it’s available now. It’s www.growwithbni.com/launch-love-worldwide. We will put it in the transcript here. Sam, thank you so much for being here today. And thank you for everything you do for BNI.

Sam:
I really enjoyed it. Thanks, Ivan.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you guys, both, for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
