title.rendered: Episode 773: Garage to Global® (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 641, first published on January 15, 2020.

Synopsis

BNI has had 35 years of consecutive growth. This was partly because they have a great concept, but also because of the strategies Dr. Misner used to take BNI from Garage to Global®.

In this week’s episode, Dr. Misner shares 12 lessons he’s learned on this path.

  1. Learn to work on your business, not just in your business. (Read The E-Myth by Michael Gerber if you’re not familiar with this concept.)
  2. Create systems, write everything down, and teach people what you’ve written down.
  3. Reverse-engineer your goals. If you have a goal for the end of the year, where do you need to be at the end of each month between January and December?
  4. Delegate. You have to delegate both responsibility and authority. Everyone you delegate should have 95% authority in that position.
  5. Know your numbers. Get daily reports on your key success factors, and get as granular as possible.
  6. Do six things a thousand times, not a thousand things six times. For BNI, it’s adding members, adding chapters, filling chapters, and telling stories. Be a dog with a bone. Persistence is a superpower.
  7. Surround yourself with great people. Pick up the book Who’s In Your Room?
  8. Ignorance on fire is better than knowledge on ice. As a new business, you can’t afford to hire knowledge on fire. Hire people with a great attitude and teach them.
  9. Culture eats strategy for breakfast. Create an amazing vision statement, know your core values, and create a great company culture. Start with your first employee.
  10. Know your mission. Get really clear about what your organization’s mission is.
  11. Work in your flame, not in your wax. Do the things you’re passionate about, and hire people for the stuff you hate doing.
  12. Share a vision that everyone is striving towards. BNI has dominated its industry in almost every market for decades because of a shared vision and a shared implementation of that vision.

Complete Transcript of Episode 773/641

Priscilla Rice
Hello, everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you doing today?

Ivan Misner
I am doing fantastic. It’s a great start of the year and I’m excited about what this year will hold for us.

Priscilla Rice
Great. Tell me a little bit about this episode title. “Garage to Global®.” What is that?

Ivan Misner
Last week we talked about the big origin story, how BNI got started. And I mentioned that in December of 1985–I started BNI in January of 1985, and in December I reflected on the year and how it went. And I was really surprised at how BNI had really struck a chord in the business community. And that was the point at which I sat down and created a plan to scale BNI.

And so what I want to do today, I actually have a webinar and you mentioned that Ivan’s Inner Circle is the sponsor for the podcast and by the way, 100% of the proceeds from Ivan’s Inner Circle goes to the BNI Foundation–100% of the proceeds I receive go to the [BNI] Foundation–and I did a very lengthy webinar series, I mean two one-hour webinars on this topic. So you’re going to get in 10 minutes, some of the stuff that I talk about in the two hours, but it’s available on Ivan’s Inner Circle. (Limited time offer – may be expired.)

I talk about the things that I wrote down that I didn’t necessarily learn in college that really helped me to scale BNI. And let’s talk about scaling BNI. We now have over 9000, over 9300 chapters in more than 70 countries.

But here’s the really amazing thing, Priscilla. We have 35 years of consecutive growth. There are not too many companies in the world that can say that they’ve not had one year of downward growth. 35 years of consecutive growth. In management theory that growth graph is what’s called a J curve. “J” like the letter J. It just goes up. And we have 35 years of consecutive growth in the organization.

I think it’s because of a great concept but it’s also about the strategy that we’ve used. And so I want to teach some of the lessons that I’ve learned in scaling my company. And just so you know, we plan on writing a book on this. I have a potential co-author. And we’re planning on writing a book, “How to Take Your Business from Garage to Global,” but let me give you some of the concepts. I hope to give you 12 concepts here if we have enough time

First, you have to learn how to work on your business, not in your business. If you have not read the book, “The E-Myth” by Michael Gerber, pick it up. It’s one of the first things I did right around that time was I read “The E-Myth” and learned about working on the business not just in the business. You have to learn how to be an entrepreneur, which is different than knowing how to do something.

The second strategy that I employed was creating systems. You have to have systems and processes, and then you have to write everything down–this is all part of the second one–to write everything down. And then you have to teach people what you’ve written down. Because learning is a leaky bucket process, stuff leaks out, unless it’s written down and done consistently. That’s number two, create systems.

Number Three. We all know about creating goals. And I think goals are extremely important. But what we’re often not taught is the importance of reverse engineering your goals. So whatever goals you set, you have to reverse engineer those. So let’s say you’ve set a goal for this year, 2020. What you want to do is you want to reverse engineer that. Where do you want to be at the end of the year? Where do you want to be in November? Where do you want to be 10 months into the year, nine months into the year, eight months into the year, seven months into the year. You want to reverse engineer that month by month so that at the end of January, you look at your goal and compare it to actual, and that tells you right away, how are you doing? And what happens is people set goals and they’re, you know, four, five, six months into it and they say, “Oh, let me look at me look at my goals. Ooh, man, I’m way off.” And that’s way too late. And so reverse engineering your goals is a critical element of scaling your business.

Number Four: Delegate. Now, everyone talks about delegating. I’m going to give you two concepts in delegating, that you may or may not have heard of. When you delegate, you have to delegate not only responsibility but authority. Delegate responsibility and authority. That means someone who is responsible, but they also have the authority to make the decisions. Now, people get worried that they’re going to make the wrong decision. I tell you now, don’t worry about that. They will make the wrong decision. And you just need to be prepared to coach and guide them when that happens. Now a new person, you know, a learner, you don’t give them full authority, give them partial authority. But as they learn the job more and more and more, you give them more and more authority, so that everyone in every position should have like 95% authority on that position. And there’s a little piece that they’ve got to come to you on. If you don’t do that everybody and their mother in your company is going to be coming to you asking what they should do. And as soon as that happens, it’s like throwing a monkey wrench into the gears of the scaling company. It doesn’t work. So delegate responsibility and authority.

Number Five: Know your numbers. No, seriously. Know your numbers. I can tell you on a daily basis, how many chapters we have in the organization and how many members we have in the organization. I get a daily report for the number of [BNI] chapters and the number of members. Now, you’ve got to get as granular as possible. If you’re a new business, you might not be able to get that report on a daily basis, but you should be able to get a weekly report on your numbers. Monthly, I think, is almost too long. You should at least have a weekly report. But as you grow and you get more employees, you should get a daily report. And the numbers you want to track are your key success factors. Your KSFs, or KPIs, key performance indicators, same thing, the key numbers that will determine whether you’re going in the right direction or the wrong direction. And you’ve got to know them cold, better than anyone else in the organization. That’s number five.

Number Six. You’ve heard me talk about this one, Priscilla. I’ve talked about it a lot. It’s, I think, the biggest failure that most businesses have. If you want to be successful in business, do six things 1000 times, not 1000 things six times I think businesses get caught up in what I call bright shiny object syndrome. They’re constantly chasing bright, shiny objects instead of focusing on doing six things 1000 times, and by the way, it doesn’t have to be six, it can be five, it can be seven, you know, it’s a handful of things over and over and over again. In BNI, it’s what we call “three plus one.” It’s adding members, adding chapters, filling chapters, and telling stories. Those are the key performance indicators for chapters. And engagement is a critical element for local chapters. So I would add engagement for a local chapter. So that’s a handful of things you got to do over and over and over again.

I have a superpower. Priscilla, would you like to know what my superpower is?

Priscilla Rice
Yeah, definitely.

Ivan Misner
I’m a dog with a bone.

Priscilla Rice
I’ve noticed.

Ivan Misner
If I have any superpower at all, it is persistence, and you’ve got to take your superpower, whatever it is, and put it to work. But one of the things that you’ve got to be consistent with is persistence. If you do six things 1000 times, you are way more likely to be able to scale your company. And if you’re constantly chasing bright, shiny objects… Oftentimes entrepreneurs are the big thinkers. And so they keep coming up with ideas, which sends their employees scurrying all around trying to do different things. If you’re an idea person, rein it in a bit. Surround yourself with people who will help you rein it in because it’s about doing six things a thousand times.

Which brings us to Number Seven, surround yourself with great people. Pick up the book, “Who’s in Your Room?” I wrote it about a year ago. It’s a fantastic book about the people that you surround yourself with. I wish I had that book–I wish somebody else wrote that book and I read it in 1985, when I started BNI, because they’re the lessons I learned in surrounding myself with the right people. Pick up a copy of “Who’s in Your Room?”

Number Eight–and you’ve heard me talk about this one as well, Priscilla. Ignorance on fire is better than knowledge on ice. It’s all about surrounding yourself with people who are on fire. They’re excited. Now the ideal thing is knowledge on fire. But if you’re a new business, you can’t afford knowledge on fire. It’s too expensive. So you got to find employees who are willing to learn. You’re willing to teach them. They come in at a reasonable price, you teach them, and they’re on fire. I was the poster child for ignorance on fire when I started BNI. I had no idea how to network. I method-acted my way through the first year of BNI. I figured it out as I went, but I was on fire. And you can make mistakes if you’re excited, and you know, when you make a mistake, you fix it as quickly as possible. But look for people that are on fire – that is critical.

Number Nine: Culture eats strategy for breakfast. Your organizational culture is critical to your success. Create an amazing vision statement. Know your core values. Create an organization with amazing culture and start when you have one employee, not when you have 10 employees. You want to start immediately because I’m here to tell you if you don’t create your company’s culture, your employees will, and it may not be the culture you’re good at. And so start early in creating organizational culture.

Number Ten. Know your mission. Get really clear about what your mission is. Know what your job as an organization or as a business is. The mission of BNI is to help members increase their business through a structured, positive, and professional referral program that enables them to develop long-term, meaningful relationships and referrals. So you know, that’s our mission. We know our mission, we stay true to our mission, it’s important to know that.

After you know your mission comes Number Eleven. Work in your flame, not in your wax. I’ve talked about this on BNI Podcast a lot. It’s so important that you work in your flame, the things that you’re excited about, that you’re passionate about. Hire people to do the stuff you hate doing. Now, sometimes you gotta do what you gotta do to get to do what you want to do. I understand that. So sometimes you have to work in your wax, but as you grow, hire people to work in your wax. It’s their flame, your wax, and you work in your flame. That’s number 11.

And Number Twelve. You’ve got to share a vision in your company that everyone is all striving towards. That’s number twelve. In BNI it’s “Changing the Way the World Does Business,” and we are clear on our mission. And if everyone is focused on the same thing, it’s amazing what can happen. If all the people in an organization row in the same direction, that organization can dominate any industry in any market against any competition at any time. BNI has dominated this industry in almost every market against all the competition for decades now. And it’s happened because of a shared vision and a shared implementation of that vision.

And so that’s a very short lesson on how I took BNI literally from my garage to a global organization. If anyone’s interested in even more information on that, check out Ivan’s Inner Circle. I’ve got a couple of hours’ worth of content on this very subject.

That was like drinking water from a fire hose there for you, Priscilla.

Priscilla Rice
Wow, that was amazing. I am interested in hearing more about that, because what a phenomenon to go from one chapter to almost–what is it almost, we’re over 9300 chapters now, right? Almost 10,000 chapters. That’s incredible.

Ivan Misner
By the way, I’ll just tell you one quick thing. I had a vision in 1985–actually it’s probably early ’86–that we could someday have 10,000 chapters. People said to me, you’re dreaming. And I said, “Yes, I am.” And here we are. Today, our 30th anniversary. Within the next year or two, we will have over 10,000 chapters worldwide.

Priscilla Rice
Wow, that’s so neat. Well, I think that’s it for this week, Dr. Misner. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares 12 lessons he learned in 35 years of consecutive growth as BNI went from Garage to Global.

title.rendered: Episode 772: The first “C”–Competence

content.rendered:
Synopsis

As Dr. Misner mentioned in Episode 771, the first “C” in Work Your Network with the 4Cs is Competence. Competence is a prerequisite for Credibility, the second “C” in the book and also the “C” in “VCP” as well, in terms of referral passing. But it’s important to remember the distinctions.

  • Just because you’re credible as a person doesn’t mean that you’re competent at your job. This is why testimonials at BNI meetings are so incredibly important because when you can describe how competent somebody is, it increases their credibility in everyone’s eyes.
  • Competence comes first in the business context, whereby credibility often comes first in a larger social context. Even someone who doesn’t like you might refer you if you are good at your job.

Get your copy of Work Your Network today at https://amzn.to/3uXa6Hk, and stay tuned for future episodes about the remaining three Cs.

Complete Transcript of Episode 772

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am headed to the south of France for a large BNI event. And then I’m gonna take a little bit more time off while I’m in France. But yeah, I’m going to an event there in France. It’s been a few years since I’ve been able to go, and I’m excited to meet the members and directors from France again.

Priscilla:
Yeah, that sounds like so much fun. Well, what is the first C? Tell us about that.

Ivan:
Well, the first C is all about Competence. And this is a follow up to the podcast that I did, called “Work Your Network” [episode 771]. And if you may recall, the 4Cs are Competence, Credibility, Clarity, and Connectivity. Dr. Oudi is a [BNI] Director in the United Arab Emirates and this was a concept that he brought to me and that we worked together and came up with his book, “Work Your Network With The 4Cs.” And you’ll be able to get a link to it, to Amazon, here on the podcast site. I’ll also be having Dr. Oudi come on a future podcast; I’m really looking forward to having him participate.

So competence is really about laying the groundwork for your efforts in BNI. Credibility and competence kind of go hand in hand in business, like they do in social interaction. But there is an important distinction. In terms of business and referral passing, you can have competence without establishing credibility, but you can’t have credibility without being competent. In the business context, competence trumps credibility. That’s right, competence comes first. No one refers people who they don’t believe are competent–ever. Competence can lead to credibility, but credibility can’t lead to competence, which is interesting. You know, you might have some credibility, but it might be from somebody who they themselves aren’t credible. And so they go hand-in-hand. Competence is a prerequisite for credibility in terms of referral passing. Just because you’re credible as a person doesn’t mean that you’re competent at your job.

A helpful way of thinking about the interaction between competence and credibility can be further understood by considering this hypothetical situation, and a personal one about Dr. Oudi. Imagine that you’re a real estate agent and your kids play football, also known as soccer, with one of Oudi’s kids. He knows nothing about your business. He only knows you from hanging out at the football/soccer field. To Dr. Oudi you seem like a nice, reliable person. He believes you have good intentions. You’re an admirable individual, good character. You could say from a credibility point of view, you would score high [with] Dr. Oudi. You’re so trustworthy in his eyes, that he would entrust you with his children and playing soccer. One day because he’s running late to pick up his son from football/soccer, he asks you to watch him. Why does he feel completely comfortable with asking you because of his perception of your credibility? This is credibility in its purest form you feel comfortable trusting the person on a personal level.

However, just because he trusts you doesn’t mean he trusts you’re good at your job. Just because he trusts you with his kids doesn’t mean he will trust that you can handle a commercial real estate referral from a very important contact of his. So as important as credibility is in our interactions with others, when it comes to doing business with others competence needs to be established first and foremost.

So let’s explore what happens when Dr. Oudi discovers that you are competent. That really kind of changes everything. Dr. Oudi has dinner with a dentist friend who happens to know you well. Over the course of the evening that dentist speaks about how good you are at your job. Suddenly you become credible to Dr. Oudi based on the endorsement made by someone Dr. Oudi knows. This is how powerful even a single endorsement can be when it speaks to your competence. This is why doing testimonials at BNI meetings are so incredibly important, because when you can describe how competent somebody is, it increases their credibility in everyone’s eyes.

So there are two subtle distinctions to be made here. The first is that although it was credibility of the dentist that led you to reach a level of credibility in the eyes of Dr. Oudi, it was information related to competency that allowed Dr. Oudi to believe you were skilled enough at your job to do business. So you can say it was the information on competence from a credible source that did the trick. The more we demonstrate competence, the more likely our reputation will help us become credible, and ultimately earn the trust of others. It’s one of the reasons why people who are on the [BNI chapter] Leadership Team, and do a good job, and they’re really competent, that people feel like “they gotta be good at what they do, they gotta be incredible because they’re so good as a leader in a chapter.” That kind of thing, it rubs off, it impacts how people look at you.

The second distinction to be made is that competence comes first in the business context, whereby credibility often comes first in a larger social context. Now, you may be wondering what this suggests, but after all, you know, people do business with people they know, like and trust. So let’s break down this commonly used expression with what we discovered about competency while Dr. Oudi and I were doing this book.

Despite the fact that people can do business with those they DON’T know, people absolutely prefer to do business with people they do know. Especially when the kind of work involves interaction with another person, as opposed to a vending machine. Here, it’s important that you know the person, but it is not enough to know their name and few other details. What’s key is to know their level of competence. Because people do business with you when there is certainty surrounding how good you are at your job. People also tend to do business with those they like, especially when there will be multiple interactions with the person again and again, when the relationship is not just a transactional relationship, but it requires time being spent on a two-way communication.

People do business with people who are good at their job, and if they like you, that’s a bonus. People may not do business with you if they don’t like you. But if you’re competent, they might send you a referral, hoping that you might get along better with them than you did. Finally, people do business with those they trust. Indeed, this is very true. But the question is: what is trust referring to? Perhaps it’s meant to imply that people do business with those that they trust; that they’re good at their job. They could be referring to the competence as trust. Competence, or your ability to do something well and effectively, is critical to reaching the confidence point that I talked about in the previous podcast.

In our context with the book, competence relates to how you conduct yourself professionally, how well you do at your job, whether members of your networking group perceive you as competent — that will absolutely directly influence their willingness to refer you. For instance, Dr. Oudi would consider your competence before referring his patients to you; he’s a chiropractor. If he doesn’t know you well enough to determine if you’re a good real estate agent, then your chances of getting a strong referral are diminished.

Competence is our first C. It’s crucial to securing strong referrals and achieving business success. Gaining referrals hinges on how other people perceive you. Their name is on the line, literally, on a referral. Their name is on the line when they offer a referral as much, if not more than the business professional they are referring. Confidence will not be reached if you do not consistently display competence. It’s essential for competence to be demonstrated before the referrals start flowing your way. Now, although the importance of competence can’t be overstated, alone it may not be the magic bullet to helping you reach the competence point on the referral competence curve.

So in one of our next podcasts, probably not the very next one but one of the next ones coming up, I will be talking, hopefully, with Dr. Oudi, about the second C – Credibility. And with that, Priscilla, that’s my information for today unless you’ve got a question for me.

Priscilla:
No, I mean, that sounds great. I’m glad you’re gonna be explaining each one of these Cs.

Ivan:
Yeah, the idea is that, you know, when you’re doing all 4Cs, it really impacts the amount of referrals that you get. And Dr. Oudi has used this concept with a number of chapters, and chapter members, to assist them in dramatically increasing their referrals. So – more to come.

Priscilla:
Right. Okay, I have one thing to say about it. It just shows you how important the membership committee is in analyzing the competence of the person that they’re accepting into the chapter. So that when we use them or hire them or refer them, we already know they’re going to be good at their job.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, I used to say, that’s a given – you got to make sure that whoever you bring in is competent. But I think it’s important, and it’s why we put this in the book, it’s important to really talk about that up front, that you want to bring in people who are very competent at what they do.

Priscilla:
Right, exactly.

Ivan:
All right. Well, in upcoming podcasts, in two upcoming podcasts, we’ll cover the next two Cs.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you so much for the great information.

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla:
This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material that’s available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: The first “C” in Work Your Network with the Four Cs is Competence. Competence is a prerequisite for Credibility in terms of referral passing.

title.rendered: Episode 771: Work Your Network

content.rendered:
Synopsis

As these survey results show, most people get their business from networking—and most people have never been taught how to network.

That gap, combined with the fact that more than half of new businesses fail within their first three years, prompted Dr. Misner to write Work Your Network with the Four Cs with Dr. Oudi Abouchacra from the UAE.

Work Your Network teaches the fundamentals of networking: Competence, Credibility, Clarity and Connectivity and how they help you progress on the Referral Confidence Curve and cross the Confidence Point sooner.

Future episodes of the podcast will address each of the Four Cs individually, but don’t wait: get your copy today.

Complete Transcript of Episode 771

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I took a little vacation at my condo in Galveston for a week. It’s right on the ocean, which is really relaxing. And within the next week, I’m going to do a BNI Brazil live Instagram, which I don’t think I’ve ever done a live Instagram. So that’ll be fun to see how that plays out.

Priscilla:
Uh huh. That sounds great.

Ivan:
Plus, I’m still Zooming around the world with the hybrid chapters and the online chapters.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh. Perfect. So what is this topic about?

Ivan:
Yeah, Work Your Network is based on, this podcast is based on a book that I just had come out with Dr. Oudi from the United Arab Emirates. And it’s called “Work Your Network with the 4Cs.” And what I wanted to do with this podcast is kind of introduce the concept of the book and talk a little bit about what the 4Cs are. But over the next couple of months, I’m going to have Dr. Oudi on once or twice, and we’ll talk at length about the 4Cs and how they apply in BNI.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
So, you know, for anyone who has been in business, even for a limited time, you recognize that the percentage of referrals in business that comes to you often comes, a large percentage of it comes, from your professional network. In fact, if you’re a small business owner, no matter what sector or industry, it’s often a really significant proportion of your business that comes to you. We did a BNI online survey a few years back, almost 4000 participants worldwide, it was open to the public, and we found that 73% of businesses reported getting most of their business from networking and referrals. That’s a compelling statistic. And if we can, I’ll actually put in the graphic in this transcript. It’s a compelling statistic.  Advertising was about 12%; P.R. was less than 5%; cold calling was less than 10%. And 73% was networking and referrals.

The irony in that statistic is that more than 87% of the people in the same survey series said that they’ve never taken a college course that covered networking or word of mouth marketing. Forget about a class actually on networking; we’re talking about a course that just discussed the topic of networking. Most people get their business from networking, and most people have never been taught how to network.

This is something I’ve mentioned many times in podcasts and my books. But here’s some hard data on it. And this is really disturbing on many levels. Business students are nearly completely unaware of how crucial networking will be to their future success. Business professionals are not investing in a source of income that is potentially responsible for nearly three quarters of their livelihood—referrals. Nor are they investing the necessary time in growing their network. And this may very well be the reason that business professionals struggle, and ultimately have serious challenges in being successful in business. It’s a crazy number, over half of all businesses fail within three years.

And so it’s very important that the way that you get your business is something that you have to focus on. Which, clearly, we try to do in BNI. Many businesses are leaving opportunities on the table and these opportunities could be really the ticket to taking their business to the next level. We wanted this book, and I’ll make sure there’s a cover of the book here. It’s available on Amazon and we should have it up here on the podcast as well so you can see it here on the podcast. We wanted this book, Dr. Oudi and I, to do what colleges and universities have failed at doing. We want to empower members and others with the fundamentals for creating a powerful personal network.

And we refer to our four key fundamentals as the 4Cs. They are Competence, Credibility, Clarity, and Connectivity. Competence, Credibility, Clarity and Connectivity. In some future podcasts, I’ll dive into each of these four, so you can build a greater network and work the network effectively.

It’s important that you’re familiar with how referrals, though, take place over time. And in BNI, the educational concept that we use is called the Referral Confidence Curve. And the Referral Confidence Curve is really important with the 4Cs. We’ll also put a graphic of this in the transcript. In a nutshell, the graph that is in this transcript depicts the process of referrals received over time. It should make sense that referrals initially happen very gradually. You know, sometimes people join BNI and they think, oh, you know, I’ve been in a month, I haven’t gotten any referrals. It takes time. There is little-to-no chance that someone is going to refer you if they don’t know anything about you; it’s just not logical to expect otherwise. And it’s important to set expectations with new members to make sure that they understand that this is going to take some time to build relationships.

Ivan:
So referrals may begin to take place initially on a limited and gradual basis. However, everything begins to change when the threshold is met, called the confidence point. The confidence point is depicted as the X on the graph. And the confidence point represents a threshold that, once it’s established, changes the curve thereafter, as you can see, in the graphic that we’ll have here in the transcript. When your network has complete confidence in you, referrals will begin to flow your way with a greater magnitude. And as a result, the curve steepens after the confidence threshold is reached. The Referral Confidence Curve assumes that when people are not confident in you, your business, or your service or product, you’re not going to get many referrals. And when the confidence threshold is hit, you can see that referrals increase more steeply. Not only do they increase in quantity, but they often increase in quality, too, because this isn’t just someone trying you out, it’s someone referring others to you with confidence and passing higher quality referrals. Well, you know, we don’t want to give our best clients, our best customers, as referrals until we know that the person is going to do a good job. So it’s not uncommon for members to test you out, to see, you know, if you’re good at what you do. They may use your product or service occasionally to see if you do a good job. And these are really important.

When a member uses you, that’s really important because they can then refer you with more confidence. You hit that confidence threshold much quicker. So the key takeaways from the confidence threshold on the graph is that if you have not established confidence with your network, you shouldn’t expect consistent, or many, referrals. If you have established confidence within your network, you should expect a steady stream of high quality referrals over time. Others having confidence in you is critical to your business. In fact, it makes all the difference between a business that inevitably fails and one that ultimately thrives.

The 4Cs which, like I said I’ll be introducing in greater detail in coming podcasts, are designed to help you gain more referrals with every handshake. Because each of them increases the speed at which others will have confidence in you. Each of our Cs – Competence, Credibility, Clarity, and Connectivity, are crucial if you’re to receive referrals because when they’re applied with precision, each has the potential to accelerate the rate at which you achieve the confidence threshold and climb steeper after the threshold is met. This means they have the potential to affect referral quality, quantity, significance, and consistency.

Now that you hopefully understand more comprehensively how referrals happen over time, and the importance of reaching the confidence threshold sooner on the Referral Confidence Curve, something that we’ve talked about in the past, we’ll be covering the 4Cs in some of our upcoming podcasts. Dr. Oudi and I will talk to you about how the 4Cs of Networking will help you build a powerful personal network, so you can create the kind of success that you deserve. And when all the members of a chapter understand these 4Cs and how to implement them effectively, they are all going to be generating more business for each other, and really making your time in BNI highly successful. That’s my message for today, Priscilla. Do you have any questions or thoughts? We’ve got a minute or so.

Priscilla:
I was really glad that you mentioned having the members try their services, if possible, because I find for myself, that’s one of the best ways to judge whether this person is a good person to refer.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, if they don’t do a good job for you, you’re not going to refer them.

Priscilla:
Exactly.

Ivan:
My experience is that members really, almost always, knock themselves out for [other] members, because they want those referrals and then they really do a good job for the referrals. I remember once, true story, Priscilla. It was my house in LA. I bought a house and I had a lot of work done on it. I had a number of contractors. And they were almost all BNI contractors. There was one specialty contractor that was doing something that you know, there was no BNI member [for]. And one of the BNI members came up to me afterwards and said, “This contractor came up to us, you know, we were standing around having a break. We’re standing there, he said, ‘Why are you guys like busting your butt so hard on this job? I mean, you guys are working really hard.’  And one of them said, ‘Yeah, well, this is the founder of BNI. You know, we want to do a good job, because he’ll refer us to other people.'” And to some extent, I think that is an example of what happens with members. You know, this is a friend of mine, I’m in a networking organization with them. I want to make sure and do a good job for them.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Also, I feel that when a new member joins your chapter, you should have a really strong desire to find work for them as soon as you can. To give them confidence that they’ve made the right choice and that they’re in the right chapter and that things are gonna really work out for them.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely correct. The quicker you do that, the more likely that person is to have success and to do well. Well, I know we’re out of time. Priscilla, thank you very much. I’ll turn it back over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much, Dr. Misner, for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Work Your Network teaches you how to progress on the Referral Confidence Curve with Competence, Credibility, Clarity, and Connectivity.

title.rendered: Episode 770: From N-O to N.D.

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dr. Lisa Renz applied to join BNI the first time she went to a meeting–and the membership committee tried to decline her application without even scheduling an interview. Lisa refused to take “No” for an answer, proved she could be a great BNI member, and is now the BNI US National Director.

Is your chapter missing out on someone who could go from “N-O” to N.D.? Here’s how to say “No” without destroying business relationships or turning people against BNI.

  • Make sure that everyone on the Membership Committee goes to the trainings.
  • Be considerate and professional. Treat people the way they want to be treated.
  • Be transparent about the application process and how the Membership Committee makes decisions.
  • Let applicants know ahead of time if they are competing against others.
  • Help applicants find a different chapter if yours can’t accept them.

If your chapter is struggling with this issue, talk to your Director Consultant, or Executive Director, or Managing Director.

Complete Transcript of Episode 770

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, you know, I’m hybrid, just like the rest of the world. I just recently did a Facebook Live, which was fantastic. I last week hosted the BNI Foundation silent auction winner at my house [for] wine tasting and a dinner. And then this week, I’m doing Ivan’s Inner Circle, which is for BNI U.S. Members and Leadership Teams. So that’s just a sampling of some of the things that I’ve been doing.

Priscilla:
Oh, that sounds like fun.

Ivan: “The wine thing in particular, I would guess, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Yeah, I bet it’s great.

Ivan:
I have a guest with me today. She is a friend. Her name is Dr. Lisa Renz. Dr. Renz is the BNI US National Director. She earned a B.A. and an M.B.A. from Saint Leo University in Business Administration. She earned a Ph.D. in Organizational Leadership with a major in Entrepreneurial Leadership from Regent University’s School of Business. Lisa serves on Regent University’s Board of Advisors for the M.B.A. program, and she’s an Adjunct Professor at Regent University in the Doctor of Strategic Leadership and M.B.A. programs.

Lisa, I just was asked to teach at Concordia in their M.B.A. program and leadership. So I’m looking forward to it. And I hope you’ve enjoyed it. She also serves at Piedmont International University. And Los Angeles Pacific University. Wow. Okay. You’ve been teaching a lot, Lisa. That’s amazing. Welcome to BNI Podcast.

Lisa:
Thank you so much. I’ve definitely enjoyed it.

Ivan:
Are you still are you still doing some teaching?

Lisa:
I am, at Regent University. And I do a little bit. Yes.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, you have an amazing story. And I’d love for you to tell how you became part of BNI.

Lisa:
Yeah, oh great, thanks. Yeah, my journey began in 2004. I was working for a very successful catering company, and we did this crazy thing–we did cold calling, I’m sure you’ve heard of it. And that’s really how we got our sales. And I just hated it, you know, basically, what it would boil down to is the first one who got to the phone when it would ring got the sale. And I just really hated it. And I got really fortunate. I had a woman who was working in Mary Kay, she invited me to this networking event. I quoted her for some catering. I didn’t get the catering, but she said, “You know, you’re a pretty sharp, professional young woman. I believe if you went to this networking thing, if people got to know, like, and trust you, I think they’d refer business to you.” So I was really excited about just the idea of what that could mean for me, because I really hated cold calling. And I really got nowhere with my sales doing that.

So I was pretty excited about it. And as the time went on, over the course of the week, I ended up calling her back and I said, “Hey, I tell you what. I’ve got two other friends who are business owners, one owns a gym, one owns a fence company. I bet you they would really like to network. Is it okay if I bring them to this meeting?” And I had no idea what I was even walking into. And she’s like, oh, yeah, bring as many people as you’d like to the meeting. And I did, I showed up to the meeting with the two people that I promised her that I would attend with. I went to the meeting and I loved it. I fell in love with it. I saw how people were working together to bring business with each other, it was just such a healthier, exciting, more – it had a lot more opportunities than anything I’d ever experienced before. And I thought this is the right place for me.

Lisa:
So I was really excited about leaving the BNI meeting, I ran back to the office. And I told the business owner about this thing–BNI–that I went to and how great it was. And how I could generate referrals for our business, how I could learn more about business, and I could really expand the opportunities for our catering company.

And he said, “Lisa, you just need to calm down. I went to a BNI meeting and I didn’t get any business. You’re not going to get any business either.” And I saw the potential in it, and I said, “Hey, just trust me, just give me a shot at this and you won’t regret it.” So he said, “Okay, I tell you what. You go ahead, you do that.”  [I] filled out the application, got the check–all on the same day, like within minutes of leaving the meeting, I took care of all that.

I called the vice president [of the BNI chapter] up and I said, “Hey, I got my application, got my check. How can I get this to you?” And he said, “Just come by my office. I’ve got a closing coming by but you can just drop it off.” And I went to his office, and I diligently dropped it off.

So probably within an hour of leaving the first BNI meeting I’d ever been to, I gave the vice president the application and a check and was ready to go. And I met him at his office and he said, “I tell you what, I’ve got this closing, it’s happening soon. So I tell you what, I’ll call you back. And I’ll schedule an interview for you.” I said, “That’s fantastic.” And so I left. So two weeks go by, yes, I did say two weeks; two weeks go by and I didn’t hear from him. So I didn’t really know what to do. I didn’t go back.

Ivan:
I hate when chapters sit on the applications like that.

Lisa:
I know. After two weeks, nobody called me so I didn’t know what to do. And I said, I’m just going to call him. So I called him on the phone and I said, “Hey, Tim, I was trying to submit my application a couple weeks ago.” And I had to remind him who I was, which was absolutely awful. Had to remind him who I was, what I was applying for, and I could hear in the background papers just shuffling. And I said, You know, when are you gonna schedule that interview for me?” He said, “Let me call you right back.” So we hung up the phone, and about 30 minutes later, the phone rang, and he called me back and he said, “Hey, I just want to let you know, I just met with the membership committee, and we reviewed your application, and we decided to decline your application.” They declined my application. So that’s the N-O, you know, that’s the first ‘no’ I got in BNI. They told me no.

So, you know, I hung up the phone. And, you know, Ivan, it didn’t sit well on me. Probably less than a minute later, I decided I’m going to call him back because I just refuse to accept his decline. So I called him back and I said, “Tim, you know, I just have to let you know that I cannot accept your declining my application. Because had you interviewed me, you would have found out that I’m a very professional person. I get in the homes of the people that I work with. I get to work with my clients for weeks, for months, for even a year to plan the most important day of their lives. And when I recommend something to them, they trust me. So right now in my hand, I’m holding five referrals. I have a referral for someone who needs new flooring; I have a referral for someone who needs a new HVAC system; I have someone who needs painting. And oh, by the way, one of my couples, they’re getting ready to buy a new house. So they’re gonna need a realtor, and they’re gonna need a new mortgage. So you Tim, as the loan officer in the chapter, would you like my referral?” And I just left with that.

He said, “I tell you what, let me call you back again.” So he hung up. And honestly, Ivan, I didn’t know if he was going to wait two weeks, 30 minutes–I had no idea. But he called me back in five minutes. Five minutes later, he called me and said, “Guess what, Lisa, the chapter has decided to accept your application in the Chapter. Congratulations!”

Ivan:
Oh, that’s embarrassing.

Lisa:
It is awful. And that’s really how my BNI story got started. It makes me sad thinking of all the people who get turned down for the craziest reasons. And they would have been just phenomenal members. It’s hard for me to imagine my life without BNI. And it makes me sad to think there are a lot of people who are kind of put, unfortunately, in this position.

Ivan:
Yeah. And I know you have some ideas on how it can be done better. Because there are times where a chapter needs to say no, it could be something really clear cut, like a classification conflict. And chapters do need to say no. So you have some thoughts on the impact of the person that you’re saying no to, and on how to deal with it more effectively.

Lisa:
Yeah, I think this is really important. I think when you decline an application, the first thing you have to remember is it puts a negative attitude in that person for the person who invited them there. So they get very upset with the person invited them there. And if so, if it was an important client to them, you really could be hurting that relationship with them. You might actually be ending that business relationship or any personal relationship with them. But one of the things that I’ve found over the years, it also taints them to all BNI members, the people in the chapter, or just even any association with BNI. I’ve run into people, they say, “Oh yeah, I applied to BNI and I was turned down. I’ll never do business with anybody who’s ever associated with BNI.”

Certainly there are people who have legitimate reasons why we can’t take all people into BNI. But when you are vetting people, you have to make sure you understand the reasons why you’re turning them down. You have to be very thoughtful in the process, to make sure that you’re not impacting them in a way that’s going to really affect them and the people who refer to them long term. So we have to be very thoughtful in how we do this. And I think a lot of people are reasonable. They understand, like, if there’s several people competing for the same spot, they understand that they aren’t necessarily gonna be the one chosen. But we have to be very considerate when we decline someone [about] the impact that it’s going to have on them, for sure.

Ivan:
So how would you recommend that we decline? And I know you wanted to talk a little bit about lifelong learning and the platinum rule. You want to mention that?

Lisa:
Absolutely. Yeah, if you have to decline someone, I think one of the things — if there’s multiple people competing, it’s very easy to let them know ahead of time, “You’re competing against multiple people. We can’t necessarily take everyone, but we will work to find a chapter for you immediately or you know, even start a chapter for you.” So most people are very accepting as long as they know, we may not get into this chapter, but there are other chapters available. We’ll work hard to get a chapter for you. So most people, they can understand that.

But I think one of the things we have to remember is we have to always act with professionalism with our visitors and with our applicants. Because when we do this, we really fulfill our mission. And I know, you know, our mission of BNI is to help our members increase their business through a structured, positive, professional referral marketing program that enables them to develop long term, meaningful relationships with quality business professionals. So everyone who interacts with BNI, we should treat them with that level of professionalism so they get that experience. And then everybody that they invite as visitors have that same experience, because they really are helping us to accomplish our mission.

Ivan:
To treat them the way they want to be treated, which is Tony Alessandra’s Platinum Rule. It’s not the golden rule, treat people the way you want to be treated, but treat people the way they want to be treated.

Lisa:
Right. And I just think by having clear communication with people, let them understand the process for how we go about, accepting and declining applications, just makes people–it gets them in a professional environment and attitude, and then they don’t have bad attitudes toward BNI when things don’t necessarily work out the way that they had hoped for and anticipated. And a lot of times I explain to them, you’d be surprised how the next chapter might actually be a better fit. So you’re kind of fortunate that you have this opportunity to experience yet another chapter. So most people are very accepting if they have to get declined.

Ivan:
Yeah, and you know, if a chapter is struggling with it, for some reason or another, they should definitely talk to their Director Consultant, or Executive Director, or Managing Director — the Director in charge of that region, to get some advice on how to do it appropriately.

Lisa:
Yeah, and that’s one of the things that we have to do, we always have to practice lifelong learning in BNI. So we need to make sure that the people who are leading our chapters at all levels go to training, that they understand how to perform their roles professionally and efficiently. And they know if they don’t know how to do something, how to go to get–that there’s resources through the Director Consultant or the Executive Director to make sure that the chapter is running efficiently and effectively. So not only the members, but their visitors have a really great experience. And that’s really the things we’re striving for.

Ivan:
So you went from N-O to N-D, the National Director for BNI in the United States. If you could just real briefly give us a few of the roles that you’ve served to go from NO to ND.

Lisa:
It’s really ironic. So here I am, the person who was told no to join the chapter. And who do you think they call six months later and said, “Hey, we need someone to serve as Vice President.” It was me. Yes. It’s really funny. So my very first leadership role in BNI was Vice President, but I have served in every role in BNI, except for Area Director. So I made sure I served in every support position. I was an Executive Director. So that way, I understand exactly what our leaders that run our organization experience. So the only thing I haven’t done is Area Director. So I’m very proud of the fact that there isn’t anything in BNI in between that I haven’t done.

Ivan:
Well, that’s pretty amazing. And, Lisa, thank you so much for all the work you do in BNI, and your work as the National Director for BNI. And I appreciate you being on the podcast today.

Lisa:
Thank you so much for having me on. I really appreciate it.

Ivan:
Thanks. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you. I think that’s it for this week. Thank you for the great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
